###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Line Printer reports and column headings

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-08-02T01:23:38+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f2a6be3_4@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<RQPVa.80500$0v4.5346018@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bg9950$4ru$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <fvXVa.81042$0v4.5381637@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vij52hndkjkla4@corp.supernews.com> <vthWa.82384$0v4.5490927@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vijjokjr40bd80@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Dennis, Daniel, 

I have never used Report Writer (to my shame...) but I know Bill rates it, and after seeing the posts from you both it made me start thinking about what I do with reports.

1. These days I believe the answer to COBOL reporting is to invoke a Word Processor.  (Use COM/OLE; problem solved...)

Let's rule that out as being non-practical for the site you are working on...

2. I looked with interest at Dennis's idea of including headings in the printline and I see merit in it. However, it DOES mean rebuilding the headings every time you use them, when all you really need to"rebuild" may be the Page number. (and the Time, if present...)

3. I wondered why I don't get excited about reporting in COBOL and then I remembered...I solved this problem 24 years ago <G>.

Here's some fairly long Procedural COBOL that provides a complete solution. It allows you to set up some headings (up to 10), vary the spacing on printlines, automatically detects the date (if present) and the Page Number  (if present),  and the time (if present), allows you to simultaneously print various documents (so you could run several reports off ONE pass of the data, at the same time), and automatically takes care of line counts and heading production, unless you manually override it. It is geared to 11 inch lineflo, but you can easily change that by changing the default line count. 

If you place the words "Date:" "Time:" and "Page:"  anywhere within the (up to) 10 headings you are allowed to pass, the module will automatically detect them and place the appropriate value following the colon.

I'm a bit embarrassed by this as I wrote it originally in the 70s for the IBM mainframe environment, then converted it to PC COBOL in the late 80s. I used it through the 90s until I started using OLE/COM to invoke the Word Processor and print standard A4 pages in a Word processor, using a Word Template, around 1999.

This code was written BEFORE COBOL II. It has NO scope delimiters, it uses full stops on statements, it uses GO TO (even with DEPENDING ..ON), and it is coded mainly in that horrible Upper Case that always looks like it is SHOUTING!!! 

Possibly worst of all, it contains duplicated code...<shudder...>

However, PERFORMs ALWAYS reference a SECTION and there is only one drop throughs which is immediately obvious (but then, I WOULD say that <G>...)...

The module calls 2 other modules which I haven't bothered to include.

In case you actually want to try running it, you could replace these module calls as follows:

AVMOVE - replace this with reference modification. It was an assembler routine I wrote in both BAL and 8086 MASM to do a "variable length MOVE" in COBOL. It took a source field and an offset, a target field and an offset and a length. Refmodding does the same and obsoleted the AVMOVE routines when it became available.

ACEDJUL - a date routine that took a Julian date and provided it as DD MMM YYYY. Since replaced by a universal date component that takes a date in any one of 8 different formats, validates and returns it in the other 7...

It's no good beating me up; I wouldn't code it like this today...<G>

It has run without error on IBM mainframes and PCs for decades. (That's all I'm going to say about it...maybe somebody might like to "modernize" it, using the same basic functional ideas? I leave that as an exercise for the reader...<G>)

For what it's worth, here goes....

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.      02/06/79
 PROGRAM-ID.       ACEPR132.
 INSTALLATION.     
 AUTHOR.           PETER E. C. DASHWOOD
 DATE-WRITTEN.     FEB '79.
*REMARKS.
******************************************************************
*
*    *** ACEPRINT ***
*
*       (A STANDARD PRINT MODULE FOR BATCH PROGRAMS
******************************************************************
*   MODIFIED FOR THE PC ENVIRONMENT IN FEB '92.
*    1. PRINT LINE CHANGED TO 132 COLUMNS for using 17 char per
*       inch printing on 132 col mode LPT1:
*    2. BP132-LINES-REM used to pass the page line limit (60 or
*       80) to support 6 or 8 lines per inch. (First call only).
******************************************************************
*
*THIS IS A COBOL SUBPROGRAM WHICH HANDLES PRINTING FOR BATCH
*REPORTS. PARAMETERS ARE PASSED BY THE CALLING PROGRAM THROUGH
*AN INTERFACE AREA. THE LINE AND PAGE COUNTS IN THIS AREA ARE
*UPDATED BY THIS MODULE.
*
*   THIS MODULE OFFERS THE FOLLOWING FEATURES:
*
*   1. PRINTING IS OPTIMISED FOR THE IBM ENVIRONMENT.
*
*   2. HEADINGS ARE PRODUCED AUTOMATICALLY WHEN REQUIRED.
*
*   3. MULTIPLE SEPARATE DOCUMENTS (UP TO 3) CAN BE PRINTED
*      FROM THE SAME PROGRAM. (THIS ENABLES REPORTS WITH SIMILAR
*      DATA TO BE PRODUCED FROM A SINGLE PROGRAM, OR MULTIPLE
*      COPIES OF THE SAME REPORT).
*
*   4. THE DATE, TIME, AND PAGE NUMBER ARE INSERTED IN THE
*      HEADINGS AUTOMATICALLY BY THIS MODULE, AT THE CORRECT
*      PLACE.
*
*   5. SHOULD A CHANGE TO DIFFERENT STATIONERY BE REQUIRED AT SOME
*      FUTURE TIME (E.G. FROM 11 INCH TO 12 INCH) ONLY THIS
*      MODULE WOULD NEED TO BE RE-COMPILED (WITH A NEW LINE-LIMIT)
*
*    *******************************************************
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
 SOURCE-COMPUTER. IBM-370.
 OBJECT-COMPUTER. IBM-370.
 SPECIAL-NAMES.
    C01 IS HEAD-OF-PAGE.
 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
 FILE-CONTROL.
     SELECT DOC1    ASSIGN TO DISK
     ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL
     FILE STATUS IS COMMON-STAT.
     SELECT DOC2    ASSIGN TO DISK
     ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL
     FILE STATUS IS COMMON-STAT.
     SELECT DOC3    ASSIGN TO DISK
     ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL
     FILE STATUS IS COMMON-STAT.
 DATA DIVISION.
 FILE SECTION.
 FD   DOC1
     VALUE OF FILE-ID 'DOC1.DOC'
     DATA RECORD IS DOC1-LINE.
 01   DOC1-LINE.
*   PRINT CONTROL CHARACTER CONFUSES PC WHEN MULTIPLE PRINT LINES
*   ARE USED SO WE COMMENT IT OUT....
*     12 PCC1       PIC X.
     12 DOC1-LINE-DATA  PIC X(132).
 FD   DOC2
     VALUE OF FILE-ID 'DOC2.DOC'
     DATA RECORD IS DOC2-LINE.
 01   DOC2-LINE.
*     12 PCC2       PIC X.
     12 DOC2-LINE-DATA PIC X(132).
 FD   DOC3
     VALUE OF FILE-ID 'DOC2.DOC'
     DATA RECORD IS DOC3-LINE.
 01   DOC3-LINE.
*     12 PCC3       PIC X.
     12 DOC3-LINE-DATA PIC X(132).
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01  SUBSCRIPTS USAGE COMPUTATIONAL.
    12 J        PIC S9(4) VALUE ZERO.
    12 K        PIC S9(4) VALUE ZERO.
    12 L        PIC S9(4) VALUE ZERO.
 01  LINE-LIMIT       PIC S9(4) COMP VALUE 60.
 01  LINE-SPACE       PIC S9(4) COMP.
 01  COMMON-STAT       PIC 99 VALUE ZERO.
 01  TABLE-OF-FILE-STATUSES.
    12 FS       PIC X OCCURS 3
        INDEXED BY FS-X1.
 01  SAVED-BP132-ACTION      PIC X.
 01  ACEDJUL-PARMS.      

    12 ADJUL-REPLY-CODE        PIC X.   
       88 ADJUL-OK  VALUE SPACE.   
       88 ADJUL-BAD-PARM-CT VALUE '5'.   
       88 ADJUL-BAD-JULIAN VALUE '4'.   
       88 ADJUL-BAD-ACE-DATE VALUE '3'.   
       88 ADJUL-BAD-YYYYMMDD VALUE '2'.   
    12 ADJUL-YYYYMMDD        PIC X(8).   
    12 ADJUL-ACE-DATE.      
       15 ADJUL-ACE-DD        PIC XX.   
       15 ADJUL-ACE-MMM        PIC XXX.   
       15 ADJUL-ACE-YY        PIC XX.   
    12 ADJUL-DATE-JULIAN        PIC X(5).   
    12 ADJUL-JULIAN-ALPHA REDEFINES ADJUL-DATE-JULIAN PIC X(5).
         
 01  BP132-HED-WORK     PIC X(1335).
 01  FLAGS.
    12 FIRST-TIME-FLAG PIC X VALUE ZERO.
       88 INITIALISING     VALUE ZERO.
 01  WS-ED-PAGE       PIC Z(3)9.
 01  DUMMY       PIC X.
 01  WS-DATE       PIC 9(6).
 01  WS-DATE-ALPHA REDEFINES WS-DATE PIC X(6).
 01  WS-ED-DATE       PIC XXBXXXBXX.
 01  FILLER REDEFINES WS-ED-DATE.
    12 WDD       PIC XX.
    12 HYPHEN1       PIC X.
    12 WMMM       PIC XXX.
    12 HYPHEN2       PIC X.
    12 WYY       PIC XX.
 01  WS-TIME    PIC 9(8).
 01  WS-TIME-ALPHA REDEFINES WS-TIME.
    12 WST-GROUP.
       15 WST-HH       PIC 99.
       15 WST-MM       PIC 99.
       15 WST-SS       PIC 99.
    12 WST-HHMMSS REDEFINES WST-GROUP PIC 9(6).
    12 FILLER       PIC XX.
 01  WS-ED-TIME       PIC 99B99B99.
 01  FILLER REDEFINES WS-ED-TIME.
    12 FILLER       PIC XX.
    12 COLON1       PIC X.
    12 FILLER       PIC XX.
    12 COLON2       PIC X.
    12 FILLER       PIC XX.
 01  XK USAGE IS INDEX.
    EJECT
 LINKAGE SECTION.
*
*
*     COPY ACEPR132.PRM.
*
*> I have included the COPY Book here for completeness...Pete
*
***********************************************************
*       BATCH PRINT INTERFACE AREA (132 CPL) *
***********************************************************
       01  BATCH-PRINT-INTERF132.
    12 BP132-REPLY-CODE PIC X.
       88 BP132-OK   VALUE SPACE.
       88 BP132-SYSTEM-ERROR  VALUE 'E'.
       88 BP132-INVALID-PARMS  VALUE 'L'.
    12 BP132-DOCUMENT-NO      PIC 9.
    12 BP132-DOC-NO-ALPHA REDEFINES BP132-DOCUMENT-NO
          PIC X.
*   VALID DOCUMENT NUMBERS ARE 1 - 3
    12 BP132-ACTION      PIC X.
       88 BP132-PRINT   VALUE 'P'.
       88 BP132-HOP-PRINT  VALUE 'H'.
       88 BP132-CLOSE   VALUE 'C'.
*
*   THE ACTIONS ARE AS FOLLOWS:
*
*   'P' => PRINT THEN SPACE THE NUMBER OF LINES IN 'SPACE-AFTER'.
*
*   'H' => SKIP TO HEAD OF PAGE; PRINT THE HEADINGS WITH
*   DATE, TIME, AND PAGE NUMBER; PRINT THE PASSED
*   PRINT LINE AFTER SPACING THE NUMBER OF LINES
*   SPECIFIED IN THE FIRST CHARACTER OF THE LAST
*   HEADING LINE; SPACE THE NUMBER OF LINES IN
*   'SPACE-AFTER'.
*
*   THIS ACTION CAN BE USED TO FORCE 'HEAD OF PAGE'.
*
*   THIS ACTION IS ALWAYS TAKEN ON THE FIRST CALL TO
*   ACEPRINT AND IS CARRIED OUT AUTOMATICALLY WHEN
*   PAGE OVERFLOW OCCURS.
*
*   IT IS THEREFORE NOT NECESSARY FOR APPLICATIONS TO
*   USE THIS ACTION CODE UNLESS THEY REQUIRE TO FORCE
*   HEAD OF PAGE IN THE MIDDLE OF A REPORT (MAYBE
*   BECAUSE OF A CONTROL BREAK OR SUB-HEADING CHANGE)
*
*   'C' => CLOSE A DOCUMENT.  THIS CALL SHOULD BE ISSUED WHEN
*   APPLICATION PROCESSING IS COMPLETE.
*
*   THIS RESETS THE OUTPUT FILE SO THAT SUBSEQUENT CALLS
*   USING THE SAME DOCUMENT NUMBER WILL BEGIN AGAIN FROM
*   PAGE 1 (NOTE THAT ANY PREVIOUS DOCUMENT WILL BE
*   OVERWRITTEN IF IT HAS NOT BEEN PRINTED OFF).  ISSUING
*   THIS CALL WITH A DOCUMENT NUMBER OF ZERO WILL CLOSE ALL
*   CURRENTLY OPEN DOCUMENTS.
*
*
    12 BP132-SPACE-AFTER      PIC 9.
    12 BP132-SPA-ALPHA REDEFINES BP132-SPACE-AFTER
       PIC X.
*   CONTAINS THE NUMBER OF LINES TO SPACE AFTER PRINTING THE
*   PASSED PRINT LINE
    12 BP132-PRINT-LINE      PIC X(132).
*   THE PASSED PRINT LINE. THIS LINE WILL BE PRINTED THEN
*   SPACING WILL OCCUR.
    12 BP132-HEADINGS      PIC X(1330).
*   UP TO 10 133 CHARACTER HEADINGS AND SUB-HEADINGS CAN BE
*   PASSED IN THIS AREA. THE FIRST CHARACTER OF EACH HEADING
*   LINE WILL CONTAIN THE NUMBER OF LINES TO BE SPACED AFTER
*   PRINTING THE HEADING LINE.
    12 FILLER REDEFINES BP132-HEADINGS.
       15 BP132-HED  OCCURS 10
                     INDEXED BY BP132-X1.
          18 BP132-HED-SPACE    PIC X.
          18 BP132-HED-DATA     PIC X(132).
    12 FILLER REDEFINES BP132-HEADINGS.
       15 BH132-CHARACTERS OCCURS 1330
                           INDEXED BY BH132-X1.
          18 BH132-CHAR  PIC X.
    12 BP132-RETURNED-FIELDS.
       15 BP132-PAGE-NUMBER    PIC S9(4) COMP.
       15 BP132-LINES-REM      PIC S9(4) COMP.
       15 BP132-NO-HEDS        PIC S9(4) COMP.
       15 BP132-DATE-OFFSET    PIC S9(4) COMP.
       15 BP132-TIME-OFFSET    PIC S9(4) COMP.
       15 BP132-PAGE-OFFSET    PIC S9(4) COMP.
       15 LINE-COUNT-132       PIC S9(4) COMP.
       15 FILLER      PIC X(10).
*
    SKIP3
**********************************************************
/
 PROCEDURE DIVISION
       USING
       BATCH-PRINT-INTERF132.
 MAINLINE  SECTION.
 VALIDATE-INTERFACE.
     IF BP132-DOC-NO-ALPHA < '1'
        OR
        BP132-DOC-NO-ALPHA > '3'
          MOVE 'L' TO BP132-REPLY-CODE
          GO TO RETURN-TO-CALLER.
     IF NOT (BP132-PRINT OR BP132-HOP-PRINT OR BP132-CLOSE)
        MOVE 'L' TO BP132-REPLY-CODE
        GO TO RETURN-TO-CALLER.
     IF BP132-SPA-ALPHA < '0'
        OR
        BP132-SPA-ALPHA > '3'
          MOVE 'L' TO BP132-REPLY-CODE
          GO TO RETURN-TO-CALLER.
 CHECK-FOR-FIRST-CALL.
*
*   IF ITS THE FIRST TIME INTO THIS MODULE WE CAN INITIALISE
*   THE FILE STATUSES AND OPEN THE DOCUMENT BEING CALLED
*
     MOVE SPACE TO BP132-REPLY-CODE.
     MOVE BP132-ACTION TO SAVED-BP132-ACTION.
     IF NOT INITIALISING
        GO TO DO-IT-NOW.
     IF BP132-LINES-REM > ZERO
        AND
        BP132-LINES-REM < 90
          MOVE BP132-LINES-REM TO LINE-LIMIT.
     MOVE SPACES TO TABLE-OF-FILE-STATUSES.
     MOVE '1' TO FIRST-TIME-FLAG.
     MOVE LOW-VALUES TO BP132-RETURNED-FIELDS.
     MOVE 'H' TO BP132-ACTION.
 DO-IT-NOW.
     SET FS-X1 TO BP132-DOCUMENT-NO.
     IF  FS (FS-X1) NOT = SPACE
         GO TO CHECK-ACTION.
 SET-UP-DOC.
     GO TO
        OPEN1
        OPEN2
        OPEN3
           DEPENDING ON BP132-DOCUMENT-NO.
 CHECK-ACTION.
     IF BP132-CLOSE
        IF BP132-DOCUMENT-NO = ZERO
           MOVE SPACE TO FIRST-TIME-FLAG
           GO TO CLOSE-ALL-OPEN-FILES
        ELSE
           GO TO
              CLOSE1
              CLOSE2
              CLOSE3
                 DEPENDING ON BP132-DOCUMENT-NO.
 PRINT-OR-PRINT-HOP.
     IF BP132-HOP-PRINT
        OR
        LINE-COUNT-132 > LINE-LIMIT
           PERFORM PREPARE-HEADINGS.
     GO TO
        ACTION1
        ACTION2
        ACTION3
           DEPENDING ON BP132-DOCUMENT-NO.
*-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 ACTION1 SECTION.
 ACTION-DOC1.
     IF BP132-HOP-PRINT
        OR
        LINE-COUNT-132 > LINE-LIMIT
           MOVE SPACES TO DOC1-LINE
           WRITE DOC1-LINE AFTER HEAD-OF-PAGE
           IF COMMON-STAT NOT = ZERO
              MOVE COMMON-STAT TO BP132-PAGE-NUMBER
              MOVE 'E' TO BP132-REPLY-CODE
              GO TO RETURN-TO-CALLER
           ELSE
              MOVE 1 TO LINE-COUNT-132
              PERFORM PRINT-HEDS1
                 VARYING BP132-X1
                    FROM 1
                      BY 1
                   UNTIL BP132-X1 > BP132-NO-HEDS.
     MOVE BP132-PRINT-LINE TO DOC1-LINE-DATA.
     MOVE BP132-SPACE-AFTER  TO LINE-SPACE.
     PERFORM PRINT-DOC1.
     GO TO RETURN-TO-CALLER.
*----------------------------------------------------------
 PRINT-HEDS1 SECTION.
 SET-HEDS1.
     MOVE BP132-HED-DATA (BP132-X1) TO DOC1-LINE-DATA.
     MOVE BP132-HED-SPACE (BP132-X1) TO LINE-SPACE.
     PERFORM PRINT-DOC1.
 PRINT-DOC1 SECTION.
 SET-DOC1.
     WRITE DOC1-LINE BEFORE LINE-SPACE.
     IF COMMON-STAT NOT = ZERO
        MOVE COMMON-STAT TO BP132-PAGE-NUMBER
        MOVE 'E' TO BP132-REPLY-CODE
        GO TO RETURN-TO-CALLER.
     COMPUTE LINE-COUNT-132 = LINE-COUNT-132 +
                              LINE-SPACE.
     COMPUTE BP132-LINES-REM = LINE-LIMIT - LINE-COUNT-132.
**************** END OF ACTION1 ********************************
    EJECT
 ACTION2 SECTION.
 ACTION-DOC2.
     IF BP132-HOP-PRINT
        OR
        LINE-COUNT-132 > LINE-LIMIT
           MOVE SPACES TO DOC2-LINE
           WRITE DOC2-LINE AFTER HEAD-OF-PAGE
           IF COMMON-STAT NOT = ZERO
              MOVE COMMON-STAT TO BP132-PAGE-NUMBER
              MOVE 'E' TO BP132-REPLY-CODE
              GO TO RETURN-TO-CALLER
           ELSE
              MOVE 1 TO LINE-COUNT-132
              PERFORM PRINT-HEDS2
                 VARYING BP132-X1
                    FROM 1
                      BY 1
                   UNTIL BP132-X1 > BP132-NO-HEDS.
     MOVE BP132-PRINT-LINE TO DOC2-LINE-DATA.
     MOVE BP132-SPACE-AFTER  TO LINE-SPACE.
     PERFORM PRINT-DOC2.
     GO TO RETURN-TO-CALLER.
*--------------------------------------------------------------
 PRINT-HEDS2 SECTION.
 SET-HEDS2.
     MOVE BP132-HED-DATA  (BP132-X1) TO DOC2-LINE-DATA.
     MOVE BP132-HED-SPACE (BP132-X1) TO LINE-SPACE.
     PERFORM PRINT-DOC2.
 PRINT-DOC2 SECTION.
 SET-DOC2.
     WRITE DOC2-LINE BEFORE LINE-SPACE.
     IF COMMON-STAT NOT = ZERO
        MOVE COMMON-STAT TO BP132-PAGE-NUMBER
        MOVE 'E' TO BP132-REPLY-CODE
        GO TO RETURN-TO-CALLER.
     COMPUTE LINE-COUNT-132 = LINE-COUNT-132 +
                              LINE-SPACE.
     COMPUTE BP132-LINES-REM = LINE-LIMIT - LINE-COUNT-132.
**************** END OF ACTION2 ********************************
    EJECT
 ACTION3 SECTION.
 ACTION-DOC3.
     IF BP132-HOP-PRINT
        OR
        LINE-COUNT-132 > LINE-LIMIT
           MOVE SPACES TO DOC3-LINE
           WRITE DOC3-LINE AFTER HEAD-OF-PAGE
           IF COMMON-STAT NOT = ZERO
              MOVE COMMON-STAT TO BP132-PAGE-NUMBER
              MOVE 'E' TO BP132-REPLY-CODE
              GO TO RETURN-TO-CALLER
           ELSE
              MOVE 1 TO LINE-COUNT-132
              PERFORM PRINT-HEDS3
                 VARYING BP132-X1
                    FROM 1
                      BY 1
                   UNTIL BP132-X1 > BP132-NO-HEDS.
     MOVE BP132-PRINT-LINE TO DOC3-LINE-DATA.
     MOVE BP132-SPACE-AFTER  TO LINE-SPACE.
     PERFORM PRINT-DOC3.
     GO TO RETURN-TO-CALLER.
*-------------------------------------------------------
 PRINT-HEDS3 SECTION.
 SET-HEDS3.
     MOVE BP132-HED-DATA (BP132-X1) TO DOC3-LINE-DATA.
     MOVE BP132-HED-SPACE (BP132-X1)  TO LINE-SPACE.
     PERFORM PRINT-DOC3.
 PRINT-DOC3 SECTION.
 SET-DOC3.
     WRITE DOC3-LINE BEFORE LINE-SPACE.
     IF COMMON-STAT NOT = ZERO
        MOVE COMMON-STAT TO BP132-PAGE-NUMBER
        MOVE 'E' TO BP132-REPLY-CODE
        GO TO RETURN-TO-CALLER.
     COMPUTE LINE-COUNT-132 = LINE-COUNT-132 +
                              LINE-SPACE.
     COMPUTE BP132-LINES-REM = LINE-LIMIT - LINE-COUNT-132.
**************** END OF ACTION3 ********************************
 RETURN-TO-CALLER SECTION.
 EXIT-THIS-PROGRAM.
     MOVE SAVED-BP132-ACTION TO BP132-ACTION.
     EXIT PROGRAM.
**************** FILE OPEN ROUTINES ****************************
 OPEN1 SECTION.
 OPEN-IT1.
     OPEN OUTPUT DOC1.
     IF COMMON-STAT NOT = ZERO
        MOVE COMMON-STAT TO BP132-PAGE-NUMBER
        MOVE 'E' TO BP132-REPLY-CODE
        GO TO RETURN-TO-CALLER.
     MOVE '1' TO FS (1).
     GO TO CHECK-ACTION.
 OPEN2 SECTION.
 OPEN-IT2.
     OPEN OUTPUT DOC2.
     IF COMMON-STAT NOT = ZERO
        MOVE COMMON-STAT TO BP132-PAGE-NUMBER
        MOVE 'E' TO BP132-REPLY-CODE
        GO TO RETURN-TO-CALLER.
     MOVE '1' TO FS (2).
     GO TO CHECK-ACTION.
 OPEN3 SECTION.
 OPEN-IT3.
     OPEN OUTPUT DOC3.
     IF COMMON-STAT NOT = ZERO
        MOVE COMMON-STAT TO BP132-PAGE-NUMBER
        MOVE 'E' TO BP132-REPLY-CODE
        GO TO RETURN-TO-CALLER.
     GO TO CHECK-ACTION.
     MOVE '1' TO FS (3).
*****************  FILE CLOSE ROUTINES  **************************
 CLOSE1 SECTION.
 CLOSE-IT1.
     CLOSE DOC1.
     IF COMMON-STAT NOT = ZERO
        MOVE COMMON-STAT TO BP132-PAGE-NUMBER
        MOVE 'E' TO BP132-REPLY-CODE.
     MOVE SPACE TO FS (1).
     GO TO RETURN-TO-CALLER.
 CLOSE2 SECTION.
 CLOSE-IT2.
     CLOSE DOC2.
     IF COMMON-STAT NOT = ZERO
        MOVE COMMON-STAT TO BP132-PAGE-NUMBER
        MOVE 'E' TO BP132-REPLY-CODE.
     MOVE SPACE TO FS (2).
     GO TO RETURN-TO-CALLER.
 CLOSE3 SECTION.
 CLOSE-IT3.
     CLOSE DOC3.
     IF COMMON-STAT NOT = ZERO
        MOVE COMMON-STAT TO BP132-PAGE-NUMBER
        MOVE 'E' TO BP132-REPLY-CODE.
     MOVE SPACE TO FS (3).
     GO TO RETURN-TO-CALLER.
 CLOSE-ALL-OPEN-FILES SECTION.
 CLOSE-ALL.
     IF FS (1)  = '1'
        CLOSE DOC1
        IF COMMON-STAT NOT = ZERO
           MOVE COMMON-STAT TO BP132-PAGE-NUMBER
           MOVE 'E' TO BP132-REPLY-CODE
           GO TO RETURN-TO-CALLER
        ELSE
           MOVE SPACE TO FS (1).
     IF FS (2)  = '1'
        CLOSE DOC2
        IF COMMON-STAT NOT = ZERO
           MOVE COMMON-STAT TO BP132-PAGE-NUMBER
           MOVE 'E' TO BP132-REPLY-CODE
           GO TO RETURN-TO-CALLER
        ELSE
           MOVE SPACE TO FS (2).
           IF FS (3)  = '1'
              CLOSE DOC3
              IF COMMON-STAT NOT = ZERO
                 MOVE COMMON-STAT TO BP132-PAGE-NUMBER
                 MOVE 'E' TO BP132-REPLY-CODE
                 GO TO RETURN-TO-CALLER
              ELSE
                 MOVE SPACE TO FS (3).
/
 PREPARE-HEADINGS SECTION.
 PREP-NOTE.
*
*   THIS SECTION CONTROLS PAGE NUMBERING, DATE, AND TIME SETTING
*   FOR THE HEADINGS.
*
*   CALCULATE THE NUMBER OF HEADINGS FIRST...
     PERFORM LOOKUP
        VARYING BP132-X1
           FROM 1
             BY 1
          UNTIL BP132-HED-SPACE (BP132-X1) = SPACE.
     SET BP132-X1       DOWN BY 1.
     SET BP132-NO-HEDS  TO BP132-X1.
     COMPUTE L = BP132-NO-HEDS * 133
     MOVE ZERO TO
               BP132-DATE-OFFSET
               BP132-TIME-OFFSET
               BP132-PAGE-OFFSET.
     SET BH132-X1 TO L.
     SET BH132-X1 UP BY 1.
     MOVE ':' TO BH132-CHAR (BH132-X1).
     SET BH132-X1 TO 1.
     GO TO CALCULATE-OFFSETS.
 LOOKUP.
     EXIT.
 CALCULATE-OFFSETS.
     SEARCH BH132-CHARACTERS
        AT END GO TO CO-EXIT
           WHEN BH132-CHAR (BH132-X1) = ':'
                NEXT SENTENCE.
     IF BH132-X1 > L
        MOVE SPACE TO BH132-CHAR (BH132-X1)
        GO TO CO-EXIT.
     SET XK TO BH132-X1.
     SET BH132-X1 DOWN BY 1.
     PERFORM LOOKUP
        VARYING BH132-X1
           FROM BH132-X1
             BY -1
          UNTIL BH132-CHAR (BH132-X1) NOT = SPACE.
     IF BH132-CHAR (BH132-X1) NOT = 'E'
*   IT'S NOT 'PAGE, DATE, OR TIME'
        SET BH132-X1 TO XK
        SET BH132-X1 UP BY 1
        GO TO CO-IGNORE
     ELSE
        SET BH132-X1 DOWN BY 1.
     IF BH132-CHAR (BH132-X1) NOT = 'G'
        IF BH132-CHAR (BH132-X1) NOT = 'T'
           IF BH132-CHAR (BH132-X1) NOT = 'M'
              SET BH132-X1 TO XK
              SET BH132-X1 UP BY 1
              GO TO CO-IGNORE
           ELSE
*   IT COULD BE 'TIME'....
              IF BH132-CHAR (BH132-X1 - 1) = 'I'
                 AND
                 BH132-CHAR (BH132-X1 - 2) = 'T'
                 AND
                 BH132-CHAR (BH132-X1 - 3) = ' '
                    SET BH132-X1 TO XK
                    SET BH132-X1 UP BY 1
                    SET BP132-TIME-OFFSET TO BH132-X1
              ELSE
                 SET BH132-X1 TO XK
                 SET BH132-X1 UP BY 1
                 GO TO CO-IGNORE
        ELSE
*   IT COULD BE 'DATE'...
           IF BH132-CHAR (BH132-X1 - 1) = 'A'
              AND
              BH132-CHAR (BH132-X1 - 2) = 'D'
              AND
              BH132-CHAR (BH132-X1 - 3) = ' '
                 SET BH132-X1 TO XK
                 SET BH132-X1 UP BY 1
                 SET BP132-DATE-OFFSET TO BH132-X1
           ELSE
              SET BH132-X1 TO XK
              SET BH132-X1 UP BY 1
              GO TO CO-IGNORE
     ELSE
*   IT COULD BE 'PAGE'...
        IF BH132-CHAR (BH132-X1 - 1) = 'A'
           AND
           BH132-CHAR (BH132-X1 - 2) = 'P'
           AND
           BH132-CHAR (BH132-X1 - 3) = ' '
              SET BH132-X1 TO XK
              SET BH132-X1 UP BY 1
              SET BP132-PAGE-OFFSET TO BH132-X1
        ELSE
           SET BH132-X1 TO XK
           SET BH132-X1 UP BY 1.
 CO-IGNORE.
     GO TO CALCULATE-OFFSETS.
 CO-EXIT.
     MOVE BP132-HEADINGS TO BP132-HED-WORK.
     IF BP132-DATE-OFFSET NOT = ZERO
        PERFORM STRING-THE-DATE
        MOVE ZERO TO J
        MOVE 9 TO L
        CALL 'AVMOVE'  USING  WS-ED-DATE
                              J
                              BP132-HED-WORK
                              BP132-DATE-OFFSET
                              L.
     IF BP132-TIME-OFFSET NOT = ZERO
        ACCEPT WS-TIME FROM TIME
        MOVE WST-HHMMSS TO WS-ED-TIME
        MOVE ':' TO COLON1  COLON2
        MOVE ZERO TO J
        MOVE 8 TO L
        CALL 'AVMOVE'  USING  WS-ED-TIME
                              J
                              BP132-HED-WORK
                              BP132-TIME-OFFSET
                              L.
     IF BP132-PAGE-OFFSET NOT = ZERO
        COMPUTE BP132-PAGE-NUMBER = BP132-PAGE-NUMBER + 1
        MOVE BP132-PAGE-NUMBER TO WS-ED-PAGE
        INSPECT WS-ED-PAGE TALLYING J
           FOR LEADING SPACES
        COMPUTE L = 4 - J
        CALL 'AVMOVE'  USING  WS-ED-PAGE
                              J
                              BP132-HED-WORK
                              BP132-PAGE-OFFSET
                              L.
     MOVE BP132-HED-WORK TO BP132-HEADINGS.
*------------------------------------------------------------
 STRING-THE-DATE SECTION.
 BUILD-WS-ED-DATE.
     MOVE SPACES TO ACEDJUL-PARMS.
     ACCEPT WS-DATE FROM DATE.
     IF WS-DATE-ALPHA > '900000'
        STRING '19' WS-DATE-ALPHA DELIMITED BY SIZE
        INTO ADJUL-YYYYMMDD
     ELSE
        STRING '20' WS-DATE-ALPHA DELIMITED BY SIZE
        INTO ADJUL-YYYYMMDD.
     CALL 'ACEDJUL' USING
     ACEDJUL-PARMS.
     MOVE ADJUL-ACE-DD  TO WDD.
     ADJUL-ACE-MMM  TO WMMM.
     MOVE ADJUL-ACE-YY  TO WYY.
     MOVE '-'     TO HYPHEN1
                     HYPHEN2.
       

So, there you have it.

This is a prime example of Ancient COBOL. It incorporates all the things we have since learned NOT to do...

But, I wonder...was it REALLY so bad...<G>? 

Pete.
```

Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:

>  DATE-WRITTEN.     FEB '79.

<snicker />

> So, there you have it.
>  
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> But, I wonder...was it REALLY so bad...<G>?

You know, I'm not so convinced that it was.  25-year-old code still 
works the same as it did back in '79 when you wrote it.  I may talk 
about styles and such, but I'm usually pretty respectful of existing 
code.  If it's doing something inefficiently, I may reengineer it, and 
if it violates our current coding standards, I may standardize it. 
However, I always make sure that I don't break what it did - for then, 
I'm not maintaining, I'm causing maintenance to be needed.  :)

Thanks for sharing this code...  I'm going to go back and digest it in a 
bit.
```

#### ↳ Re: Line Printer reports and column headings

- **From:** codecrafters@cix.compulink.co.uk
- **Date:** 2003-08-18T23:18:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bhrmso$j22$1@thorium.cix.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<RQPVa.80500$0v4.5346018@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Um, not criticising for the sake of it, but: this is very handy for 
simpler stuff but it tends to get very awkward for groups of shorter 
fields (where headings often overlap) when it ends up a right old 
mishmash.

The new standard offers very long lines of code, which might reduce the 
utility of my little trick, but I think it's still valid: leave a "ruler" 
line (or lines, probably) in the program, sort-of thusly (I just show one, 
but you can see the idea:

01  WD-Print-Lines.
    05  WD-PH1-Print-Header-1.
        10  WD-PH1-CC               Pic X(001) Value '1'.
        10  WD-PH1-Data             Pic X(132) Value 
*       '----+----1----+----2----+----3----+----4----+----5 etc. etc.
        ' Actual Report First Line.                         etc. etc.      
  
Then, when you need to change the layout, move the detail line fields 
about (to columns you know) and do the same with your headings. Simple - 
but it does work, and like your technique (but with much less code) it 
saves all that tedious mucking about with looking at what column you're in 
on the screen and working out which column that is on the paper. Give it a 
try :-)

Sometimes there's an argument for using reference modification for filling 
in report fields - it works brilliantly for interactively-defined reports 
- but I confess I prefer to see named fields if possible as I can then see 
what editing gets done right on the spot. 

Pete.
```

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>

In article <viravq6omnaaa6@corp.supernews.com>,
 LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote:

> Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
> 
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> . Exit Message.

I-m-on-MVS-OS390-zOS.

   Designed in the 60's using all the tricks learned from the 50's.  And 
has not changed much in its fundamental assumptions since.  80 and 133 
are still magic numbers in the system.  Newlines/CR/LF have been bolted 
onto the side, but they still act like an add-on.

   But they are virtual, I haven't seen a real one since 1994 (when a 
christmas wreath was made out of an object deck by a coworker)...and I 
tossed out all my leftovers the same year in a fit of garage cleaning.

   If I try the style period para period I get the nice error that a 
period was found in area a, but it should be in area b.  But if I move 
the para into area B, then that is invalid.

   Of course, the whole discussion is pointless, because even if the 
compiler supported format free I would still need years to get the 
source control and tools supporting it.

   .
```

## Thread: Power X Sql Odbc Fields — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Power X Sql Odbc Fields

- **From:** Carlos lages <member129@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-01T21:09:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3177814.1059772153@dbforums.com>`

```

hi, folks
I  am using Power with SqlServe
i have two  fields

Field1   defined as Numeric (18,0)
Filed2    defined as Nuneric (5,2)

Field1  works fine, I Insert, Update , etc
Filed2  Dont works, I accept  12,33 in forms, but using
the method writeRecord  , I get  Invalid  method, Properties
But if I type 12 only, he writes 12

in other words, where am i wrong ?

Tks
Carlos Lages
ps. Power 6.1 x Sql 2000 server
```

#### ↳ Re: Power X Sql Odbc Fields

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-08-04T03:52:34+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f2d307e_2@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3177814.1059772153@dbforums.com>`

```
Carlos,

Have you specified DECIMAL POINT is COMMA under SPECIAL NAMES?

It looks to me like the database knows about the decimal places but the
conversion is failing from COBOL. COBOL figures a comma is just a separator
UNLESS you tell it that this is a decimal.

Make sure your MS local settings on the client and the server reflect the
use of comma as decimal point also.

Pete.

"Carlos lages" <member129@dbforums.com> wrote in message
news:3177814.1059772153@dbforums.com...
>
> hi, folks
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Posted via http://dbforums.com
```

<a id="t-7524e72870"></a>
## Thread: Cobol 85 — 58 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Cobol 85

- **From:** "TerryMac" <tmaccaro@rochester.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-01T23:05:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MmCWa.17993$I_3.581@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
I've only posted on this board a few times to get advice. I'm a student in
Cobol and I have one more program to write before my class ends next
Wednesday.

The project is about inputing data from a file of book inventory records and
storing them in a table. I must check the book order records being input for
the following errors; range errors, type errors and reasonableness.  The
data file is sorted in ascending order by ISBN number (i.e. 9-999-99999-9
including the hyphens) I'm also to verify that the input data from the file
is in sequence.  If this error occurs, I have to print a message and stop
the program. Invalid book order data will be output to an error file and an
entry will be made in an error report.  The program will input from a screen
a request for a text book by ISBN or by Title.  I must do a binary search if
by ISBN and a linear/serial search if by Title. The program will show
different display screens ( a menu, a search menu, a screen if a file is not
found....etc.).

I have 90% of the code completed.  I think I have a pretty good handle on
the program.  The only problem I have is how to validate the ISBN field.  I
know how to check for proper sequence.  Should I have the program validate
the field before I check for sequence?  What type of errors should I check
for ?  I have to use a Pic X because the field includes hyphens not just
numbers.  The requirements for the field that my professor gave me is this:

The field is required, it must have 10 digits, in the format of
9-999-99999-9 including hyphens.

I was also wondering if someone could take a look at my code and give me any
advice.

Thanks!
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol 85

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-01T23:29:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CJCWa.83923$0v4.5596312@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<MmCWa.17993$I_3.581@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```

TerryMac <tmaccaro@rochester.rr.com> wrote in message
news:MmCWa.17993$I_3.581@twister.nyroc.rr.com...
>
> The field is required, it must have 10 digits, in the format of
> 9-999-99999-9 including hyphens.
>
> I was also wondering if someone could take a look at my code and give me
any
> advice.

No need.

Just define the 7 individual elements of the ISBN field
correctly and the rest will be easy.

Check for sequence first.  Then validate.

ISBN field must be > SPACES
Cols #2, #6 and #12 must be equal to "-"
Cols #1, #3-5, #7-11 and #13 must be Numeric
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol 85

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-08-02T14:31:44+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f2b262d_1@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<MmCWa.17993$I_3.581@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <CJCWa.83923$0v4.5596312@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote in message
news:CJCWa.83923$0v4.5596312@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
>
> TerryMac <tmaccaro@rochester.rr.com> wrote in message
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It isn't necessary to define all seven fields if you do the following:

 working-storage section.

 01  input-ISBN pic x(13) value '1-234-56789-0'.

 01  ISBN-fields.
     12 ISBN1 pic 9.
     12 ISBN2 pic 999.
     12 ISBN3 pic 9(5).
     12 ISBN4 pic 9.

 procedure division.

     unstring input-ISBN
        delimited by '-'
          into   ISBN1
                 ISBN2
                 ISBN3
                 ISBN4

     if ISBN-fields NUMERIC
        display 'Hooray!'
     else
        display 'Bummer!'
     end-if.


> Check for sequence first.  Then validate.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol 85

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-02T03:14:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3f2b262d_1@news.athenanews.com...
>
> "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote in message
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > I was also wondering if someone could take a look at my code and give
me
> > any
> > > advice.
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>      end-if.

I realize that.  I was advising a beginner at a beginner's level
by providing him with some specifications that a beginner
would be able to follow and develop the necessary code from,
and hopefully learn something in the process.

The student would then be able to explain the solution
and defend the logic if challenged by the instructor,
if the solution is more elegant than the student
should have been able to come up with working alone.

I left him some work to do.  You did not, so he probably will not
be able to stand up in front of the class and do a code walkthru.
We took a different approach to the same problem, that is all.

By the same token, if you use my solution,
it isn't necessary to define 2 ISBN storage fields, as you had to.

> > Check for sequence first.  Then validate.
> >
> > ISBN field must be > SPACES
> > Cols #2, #6 and #12 must be equal to "-"
> > Cols #1, #3-5, #7-11 and #13 must be Numeric
```

>I was advising a beginner at a beginner's level
> by providing him with some specifications that a beginner
> would be able to follow and develop the necessary code from,

What is there about a two statement solution that would be beyond a
beginner?
>
> The student would then be able to explain the solution
> and defend the logic if challenged by the instructor,
> if the solution is more elegant than the student
> should have been able to come up with working alone.

Would you exclude beginners from exposure to elegance in code?

>
> I left him some work to do.  You did not, so he probably will not
> be able to stand up in front of the class and do a code walkthru.

Wanna bet?! It is a "walkthrough" of 2 statements. How complex is that?

> We took a different approach to the same problem, that is all.
>
> By the same token, if you use my solution,
> it isn't necessary to define 2 ISBN storage fields, as you had to.

No, you would define 7 fields instead.

Then you told him to  do 7 validation checks where one is sufficient.

I venture to suggest you haven't done a lot of work with students. They are
NOT stupid, they are just inexperienced.

And they deserve to have the best possible examples, if they are going to be
given examples.

I accept your point that giving a full solution is not always a good thing.

However, in this instance as I was using a construct (unstring) which might
well be new to him (although it is there in the text books), it made sense
to show a full solution.

>
> > > Check for sequence first.  Then validate.
> > >

That was good advice... I have no problem with that....

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol 85

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-02T07:21:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_DJWa.2509$kP6.10@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<MmCWa.17993$I_3.581@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <CJCWa.83923$0v4.5596312@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f2b262d_1@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Pete,
  What would happen if the input were

  1-2-3-4-5-6-7-8-9

I think it would PASS your test.

Similarly, so would
 9-9-9-9

An "interesting" solution (using the "Cobol 85" from the subject line) and
the desired 9-999-99999-9 format would be:

Evalute input-ISBN (2:1) also input-ISBN (6:1) Also input-ISBN (12:1)
    Also input-ISBN (1:1) also input-ISBN (3:3) Also input-ISBN (7:5) Also
input-ISBN (13:1)
 When "-" also "-" also "-" also "-" Also Numeric Also Numeric Also Numeric
Also Numeric
     Perform Good-ISBN
 When Other
     Perform Bad-ISBN
 End-Evaluate

NOTE Well: this is a test for ISBN that matches *this* school assignment and
NOT the "possible" valid ISBN's pointed out in another response to the
thread.  To test for the "actual" format of an ISBN (per that other note)
significantly more coding would be required.

P.S. I think that the
   Evaluate Field-name
     When  class-test-name

format is valid in the '85 Standard, but haven't tried it.  If not, the last
4 tests in my example code would need to be changed to
   Evaluate true
        When input-ISBN (start-pos: length) Numeric

format
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol 85

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-08-02T09:05:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F2B7466.B88F30EF@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<MmCWa.17993$I_3.581@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <CJCWa.83923$0v4.5596312@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f2b262d_1@news.athenanews.com> <_DJWa.2509$kP6.10@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> Pete,
>   What would happen if the input were
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> format

Hold the press !. It's as hot as Hell up here at 02:25, plus forest fire smoke
drifting north some 300 miles from Montana, southern Alberta and BC; so I was up
giving this some thought before I read your message <G>.

All in all, given the rigid format of ISBNs - not elegant but probably the
following is the simplest even though it looks wordy. A slight variation on
Pete's theme :-

*>-------------------------------------------

Program-id.  CheckISBN.

Working-storage section.

 01 n                       pic 9(03).
 01 input-ISBN       pic x(13).
 01 input-2 redefines input-ISBN.
    05 ISBN1          pic 9(01).
    05 Hyphen1       pic x.
    05 ISBN2          pic 9(03).
    05 Hyphen2       pic x.
    05 ISBN3          pic 9(05).
    05 Hyphen3       pic x.
    05 CheckDigit    pic 9(01).

 01 T1.
    05  filler pic x(13) value '1-234-56789-0'.
    05  filler pic x(13) value '1 234 56789 0'.
    05  filler pic x(13) value '1234567890'.
 01 T2 redefines T1.
    05 InputField occurs 3 pic x(13).

 procedure division.

 perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > 3
     move InputField(n) to input-ISBN

     if (Hyphen1 = "-" )   and
        (Hyphen2 = "-" )   and
        (Hyphen2 = "-" )   and
        (ISBN1 is numeric) and
        (ISBN2 is numeric) and
        (ISBN3 is numeric) and
        (CheckDigit is numeric)
        display
        "OK so far - now we use Mod 11"
        perform VALIDATE-CHECK-DIGIT

     else display
         "Invalid ISBN - Enter as n-nnn-nnnnn-n"
     end-if

 End-perform
 .
 STOP RUN.

 VALIDATE-CHECK-DIGIT.

*> Left to the reader to figure out from Jerry's message !

*>-----------------------------

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol 85

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-08-03T04:04:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F2C7BAC.1E0880D5@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<MmCWa.17993$I_3.581@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <CJCWa.83923$0v4.5596312@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f2b262d_1@news.athenanews.com> <_DJWa.2509$kP6.10@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net> <3F2B7466.B88F30EF@shaw.ca> <3f2bf053_1@news.athenanews.com>`

```


"Peter E.C. Dashwood" wrote:

> Jimmy, the original solution is watertight if the pictures are changed to X.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Got that from Jerry's reference to :-

http://www.isbn.org/standards/home/isbn/international/html/usm4.htm

which shows how the Mod 11 is calculated.

As regards the cosmetic appearance of numbers, for the most part we know that
ISBNs are read with data wands (particularly in lending libraries). But for
human readability the hyphens are a must. Which would you rather I quoted if I
was suggesting to you to get Will's 'Elements of OO" ? :-

ISBN 0-9655945-6-4            or    ISBN  0965594564

Hmm. Let's just check that out :-

(0 * 10) + (9 * 9) + (6 * 8) + (5 * 7) + (5 * 6) + (9 * 5) + (4 * 4) + (5 * 3) +
(6 * 2)  =

0  + 81 + 48 + 35 + 30 + 45 + 16 + 15 + 12 =  282 / 11 = 25 Remainder 6;

10 - R6  = 4 = CHECK DIGIT

Your revised program (see below) WORKS !. I do a lot of STRINGing but have never
found a need for UNSTRINGing. (And after close to 23 years I doubt I'm  going to
find a need for UNSTRINGing <G>).

To try and figure out why your example worked I went back to the M/F Language
Ref Manual. Usual stuff - syntax diagram saying, "literal this", "identifier-n"
etc., etc. - half-inched from the Standard and probably fairly verbatim. Then
started reading the following text. By the time I'd gotten to clause 4 the
syntax diagram had shuffled off the top of the screen, so when I read "Literal-1
must be and Literal-2 must be", my eyes start to glaze over. "Do I really need
this ?". I gave up !

I just can't see the logic of your example working correctly when the delimiter
is supposed to be "-"  and some of the test examples don't have "-" . Just for
the hell of it, I replaced the spaces with an ampersand - STILL WORKS !

However when it comes to simplicity - full marks to Dennis for finishing up with
his 0-000-00000-0. It wasn't part of the exercise, but if you did want to check
the Mod 11 then I would suggest in the Dennis example you'd have to move to
separate fields  to get the breakdown of digits.

Bill's example. Well "interesting" - Yes  <G>.

Bye-the-bye - You mentioned ID cards. Probably taking place elsewhere in N.
America but Alberta Health Care is giving very serious consideration to Health
Care Smart Cards - micro chipped to store your medical history. Beats the
situation when the family GP says, "When was it that this first happened to you
?'. Wont happen with the 'oldies' but the younger guys are computer literate -
and will be getting that information for themselves from your Smart Card.

Jimmy

Example follows - each display shows a "Pass" and consistently displays the
numerics in the same pigeon holes.
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Program-id.  UnString2.

Working-storage section.

 01 n          pic 9(03).
 01 input-ISBN pic x(13).

 01 T1.
    05  filler pic x(13) value '1-234-56789-0'.
    05  filler pic x(13) value '1&234&56789&0'.
    05  filler pic x(13) value '1234567890'.
    05  filler pic x(13) value '123456789&0'.
    05  filler pic x(13) value '123&456&78&90'.
 01 T2 redefines T1.
    05 InputField occurs 5 pic x(13).

 01  ISBN-fields.
     12 ISBN1 pic x.
     12 ISBN2 pic x(03).
     12 ISBN3 pic x(05).
     12 ISBN4 pic x.

 procedure division.

 perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > 5
     move InputField(n) to input-ISBN

     unstring input-ISBN
        delimited by "-"
          into   ISBN1
                 ISBN2
                 ISBN3
                 ISBN4

     if ISBN-fields NUMERIC
        display ISBN1 " " ISBN2 " " ISBN3 " " ISBN4
        display n '  Hooray!'
     else
        display n '  Bummer!'
     end-if

 End-perform
 .
*>-----------------------------------------------
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f2d26ae_4@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<MmCWa.17993$I_3.581@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <CJCWa.83923$0v4.5596312@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f2b262d_1@news.athenanews.com> <_DJWa.2509$kP6.10@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net> <3F2B7466.B88F30EF@shaw.ca> <3f2bf053_1@news.athenanews.com> <3F2C7BAC.1E0880D5@shaw.ca> <vip688a603ije3@corp.supernews.com> <3F2C92E5.DFC9522D@shaw.ca> <vip9o9teo5p5d@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:vip9o9teo5p5d@corp.supernews.com...
> The 'move spaces to ISBN-fields' before the 'unstring'.
>
> The program was leaving numerics in fields from the
> first good test, thus allowing the others tests to
> succeed.

No it wasn't.

But I agree it certainly looked that way.

I didn't clear the fields in the original version because it wasn't
iterated, as Jimmy's version was.
(really, Jimmy should have put this line in when he iterated it....(Wasn't
me, Sir...it was HIM!!!!  <G>))

The UNSTRING unstrings each field into a single work area, which it
initialises on each occasion. When it gets the required delimiter (or "end
of field condition") it MOVES the Work Area, according to the rules for
COBOL move, to the field identified in the UNSTRING statement. In this way
it can cater for fields that are too long, by truncating them to the size of
the field defined in the UNSTRING statement, and pad fields that are too
short with spaces, just like any other COBOL Alphanumeric MOVE.

It still works even with your suggested change, Rick.

I ran Jimmy's version after adding the one line change you suggested and
obtained the following result:

1 234 56789 0
001 Hooray!
002 Bummer!
003 Bummer!
004 Bummer!
005 Bummer!

...which is exactly what it should produce.

I agree it is good practice to initialise the target fields of both STRING
and UNSTRING immediately before using these verbs, and I usually do.

On this occasion I "got away with it" but it wasn't good practice.

(Very well spotted, BTW <G> I'm impressed you saw it...)

Pete.
```

Rick,

I stand corrected. It was doing just what you said.

I misunderstood your comment and thought that you were suggesting the code
was not initialising each of the fields in the UNSTRING, when it should.

In fact, because Jimmy's data had no hyphens in it, the whole input string
was being "unstrung" into the first field only, as you pointed out. The
remaining fields weren't initialised because the "UNSTRING" had finished.
You realised all of this, but I didn't, because I was using different data
from Jimmy and I was doing non-iterative tests.

I realised it after some private correspondence with Jimmy and he sent me
his results. As soon as I saw the same string being output from every test,
the penny dropped.

I didn't realise the results that Jimmy was getting, and when he said it
"WORK"ed I took that to mean that the code was sound. He was really trying
to tell me that it was "passing" when it should have "failed".

Thanks for pointing out the requirement to initialize and I'm sorry I didn't
agree that the fields were being left intact, when they obviously were.
(They weren't with my data...).

Pete.

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Your revised program (see below) WORKS !. I do a lot of STRINGing but have
never
> found a need for UNSTRINGing. (And after close to 23 years I doubt I'm
going to
> find a need for UNSTRINGing <G>).

I'm very relieved <G>. It's a funny thing, we can spend years writing
programs, we know exactly what we are doing and why, and what the predicted
outcome should be, and yet there is always that lingering doubt that it
won't function properly, and then the relief when it does...<G> (Well, it's
true for me; maybe others are less insecure...)
>
> To try and figure out why your example worked I went back to the M/F
Language
> Ref Manual. Usual stuff - syntax diagram saying, "literal this",
"identifier-n"
> etc., etc. - half-inched from the Standard and probably fairly verbatim.
Then
> started reading the following text. By the time I'd gotten to clause 4 the
> syntax diagram had shuffled off the top of the screen, so when I read
"Literal-1
> must be and Literal-2 must be", my eyes start to glaze over. "Do I really
need
> this ?". I gave up !
>

I don't blame you. I know it's a complex construct with several different
options, but wouldn't you think they could explain it a bit better? Or give
coded examples of each option... ? Fujitsu are good in this regard and they
do give code examples, but still not as many as would be helpful when
experimenting.

Let me try and explain in plain English, how I arrived at this solution and
why it works...

The input string has a fixed format that REQUIRES hyphens in certain
positions. Therefore it must also require EXACTLY the right number of
characters between the hyphens. (You can look at the numbers or look at the
hyphens; they are the shadow of each other...). AND these characters MUST be
NUMERIC.

UNSTRING looks at a character string and breaks it up into defined fields,
using a pointer or a delimiter character. (Exactly the opposite of what
STRING does...). If you use a delimiter, it is discarded (you need to use a
pointer if you want it stored, or you can specify WITH DELIMITER)

One way (it is a bit lateral thinking) to determine that the hyphens are
always in the right place is to use them as delimiters in an UNSTRING, and
then make sure that we always have exactly as many characters as are
required by the format, in the unstrung fields.

This kills two birds with one stone. If a hyphen is misplaced, the correct
number of characters will not be placed in the field currently receiving
unstrung characters. It can only be too short or too long. If it is too
short, it will be padded with spaces. If it is too long it will be stored
OK, but the next hyphen will now be misplaced. The final field MUST be too
short as characters have been "used up" by the fields that were too long. So
either way, if ANY of the unstrung fields are of incorrect length, there
will be a space in at least one of them. If there is a space in one of them,
when we test the group for NUMERIC, it will fail. ONLY if EXACTLY the right
number of characters was unstrung into into each field defined for the
UNSTRING, will we have NO spaces in the fields. (i.e. the hyphens must have
been in the right place.)

So, using hyphen as the delimiter ensures that hyphens were present and that
they were in the right place. If you used anything else as a delimiter, you
would simply have a field too long and the succeeding fields would be empty
and therefore not numeric. (See above, unstrung fields are either exactly
the right length (correct number of characters between hyphens) or too long,
or too short, and in either of these cases one or more of the unstrung
fields will have one or more spaces in it.)

So UNSTRING has done a pretty good job of validating that hyphens were used
as delimiters and that the fields are of the correct length. But it doesn't
guarantee they are numeric. By unstringing into fields under a group level,
we can test the group for numeric and our problem is solved. If everything
unstrung correctly (and it can only do that if the hyphens are in the right
place) the fields under the group level will be full of characters. If even
ONE of these is NOT Numeric (it could be a space caused by a misplaced
hyphen (see above), or it could be a non numeric character introduced
erroneously into one of the "numbers", the check will fail. You tested it
and saw that this is so.

If you manage to get your head around this (and I don't suggest you sweat it
<G>) then consider why it DIDN'T work when I made the stupid error of using
numeric fields... <G>


> I just can't see the logic of your example working correctly when the
delimiter
> is supposed to be "-"  and some of the test examples don't have "-" . Just
for

>

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **References:** `<MmCWa.17993$I_3.581@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <CJCWa.83923$0v4.5596312@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f2b262d_1@news.athenanews.com> <_DJWa.2509$kP6.10@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net> <3F2B7466.B88F30EF@shaw.ca> <3f2bf053_1@news.athenanews.com> <3F2C7BAC.1E0880D5@shaw.ca>`

```
Just 1 comment below:

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3F2C7BAC.1E0880D5@shaw.ca...
|
|
| However when it comes to simplicity - full marks to Dennis for finishing
up with
| his 0-000-00000-0. It wasn't part of the exercise, but if you did want to
check
| the Mod 11 then I would suggest in the Dennis example you'd have to move
to
| separate fields  to get the breakdown of digits.

Yes, I am simple minded<G>.

I have worked with Mod 10 check digits in the past, and on the old sloooooow
processors we had, I came up with a solution that also used the CONVERTING.

(you have to let the computer generate the conversion tables as a one time
shot. You can do this to produce source code, or you can initialize the
tables in your program startup routine.)

What we are doing is calculating constants.
Each digit in the source has ONE, and only ONE, result. (6*6 is always 36
X'24').
If you convert 6 to x'24'  you have the product in one byte in binary format
(9*10 = 90, so you don't need to worry about overflow).
You need a conversion table for each position (10-2).
after you convert each position you sum the one character binary numbers,
and do your calculation for the check digit.

Referencing the indivual fields can be done usign reference modification.
```

Bill,
  I tried
     Evaluate Field-name
       When  class-test-name
previously (for a different problem), it didn't work.

I used 'evaluate false' as in this code, which I did test.

           evaluate false
             when input-ISBN (1:1) numeric
             when input-ISBN (2:1) = "-"
             when input-ISBN (3:3) numeric
             when input-ISBN (6:1) = "-"
             when input-ISBN (7:5) numeric
             when input-ISBN (12:1) = "-"
             when input-ISBN (13:1) numeric
               display "ISBN not valid"
             when other
               display "ISBN valid"
           end-evaluate

The above may be amended to identify the first failure.
---------------
Rick Smith

If the pictures are changed to X, the code is watertight.

 01  ISBN-fields.
     12 ISBN1 pic x.
     12 ISBN2 pic xxx.
     12 ISBN3 pic x(5).
     12 ISBN4 pic x.

> An "interesting" solution (using the "Cobol 85" from the subject line) and
> the desired 9-999-99999-9 format would be:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>  When "-" also "-" also "-" also "-" Also Numeric Also Numeric Also
Numeric

TerryMac wrote:

> I've only posted on this board a few times to get advice. I'm a student in
> Cobol and I have one more program to write before my class ends next
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks!

Feel for you TerryMac - when you start in programming you tend to dance around a
problem which can be quite easily solved, once you have accumulated some skill..
Not the only solution, but try the following

move ISBNNumber(should be 13 chars)  to Text13 - now use Reference Modification
:-

if   (Text13(2:1) = "-" ) AND:
    (Text13(6:1) = "-" ) AND
    (Text13(12:1) = "-")
    then so far we are OK - no 'Hyphen' errors

If OK above
    inspect Text13 replacing all  "-" by '0"

if Text13 is NUMERIC
    then we are OK

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

As you say, Jimmy, not the only solution...

Here's one that doesn't need compund IFs, refmodding, or INSPECT...(Wouldn't
you need to use INSPECT again to replace the hyphens for display?....)

 working-storage section.

 01  input-ISBN pic x(13) value '1-234-56789-0'.

 01  ISBN-fields.
     12 ISBN1 pic 9.
     12 ISBN2 pic 999.
     12 ISBN3 pic 9(5).
     12 ISBN4 pic 9.

 procedure division.

     unstring input-ISBN
        delimited by '-'
          into   ISBN1
                 ISBN2
                 ISBN3
                 ISBN4

     if ISBN-fields NUMERIC
        display 'Hooray!'
     else
        display 'Bummer!'
     end-if.

Pete.



> Jimmy, Calgary AB

- **References:** `<MmCWa.17993$I_3.581@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <3F2AEFEC.CDB0DBE6@shaw.ca> <3f2b2488_1@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" wrote:

> James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
> news:3F2AEFEC.CDB0DBE6@shaw.ca...
…[77 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Nope . The original input is still intact. That's why I moved to a (temp)
Text13.

>
>  working-storage section.
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>

At first glance, your solution looked simple - so I thought I'd give it a try
with variations :-
-------------------------------------
Program-id.  UnStringISBN.

Working-storage section.
 01 n          pic 9(03).
 01 input-ISBN pic x(13).

 01 T1.
    05  filler pic x(13) value '1-234-56789-0'.
    05  filler pic x(13) value '1 234 56789 0'.
    05  filler pic x(13) value '1234567890'.
 01 T2 redefines T1.
    05 InputField occurs 3 pic x(13).

 01  ISBN-fields.
     12 ISBN1 pic 9.
     12 ISBN2 pic 999.
     12 ISBN3 pic 9(5).
     12 ISBN4 pic 9.

 procedure division.

 perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > 3
     move InputField(n) to input-ISBN

     unstring input-ISBN
        delimited by '-'
          into   ISBN1
                 ISBN2
                 ISBN3
                 ISBN4

     if ISBN-fields NUMERIC
        display n '  Hooray!'
     else
        display n '  Bummer!'
     end-if

 End-perform
------------------------

The only one that worked was #1 - the other two came up with "illegal -
non-numeric values".

I ran up against this type of problem with telephone numbers about two years ago
when signing up for something on the Web -  "Enter your telephone number". What
did they want ? :-

4032722509
403 272 2509
403-272-2509

They kicked out until I put in the latter. (DUMB !).

There's a GUI feature in N/E to 'mask' input fields. No doubt you have something
similar in F/J.

    Telephone # [999-999-9999]
    Date 1         [ccyy-mm-dd]
    Date 2        [mm/dd/yy]

One other thing - his professor was 'fussy' about him having those hyphens <G>.

> > Jimmy, Calgary AB
>
```

That's a really cool test, Jimmy. I'm impressed...<G>.

Well, they SHOULD fail, but I understand what you're saying.

Do me a favour and try re-running with the ISBN numbers defined as pic x.

Like this...

 01  ISBN-fields.
     12 ISBN1 pic x.
     12 ISBN2 pic xxx.
     12 ISBN3 pic x(5).
     12 ISBN4 pic x.

The whole idea was to ensure that the format demanded by the Professor was
adhered to, but in my haste I made the Numbers Numeric (how dumb was
THAT?!). Had they been alphanumeric the original concept of the solution
should have worked. I tested it with a couple of possibilities but Bill was
right in what he said. I guess I could split hairs and argue that
1-2-3-4-5-6-7-8-9 is more than 13 characters, and therefore exceeds the
input field size, but I'd only be weaseling out of the fact that I got it
wrong....<G>

>
> I ran up against this type of problem with telephone numbers about two
years ago
> when signing up for something on the Web -  "Enter your telephone number".
What
> did they want ? :-
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> They kicked out until I put in the latter. (DUMB !).

Yes, I use Java script to strip out anything except numbers (including
spaces and all special characters) and + (the symbol for the International
access code) in 'phone numbers on web pages. My approach is that Web Pages
should be as User Friendly as possible, with the minimum of mandatory
fields, and should cater for the World rather than just one country. You
could input your phone number in any of the above formats on my Web Pages
and it would be accepted.

>
> There's a GUI feature in N/E to 'mask' input fields. No doubt you have
something
> similar in F/J.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes, there is, but, to be honest, I never use it. I use String2Num on the
Web Page for fields that should have ONLY numbers in them, and I use the
same component on the back end  (after JavaScript has pulled out the spaces
and special characters) for fields that MIGHT have extraneous characters in
them.

> One other thing - his professor was 'fussy' about him having those hyphens
<G>.
>

Yes, I know. That's why I took great care to see that the only valid format
was one where the hyphens are in the right place.

Try it with the pic x fields and you'll see what I mean.

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol 85

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-01T22:04:15-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<I1GdnfhfZYayt7aiXTWJjQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<MmCWa.17993$I_3.581@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
TerryMac wrote:
> I've only posted on this board a few times to get advice. I'm a
> student in Cobol and I have one more program to write before my class
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> 9-999-99999-9 including hyphens.

As a pedantic fuddy-duddy, it's "ISBN" not "ISBN number."

The ISBN is composed of 4 sets of characters, separated by three space or
dashes (preferred).

The first set is the country identifier and can be from 1 to 5 digits long
in the following fashion:
0-7
80-94
950-997
9980-9989
99900-99999

For example, 0, 1 = U.S. & U.K., 9974 = Uruguay, 99913 = Andorra, 965 =
Israel

The second set is the publisher identifier and can be from two to seven
digits long in the following fashion:
00-19
200-699
7000-8499
85000-89999
900000-949999
9500000-9999999

It will be seen that while there can be a publisher with the prefix 699,
there cannot be a publisher with the prefix 6991. Therefore, even with the
dashes omitted, one can easily tell the ISBN 0699102340 is published by
publisher "699."

The third set is the unique serial number assigned by the publisher for the
particular work.

The last set is the check-digit. It can be a digit from 0 through 9 or the
letter "X" computed in the following fashion:

Multiply each of the 1st 9 digits of the ISBN by the following corresponding
factors: 10, 9, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2. Sum each of these multiplications. This
sum, modulo 11, is the check-digit (if the difference is 10, the check-digit
is "X"). For example using the ISBN above
(0x10) + (6x9) + (9x8) + (9x7) + (1x6) + (0x5) + (2x4) + (3x3) + (4x2) =
0 + 54 + 72 + 63 + 6 + 0 + 8 + 9 + 8 = 220
220 Mod 11 = 0, which matches the last digit.

Point of all this is, the dashes are for human consumption. In a proper
world, you should remove all the dashes, squeeze the result to ten
characters and search, sort, compare, update, and all that stuff on the ten
character compacted ISBN (making sure the ISBN you have is correct based on
the check-digit computation). Then, when displaying for a human, put the
dashes back in the right place according to the schema above. We get the
dashes in the right place here with only a few hundred lines of code.

More information at:
http://www.isbn.org/standards/home/isbn/international/html/usm4.htm

Our next class will cover converting an ISBN to a Bookland EAN (European
Article Number - the barcode on books beginning with "978" (soon to be "979"
also)) and vice-versa.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol 85

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-08-02T04:13:37+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3F2B2E87.BB0AFD7@shaw.ca>`

```


JerryMouse wrote:

> <snip> The third set is the unique serial number assigned by the publisher for
> the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

As soon as you wrote use "X" I knew we were into Mod 11 check digits. Used them
since 1963. NCC Manchester produced a book on Systems Analysis in the md-70s.
Mod 11 has an overall performance of 99% + accuracy. They gave a table of five
sub levels, (a) an incorrect digit written (b) digits transposed (c) -sorry (c)
to (e) I've forgotten. The lowest of the five didn't drop below about 97.8%
accuracy.

Then I move to a department store group where a somewhat highly strung
individual was in charge of designing a system for Ladies' Fashions - using
(Litton Industries) Kimball tags - 40-column punched cards. He thought it was
real neat when I suggested Mod 11. Then later he threw a tantrum when he
realized the Kimballl punching equipment didn't have an "X". "What's the big
deal ?", I asked. "Just ignore those numbers with a '10' as the check-digit".

>
> More information at:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> also)) and vice-versa.

Better mark that "Advanced Course" - somehow I don't think TerryMac will be an
attendee <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol 85

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-02T07:49:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jFGdncDNIuzaLraiXTWJkA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<MmCWa.17993$I_3.581@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <I1GdnfhfZYayt7aiXTWJjQ@giganews.com> <3F2B2E87.BB0AFD7@shaw.ca>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
>>
>> Our next class will cover converting an ISBN to a Bookland EAN
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Nah, it's easy. Given and ISBN, throw away the check-digit, put "978" on the
front of the remaining nine, and compute a new check-digit for the 13th
position. The check-digit computation differs both in the manner of
calculation and that it's Mod 10 (no more "X").

To go from EAN to ISBN, throw away the "978" from the front and the
check-digit from the tail. Compute a new check-digit (the ISBN kind) from
the remaining nine digits.

Interesting observation on check-digits. While almost every identifier in
the universe has a check-digit (merchandise, your credit-card number,
driver's license, passport, etc.), the U.S. Social Security Number does NOT
have a check-digit. "Why not?" is a mystery in that legislation to ADD a
check-digit would be trivial. Of course adding a digit would mean that every
payroll system, and every system that reported to the government would have
to change. More job security for COBOL programmers.

I'm gonna start a movement! And after I get back from the smallest room in
the house, I'm gonna write my congress-critter.

Anyway, not having a check-digit in the SSN makes it easy to make a
"mistake" (nudge-nudge, wink-wink) when officious below-minimimum-wage
trolls insist on having your SSN "for the files." Only the government can
tell, officially, that the proffered number is wrong - and they're not
saying.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol 85

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-08-03T05:41:47+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f2bf895_1@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<MmCWa.17993$I_3.581@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <I1GdnfhfZYayt7aiXTWJjQ@giganews.com> <3F2B2E87.BB0AFD7@shaw.ca> <jFGdncDNIuzaLraiXTWJkA@giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:jFGdncDNIuzaLraiXTWJkA@giganews.com...
> James J. Gavan wrote:
> >>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Nah, it's easy. Given and ISBN, throw away the check-digit, put "978" on
the
> front of the remaining nine, and compute a new check-digit for the 13th
> position. The check-digit computation differs both in the manner of
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> driver's license, passport, etc.), the U.S. Social Security Number does
NOT
> have a check-digit. "Why not?" is a mystery in that legislation to ADD a
> check-digit would be trivial. Of course adding a digit would mean that
every
> payroll system, and every system that reported to the government would
have
> to change. More job security for COBOL programmers.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Thirty years ago the New Zealand Computer Society (of which I was a founder
Member, but I resigned in protest when they got so up themselves that they
decided to insist on having a "licensed practitioner" on every IT site)
decided to try and implement a unique identifier for everyone in the
country. (It was initiated by some senior staff at Auckland hospital, with
the highest of intentions...universal medical records, know what they're
allergic to if they're admitted unconscious, etc.)

Given that NZ at that time had just over 3,000,000 people, it wasn't such an
impossible task. Nevertheless, we decided to look around at similar systems
overseas.

This brings me to the US SSN.

The official (though not "Public") word at that time was that around 20% of
all SSNs were either the wrong person or the wrong number. There was a
traffic in these numbers for illegal immigrants and they really weren't
worth the cardboard they were printed on. On this basis we ruled out the
idea of having a totally numeric identifier. (Actually, the whole project
was abandoned for reasons which I won't go into here, but it wouldn't
surprise me to see it get revived again as we come up to 4,000,000 and with
the current political climate and furore over using Driver's Licenses as ID
cards...)

I did not know they had no check digits until I read Jerry's post. It
accounts for what we were told by US Authorities.

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol 85

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-02T20:20:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<E2VWa.81705$3o3.5627932@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<MmCWa.17993$I_3.581@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
Top Post ONLY:

TerryMac,

Here is a different method to validate the ISBN in your problem.

If it doesn't work, I'm sure someone will point that out.

 01  IND-INDICATORS.

     05                         PIC X.
       88  ISBN-VALID                   VALUE 'Y'.
       88  ISBN-INVALID                 VALUE 'N'.

 01  WK-ISBN                    PIC X(13).

 0000-VALIDATE-ISBN.

     MOVE INPUT-ISBN   TO  WK-ISBN

     INSPECT  WK-ISBN
              CONVERTING  '123456789'
                                  TO  '000000000'

     IF  WK-ISBN  =  '0-000-00000-0'
         SET  ISBN-VALID  TO  TRUE
     ELSE
         SET  ISBN-INVALID  TO  TRUE
     END-IF.

THAT'S ALL FOLKS!!!

upper/lower case subroutines for several years now.   Handy little bugger.
IE-       INSPECT  my-key
              CONVERTING
                 'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz'
          TO  'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ'

Donald

>
> Remember when (IBM) COBOL II, first came out, there was an uproar over the
> demise of the TRANSFORM.
>

Yes, I remember it well. I had only ever used TRANSFORM once (it was to
translate from EBCDIC to ASCII) but some of the guys were using it in place
of EXAMINE (now INSPECT <G>)

I never made the connection between TRANSFORM and INSPECT... CONVERTING
because I hadn't seen CONVERTING until I saw it here.

Now that you mention it, I see the similarity with TRANSFORM <G>

Pete.
```

IBM has documented INSPECT CONVERTING as the replacement for TRANSFORM for
the last 15 years (or so).  See:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3mg10/4.1.5

and look at the topic,

 "TRANSFORM statement"
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol 85

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-03T19:59:31-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<virbvjke1tt678@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<MmCWa.17993$I_3.581@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<MmCWa.17993$I_3.581@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
TerryMac wrote:

> I have 90% of the code completed.  I think I have a pretty good handle on
> the program.  The only problem I have is how to validate the ISBN field.  I
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 9-999-99999-9 including hyphens.

I would validate it before searching - saves what would end up being a 
full file search.  What I would do is define a separate record in 
working storage, like

01  ISBN.
     12  ISBN-Nbr-1         Pic 9(01).
         88  ISBN-1st-Part-Valid  Values 0 Thru 9.
     12                     Pic X(01).
     12  ISBN-Nbr-2         Pic 9(03).
         88  ISBN-2nd-Part-Valid  Values 0 Thru 999.
     12                     Pic X(01).
     12  ISBN-Nbr-3         Pic 9(05).
         88  ISBN-3rd-Part-Valid  Values 0 Thru 99999.
     12                     Pic X(01).
     12  ISBN-Nbr-4         Pic 9(01).
         88  ISBN-4th-Part-Valid  Values 0 Thru 9.

, then you can move the input to "ISBN" and check each of the 4 values. 
   The 01-level is, by definition, alphanumeric (as are all group 
items), so your input with the dashes will go in there.  If it's 
well-formed, the dashes will fall into the fillers, and you can say 
something like

     If    ISBN-1st-Part-Valid
       And ISBN-2nd-Part-Valid
       And ISBN-3rd-Part-Valid
       And ISBN-4th-Part-Valid
         Perform [Do-the-search]
     Else
         Perform [Input-Invalid]
     End-If

Of course, if validating the ISBN is going to be something you do for 
more than one option, you could write a separate paragraph for that (or 
even a nested subprogram, if you're really wanting to play with stuff), 
and have it set or return a switch that says whether the ISBN in its 
entirety is valid.  :)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol 85

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-04T14:56:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XuuXa.87345$0v4.5876546@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<MmCWa.17993$I_3.581@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <virbvjke1tt678@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"LX-i" <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message
news:virbvjke1tt678@corp.supernews.com...
| TerryMac wrote:
|
| > I have 90% of the code completed.  I think I have a pretty good handle
on
| > the program.  The only problem I have is how to validate the ISBN field.
I
| > know how to check for proper sequence.  Should I have the program
validate
| > the field before I check for sequence?  What type of errors should I
check
| > for ?  I have to use a Pic X because the field includes hyphens not just
| > numbers.  The requirements for the field that my professor gave me is
this:
| >
| > The field is required, it must have 10 digits, in the format of
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
| entirety is valid.  :)

I don't think it will work.

Your fields are defined as numeric.

What would happen if you had "9-999-9999--9" or "9-999-99-99-9"?
(On the platforms I'm familiar with, you might get a program exception, or
worse yet the data would incorrectly pass the check.)

Changing them to alphanumeric (PIC X) wouldn't solve the problem.
A range of  '000' to '999' would still accept "0G2".
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol 85

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-08-06T18:59:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```


TerryMac wrote:

> I've only posted on this board a few times to get advice. I'm a student in
> Cobol and I have one more program to write before my class ends next
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks!

second quote :-

>
> I have 90% of the code completed.  I think I have a pretty good handle on
> the program.  The only problem I have is how to validate the ISBN field.

Miss or Ms. Terry (your preference !),

Having said you've written 90% of the program and need help, sending me ONLY the
Professor's specification - NO WAY !. You will only get help if we see your
attempt at writing the source.

Post it here - NOT direct to me. It would help to post the Prof's spec as well.
Take the time to type it in your message - the xxx.tif you sent me is still
fairly unreadable even when I view it with a full screen.. The spec given by the
Prof is comprehensive, requires some thought, but not over challenging, if
you've been following through on prior lessons.

As regards that one query above, Dennis has already given you a solution - based
on your Prof's definition - ISBN = 10 digits and is in the format x-xxx-xxxxx-x.
So in your program include the following :-

I've changed the flags because I prefer numerics, but does same thing.

-------------------------------------------------------------
Working-storage section.

01 ISBN-Flag.                                        pic 9.
   88 ValidISBN                                      value 1.
   88 InvalidISBN                                    value 0.

01 TempISBN                                        pic x(13).

Procedure Division.

perform  VALIDATE-ISBN-FORMAT

if ValidISBN etc....
    ..........
    do other stuff

STOP RUN.
.
VALIDATE-ISBN-FORMAT.

move Input-ISBN to Temp-ISBN
inspect Temp-ISBN converting "123456789" to "000000000"

if   Temp-ISBN = '0-000-00000-0'
     set ValidISBN to true

else set InvalidISBN to true
End-if
----------------------------------------------------

BTW - you haven't told us - which compiler and operating system ? I'm assuming
you are using a DOS text display, (either simple ANSI Accept/Display or a Screen
Section ) ?????

As implied - if perhaps you are floundering on this one, you'd better earmark
for the next course.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol 85

- **From:** "TerryMac" <tmaccaro@rochester.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-07T01:35:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<j1iYa.165856$EQ5.10353@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<MmCWa.17993$I_3.581@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <3F3145AB.C95572AE@shaw.ca>`

```
Okay I'll post the code and the Professor's spec's tomorrow.   Just to let
you know, I'm using the College's computers to compile the code.  We're
using Micro Focus Object Cobol V4.1.30 for Unix.  The operating system is
Unix.  Which is pretty cool because I've learned some basic Unix commands.

Just to let you know, I dropped the course and will take it again in the
spring. In the meantime, I'd like to continue working on solving this
assignment. It will keep me from forgetting alot of what I've already
learned.

Thanks!
