###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future reuse of code

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-14T23:37:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F2V_a.20510$vo2.10997@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<45ab836a.0308031802.2c9685eb@posting.google.com> <796f488f.0308042203.641afe64@posting.google.com> <bha4np$9lm$00$1@news.t-online.com> <3f38cfd6_3@news.athenanews.com> <slrnbjhm1i.2gg0.marcov@toad.stack.nl> <3f3908a5_6@news.athenanews.com> <slrnbjico8.dqv.marcov@toad.stack.nl> <3f397a8a_3@news.athenanews.com> <pvw_a.17582$vo2.15788@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net> <3f3aa59a.49770661@news.central.cox.net> <3f3ac358_5@news.athenanews.com> <fHL_a.9196$VG.422001@news20.bellglobal.com> <3f3c19f5_4@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`

```
So, Pete -
   Do you think that the design of a "Ferrari" are now "permanent"?  Aren't
there requirements for improved TOOLS to create new models of Ferraris? Are
the tools used to create new models of Ferraris "automatically" made without
other tools being designed/enhanced to make these new tools?

From my perspective, I would expect the development of "low-level"
programming tools (to create other tools and applications) to be "fully
automated" about 5 to 10 years after there is an END to the development of
new "low-level" tools for the creation of new airplanes and cars.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future reuse of code

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-08-15T12:51:58+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f3c2f7c_3@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<45ab836a.0308031802.2c9685eb@posting.google.com> <796f488f.0308042203.641afe64@posting.google.com> <bha4np$9lm$00$1@news.t-online.com> <3f38cfd6_3@news.athenanews.com> <slrnbjhm1i.2gg0.marcov@toad.stack.nl> <3f3908a5_6@news.athenanews.com> <slrnbjico8.dqv.marcov@toad.stack.nl> <3f397a8a_3@news.athenanews.com> <pvw_a.17582$vo2.15788@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net> <3f3aa59a.49770661@news.central.cox.net> <3f3ac358_5@news.athenanews.com> <fHL_a.9196$VG.422001@news20.bellglobal.com> <3f3c19f5_4@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <F2V_a.20510$vo2.10997@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:F2V_a.20510$vo2.10997@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net...
> So, Pete -
>    Do you think that the design of a "Ferrari" are now "permanent"?

No, but they haven't changed much in the past 5 years...

New models are produced for the sake of Marketing and in response to
competitive pressure in the Marketplace, not because of any technical
breakthrough.

We could certainly "get by" using the latest model, indefinitely... Give me
one; I'll keep it until it wears out<G>!

Anyway, it was an analogy, Bill, and, as such, I don't intend to digress
into it...

>Aren't
> there requirements for improved TOOLS to create new models of Ferraris?
Are
> the tools used to create new models of Ferraris "automatically" made
without
> other tools being designed/enhanced to make these new tools?
>

I don't intend to debate the manufacture of Ferraris in this context, but if
I did, I'd point out that many of the tools used in the FIAT factories are
fully automated and require no human intervention.

> From my perspective, I would expect the development of "low-level"
> programming tools (to create other tools and applications) to be "fully
> automated" about 5 to 10 years after there is an END to the development of
> new "low-level" tools for the creation of new airplanes and cars.
>

My argument is not about tool manufacture. (That is only ONE part of it). As
stated elsewhere, it will be a combination of people, tools, and
methodolgies, combined with more advanced paradigms, that will bring about
the obsolescence of Procedural coding. (I believe this process is already
happening and within 15 years it will be complete.)

How many people continued writing spaghetti code after Dyjkstra gave us
"structure"? When was the last time you had to maintain convoluted code full
of GO TOs , that WASN'T written more than 10 years ago?

One paradigm replaces another when it is seen to be "better".

(We can argue all day about what "better" is in this context...)

"OO is better than procedural."

"Components are better than OO."

These statements are arguable opinions.

But the fact is that (at least for me) they are true.

So I use components and am continuing to move towards Component Based design
and development, without tying myself to ONE set of source code or ONE
programming language. And I find the OO paradigm more useful than Procedural
Code, so I don't write a lot of procedural code anymore.

In the broader context of this discussion I have posited a number of
arguments regarding the pressure being exerted by the market on commercial
computer systems.

The user base is demanding simpler interfaces, less specialist skill
required in dealing with computers. History has shown that the market tends
to get what it wants (there are fortunes to made in providing smarter
software; this alone will drive its development.)

I believe all these factors (plus a lot more which I won't go into here)
will lead to the decline of Procedural Code in commercial systems
development.

It's very difficult for people who make a living writing Procedural Code (or
have done so most of their lives, or are connected with companies that are
selling computer products based around the continued use of procedural code)
to step "outside the square" and take an unbiased look at the Marketplace.

I didn't expect everyone to "roll over" when I started this debate <G>.

And I really don't mind whether people agree with my view or not. (It
remains my view, either way...<G>)

There have been some excellent posts in response and some of them have made
me think through my position on this. I MIGHT be too optimistic with the
timescale (I have a natural tendency towards optimism...<G>), perhaps it
will take longer, perhaps it won't be on as wide a scale as I envision it,
but I am still prepared to nail my colours to the mast here and state that
within 15 years, procedural code will not be generally written in-house in
support of commercial computer systems.

So, what exactly is YOUR position, Bill? (Or are you just disagreeing with
mine? <G>)

Pete.

> -- 
> Bill Klein
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> > I'm not sure about that one. The fundamantals of Business haven't
changed.
> > It is certainly true that markets are much more dynamic than they were
and
> > that means Business systems have to be much more responsive.
> >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future reuse of code

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-08-14T10:42:21+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.java.programmer,comp.lang.pascal.ansi-iso
- **Message-ID:** `<3f3abf9b_1@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<45ab836a.0308031802.2c9685eb@posting.google.com> <796f488f.0308042203.641afe64@posting.google.com> <bha4np$9lm$00$1@news.t-online.com> <3f38cfd6_3@news.athenanews.com> <slrnbjhm1i.2gg0.marcov@toad.stack.nl> <3f3908a5_6@news.athenanews.com> <slrnbjico8.dqv.marcov@toad.stack.nl> <3f397a8a_3@news.athenanews.com> <pvw_a.17582$vo2.15788@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:pvw_a.17582$vo2.15788@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net...
> One of the comments that I have frequently made in response to Peter's
posts
> in comp.lang.cobol (and which I will now share with the other newsgroups
on
> this list) is that IF (and I have mixed opinions on this) the "future" of
> business (particularly) computer programming moves more and more into
> "component - mix-and-build / drag-and-drop type" end user application
design
> and DEVELOPMENT, that it will STILL require "someone" at the other end of
> the new (and improved) tools doing the "lower-level" programming that
> provides such tools (and components and libraries).
>

It is normal and natural for programmers to see things from a programming
perspective.

What I am suggesting is bigger than that.

I foresee a time when there will be no need for the "lower level"
programming you are talking about, Bill.

The job of building the tools will be complete. And "smart software" will do
the enhancements to it.

There are already specialised tools and wizards to do specific jobs.
(Network management, for instance). Programmers are not redeveloping these
tools; you could argue that they are "complete". They work and do the job
they are designed for. In fact, it would not be desirable to have
programmers "fiddling" with them.

One of the points often overlooked about component based systems is that
these components encapsulate EXPERTISE as well as functionality. I have
purchased components to provide specific functionality, initially to save me
the time of writing it myself, then found that the component provided
Methods I would never have dreamed of (in addition to the ones I needed),
because the programmer who wrote it had many years of EXPERTISE in that
particular area and was aware of things I couldn't be aware of unless I had
also spent years in a particular niche.

I only have one lifetime and I cannot be an expert in everything. Sooner or
later it is necessary to trust someone else's experience.

Your assumption that there will ALWAYS be a requirement for "low level"
programmers to keep maintaining tools is, at best, arguable, at worst, just
dead wrong.


> I can well imagine the current "trend" away from every medium-to-large
> shop/business/company from having their own (semi-)large programming staff
> to using a combination of off-the-shelf (but customizable) applications
and
> end-user available "tools".  However, both of these require SOMEONE
> somewhere designing/programming the tools themselves.
>

But not INDEFINITELY.

> Whether this programming is done in OO, "procedural", waterfall, or
whatever
> type environments, is not something that my "crystal ball" tells me (yet).
>

It really doesn't matter. Sooner or later, the job will be "done"...

> If anything, my GUESS is that the "end users" of such tools will be LESS
> tolerant of "iterative find a mistake and fix it" applications than users
of
> in-house developed applications.  This means that I can see the "return"
of
> more strictly enforced "sign-off" of design, testing, development phases
of
> the TOOLS and components that are delivered to "purchasers".
>

Well, your guess is wide of the mark, Bill. The iteration is required to get
closer to the goals, within the time allocated to do so (timebox). These ARE
"in-house developed applications". I have managed projects where we did this
(no guessing involved). It is foreign to the way you may have been used to
working, but it is VERY successful, PARTICULARLY with end users. (Why
wouldn't it be? They are involved in the process throughout and feel that it
is as much theirs as ITs. Users and IT people work together to achieve
specific goals within a specified time period. Programmers write code (or
select and drop components) towards the achievement of the common goal.
Users can see something taking shape as the process unfolds, and they are
able to ensure it meets CURRENT (today's) business requirements and balance
the priorities according to the Business needs.

On such a project, users and programmers discuss requirements together and
the programmers cut code. It is not such a big leap to have smart software
cut the code. I believe that is what will happen within the next 15 years.
It is just too expensive to maintain "multi-lingual" programming departments
in-house. Smart software will take on this role. Even if it isn't
"intelligent", this lack can be compensated for by keeping Humans in the
loop. These Humans will be end Users, not technicians.

Pete.
```

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3f3abf9b_1@news.athenanews.com...
> It is normal and natural for programmers to see things from a programming
> perspective.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> programming you are talking about, Bill.
What is a "lower level" programmer.  The boundaries will shift but there
will still always be the lower level...maybe it's not a bit and byte
programmer but a trainer.  It's still a form of programming.  Even in a
sophisticated piece of adaptive software someone would have to provide the
learning environment - initially, until that role gets replaced and we move
along the escalator again.  I still see this as a role of a "programmer"
more than an "end user" - though the lines may become blurry.

> The job of building the tools will be complete. And "smart software" will
do
> the enhancements to it.
Don't forget the cost factor.  It's still cheaper to pay a labourer 80c a
day to strip bark from wood for 10 hours than it is to maintain the
machinery to do it.  Money will be the determining factor of what will and
won't happen - and soon after the class struggle.  I assume the same to be
more true in IT - it already is happening....free overtime is cheaper than a
good toolset.

> There are already specialised tools and wizards to do specific jobs.
> (Network management, for instance). Programmers are not redeveloping these
> tools; you could argue that they are "complete". They work and do the job
> they are designed for. In fact, it would not be desirable to have
> programmers "fiddling" with them.

Will the same TCP/IP still be used used in 15 years? Can we still use the
same network management tools when routers are obsolete, switches aren't
what they used to be.

Hardware *still* drives software.  Plug n Play never saved the day....I
don't see tools fixing themselves to work with the hardware unless there is
another operating environment layer...made by...

Another important thing you don't consider is the power of the hobbyist and
the yearning that some people have to do things.
People could all drive automatic cars.   But they still opt for manual.
People could ride in buses that drop them off at the exact destination, but
people like to drive and do. The business world isn't shielded from this.
Success of tools is still dependent on their usage.  Unless people all jump
high onto a new set I think 15 years is too short for anything dominant to
come along and shape the IT world you envision.  Linux didn't happen because
of Redhat or IBM but because of the underlying support from regular workers.

> I only have one lifetime and I cannot be an expert in everything. Sooner
or
> later it is necessary to trust someone else's experience.
> Your assumption that there will ALWAYS be a requirement for "low level"
> programmers to keep maintaining tools is, at best, arguable, at worst,
just
> dead wrong.
Not talking for Bill....maybe your view of "low level" is too narrow  ;-)

> Smart software will take on this role. Even if it isn't
> "intelligent", this lack can be compensated for by keeping Humans in the
> loop. These Humans will be end Users, not technicians.
What happens when the end User cannot get the results he wants...who's he
gonna call?

User: "Give me X"..
I can give you a kinda X
User: "Give me X"
I can give you a kinda X
User: "Give me X"
I can give you a kinda X
User: "Hey tech geek..can you get this thing to give me X?"
User: "Hey, where'd you go....hey tech geek...."
User: "Hellooo?..is anyone there...."
I can give you a kinda X, you still want it?

If I had to guess....Bill is too narrow and you're too wide....we'll get
something in between I'm sure (in 15 years!)

Last post on the matter - though I will no doubt keep reading :-)

JCE

btw: I hope you're a little wrong...because I still got to ride this out for
another few decades whilst you are relaxing on the shore somewhere.
```

"Marco van de Voort" <marcov@stack.nl> wrote in message
news:slrnbjico8.dqv.marcov@toad.stack.nl...
| In article <3f3908a5_6@news.athenanews.com>, Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
| >>
| >> On the contrary, specially for these kinds of users, sequential jobs
are a
| >> way of thinking that is normal to them.
| >>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
|

In 15 years, the world will be very different.

CASE will continue to mature, and if you think it will be impossible to
generate complete Business systems, that's your opinion, and you have every
right to express it.

I don't think it's outside the realm of possibility.

FROM:
http://www.cis.drexel.edu/faculty/gasson/courses/isys200s/Wk5.ppt.

CASE tools may be divided into several categories

Upper CASE (also called front-end CASE) tools, used to perform analysis and
design

Lower CASE (also called back-end CASE).  These tools generate computer
language source code from CASE design

Integrated CASE, performing both upper and lower CASE functions

FROM:
http://www.umsl.edu/~sauter/analysis/488_f02_papers/CASE.html

"CASE Tool Time"

MSIS 488 Fall 2002

Walt Jones

Just in 'case' you have been hibernating for the last several decades,
system development keeps evolving, and the technological tools that enable
system development are evolving as well. 'Case' in point: the use of
Computer Aided Software Engineering (CASE) tools is becoming more
commonplace for all phases of the System Development Life Cycle (SDLC).
These include data modeling tools, analysis and design specification tools,
user interface prototyping tools and code generator tools. Not meaning to
get on your 'case', but if your line of work involves system development,
especially large-scale system development, then the 'case' has been made for
the use of CASE tools.

FROM:
http://www.techweb.com/encyclopedia/defineterm?term=I-case&Define.x=26&Defin
e.y=7

 Search TechWeb For:

 I-CASE

(Integrated-CASE) CASE systems that generate applications code directly from
design specifications. Features include support for rapid prototyping,
modeling the data and processing and drawing logic diagrams.
```

I said that about IBM in 1984...<G>

(No, I didn't really... but they almost lost the company when they produced
a small computer that was universally available, dispelled a lot of the
"Myth and Magic" that surrounded computer technology at the time, and put
computing within the reach of everybody. Ironically, prior to that they had
behaved in a very bad way on numerous occasions and were perceived very much
as MicroSoft is today...the one time they did something really good and
noble, it pushed them into loss for the first time in decades...)

fair and resonable, could put aside our consuming envy of the wealth of Bill
Gates, could forgive all the times we faced the Blue Screen Of Death (and
see it as part of the natural evolution of PC technology), I have a feeling
that we could then acknowledge that WITHOUT MicroSoft the World would be a
very different place.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future reuse of code

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-08-13T03:41:20+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.java.programmer,comp.lang.pascal.ansi-iso

- **References:** `<45ab836a.0308031802.2c9685eb@posting.google.com> <796f488f.0308042203.641afe64@posting.google.com> <bha4np$9lm$00$1@news.t-online.com> <3f38cfd6_3@news.athenanews.com> <3F38DA6F.EFFBF81B@gascad.at>`

"Karl Heinz Buchegger" <kbuchegg@gascad.at> wrote in message
news:3F38DA6F.EFFBF81B@gascad.at...
>
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > in the form of Delphi/Kylix. I am currently writing Kylix software,
most
> > > of the cutting edge routines (that do the real work rather than the
user
> > > interface) are straight plug-ins of 15 year old Turbo-Pascal code. Now
> > > with Borland going for cross-platform (Windozze/Unix) compatibility
> > > there is no reason why Pascal should die in the foreseable future.
> >
> > There are 400,000,000 reasons why ALL procedural languages (including
COBOL
> > and PASCAL) should "die" in the not-too-distant future. (I don't know
your
> > definition of "foreseeable" but mine is around 20 years...)
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> with PROLOG and 10 years from now PROLOG will replace traditional
procedural
> computer languages completely. So, where is PROLOG today, 20 years later?
>
> [snip a lot of interesting thoughts]

Yes, I remember the Japanese PROLOG push and the drive to develop the first
AI Operating System.

It certainly failed.

So did attempts to build a lacemaking machine in the late 18th century in
England. The received wisdom was that it was impossible because the process
of making lace was just too intricate.

It took countless attempts, ruined families, suicides, and 30 years, but the
machine is viewable today in the Lace museum in Nottingham.

To answer your very fair question (... and replaced by what?), I believe
that new methodologies for system development will arise in response to the
pressure from the Marketplace. I have already seen interesting departures
from traditional methods that achieved much faster results and were much
more flexible. The key to these approaches is a more RAD like process with
iteration and interaction by users.  Currently, we have programmers and
"Quick Build" tools in the loop, but it is only a matter of time before
smarter software will take on these functions. Eventually, end-users will
interact with smart software to achieve what they want, and there will be no
programmer in the loop at all.

There is far too much on this to go into here (sorry, I know that sounds
like a cop out, but I have been writing on this subject for some years now
and have been using alternative approaches in the real world in industry
with results that are very encouraging.), but I will close by saying that
everything I am saying is simply extrapolation from what is happening NOW. I
claim no psychic powers, just good observation and a lifetime of experience
in IT.

>
> > Bottom Line: Don't get smug about COBOL dying and PASCAL surviving; they
are
> > on the same parachute and the ground is coming up....
>
> Procedural languages will be there for a long time. The languages may be
different,
> but still use the same principle. Knowing how to program in this paradigm
will still
> be the entry key to programming those languages. The rest is syntactic
> sugar (simplified).

Well, time will tell...<G>

Pete.
```

textual representations of procedural programming is something which requires
skills, skills we cannot expect from ordinary users. I have thought about
some sort of graphical programming. The problem seems to be: It is relatively
easy to come up with such a thing for a specific topic (eg. image manipulation),
but as I see it, it is hard to generalize this to general programming.

with

will

be no

> >

now

that
> > everything I am saying is simply extrapolation from what is happening
NOW. I
> > claim no psychic powers, just good observation and a lifetime of
experience
> > in IT.
>
> Do you have some links on that subject?

Here are just two articles which give some insight into the technology which
I believe will enable the Future I have been describing:

www.aboutlegacycoding.com/Archives/V3/V30501.asp (This is about what's wrong
with what we do now, in terms of development methodology...It was the
attempt to break out of this way of working which suggested to me how things
might work in the future.)

www.aboutlegacycoding.com/Archives/V3/V30201.asp  (This is about the
direction I see programming technology going in. The emerging component
based systems will provide the basis for the User interaction I have been
postulating. Components are platform independent, small, with encapsulated
functionality and consistent and robust interfaces. These attributes are
just what is needed to respond to rapid change and provide flexibility.)

I'm sorry, these are links to just two of the articles I have written which
are pertinent. I don't normally promote my own work, but you did ask for
some links and these will at least give you an idea of where I'm coming
from.


> I am personally interested in that subject. As you say: the old,
traditional
> textual representations of procedural programming is something which
requires
> skills, skills we cannot expect from ordinary users. I have thought about
> some sort of graphical programming. The problem seems to be: It is
relatively
> easy to come up with such a thing for a specific topic (eg. image
manipulation),
> but as I see it, it is hard to generalize this to general programming.
>
Yes, that's right. There are models where we can achieve a general result if
we use a Human in the loop. (Basically, the Human is providing the
intelligence and discernment to decide whether a given proposed result is
acceptable or not.) By the key processes of ITERATION and INTERACTION, a
better and better solution can be arrived at. (If you like, the solution is
"evolved" towards...this varies markedly from the "traditional" IT approach
where the solution is "designed" from scratch, then built.)

Consider this...

Small Businessman goes to the "computer shop" and purchases a "small
business computer".

(Let's assume he is a very competent Businessman and knows his trade
extremely well, but he isn't big on computer technology, apart from the
basic "computer literacy" that children are growing up with today...).

He gets home, unpacks the computer, uses an interactive DVD (or similar) to
connect everything up, and switches on.

B'man:       "Print me an invoice."
Computer: "What's an invoice?".
(This is a contrived example because the machine would certainly "know" what
an invoice is...but bear with me a little longer...)
B'man:        "An invoice is a document that records the details of a sale
transaction."
Computer:    "Ah, I know about Sales. That is a transaction where a CUSTOMER
purchases a PRODUCT."
(The machine comes equipped with the "concept" of a CUSTOMER and a PRODUCT,
among others. It also recognises the transactions we would expect to be
associated with a small business.)
Computer:    "So you will want details of the CUSTOMER and the PRODUCT on
this INVOICE?"
B'Man:         "Yes."
Computer:    "Like this...?"
(It produces a document...)
B'Man:        "Yes, but I need to see how many and what the unit price was.
Then calculate the total and add Sales Tax."
Computer:    "How's this...?"
B'Man:         "That's right. Put the totals for each product in a separate
column. And move the name and address details to here." (He indicates on the
screen with his finger or a pointing device.) "Print the overall total
payable in blue."
Computer:    "Like this..?"
B'Man:        "Yes, exactly like that."

There are some assumptions in the above whimsy (the computer has certain
inbuilt "concepts" - [you could think of this as a set of components with
all the attributes and Methods of a CUSTOMER, for instance], a natural
language interface, a "test" database for each of its concepts, however, all
of these things are possible with today's technology, and what is currently
"bleeding edge" will be passe in 15 years...), but I don't think anyone with
a programming background would say it was "impossible" or "infeasible".

Of course this demonstrates a solution to just one class of problem. There
are many others. But there are many other solutions also...

The bottom line is that "general" solutions ARE obtainable (note that in the
example above, we never know what particular "business" our "Businessman" is
in...the solution works for ALL small businesses, or can be "tailored"
easily to accommodate exceptions to the "norm".)

The keys to success in this are Interaction and Iteration. The Human
provides the "intelligence".(It is really "discernment"...)

But a time will come when the software will be capable of evaluating its own
results, matching them against stated requirements, rebuilding an entire
system in seconds to ensure that what is required is delivered. (Then doing
it all again, without complaint, when the User changes his mind <G>).

"Evolved" systems show every indication of being at least as good as
designed ones.

And they don't require computer skills on the part of the User.

Pete.
```

Nope, none of the above.

Everything I know, I learned on the shop floor. (In 38 years, even if I was
a slow learner (which I'm not...<G>), I'd have to pick up SOMETHING,
wouldn't I?)

I realise that there is a wealth of excellent work available now and I would
encourage young people to read it. (It's a bit late for me...<G>).

Try and understand that in 1965 not a lot was known about the theory of
computing, and what was known was not proliferated because it could mean
competitive advantage. Try to imagine a world WITHOUT standard "best
practices", installation standards, computing science courses, standard
algorithms even... We did things and got programs working. Next time we
wrote a program, we tried to make it "better" than the last one we wrote. (I
still do that to this day, but I realise there are thngs I could do better
that I am not going to change now...).

Pete.

be no

now

that

experience

> Do you have some links on that subject?
> I am personally interested in that subject. As you say: the old,
traditional
> textual representations of procedural programming is something which
requires
> skills, skills we cannot expect from ordinary users. I have thought about
> some sort of graphical programming. The problem seems to be: It is
relatively
> easy to come up with such a thing for a specific topic (eg. image
manipulation),
> but as I see it, it is hard to generalize this to general programming.
Karl,
Some years ago (maybe 20) there was work on a system that allowed users to
"draw" a flowchart of what was wanted in an application and the system would
create a C program to do it. This was put out by Mix Software (and I don't
remember the name right now) and I used it a little bit and it worked. It
could do fairly simple programs, but it had all the cononical processing
structures and was fairly complete. I don't know what became of it.

At IBM (circa 1965) we worked a little with a code generator that took
flowchart input and produced S/360 assembler code. It was never fully
operational and as flowcharting fell out of favor (remember HIPO charts?) we
dropped work on it.

Other than that, I developed a logical table parser (circa 1978) that
generated COBOL code with if-then-else structure to do the logic table
selections. It was a kludge, but was used briefly by the Federal Reserve
Bank of Philadelphia. It never caught on with the programming staff.

Good luck.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future reuse of code

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Roedy Green <roedy@mindprod.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-12T18:23:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.java.programmer,comp.lang.pascal.ansi-iso
- **Message-ID:** `<5nbijvcr7g7pabrrecne2a2djcieefuqnr@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<45ab836a.0308031802.2c9685eb@posting.google.com> <796f488f.0308042203.641afe64@posting.google.com> <bha4np$9lm$00$1@news.t-online.com> <3f38cfd6_3@news.athenanews.com>`

```
On Tue, 12 Aug 2003 23:27:07 +1200, "Peter E.C. Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote or quoted :

>Procedural code is already into Gotterdammerung. It takes too long, requires
>too much skill, is too inflexible (the accelerating rate of change in the
>Marketplace and in technology is another reason why it is doomed to
>extinction) and, overall, costs far too much.

What other options do we have?

1. OO -- also requires considerable skill.

2. FORTH where you create a language for solving problems in a
particular domain.  The users of the language just string words
together. Usually only a handful of people understand how it works
under the covers.

3. Spreadsheets, where the emphasis is on relationships, not on
precise order of computation.  The complexity is added gradually with
real life data used to test at every stage.

4. wizards, where you configure some generic application into a custom
app.

5. query by example.

6. training neural nets.


Spreadsheet logic is the one with the lowest threshold of technology
required to integrate it into Java.  It should be possible to write
generic apps, e.g. a retail sales package, and have the customer or
someone with minimal skill, customise it with bits of spreadsheet
logic.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future reuse of code

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Ian Woods <newspub@wuggy.org>
- **Date:** 2003-08-13T01:17:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.java.programmer,comp.lang.pascal.ansi-iso
- **Message-ID:** `<Xns93D6175DC976Dnewspubwuggyorg@217.32.252.50>`
- **References:** `<45ab836a.0308031802.2c9685eb@posting.google.com> <796f488f.0308042203.641afe64@posting.google.com> <bha4np$9lm$00$1@news.t-online.com> <3f38cfd6_3@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in
news:3f38cfd6_3@news.athenanews.com: 

> 
> "Dr Engelbert Buxbaum" <engelbert_buxbaum@hotmail.com> wrote in
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> provide them with computer solutions to business problems.

Unfortunately, the amount of people I meet who respond with something 
like "Oh, I don't know anything about computers." whenever I'm introduced 
to new groups of people is quite distressing. They seem to revel in their 
lack of ability to use a company, telling me about all the silly things 
they do like typing memos in a spreadsheet package. 

You're right: these people are not about to become computer programmers. 
They have about as much wish to learn how to use a computer more than is 
exactly required for them to keep their job as I do to be one of them.

These people don't drive the market: they 'enable' it's stagnatation. 
Companies who target them make the same old stuff and find ever more 
inventive ways to sell it to people, most of whom don't really need it 
but are convinced that they do.

The people who you refer to do exist, but they're certainly not a large 
enough market to bother catering for many companies, some who are 
believed to be supreme in our field. The actual 'geeks' who are above 
them in computer literacy, many of them programmers, want to see changes 
and quickly but they're not sufficient to sway the 'general' market to 
their will. Even together, the 'power users' and 'the geeks' are 
virtually drowned out by the point-and-drool masses who band together 
with their mantra of "We know nothing.".

> The Marketplace calls the shots.
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> of this Century, MAYBE, it is early days yet... <G>)

Unless Computer Intelligence (rather than AI) is perfected, and then 
widely accepted, I can't see this future coming to pass quite so quickly. 
:)

Instead of 2015, I'm going to look at 2010 a mere 7 years from now and, 
for reference, I'm going to use 1996.

Since 1996 there have been some changes for the 'majority' of large 
computer users. The 'hot' OSs now are a little different, and they're 
bundled with a few more toys which reflect the hardware in a standard 
retail PC. The kinds of things computers are used for haven't much 
changed since 1996 except for increased popularity.

Quite a few things have changed at the back end of systems the end users 
interact with, as the inevitable waxing and waning of technologies 
continues. To be honest, I'd be surprised if anyone other than the geeks 
and perhaps 1 or 2 levels of management directly above them actually 
care.

Fast forward seven years from now, based on where we were 7 years ago, I 
can't really see such sweeping changes being likely. Yes, there will be 
changes, but I certainly think it extremely unlikely that the programming 
profession will have been drastically shrunk in importance or usefulness.

> Procedural code is already into Gotterdammerung. It takes too long,
> requires too much skill, is too inflexible (the accelerating rate of
> change in the Marketplace and in technology is another reason why it
> is doomed to extinction) and, overall, costs far too much.

As always, it all depends.

Company X makes a product which contains software and suitable hardware 
to run that software. The software is essentially a one off cost. The 
hardware has a cost per unit.

If I make a million units at a manufacturing cost of �30 per unit (which, 
is an absolute pittance) that's �30,000,000. Now, is it worth trimming 
off �4 a unit by using a less powerful processor which is harder to write 
software for? Well, harder in this case means more expensive: you need 
more people who are more skilled. Is it going to be �4,000,000 more 
expensive?

In some circumstances it's better to use a more efficient language even 
though it takes more skill, higher paid employees and more of them to get 
the job done on time.

The only way that it's 'doomed to extinction' is if you can find a way of 
dropping the unit cost even further - especially if you were also 
dropping the development cost.

I really can't see this changing for quite some time, though change it 
will. How, precisely, it will change I've no idea since it's more to do 
with hardware than it is with software.

Outside of this sort of arena, procedural code has already waned taken 
over by it's slightly newer and 'better' cousin.

> For the last 5 decades Commerce and Industry (Scientific computing is
> a horse of different colour, and is excluded from this discussion...)
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> amounts being spent on in-house IT.

Good. :) Perhaps they'll have the sense to get off the 'forced 
obsolescence' cycle where working, useful and well used software (and 
hardware) is abolished in favour of something new, buggy and where the 
staff need a year or more to be fully retrained in it's use.

It might also reduce the amount of pointless computerisation which goes 
on, where the cost of setting up and maintaning (and replacing every 3-5 
years) completely obliterates whatever financial advantages such systems 
are supposed to provide.

> As fast as they get people trained
> they either leave or need new skills... Why bother? Why should an
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> offshore outsourcing, but it is unlikely.

Ah, but there is still a market for it - and one which is perhaps growing 
well. That it isn't quite so prevalent in your desktop or your companies 
network doesn't mean it's not prevalent in the other 9 computers in your 
room and about your person.
 
> Bottom Line: Don't get smug about COBOL dying and PASCAL surviving;
> they are on the same parachute and the ground is coming up....
> 
> Pete.
 
All languages and tools go through different forms. Ultimately, a 
programming language is a tool for performing a task. That now I may use 
language X rather than language Y is no different than any other 
technological progress: circumstances change and the tools of the trade 
are adapted to meet that change.

Nails are still nails, and hammers are still hammers. They meet a need 
and meet it well. Their shape and materials have changed a lot, but the 
basic function and concept is the same. It's no different with any other 
tool, be it an object you can find in your toolbox hiding under the 
socket set or a virtual thing like a programming language. They will 
change with their requirements but they're still fundamentally the same.

Ian Woods
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future reuse of code

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Stephane Richard" <stephane.richard@verizon.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-14T01:02:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.java.programmer,comp.lang.pascal.ansi-iso
- **Message-ID:** `<HcB_a.2659$jw4.1195@nwrdny03.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<45ab836a.0308031802.2c9685eb@posting.google.com> <796f488f.0308042203.641afe64@posting.google.com> <bha4np$9lm$00$1@news.t-online.com> <3f38cfd6_3@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Are you saying that in 20 years, a programmer wont have the tools to make
his own programming language, his own OS should he or she decide to?  And
they call that progress?  I call it going backwards here.   If this is what
I'm gonna face in 20 years, I'll be making endless copies of DOS Linux and
maybe OS/2 so that I have the choice to do what I want.  (note that I didn't
mention Windows ;-)

Here's my view of things, from my point of view, so you can't sue me for
saying this...hehehe.

We haven't even begun to touch the tip of the iceburg into what we can do as
far as software development goes.  And while microsoft seems to be amazed by
it's Windows, Where it's been, where it is now, and where's it's going to
be, since about 3 to 4 thousand programmers went into the making of Windows,
I'm not impressed by those results.  This having been said, So far, all
we've done for all these decades, is make the computer do that we dont want
to do.  (Hefty calculations, any repetitive tasks, games (not for the same
reasons of course :-).  But we haven't even begun to tap into the potential
that's ahead of us.

To me What you are suggesting is that we let the others come up with the new
stuff, give the users the ability adjust/change what the user did through
the use of somewhat flexible modules, and that's it for the programmer?  I'm
thinking much longer term than that.  After this step of yours happens, do
you really think that everything will have been made that can be made in the
whole computer industry?  I beg to differ, as this approach the the future
of computing is one of many thousands of avenues, and I'm not saying there's
only that way out of it, even if this ever gets made, it wont close the door
to the rest of the potentials that still are, to date, untouched.

But that's my vision of it, once your implementation exists and is stable,
do you think the users, ever so evolving as you say (which I do have to
agree that they are) will stay contended with this?  that they wont want
more?  Give a man an inch, he'll take foot, etc etc etc....I dont seen that
human behavior stopping anytime soon.  To stop that human behavior, we might
as well stop populating since after 5 billion people we can safely assume
we've conceived every possible kind of human being?  not at all :-).  far
from it.  And the same goes for programming.  Your view is one of many
parralel views, views that will all equally evolve, each in their own
specific ways, each towards very specific and unique goals.  And as long as
they are computers, there will be programmers.  And programming languages
that will range from low level to high level.  The way Pascal is adjusting
to the current reality of development, I dont fear that it can adapt to any
new programming concept we can throw at it.  It's been doign great at
adapting thus far.

Remember, software development is not a user only oriented concept. :-) at
least not in my book.

And that's my 0.02 cents worth :-)....(ok maybe there's a couple dollars in
there instead :-).
```

Thanks for the response, Stephane.

Some good points.

My comments below...

"Stephane Richard" <stephane.richard@verizon.net> wrote in message
news:HcB_a.2659$jw4.1195@nwrdny03.gnilink.net...
> Are you saying that in 20 years, a programmer wont have the tools to make
> his own programming language, his own OS should he or she decide to?  And
> they call that progress?  I call it going backwards here.   If this is
what
> I'm gonna face in 20 years, I'll be making endless copies of DOS Linux and
> maybe OS/2 so that I have the choice to do what I want.  (note that I
didn't
> mention Windows ;-)
>

No. I'm not saying that. The "limits" of my view are bounded by BUSINESS (ie
commercial) application development. I don't know what will happen in
programming research labs (and, to be blunt, I'm not really
interested...hopefully, there will be some exciting developments, that will
eventually trickle through to commerce, but that isn't where my particular
personal interest lies.)

> Here's my view of things, from my point of view, so you can't sue me for
> saying this...hehehe.
>
> We haven't even begun to touch the tip of the iceburg into what we can do
as
> far as software development goes.  And while microsoft seems to be amazed
by
> it's Windows, Where it's been, where it is now, and where's it's going to
> be, since about 3 to 4 thousand programmers went into the making of
Windows,
> I'm not impressed by those results.  This having been said, So far, all
> we've done for all these decades, is make the computer do that we dont
want
> to do.  (Hefty calculations, any repetitive tasks, games (not for the same
> reasons of course :-).  But we haven't even begun to tap into the
potential
> that's ahead of us.
>

I would agree with the above. The question is HOW we unleash this potential
in the future. I believe it won't be by Procedural code. (There are many
reasons for this... and I am NOT suggesting that NO-ONE will be writing
procedural code. It just won't be commercially viable to develop business
solutions in this way. In fact, it isn't even today. It's just that there
has been no alternative for 40 years...)

> To me What you are suggesting is that we let the others come up with the
new
> stuff, give the users the ability adjust/change what the user did through
> the use of somewhat flexible modules, and that's it for the programmer?
I'm
> thinking much longer term than that.  After this step of yours happens, do
> you really think that everything will have been made that can be made in
the
> whole computer industry?

No, that is pushing my argument beyond its limits and I couldn't defend such
a position. I am not speculating on  the "whole computer industry", only
commercial systems development.


> I beg to differ, as this approach the the future
> of computing is one of many thousands of avenues, and I'm not saying
there's
> only that way out of it, even if this ever gets made, it wont close the
door
> to the rest of the potentials that still are, to date, untouched.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> more?  Give a man an inch, he'll take foot, etc etc etc....I dont seen
that
> human behavior stopping anytime soon.  To stop that human behavior, we
might
> as well stop populating since after 5 billion people we can safely assume
> we've conceived every possible kind of human being?  not at all :-).  far
> from it.  And the same goes for programming.  Your view is one of many
> parralel views, views that will all equally evolve, each in their own
> specific ways, each towards very specific and unique goals.  And as long
as
> they are computers, there will be programmers.

The question is whether those "programmers" will be human... We already have
computers that monitor computers. In heuristic systems, programs modify
themselves so fast that the only way to know what happened is to monitor it
with another computer. While this is outside the arena I have defined for my
speculation, it is interesting and fun to look at.

I see a time when some programs will attain a result (based on trial and
error and a program modifying itself several trillion times within a few
minutes) where it will not be (humanly) possible to know HOW it arrived at
the solution. We'll just be thankful we HAVE a solution. There will be no
way of knowing whether the solution is optimum. The best we could do is run
the program again and hope for a shorter result...

This means a computer will attain a result and NO-ONE will know HOW it did
it.

As a Programmer, you may find this an exciting prospect, or you may find it
terrifying. (personally, I'm in the former category). No matter how you feel
about it, it will happen (has already, actually...).

Your statement regarding computers and programmers may be suspect.


> And programming languages
> that will range from low level to high level.  The way Pascal is adjusting
> to the current reality of development, I dont fear that it can adapt to
any
> new programming concept we can throw at it.  It's been doign great at
> adapting thus far.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> And that's my 0.02 cents worth :-)....(ok maybe there's a couple dollars
in
> there instead :-).
>
I have tried to give you some return on your investment...<G>

Pete.

A few comments:
I don't have 100% control now, when I give an assignment to another
programmer, I don't know what I'm going to get. The solution may be
effective, and efficient, but experience tells me it may also be neither.

As time goes on, I find I go into my bag of tricks less frequently.
I can't justify going the extra mile when a solution that is less than
optimal runs in an acceptable time frame.
Remember machines are getting faster, and larger, and sometimes the solution
for an inefficient programs is "buy a faster machine".

| As a user, that need
| becomes secondary to those "results" you mention but at what price?  How
| much more is a user (commercial or not) willing to be responsible for if
| everything fails because he was empowered to do it instead of a developer?

In the past we have had "user friendly" tools, and the user never used them.
It will be the same with system modeling.
There will be modeling "experts" who do the work.

<<snip>>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future reuse of code

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-08-16T01:12:13+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.java.programmer,comp.lang.pascal.ansi-iso
- **Message-ID:** `<3f3cdcfc_1@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<45ab836a.0308031802.2c9685eb@posting.google.com> <796f488f.0308042203.641afe64@posting.google.com> <bha4np$9lm$00$1@news.t-online.com> <3f38cfd6_3@news.athenanews.com> <HcB_a.2659$jw4.1195@nwrdny03.gnilink.net> <3f3c207c_4@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <oxX_a.18673$UB4.13645@nwrdny01.gnilink.net> <Ef3%a.101423$0v4.7002733@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:Ef3%a.101423$0v4.7002733@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
>
<snip>

> In the past we have had "user friendly" tools, and the user never used
them.
> It will be the same with system modeling.
> There will be modeling "experts" who do the work.
>
Only if we use modelling.

Suppose they just build something like what they are after, and then refine
it until it does what they want? (See the sample dialogue I posted between a
Businessman and a computer). There are many classes of problem where it
ISN'T necessary to model. In fact, modelling is more properly a part of the
"design" school rather than the "evolve" school.

But, in the interests of a lively discussion <G>, let's say you're right on
that.

So the modeller does the model and works with the user and they jointly
refine it.

How long do you think it would be before the user is capable of doing the
modelling?

(In other words, he (the user) has learned how to do the modelling by
watching someone do it, in a context which he (the user) completely
understands because it is HIS application requirement that is being met.)

It will depend on how complex the modelling tool is, won't it?

This is where the smart software comes in. If the interface is simple and
intuitive, it won't take very long at all.

If you need a 2 month course in order to be able to use the modelling tool,
then we haven't really made much progress.

It is important to recognise that there is a difference between designing
something and then building it (as we currently do, for the most part), and
building something, then iteratively modifying the design until it does what
you want.

Traditionally this latter has been frowned on. It smacks of cardboard and
string and Mickey Mouse solutions. Anyone who's a Programmer will probably
have encountered the "hot shot" who comes in, sits down, and starts coding.
Produces the goods in a day, then when it falls over in production, he or
someone else spends 4 days debugging it because it wasn't "designed"...

Well, that is certainly a good argument for going the "design" approach. (As
a matter of fact, I endorse the "design" approach for manually written
program code...wouldn't argue against it because it has a long, successful,
track record...<G>)

But hold on a minute...we aren't talking about a Human producing code, or
debugging it, or maintaining it.

Instead it will be done by software that is smart enough to do it, using
libraries of known functions and components, selected in response to user
requirements that have been elicited and refined by INTERACTION between the
user and the software.

Only the FUNCTIONALITY needs to be focused on; NOT the programming. Under
these circumstances, the "evolve" method has some outstanding benefits. Not
the least of which is that a Programmer or technically adept "modeller" is
not required. (That may or may not be an advantage, depending on your point
of view, but from a cost accounting POV it is a huge advantage...)

In 15 years I believe we will see users developing their own computer
systems, interfacing with the spreadsheets and databases they are becooming
proficient with, using the evolutionary model methodology, and smart
software to do the "grunt" work.

Power to the People! (And, Freedom for Tooting!).

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future reuse of code

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-15T23:57:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.java.programmer,comp.lang.pascal.ansi-iso
- **Message-ID:** `<yre%a.102090$0v4.7051231@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<45ab836a.0308031802.2c9685eb@posting.google.com> <796f488f.0308042203.641afe64@posting.google.com> <bha4np$9lm$00$1@news.t-online.com> <3f38cfd6_3@news.athenanews.com> <HcB_a.2659$jw4.1195@nwrdny03.gnilink.net> <3f3c207c_4@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <oxX_a.18673$UB4.13645@nwrdny01.gnilink.net> <Ef3%a.101423$0v4.7002733@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f3cdcfc_1@news.athenanews.com>`

```

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3f3cdcfc_1@news.athenanews.com...
|
| "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
| Suppose they just build something like what they are after, and then
refine
| it until it does what they want? (See the sample dialogue I posted between
a
| Businessman and a computer). There are many classes of problem where it
| ISN'T necessary to model. In fact, modelling is more properly a part of
the
| "design" school rather than the "evolve" school.

This sounds like the Waterfall.
I agree it's a shorter path from one step to the other, but it's the
Waterfall.

I feel that the main benefit of modeling is that the model documents the
current system.
The Waterfall methods that I've seen used, produce mountains of
documentation, that fail to provide a comprehensive definition of the
current system.

It seems that the original system design, if it was documented, is never
ammended as the system progresses through the evolutionary process.

|
| But, in the interests of a lively discussion <G>, let's say you're right
on
| that.
|
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
| modelling?

It isn't capability, it's the desire to do it.

Most of the users I deal with are involved in several projects.
I am usually dealing with several levels of management from more than one
operational area.
They are part of the development team because they want to ensure that their
needs aren't overlooked, and they want to get the maximum return on their
investment.
Everyone provides input in the discussions, and their knowledge, experience,
and stated business goals are essential to the successful delivery of the
final product.
IT, in some respects, is the project co-ordinator.

It's sometimes difficult to schedule a meeting, because I need to find a
common open time on everyone's schedule.
They are involved in other aspects of the business, especially the
management of their respective areas of the business.

If they were to assume the responsibility of modeling, the project would
probably falter due to differing priorities, personnel availability, and,
probably, a lack of co-ordination.

A dedicated modeling staff is needed to address all the issues as a whole,
and consult with individuals on an as needed basis. Inter-dependent
components can be incorporated in the design concurrently, and evaluated in
a more timely manner.
Co-ordination is the key.

In short, there is no "one" user.

| (In other words, he (the user) has learned how to do the modelling by
| watching someone do it, in a context which he (the user) completely
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
| If you need a 2 month course in order to be able to use the modelling
tool,
| then we haven't really made much progress.
|
| It is important to recognise that there is a difference between designing
| something and then building it (as we currently do, for the most part),
and
| building something, then iteratively modifying the design until it does
what
| you want.
|
| Traditionally this latter has been frowned on. It smacks of cardboard and
| string and Mickey Mouse solutions. Anyone who's a Programmer will probably
| have encountered the "hot shot" who comes in, sits down, and starts
coding.
| Produces the goods in a day, then when it falls over in production, he or
| someone else spends 4 days debugging it because it wasn't "designed"...
|
| Well, that is certainly a good argument for going the "design" approach.
(As
| a matter of fact, I endorse the "design" approach for manually written
| program code...wouldn't argue against it because it has a long,
successful,
| track record...<G>)
|
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
| requirements that have been elicited and refined by INTERACTION between
the
| user and the software.

No, between the "modeler", and the software <G>.

|
| Only the FUNCTIONALITY needs to be focused on; NOT the programming. Under
| these circumstances, the "evolve" method has some outstanding benefits.
Not
| the least of which is that a Programmer or technically adept "modeller" is
| not required. (That may or may not be an advantage, depending on your
point
| of view, but from a cost accounting POV it is a huge advantage...)

Except for one thing, the "modeler" may at this point be adept at
communicating with the tool, and be worth the extra expense.
Imprecise wording may take more time for the user to correct than it's
worth.

|
| In 15 years I believe we will see users developing their own computer
| systems, interfacing with the spreadsheets and databases they are
becooming
| proficient with, using the evolutionary model methodology, and smart
| software to do the "grunt" work.

Pete,
Some programmers have trouble developing their own computer systems.

You see users using these tools, I see tool experts using them.

The bottom line is, does the user have the time, and the desire to do it, or
will this be "deligated" to a specialist.

| Power to the People! (And, Freedom for Tooting!).

I see you're not from the north-east section of the U.S.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future reuse of code

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Dr Engelbert Buxbaum <engelbert_buxbaum@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-19T09:14:52+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.java.programmer,comp.lang.pascal.ansi-iso
- **Message-ID:** `<bhsipd$pb8$04$1@news.t-online.com>`
- **References:** `<45ab836a.0308031802.2c9685eb@posting.google.com> <796f488f.0308042203.641afe64@posting.google.com> <bha4np$9lm$00$1@news.t-online.com> <3f38cfd6_3@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:


> We may have our own favourite Languages and we can poddle away in a corner
> somewhere cutting code for the fun of it, but the real world demands that it
> get solutions. By 2015 a new generation of development software will see
> "programmers" removed from the loop and end users interacting and iterating
> with smart software until they get what they want.

Sounds like something big, clumpsy, slow and limited to some narrow set
of tasks, that one could just as well do without. 
 
> Procedural code is already into Gotterdammerung. It takes too long, requires
> too much skill, is too inflexible (the accelerating rate of change in the
> Marketplace and in technology is another reason why it is doomed to
> extinction) and, overall, costs far too much.

I have a different experience. Whenever I have a complicated data
evaluation task, I write a little pascal program to parse the data
(which sometimes come in a strange format out of lab equipment) and do
the necessary calcualtions. I keep a library of common task like
integrating statistical distributions and the like, which can be
recycled.

This is frequently easier than using a spreadsheet for that purpose,
which was supposed to be the easy solution for people who can not or do
not want to programm.

Such programs can be quick and dirty jobs with the charme of a
Unix-filter, as I am the only one using them. For that reason I am even
disappointed by the development that Delphi/Kylix has taken over the
last 10 years: Bigger, more complicated and worse documented. It may
help profesional programmers who need to make nice user interfaces. But
little guys like me who just need a job done were better off with good
old Turbo Pascal.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future reuse of code

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-08-19T22:43:31+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.java.programmer,comp.lang.pascal.ansi-iso
- **Message-ID:** `<3f42004e_5@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<45ab836a.0308031802.2c9685eb@posting.google.com> <796f488f.0308042203.641afe64@posting.google.com> <bha4np$9lm$00$1@news.t-online.com> <3f38cfd6_3@news.athenanews.com> <bhsipd$pb8$04$1@news.t-online.com>`

```

"Dr Engelbert Buxbaum" <engelbert_buxbaum@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:bhsipd$pb8$04$1@news.t-online.com...
> Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
>
>
> > We may have our own favourite Languages and we can poddle away in a
corner
> > somewhere cutting code for the fun of it, but the real world demands
that it
> > get solutions. By 2015 a new generation of development software will see
> > "programmers" removed from the loop and end users interacting and
iterating
> > with smart software until they get what they want.
>
> Sounds like something big, clumpsy, slow and limited to some narrow set
> of tasks, that one could just as well do without.
>

Yes, it could sound that way. However, I am talking about a different world
to the one you describe, below.

It is a world where, currently, groups of people in a given business need to
translate their business requirements for computer support into actual
computer code that supports their processes.  As they are not technical,
they need to interface with a technical group (IT). There is a "standard"
methodology for doing this (it is called "The Waterfall") which ensures that
the various development phases are complete, agreed, and signed off. This
methodology is fatally flawed but up until a few years ago there was little
alternative to it. (please see:
http://www.aboutlegacycoding.com/Archives/V3/V30501.asp  and
http://www.aboutlegacycoding.com/Archives/V3/V30201.asp) for the background
to my position on this.)

An alternative methodology is Rapid Application Development (RAD) where the
users work jointly with IT to achieve technical goals. These goals are
jointly identified and "timeboxed". There is far too much to go into here,
but having tried this approach on a number of projects, and found it to be
significantly better than the Waterfall, I got to thinking about WHY it is
better. The keys to its success are ITERATION and INTERACTION.

Very often users don't know what they want, but they know what they DON'T
want. If they can see some "dry runs" or prototypes, it helps them to focus
on what is really important, organise the priorities better, and obtain
systems that are more "up to the minute" in terms of meeting Business
requirement.  So, by INTERACTING with IT in joint workshops, initial
solutions can be obtained and these can then be refined by ITERATION.The
process of doing this is much more responsive than the Waterfall and so the
results obtained are much more likely to refelect the CURRENT Business
requirement.

Currently, we have done this with programmers writing the code. It seems to
me that in 15 years we could have smart software that can generate code in
milliseconds, throw it away and do it again in response to interaction with
a User. (The details of this "generated code" are, again, too much to go
into here, but it is reasonable to suppose that component technology and OO
programming and design will certainly play a part in it (see the links
above). In fact, I believe it is the advent of these two technologies (one
actually an extension of the other) that has made this solution even
possible. This "smart" software will achieve much larger re-use of code than
is currently the case with human programmers.


> > Procedural code is already into Gotterdammerung. It takes too long,
requires
> > too much skill, is too inflexible (the accelerating rate of change in
the
> > Marketplace and in technology is another reason why it is doomed to
> > extinction) and, overall, costs far too much.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> recycled.

Yes, I agree. In the circumstances you describe, there is no reason why you
shouldn't do what you do. I am not suggesting my proposed solution is right
for every context.

>
> This is frequently easier than using a spreadsheet for that purpose,
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> old Turbo Pascal.

Fortunately, you can still use Turbo Pascal (or Delphi or whatever you are
comfortable with...). As you are your own user there probably isn't too much
problem with whatever you use.

There is a HUGE amount behind the position I have adopted and it is
certainly more than I care to post in this forum. I have given links (in
response to requests) that give some insight into where I am coming from and
why I believe the approach proposed will eventually come to fruition...
(basically, it is a logical extrapolation from what I have seen work in
practice in corporations).

I believe that the commercial marketplace will drive the development of such
systems.

Pete.
```

```
James

How much time will be spect maintaining the code?

If the code willbe stable and not maintained too often, then development
time will be more important than maintenance time.

However, if it will be maintained quite frequently and you don't mind a
longer development time, just go with "C" or Cobol, depending on the type of
application.

If development cost is critical, then just choose any development language
that is not undergoing a lot of chnge at the moment.  Probably one that you
have skills to use already.

Jim

#### ↳ Re: Future reuse of code

- **From:** "Danny Maijer" <info@liveartists.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-06T19:43:34+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.java.programmer,comp.lang.pascal.ansi-iso

"James Cameron" <james.cameron@bindereng.com.au> schreef in bericht
news:45ab836a.0308031802.2c9685eb@posting.google.com...
> Hi I'm developing a program and the client is worried about future
> reuse of the code. Say 5, 10, 15 years down the road.

How about even 20 years down the road ? One of my former colleagues had a
crush on date handling. Up to now I am still using his code (with no changes
made ever since) in new applications and when maintaining other apps. His
coding even supports the gregorian date change in 1560......I never needed
that however.

That code was written for a NCR IMOS system. In plain ANSII cobol. As you
will read in the other replies on this thread there are some standards such
piece of code needs to meet. Like no user interface (only parameter
handling) and plain standard coding (no proprietry routines should be used).

I have put lots of this kind of routines in Fujitsu Cobol DLL's which makes
them accessable for other languages too....To be frankly honest, I never
looked at the source code of these routines. I dont need to, they work !
(Thanks to Robert Veldwijk).

Regards, Danny.

BTW, When I started programming in COBOL I was warned to upgrade my skills
to other languages (which I did) because COBOL would be dead and gone within
5 years of that moment. That was 1984......The main part of my business is
still COBOL today :)
```

Dear community. 

I am disturbing you to tell you that the new Rpv Steel is available in
our website for downloading. http://www.rpvreport.com

Rpv is the only tool that lets you produce Windows output from a Dos,
Windows or even Unix based application with no effort using any
programming language.

From Clipper to VB or from COBOL to GWBASIC, Rpv can improve your
applications giving them professional printouts.

The Steel version of Rpv introduces charts and a new kind of file: RPX
that takes only data and transforms it into a professional report.

With this new kind of file is not necessary TO RECOMPILE your
application anymore when your customer asks for some change.

There are 100 developer licenses with priority support available free
of charge for programmers. Just visit us at http://www.rpvreport.com
and you can obtain one of those.

Feel free to contact me if you need more information or technical help
about Rpv.

Thanks for your time.
Lito

<a id="t-bd3a96ec89"></a>
## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2003-08-05T10:08:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1060078082@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

> <howard@brazee.net> writes
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Alistair Maclean of the clan Maclean <G>

Nae true laddie ! My Concise OED (1964) : Slash : Make sweeping or random cuts with
sword, knife, whip etc....... So it isn't slang usage when used to indicate
'cutting' with a weapon, sweeping criticism, or 'slashed sleeves' (Tudor or Stuart
dress style).

Anyway, how about, "I'm dying for a pee, I'll go SPLASH me boots " <G>.

Jimmy
```

Alistair Maclean wrote:

> >
> >Alistair Maclean of the clan Maclean <G>
>
> ... Of Duart.

Thanks for the correction. <G> Actually I threw the 'title bit' in to be
mischievous. What prompted it was I'd seen an obit in either the weekly
International Express or our local paper. Can't remember the clan name but what
attracted me was the title - something like the following :-

The Lord MacGrath, of the clan MacGrath of MacGrath

His claim to fame - he was the chief honcho for the Freemasons or Toc-H or some
such, until he kicked the bucket. You may recognize who I'm referring to.

I know he'll comment - Howard are you familiar with 'kick the bucket ' ? <G>

Jimmy

#### ↳ Re: Compiler

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-05T16:40:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308051540.77ae3666@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<gKednQhdapCdZ7KiU-KYgw@comcast.com>`

```
"Barghest" <barghest@comcast.net> wrote 

> Hi, I'm taking a COBOL class in the fall and I've been reading up on the
> language but can someone recommend a good student version compiler that I
> could play around with? I have a win2k system and a linux system. I
> downloaded OpenCOBOL for my linux system but from what I was reading all
> opencobol does is convert COBOL to C.

OpenCOBOL uses C as an intermediate step in producing an executable
program. This is not much different from other compilers in that
source code does result in a program that will run.

There is also Tiny-Cobol and Kobol for Linux and/or Windows. A demo
version of Kobol can be found at www.TheKompany.com, it is cheap to
buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Compiler

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-06T01:02:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gsYXa.7189$WM4.1524@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<gKednQhdapCdZ7KiU-KYgw@comcast.com> <217e491a.0308051540.77ae3666@posting.google.com>`

```
A number of the books that are documented in the COBOL FAQ come with a
compiler.  Often recommended in this newsgroup is "our own" Thane Hubbell's

 "Sam's Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours"

See:
 http://cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm#books_24_hous
```

#### ↳ Re: Compiler

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-05T21:19:40-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Barghest wrote:
> Hi, I'm taking a COBOL class in the fall and I've been reading up on the
> language but can someone recommend a good student version compiler that I
> could play around with? I have a win2k system and a linux system. I
> downloaded OpenCOBOL for my linux system but from what I was reading all
> opencobol does is convert COBOL to C.

Fujitsu has a free COBOL compiler for download from their site.  It 
doesn't support all the bells and whistles, but it does do enough so you 
can get started.
```

- **From:** carolus <carolusm@tin.it>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4s70jvcganb91e42h34ugahuqai5a32fnn@4ax.com>`

```
Hi there,
 
I am currently working on a proposal for a software aquisition. i'm
using cocomo to estimate the maintenance effort in, say 2 years.

does anybody has some similar experience ?

does anybody used different tools, models to estimate the maintenance
cost? actually i tested, on hystorical data,  a model based on average
defects and average time of solutions and that seemed to work quite
better.

thanks

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>

"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:zsWXa.89075$0v4.6021199@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
> The modification needed to convert an X to 0 in the last position is
simple.
>
> AND the original code met the specification.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> We are talking about a student's assignment.

Why typical cobol development environments get a bad name.

One person solves it in one statement.....

Then a number of presumably seasoned working professionals working as a team
assume that the requirement was insufficient, and that the solution - whilst
solving the requirement statement - was, obviously, therefore, also
insufficient....meanwhile, Terry (she) has run out time and yet another
deadline has been missed.

JCE
```

jce wrote:

> One person solves it in one statement.....
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> deadline has been missed.

And exactly _what_ made Terry wait spellbound until the time ran out ?

Is it that Terry was forced to wait until discussions had completed before

while someone here still may have something to say ?  Are we to be 
restricted to only ever answering the questions of students and may add no

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xEkYa.17844$K4.1012324@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<MmCWa.17993$I_3.581@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <3F2AEFEC.CDB0DBE6@shaw.ca> <0sevivsujjhns19s31hbhh732ap2aiesdc@4ax.com> <3F2FE080.CCA12A11@shaw.ca> <t370jv45dr4ifqhdhu39ve70nhsc1o3k3p@4ax.com> <zsWXa.89075$0v4.6021199@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <NP_Xa.18084$qg3.1021251@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <3f310171_1@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3f310171_1@news.athenanews.com...
> "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message

> Dennis gave an elegant solution in 2 statements. ( INSPECT... CONVERTING,
> IF...)
> I solved in 2 statements which were pretty simple. (UNSTRING, IF...)
> But I can't seem to find ANY solution from JCE...<G>
Dennis' is the one to which I referred to as one statement when it was in
fact two.  Once I read this one and the comment from yourself - "THIS IS SO
COOL"....I figured everyone would be stunned into silence and be amazed how
sometimes something so simple will just jump out and make a
statement....This was the effect it had on me (sue me).  And I mean
this...it was one of those..."that's such a fricking cool way of doing
it"...moments that are so rare.

Also as it is with those new bands that are into the 80's - if you can't
beat the original don't try and do a cover.  Could I have improved that 2
liner? I doubt it.  Would you be interested in my more complex solution?

> So, just exactly what makes you feel qualified to criticise those of us
who
> DID have a go <G>?
I didn't criticize anyone who had a go.  I just find it interesting that
people take a requirement and invent new ones to keep themselves amused -
this is something that I see every day in *real* life.  Developers/Software
engineers interpreting requests that aren't there (sometimes important
improvements/decisions can be made but usually it isn't...).
I guess I was being <sarcastic> which is my usual mode.....though I do
occasionally try and help where I can...

> And your contribution (apart from offering observations from the sideline,
> after the fact) was...?

    http://www.ibm.com/isource/cgi-bin/goto?it=usa_annred&on=203-199

is an announcement that starts out:

"IBM SOAP for CICS feature delivers fully supported SOAP access to CICS

At a glance
The SOAP for CICS feature provides a mechanism which allows CICS
applications, written in any supported programming language, to communicate
via the Simple Object Access Protocol (SOAP). Transports are provided over
Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) and WebSphere MQ. Both inbound and
outbound function is provided. The implementation supports SOAP 1.1."
