### Re: Cobol 85

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-06T01:57:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3gZXa.89294$0v4.6037315@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<MmCWa.17993$I_3.581@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <3F2AEFEC.CDB0DBE6@shaw.ca> <3f2b2488_1@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <9bgvivgfk2com07946n1o8sc2ci0tvvquk@4ax.com>`

```
Ya got an error or 2.
see below.

"Volker Bandke" <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com> wrote in message
news:9bgvivgfk2com07946n1o8sc2ci0tvvquk@4ax.com...
| Hm, did anyone check the rules?  The ISBN is a weighted (10 to 2)
| modul0 11 check digit, which means the result can be 0 to 10.  If the
…[88 more quoted lines elided]…
|  IF N4-LEN > 1 THEN

You could have checked for this at the top (minor point).
if  ISBN-INPUT (12:1)  not = '-'

|    DISPLAY 'OOPS, more than 1 check digit'
|    STOP RUN
|  END-IF

You should have checked the lengths
N1  > 5  bad
N2  > 7  bad
N3  > 6  bad
Any length of ZERO bad

|
|  STRING N1 DELIMITED BY SIZE
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
|  END-IF

It's doubtful that you would get past this test.

You should have coded the string with reference modification
  STRING N1(1:N1-LEN) DELIMITED BY SIZE
         N2 (1:N2-LEN) DELIMITED BY SIZE
         N3 (1:N3-LEN) DELIMITED BY SIZE
  INTO OUTPUT-ISDN


|  CHECK-DIGIT=0
|  PERFORM VARYING I FROM 1 TO 9
|     COMPUTE CHECK-DIGIT = CHECK-DIGIT + OUTPUT-DIGIT * (11-I)
|  END-PERFORM
|  DIVIDE CHECK-DIGIT BY 11 REMAINDER CHECK-DIGIT

The calculation of the check digit is incorrect.
It isn't the remainder.
it's  11 -  remainder.
(the check digit when added to the sum makes the sum evenly divisible by 11)

You also forgot to subscript OUTPUT-DIGIT.
|
|  IF N4 = CHECK-DIGIT OR (N4 = 'X' AND CHECK-DIGIT = 10) THEN
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
|
```

<a id="t-ca98960d97"></a>
## Thread: Dumb WAIT question — 20 msgs

### Dumb WAIT question

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-06T03:57:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<M0%Xa.18086$qg3.1022798@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
I am of the understanding that cobol does not have a wait or sleep....
I'm sure that there is some LE function I can call but what is the quickest
and most simple method of having a "WAIT".

I don't know assembler so cannot code to the STIMER macro.
I've seen some asm here:
http://www.mainframeweek.com/journals/articles/0036/Wait+function+for+REXX+programs

but I don't use anything I cannot support.

Is there an LE function precisely for this - or some method of SIGNALling ?
Just a link would suffice - I have workarounds but nothing "elegant".

This is Batch and NOT CICS.
This is on IBM z/OS running Enterprise COBOL

Thanks

JCE
```

#### ↳ Re: Dumb WAIT question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-06T04:51:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CP%Xa.6008$kP6.1887@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<M0%Xa.18086$qg3.1022798@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
Do a dynamic (or static) CALL to

   ILBOWAT0  (first is letter "O" - 2nd in number "0")

Pass it a half-word binary (if that doesn't work, pass it a full-word
binary, I can never remember which) with the number of seconds to WAIT.

The parameter must be BELOW the line (i.e. compile with DATA(24) or have an
intermediate program compiled with DATA(24))

This is an ANCIENT OS/VS COBOL routine that is still available in the LE
library - and works for any LE-enabled program.
```

#### ↳ Re: Dumb WAIT question

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-06T03:19:01-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-4A1D75.03190106082003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<M0%Xa.18086$qg3.1022798@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
In article <M0%Xa.18086$qg3.1022798@twister.tampabay.rr.com>,
 "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote:

> I am of the understanding that cobol does not have a wait or sleep....
> I'm sure that there is some LE function I can call but what is the quickest
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> JCE

Then STIMER is your best bet.  I don't think LE has anything that will 
help you.

It is rather simple to use, here is the simple example:

CSECT URPGMNM
      INIT SAVE=(14,12)      <---- whatever standard entry you need
      LR   R1,R1             <---- address your parameter list
      L    R1,0(R1)          <---- assume a fullword passed as parm
      STIMER WAIT,INTVL=(R1) <---- wait that number of ticks
      EXIT RC=0              <---- return to caller
      END  URPGMNM

You will need to find out your shops init/exit standards and use their 
macros, but otherwise that is it...

Your example went over the top.  Checking out the Rexx environment 
blocks, et al...

Just do the simple 5 line thing and call it via TSO CALL.
```

#### ↳ Re: Dumb WAIT question

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-06T06:52:51-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bgqm9a$3vf$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<M0%Xa.18086$qg3.1022798@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
You can call the prehistoric OS/VS COBOL run-time module "ILBOWAT0".

For syntax examples, go to -

http://groups.google.com/googlegroups/deja_announcement.html

and search news:comp.lang.cobol for "ILBOWAT0". You'll get many hits.

Bill
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb WAIT question

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-06T11:15:35-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bgr846$jaa$1@news.btv.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<M0%Xa.18086$qg3.1022798@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <9dq1jvcprm1h7dvsuc5efeg4mftepc19l4@4ax.com>`

```
I'm getting a DB2 contention deadly timeout.  Right now we get a -911 and
the job goes down - the alternatives are to autorestart, just try again
repeatedly, or wait (or anything else you can think of).
Given that the time wait is usually about 2-3-4-5 minutes, I figured it
would be best if I could get the program to waste 4 minutes doing something
that isn't a tight loop.

I have to try and remember to include the "why" when I talk to this group
because the alternative solutions are almost always as good (if not better)
than the straight answer :-)

Thanks to ALL of you who posted so quickly...I am going to look at some of
these alternatives to see if I can do this.

JCE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb WAIT question

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-06T16:35:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a7aYa.87344$3o3.6038900@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<M0%Xa.18086$qg3.1022798@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <9dq1jvcprm1h7dvsuc5efeg4mftepc19l4@4ax.com> <bgr846$jaa$1@news.btv.ibm.com>`

```

jce <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:bgr846$jaa$1@news.btv.ibm.com...
> I'm getting a DB2 contention deadly timeout.  Right now we get a -911 and
> the job goes down - the alternatives are to autorestart, just try again
> repeatedly, or wait (or anything else you can think of).
> Given that the time wait is usually about 2-3-4-5 minutes, I figured it
> would be best if I could get the program to waste 4 minutes doing
something
> that isn't a tight loop.
>
> I have to try and remember to include the "why" when I talk to this group
> because the alternative solutions are almost always as good (if not
better)
> than the straight answer :-)
>
> Thanks to ALL of you who posted so quickly...I am going to look at some
of
> these alternatives to see if I can do this.

I would not put a production program to sleep.

If it never wakes up, programs which are dependent upon its completion
will be held up, and the Master Scheduler will remove you
from his Christmas Card List.

Other possible issues to research
http://www.quest-pipelines.com/pipelines/db2/tips00.htm#june

This is from the Sybase manual
If you receive DB2 SQL-911 errors, be sure that the LTMOBJECTS system table
does not share a tablespace with a primary table.

To correct DB2 SQL-911 errors
Use the same user ID and password that originally created the system tables
(usually LTMADMIN).
Shut down Replication Agent.
Save the data from the system tables using your site's normal backup
procedures.
Drop all three system tables.
Create all three system tables in a different tablespace or table blocks.
Restore the contents of the system tables saved in step 3.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb WAIT question

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-06T13:27:34-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bgrdr2$8ge$1@news.btv.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<M0%Xa.18086$qg3.1022798@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <9dq1jvcprm1h7dvsuc5efeg4mftepc19l4@4ax.com> <bgr846$jaa$1@news.btv.ibm.com> <a7aYa.87344$3o3.6038900@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote in message
news:a7aYa.87344$3o3.6038900@bgtnsc05-
> I would not put a production program to sleep.

************Under what condition would this happen?*************

The situation we have is predictable..we have multiple jobs executing
against the same tables doing mass delete/inserts.  It is not the operative
position and we are looking, and have looked at other options but this is
the most straightforward (KIS).

The issue is annoying because each program is mass delete/inserting based on
a different key field but we are getting page level locking in spite of
this.  We full reorg the table regularly which should limit this but this
has not worked.

The six serious solutions are:
1) Partition the table and thus separating the data which has other data
management implications when adding new data which means we have to actively
maintain

2) Set up OPC maintained negative dependencies - however this is consuming
with a every job to every jobdependency and means we have to actively
maintain(I've been told we cannot do an APPLID level negative dependency)

3) Capture the -911 and SLEEP and retry which is a one time works everywhere

4) Change the schedules to run the jobs as a special bi-monthly/on demand
run versus daily (the original requirement).  This is not trivial - but
isn't what they wanted - but IS what I am want...this resolves this and many
other issues we have.

5) Auto-Restart the job if it goes down with - 911.

6) Quit.  Move to the beach and retire (if only I was old enough).

The option (3) is the least impact - seemingly simple solution that requires
no future management of resources; however, your statement scares me a
little.

I would like to use this in other places too - similar to an MQSeries GET
WITH WAIT option.  I don't want to execute a tight loop that churns CPU for
5-10 minutes.


> If it never wakes up, programs which are dependent upon its completion
> will be held up, and the Master Scheduler will remove you
> from his Christmas Card List.
I'm not on it now :-)

I'm gone for the rest of week but I will look on monday for responses !

JCE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb WAIT question

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-06T19:40:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xQcYa.90242$0v4.6108981@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<M0%Xa.18086$qg3.1022798@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <9dq1jvcprm1h7dvsuc5efeg4mftepc19l4@4ax.com> <bgr846$jaa$1@news.btv.ibm.com> <a7aYa.87344$3o3.6038900@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bgrdr2$8ge$1@news.btv.ibm.com>`

```

jce <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:bgrdr2$8ge$1@news.btv.ibm.com...
> "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote in message
> news:a7aYa.87344$3o3.6038900@bgtnsc05-
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> The situation we have is predictable..

It isn't the situation that is a concern.  File deadlocks are a way of
life,
 and need to be dealt with.

However, if I am a sys admin and developers start chewing up my CPU cycles
or hogging my program initiators with sleeping programs,
then I am going to intervene hard, starting with killing those batch jobs.

I see Option #1 as worthy of further study and consultation with the DBA,
unless you are the DBA, in which case it is in your lap.

I see Option #4 as worthy of further discussion with the user community,
to assess their resolve in the matter of job frequency.

However, if bi-monthly is even being considered, then the updates are
not terribly time-sensitive, so why not trap the -911 error,
write the data record to an unprocessed data file,
and concatenate the unprocessed data files
from each job back into the next day's batch job,
or perhaps into a single-threaded cleanup job
after all the other jobs have completed for the day?

It is difficult to analyze this remotely, but I would shelf the easy
solution
until I was positive that my brainstorming had run its course
with little chance of any possible stones left unturned.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb WAIT question

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-07T23:02:44+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4da5jvgmtdg5u6sr2b9agfc18qek13b75r@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<M0%Xa.18086$qg3.1022798@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <9dq1jvcprm1h7dvsuc5efeg4mftepc19l4@4ax.com> <bgr846$jaa$1@news.btv.ibm.com> <a7aYa.87344$3o3.6038900@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bgrdr2$8ge$1@news.btv.ibm.com>`

```
On Wed, 6 Aug 2003 13:27:34 -0400 "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote:

:>"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote in message
:>news:a7aYa.87344$3o3.6038900@bgtnsc05-

:>> I would not put a production program to sleep.

:>************Under what condition would this happen?*************

:>The situation we have is predictable..we have multiple jobs executing
:>against the same tables doing mass delete/inserts.  It is not the operative
:>position and we are looking, and have looked at other options but this is
:>the most straightforward (KIS).

:>The issue is annoying because each program is mass delete/inserting based on
:>a different key field but we are getting page level locking in spite of
:>this.  We full reorg the table regularly which should limit this but this
:>has not worked.

:>The six serious solutions are:
:>1) Partition the table and thus separating the data which has other data
:>management implications when adding new data which means we have to actively
:>maintain

:>2) Set up OPC maintained negative dependencies - however this is consuming
:>with a every job to every jobdependency and means we have to actively
:>maintain(I've been told we cannot do an APPLID level negative dependency)

Or set up a sequence of jobs.

:>3) Capture the -911 and SLEEP and retry which is a one time works everywhere

How many times?

What if some idiot^W user is in SPUFI?

:>4) Change the schedules to run the jobs as a special bi-monthly/on demand
:>run versus daily (the original requirement).  This is not trivial - but
:>isn't what they wanted - but IS what I am want...this resolves this and many
:>other issues we have.

As the batch window is wide open, a sequence of jobs sounds like a good idea.

You can add some automation to attempt to handle the conflicts with on-line
users.

:>5) Auto-Restart the job if it goes down with - 911.

Can be expensive.

:>6) Quit.  Move to the beach and retire (if only I was old enough).

:>The option (3) is the least impact - seemingly simple solution that requires
:>no future management of resources; however, your statement scares me a
:>little.

It has its own possible problems.

:>I would like to use this in other places too - similar to an MQSeries GET
:>WITH WAIT option.  I don't want to execute a tight loop that churns CPU for
:>5-10 minutes.

:>> If it never wakes up, programs which are dependent upon its completion
:>> will be held up, and the Master Scheduler will remove you
:>> from his Christmas Card List.
:>I'm not on it now :-)

:>I'm gone for the rest of week but I will look on monday for responses !
```

```
TOP POST ONLY:

Be sure to commit, or rollback before you go to sleep.
You could create a deadly embrace.

Make sure that your program, and the others involved in the process are
doing frequent commits.

Sometimes setting up a "batch" control table can help, if you are doing mass
updates.

THAT'S ALL FOLKS!!

"jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:bgr846$jaa$1@news.btv.ibm.com...
| I'm getting a DB2 contention deadly timeout.  Right now we get a -911 and

| repeatedly, or wait (or anything else you can think of).
| Given that the time wait is usually about 2-3-4-5 minutes, I figured it
| would be best if I could get the program to waste 4 minutes doing
something
| that isn't a tight loop.
|
| I have to try and remember to include the "why" when I talk to this group
| because the alternative solutions are almost always as good (if not
better)
| than the straight answer :-)
|
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
|
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb WAIT question

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-06T20:49:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308061949.7b1b5ed4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<M0%Xa.18086$qg3.1022798@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <6f6Ya.89827$0v4.6079516@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bgrbjl$k6o$1@news.btv.ibm.com> <RZdYa.90293$0v4.6114393@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote

> Why?
> You didn't write a tight loop checking a time interval, like a co-worker of
> mine once did for the same reason you posted.

Many MS-DOS programmers appeared to not even consider that there ever
could be 'another program' and would sit in timer loops or 'kbhit()'
loops and would maximise the CPU time wasted by the program.

On straight MS-DOS this was usually not a problem, if TSRs ran they
would do so on the interrupt timer and this would wrench the CPU from
the program.

Many ways existed of multi-tasking DOS programs, from DeskView to
Windows to Multiuser-DOS.  On these the CPU _could_ be used by another
task, and timer loops and kbhit() loops simply degraded overall
performance while giving no benefit to the program doing them.

On MS-DOS there was no 'sleep', but there _was_ a way of despatching
the CPU time to the next task, using INTx2F AX=1680.

Many MS-DOS programmers moved on to Windows where they probably still
do the same thing.

Still, who cares ?  Just tell the user to buy a processor upgrade.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dumb WAIT question

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-07T07:58:44-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-8EBAAD.07584407082003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<M0%Xa.18086$qg3.1022798@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <6f6Ya.89827$0v4.6079516@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bgrbjl$k6o$1@news.btv.ibm.com> <RZdYa.90293$0v4.6114393@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0308061949.7b1b5ed4@posting.google.com>`

```
I'm sorry, I misstated the needs for STIMER.

(I know it isn't needed, and I plan to see if ILBWAT0 is available at my 
site, but just for completeness)

STIMER does SVC, not call, so you don't need any standard linkage:

   TAKEANAP  RSECT
             DS     0D
             LR     1,0(1) Ptr to Pic S9(8) Binary.
             STIMER WAIT,BINTVL=(1)
             XR     15,15  
             BR     14
             END    TAKEANAP

This will wait whatever number of ticks you put in that fullword parm.  
It will also assemble as is with no other includes.

Have fun.
```

<a id="t-8e340f3731"></a>
## Thread: Cobol 85 — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Re: Cobol 85

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-08-06T05:53:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F308CB6.1936D86D@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<MmCWa.17993$I_3.581@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <3F2AEFEC.CDB0DBE6@shaw.ca> <3f2b2488_1@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <9bgvivgfk2com07946n1o8sc2ci0tvvquk@4ax.com>`

```


Volker Bandke wrote:

> Hm, did anyone check the rules?  The ISBN is a weighted (10 to 2)
> modul0 11 check digit, which means the result can be 0 to 10.  If the
> check digit would be 10, an X is printed.

OK  Let's go through it again <G>

1. The student's task was to determine the ('fixed') format but wasn't asked to validate the check digit. That got introduced because Jerry quoted the same check digit reference you did for ISBNs. So her professor specifically gave her the format
n-nnn-nnnnn-n, (either to keep it simple or he didn't know Mod 11 can give you an 'X'. Chances are, he is an academic, not a working stiff like you who teaches COBOL on the side <G>).

2. Yes Mod 11 can give you a 'result' of 0 through 10. So you have two choices, (a) ignore, that is, don't use numbers having  check digits of '10', or, (b) alternatively substitute 'X" - which was what we did back in the 60's, (except for the
merchandise Kimball tags we produced which only punch-holed numerics - zero alpha).

3. We know from the ISBN doc that the format is a bit of a moving target, but must have at least 10 digits, including the check digit (0-9 or X). Again this can probably be improved upon, but this is my first shot. And as 'legitimate' numbers I'll use
the three ISBN examples (0-571-08989-5, 90-70002-34-5 and 0-8436-1072-7) plus I'll throw in a legit number with 'X' as the check digit. Let's work on the assumption we 'prefer' the numbers to have the hyphens for readability, but they are going to
move depending upon the numeric  format - which I don't cover.

I think the approach from Dennis was just great, given the student had a fixed format and no check digit calculation was asked for.

I'm sure others can fine-tune the following, (and hopefully I haven't goofed - it's at least 20 years since I wrote a Mod 11 routine ) :-

Jimmy

*>-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
Program-id.  CheckISBN2.

*> See ISBN Document :-

*> http://www.isbn.org/standards/home/isbn/
*>                     international/html/usm4.htm
*>
*> To summarize, the ISBN must be a total of 10 digits.
*> The tenth is the check digit and where the result is '10'
*> the character 'X' is substituted
*>
*> Groupings of digits vary, separated by hyphens of which
*> there must be three. We can determine where the hyphens
*> should appear from the ISBN document. This would add
*> additional logic to get printing format accuracy.
*> It isn't done here !

*> So, including hyphens the minimum field length is 13 chars

Working-storage section.

 01 n                       pic 9(03).
 01 n2                      pic 9(03).
 01 CharCounter             pic 9(02).
 01 DigitCounter            pic 9(02).
 01 aRemainder              pic v9(02).
 01 aResult                 pic 9(04)v9(02).
 01 aSum                    pic 9(05).

 01 input-ISBN.
    05 aChar occurs 13 pic x.

 01 Temp-Number.
    05 aNumber occurs 10 pic 9(02).

 01 T1.

    *> Valid formats and check digits

    05  filler pic x(13) value '0-8436-1072-7'.
    05  filler pic x(13) value '90-70002-34-5'.
    05  filler pic x(13) value '0-330-28987-X'.
    05  filler pic x(13) value '0-571-08989-5'.

    *> Invalid Numbers or mising hypehns

    05  filler pic x(13) value '0 571 08989 5'.
    05  filler pic x(13) value '0571089895'.
    05  filler pic x(13) value '0-571-08985-9'.
    05  filler pic x(13) value '00-70002-34-5'.
    05  filler pic x(13) value '0-8346-1072-6'.
    05  filler pic x(13) value '123-4567890'.
    05  filler pic x(13) value '123-456-789-0'.
    05  filler pic x(13) value '123 456 78 90'.

 01 T2 redefines T1.
    05 TableField occurs 12 pic x(13).

Procedure Division.

Perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > 12

   initialize Input-ISBN, CharCounter, DigitCounter
   move TableField(n) to input-ISBN

   perform varying n2 from 1 by 1 until n2 > 13  OR
           DigitCounter = 10

     Evaluate true
       when aChar(n2) is numeric
            add 1 to DigitCounter
            move aChar(n2) to aNumber(DigitCounter)

       when aChar(n2) = "-"
            add 1 to CharCounter

       when aChar(n2) = "X"
            add 1 to DigitCounter
            move 10 to aNumber(DigitCounter)
            EXIT PERFORM
     End-Evaluate

   End-perform

   Evaluate true

     when CharCounter <> 3 display
         "Oops ! ISBN " Input-ISBN " Must have three hyphens"
     when DigitCounter = 10
          perform VALIDATE-CHECK-DIGIT
     when other display
          "Oops ! ISBN " Input-ISBN " Insufficient digits"

   End-Evaluate

End-perform

STOP RUN
   .
VALIDATE-CHECK-DIGIT.

compute aSum =
  (aNumber(1) * 10) +
  (aNumber(2) * 09) +
  (aNumber(3) * 08) +
  (aNumber(4) * 07) +
  (aNumber(5) * 06) +
  (aNumber(6) * 05) +
  (aNumber(7) * 04) +
  (aNumber(8) * 03) +
  (aNumber(9) * 02) +
  (aNumber(10)    )

divide 11 into aSum giving aResult
move aResult to aRemainder

*> Note : I didn't get it to work using :-

*> divide 11 into aSum giving aResult pic 9(04)
*>                            remainder aRemainder pic 9(02).

Evaluate true
 when aRemainder = 0
      display "ISBN " Input-ISBN  "  is valid"
 when other
      display "ISBN " Input-ISBN  "  is INVALID"
End-Evaluate
.
*>--------------------------


>
```

## Thread: Routine Needed — 52 this month / 56 total

### Routine Needed

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-07T09:28:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com>`

```
I need a routine for a Windows environment that can be tickled to life by a
COBOL program (subroutine, plain code, Active-X, whatever) that will
compress a string.

The data to be compressed is 50 to 500 bytes, letters, numbers, spaces, and
punctuation.

Obviously, the routine has to be able to UNcompress also.

The goal of this drill is to minimize disk storage requirements. In the
application, there are up to 8 million records with an average of about 350
bytes each (about 3 Gig).

A reduction of 75% would be nice, but I'll settle for what I can get.

Any suggestions?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Routine Needed

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-07T20:03:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FgyYa.91493$0v4.6214464@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com> <DKtYa.91130$0v4.6192630@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <2fSdnd8KN-UlP6-iU-KYgg@giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:2fSdnd8KN-UlP6-iU-KYgg@giganews.com...
| Harley wrote:
| > "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
| I perhaps misstated the requirement. The file can be described as a
relative
| record, variable length file (although it's not that exactly). Each record
| of which looks like:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
| It's the (name,address, etc.) stuff I want to compact.

If you can't find what you want.

This is off-the-wall but,

You could "integerize" the data.
It would take some work on your part.

Assign a number (1-65535) to each word in the name & address (excluding
numbers).
Each "word" would then be 2 characters, if you store in binary.
Use a different delimiter before "in the clear" data (comma?), and a
non-character delimiter before the integers.

This may sound absurd, but if you create a file with the individual words in
your file, sort it, and remove the duplicates, you will probably find that
65535 words are more than enough, if not there are ways to use multiple
lists.

This introduces another problem, maintaining the word/integer conversion
table, but then life is never easy.
```

```
Dennis,

this is NOT absurd.

I wrote something that did exactly this for British Rail some 20 years ago.

It combined RLE (Run Length Encoding) with word indexing using a binary
field exactly as you describe.

However, I believe Jerry's requirement can be best be met by simply using a
WinZip Component and activating it as records are created and retrieved.

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com> <DKtYa.91130$0v4.6192630@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <2fSdnd8KN-UlP6-iU-KYgg@giganews.com>`

```
Sorry for my previous post, I was feeling a bit goofy.

This one is serious.

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:2fSdnd8KN-UlP6-iU-KYgg@giganews.com...
| Harley wrote:
| > "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
| I perhaps misstated the requirement. The file can be described as a
relative
| record, variable length file (although it's not that exactly). Each record
| of which looks like:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
|

You may not need or want to compress at all.
What you are describing is fairly common.
You may have too much data in a single record.

What you may want to do is split the logical file into 2 or more physical
files.
Name & address data takes up a lot of space, and for the most part it's
rarely needed.
So, if you place the name & address in a parallel file (same key as main
file) you reduce the amount of data you have to pass when you are looking
for conditions not in the name & address.
If you have data that is frequently accessed, you may want to place it in a
file with a small record length to speed up the processes that query that
data.
Once you find what you want, you can use random access to get the additional
data to complete the full "record".

You can also partition "active" records from historical records, depending
on the system requirements.

The more "dead" data that you remove from processing, the better your run
times will be.

(I just spoke to the director at a previous place of employment, and he told
me they implemented my suggestion to move the name & address to a separate
file, and processing times dropped. They also implemented my suggestion that
"active" records be separated from historical records with another drop in
processing times.)
```

```
In article <Ch2cnXUMUs4bfa-iXTWJhg@giganews.com>,
 "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:
> 
> One file, two file, 50 files. It still takes up disk space. It's the disk
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I'm sorry, I hit the post button before I was finished with that.

If processing time is irrelevant, then you could compress the entire 
file.  But 8 million X 500 bytes is still a pretty large file to cope 
with, and I'm sure the 1000 or so daily users would be somewhat grumpy 
about it.

Why not set up an index file with key ranges.  Then break the 8M record 
file into many little distinct, zipped, files, one per index entry.

Are the accesses random?  Can you predict a frequency by key?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Routine Needed

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-08T13:27:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UyNYa.2633$Ih1.970122@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com> <DKtYa.91130$0v4.6192630@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <2fSdnd8KN-UlP6-iU-KYgg@giganews.com> <x8AYa.91625$0v4.6222441@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Ch2cnXUMUs4bfa-iXTWJhg@giganews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-C08908.09095608082003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
>My thought was I could take the twelve fields and compress them on the
>build, before the file is written. Then, when read, take the 470-byte hunk
>and de-compress it. I would get the same twelve fields as before. The
>un-compress routine would turn the fixed-length fields of the next record
>into jibberish, but I don't care

As long as you have the starting offset, why not compress the first 30
bytes, too?Your'e using 150 Mb (30 bytes x 5m records) just for the "fixed
field" stuff.  No reason not to compress it, since you're uncompressing
anyway.

Or, go to a "block" scheme in which you store, say, 100 ot 1000 records in a
block. Your index file will now store block offset+ record number (or maybe
an offset) . More data compressed at one time should get you better
'overall' compression because of less ovehead - the token tables aren't
recreated for each record.. i.e, more data rates to be compressible purely
by token, since there is more data in the sample).

With your further description of your system, I'm surprised you don't take
advantage of the "automatic" compression mentioned earlier for keyed files.
```

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
>> My thought was I could take the twelve fields and compress them on
>> the build, before the file is written. Then, when read, take the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> uncompressing anyway.

OK, I'm easy. When I get a compress routine, I can certainly do that. But...

I can't compress anything right now - that's the problem - I'm searching for
a compression routine. Of the twelve fields in the compacted area, I'm
stripping trailing blanks and concatenating the result, separating the
fields by tabs.

For example, the first of these twelve fields may consist of 1 to 200
non-blank characters (think name of a company); the average is about 50.
Therefore, by truncating the trailing blanks, I get a saving of 150 bytes
per input record for that one field. That having been said, after the twelve
fields are truncated and concatenated, I end up with (an average) of about
150 bytes. It's these 150 bytes I want to compress. In the first 30 bytes,
there are no spaces.

In the best of all possible worlds, I would like to hand a routine :

[uncompressed-string, string-length]

and get back

[compressed-string, new-string-length]

When I say 'compressed,' I mean something like LZH or ZIP (but not the
actual ZIP because it has a header which is bigger than my record).

>
> Or, go to a "block" scheme in which you store, say, 100 ot 1000
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> keyed files.

I did. For a test file, my current scheme is 549.4 Mb. An ISAM file is 703.2
Mb.

Apparently the FJ-ISAM compression routine does not tamper with random data,
only repeated-character strings (like spaces). Of these, I have none (or
close thereto).
```

- **Date:** 2003-08-08T17:14:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1UQYa.10$rL.3813555@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com> <DKtYa.91130$0v4.6192630@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <2fSdnd8KN-UlP6-iU-KYgg@giganews.com> <x8AYa.91625$0v4.6222441@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Ch2cnXUMUs4bfa-iXTWJhg@giganews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-C08908.09095608082003@corp.supernews.com> <UyNYa.2633$Ih1.970122@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <5mCdnUQ0MYbPTa6iXTWJhQ@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:5mCdnUQ0MYbPTa6iXTWJhQ@giganews.com...
[snip]
>
> I did. For a test file, my current scheme is 549.4 Mb. An ISAM file is
703.2
> Mb.
>
> Apparently the FJ-ISAM compression routine does not tamper with random
data,
> only repeated-character strings (like spaces). Of these, I have none (or
> close thereto).

[Please forgive me if this is not still pertinent, as I have not been able
to read the entire thread.]

The Fujitsu indexed file data record compression is run-length oriented,
which compresses 2 or more repeating spaces, ascii zero, and binary zero;
and 3 or more of any other repeating character.  In other words, it is
designed to do for you what you already appear to have done in your build
program.

The Fujitsu indexed file system is also block oriented, so it should be
possible to use regular ZIP on the indexed file to further reduce the file
size for distribution (say, on a CD-ROM, as a self-extracting .exe).  This
will effectively eliminate any slack bytes due to blocking, and will be able
to benefit from duplications across records.

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Routine Needed

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-08T17:59:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qyRYa.2873$vo2.1081@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com> <DKtYa.91130$0v4.6192630@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <2fSdnd8KN-UlP6-iU-KYgg@giganews.com> <x8AYa.91625$0v4.6222441@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Ch2cnXUMUs4bfa-iXTWJhg@giganews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-C08908.09095608082003@corp.supernews.com> <UyNYa.2633$Ih1.970122@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <5mCdnUQ0MYbPTa6iXTWJhQ@giganews.com>`

```
Jerry,
  I may be missing something and what I am about to suggest is NOT "ANSI
conforming COBOL" but works for all compilers that "emulate" an old (bad?)
habit of IBM's.

Why not make each of your 12 "variable" fields an ODO (Occurs Depending
On) - where you do NOT include your "trailing blanks" but DO include a
"length" field (number of bytes) at the beginning of each "group" (or even
better all 12 lengths before the 1st ODO).

Assuming "most" of your fields have more than a "couple" spaces, this should
reduce the size a lot.  If many of the fields in include LOTS of trailing
spaces, this should reduce the size a LOT and won't require any compression
routine.

Again, having "multiple" ODO's in the same record will NOT work with all
compilers, but will with IBM, Micro Focus, Fujitsu, Realia - and probably
several others.
```

- **References:** `<87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com> <DKtYa.91130$0v4.6192630@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <2fSdnd8KN-UlP6-iU-KYgg@giganews.com> <x8AYa.91625$0v4.6222441@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Ch2cnXUMUs4bfa-iXTWJhg@giganews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-C08908.09095608082003@corp.supernews.com> <UyNYa.2633$Ih1.970122@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <5mCdnUQ0MYbPTa6iXTWJhQ@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:5mCdnUQ0MYbPTa6iXTWJhQ@giganews.com...
>
> In the best of all possible worlds, I would like to hand a routine :
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> [compressed-string, new-string-length]

The ZLIB dll does EXACTLY that.  Well, actually you would call the compress
in COBOL like...

CALL Compress-function
  USING   BY REFERENCE string-to-be-compressed
               BY VALUE  string-to-be-compressed-size
               BY REFERENCE Output-buffer
               BY REFERENCE Output-buffer-size
    RETURNING status-code.

If you don't have enough output buffer, the status code will tell you that
and you can PERFORM UNTIL Status-Code equal zlib-success OR I-GIVE-UP

Similar call for decompression....

More help needed? Contact my office for rates and availability.
```

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote 

> One file, two file, 50 files. It still takes up disk space. It's the disk
> space I'm trying to minimize.
> 
> Processing time is irrelevant inasmuch as there are fewer than 1000 accesses
> to this file per day.

Hold the file centrally and get your client software to access it via
the internet.  Use SOAP, a socket connect, or just a web server with
CGI, to validate access, enquire on the file and return the records
requested.

This way distribution and remote updating is eliminated.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Routine Needed

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-07T20:00:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308071900.15864605@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com> <3213885.1060277492@dbforums.com> <CoidnQhIis3TOa-iU-KYgw@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote

> [name-tab-address-tab-city-tab-state-tab-postal code-tab-telephone-tab]
> 
> That I want to compress using LZH or similar.

You may find that any further compression results in _larger_ amounts
of data.

One way to do compression is to allocate fewer bits per character.
First of all find the frequency of each character in the data and then
allocate 2 bit to the most frequent, 3 bits to the next 2 most
frequent, 4 bits to the next 4 etc.  It sounds improbable but it
works. Some of the least frequent have more than 8 bits given to them,
but you won't have those.

The killer is that a table of the bit allocations must be stored along
with the data.  For a megabyte file or so this is insignificant, but
for 500 byte data it may be more than the compressed data itself.

Another way is to search in the data for repeating strings > 3
characters long and hold those once with a flag of 2 or 3 bytes. 
Again for a megabyte of text this gives good savings, but for 500
bytes will result in expansion, not compression.

What works for megabyte files does not fit small data.  Anyway you've
already compressed it as much as some mechanisms do.

For a lossy mechanism you could put each character into 6 bits -
convert lower case to upper to cut down to only a 64 character set. 
That only gets 25% additional reduction though.
```

```
on frequency tables that are of significant size.

impossible, or, at least, impractical.
```

```
likely mean that you won't get maximum compression of the data,
but you will be spared the overhead of saving the tables.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Routine Needed

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-07T17:31:09-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308071631.2c7f2437@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com> <3214230.1060279392@dbforums.com>`

```
gmspano <member9303@dbforums.com> wrote 

> The best way is to crypt the string and store its values in the file.

In what way is the encryption smaller than the original ?

While compression and encryption both have the potential to make the
data unreadable, making it unreadable with encryption does not make it
compressed.

In fact encrypting the data will defeat any RLE compression as it will
change runs of spaces and of zeroes into seemingly random sequences of
characters and so, using Fujitsu for example, encrypted data will take
up _much_more_ disk spaces than plain data.

'best way' ?, I don't think so.
```

#### ↳ Re: Routine Needed

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-07T12:55:47-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308071155.4726ee91@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote 

> The goal of this drill is to minimize disk storage requirements. In the
> application, there are up to 8 million records with an average of about 350
> bytes each (about 3 Gig).


Fujitsu does some form of RLE (Run Length Encoding) on Indexed files
so the data space used by the file is less than bytes * records, but
compression depends on data.  Lots of runs of spaces and zeroes get
compressed quite well.

Microfocus has DATACOMPRESS compiler option which may be set on
individual files by using the $SET directive in the code around the
SELECT.  This is also RLE and won't compress random data but works
well with lots of spaces and zeroes.

I have used this to reduce an 80 Mbyte file to around 6 MBytes. 
Actually this was more than could normally be expected.  The product
file for a client was 80Mb and I developed a catalogue/order sytsem
for distribution to customers.  They could browse the products and
make up an order that was faxed to the client.  A version of the
product file was created with only the appropriate fields
(descriptions, trade price, retail, etc) and so lots of spaces and
zeros.
Using PKZip the whole system including programs (Cobol+Windows API)
and data just fitted on a 1.44 Mb floppy.

With Windows 98/SE/ME you can also still use drivespace or doublespace
disk compression, but this is not available in XP.

Otherwise you can do your own compression using STRING/UNSTRING by
reducing each field to minimum length (eg numeric to edited and left
justifying) and then string together using, say, | as a field
separator.

Following another topic you can 'compress' by using smaller
representations of numeric: - using binary or packed decimal and have
dates as YYDDD in packed for 3 bytes      ;-)

The compression you can get is dependant on the data - and they will
not be additive - ie stringing fields to variable length records will
defeat Fujitsu and MF RLE compression as you remove the runs they work
on.
```

#### ↳ Re: Routine Needed

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-08-08T12:19:32+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f32ed58_4@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com>`

```
Jerry,

you probably don't want to re-invent the wheel. (Especially when there is a
plethora of wheels to choose from).

WinZip has routines aimed specifically at Address compression (although you
say it isn't really addresses you are looking to compress.). The algorithms
in WinZip have been improved over time and have held their own in the face
of numerous benchmarks. They are robust and reliable.

You probably already have WinZip on your system. Individual users can
license it or not for personal use. (I recommend licensing it because it is
worth the money.)

Why not simply write an ActiveX component that has "compress" and
"decompress" methods using COM with WinZip?

Drop this component into any application where you need these facilities and
simply invoke these methods from COBOL.

If you like this idea but are not confident about doing it, I'll certainly
help.

If you just want a solution, I can provide you with the component, but I'll
charge you for my time.

Pete.

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com...
> I need a routine for a Windows environment that can be tickled to life by
a
> COBOL program (subroutine, plain code, Active-X, whatever) that will
> compress a string.
>
> The data to be compressed is 50 to 500 bytes, letters, numbers, spaces,
and
> punctuation.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> application, there are up to 8 million records with an average of about
350
> bytes each (about 3 Gig).
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
component,

> Thanks, but I've already got a ZIP Active-X component. My
ZIPper will do
> memory-to-memory compression, but the problem is that the ZIP
header is
> longer than the data field I'm trying to compress! Remember,
I'm trying to
> take a single field from 50 to 350 bytes and mash it up such
that it's
> between, say, 30 and 150 bytes in length.

As others have pointed out, compressing this little data with a
general compression routine may result in more data, not less.
If you're game to try Deflate (used by zip and zlib,) I would
suggest compressing with static trees.  This will have the
compression operate without having to save anything other than
you compressed data, and will mitigate your "fixed cost."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Routine Needed

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-08T01:09:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308080009.60b4ed97@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com> <3f32ed58_4@news.athenanews.com> <58qdnbAz14dLlq6iXTWJjA@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote 

> Thanks, but I've already got a ZIP Active-X component. My ZIPper will do
> memory-to-memory compression, but the problem is that the ZIP header is
> longer than the data field I'm trying to compress! 

Exactly !!

> Remember, I'm trying to
> take a single field from 50 to 350 bytes and mash it up such that it's
> between, say, 30 and 150 bytes in length.

You can't do that.  Given that there are A-Z, a-z, 0-9 and a dozen or
two of special characters - say 80 different characters, then each has
around 6.3 bits of information. 350 actual data characters (ie after
removing redundant spaces) will require at least 350 * 6.3 / 8 bytes. 
However you may be stuck with 7 bits per character for technical
reasons, or 6 bits with data loss (eg uppercase only).

You could try a fixed character frequency table (based on average in
the whole file - or a guess) for a variable bit size compression. 
This will get closer to the 6.3 bits average, maybe even do better
depending on distribution.

Using a common word table is probably your only hope of reducing data
size, but is entirely data dependent on how successful it might be.

Why not just use 3 GByes ?

You may need to avoid any file size limits by doing it in several file
sections.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Routine Needed

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-08T03:58:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bgvonh0esn@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com> <3f32ed58_4@news.athenanews.com> <58qdnbAz14dLlq6iXTWJjA@giganews.com>`

```
I took your original posting and ran it through zlib's Deflate.
Successively truncating the message gives an anecdotal measure
of the compression's performance:

raw    compressed
size   size
  0      8   'probably just literally storing the bytes
  1      9   'probably just literally storing the bytes
  2     10   'probably just literally storing the bytes
  7     15   'probably just literally storing the bytes
 15     23   'probably just literally storing the bytes
 32     38
 64     66
 85     85
102     94
115    107
140    124
167    141
260    197
323    225
504    328
575    371
593    384

If I recall correctly, the compressed block is prefixed by some
parameters and perhaps a checksum -- that would account for
those 8 bytes.  It depends upon where your mean is in the
50-500 character range.  If it's closer to 50, I don't think
deflate can help.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Routine Needed

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-08T01:49:34-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bgvh6106l8@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com> <zoCdnaElgviUa6-iU-KYuQ@comcast.com> <W7Gdnbc-NaxBka6iU-KYgw@giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:W7Gdnbc-NaxBka6iU-KYgw@giganews.com...
> JJ wrote:
> > Do a google search for "zlib.dll".
> >
>
> Thanks. Zlib seems to make ZIP files. I don't want to
compress a file - I
> want to compress each of the (up to) 8 million records within
a file.

zlib is a Deflate-style compression library.  It's compatible
with the one that pkzip/winzip uses, but the library is just
the compression stuff -- no header or directory caching.  You
can find the source code here:

http://www.gzip.org/zlib/

I've used the code for a few years and have found it to be
reliable.

I wasn't going to mention it, because I thought you were
looking for a COBOL solution.  Since you showed some interest
in an ActiveX component, however, zlib definitely deserves a
look.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Routine Needed

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-08T09:49:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:W7Gdnbc-NaxBka6iU-KYgw@giganews.com...
> JJ wrote:
> > Do a google search for "zlib.dll".

I just pulled DL'd  ZLIB for one of my projects and it works just ducky. (My
app is not a cobol application, but ZLIB is just a Win/32 DLL and can be
called from any application).

Your problem trying to compress each *record* rather than the entire file is
that if you want to use random access on the data you are going to have to
create and manage some kind of index, since each record will not compress to
the same size; and without an index, you'll never find your records again.

If sequential access is OK, you could store the compressed length in the
data stream, that is, your data stream will look like..

<compresseseddatalength><data><compresseseddatalength><data><compresseseddat
alength><data>....

I lost track here... what is the goal again? Is is to save memory (disk or
RAM) or encrypt the data?

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Routine Needed

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-08T07:44:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u7Ocnf2CDuSDBq6iU-KYuQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com> <zoCdnaElgviUa6-iU-KYuQ@comcast.com> <W7Gdnbc-NaxBka6iU-KYgw@giganews.com> <gmKYa.2593$Ih1.939549@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
> news:W7Gdnbc-NaxBka6iU-KYgw@giganews.com...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> you'll never find your records again.

Sure. The scheme you suggest (and the one I use) works swell (the
cross-reference file point to the beginning byte of each record). For
example, if the tenth record in the file is SMITH, a query of the
cross-reference file for "SMITH" might return 3456 (meaning the record
containing "SMITH" begins at byte 3456 within the file). Then the master is
read at diplacement 3456 for 500 bytes.

The file's just bigger than I would like it to be.

>
> If sequential access is OK, you could store the compressed length in
> the data stream, that is, your data stream will look like..
>
>
<compresseseddatalength><data><compresseseddatalength><data><compresseseddat
> alength><data>....

Not exactly - I don't need the <compresseddatalength>.  Here's why. I know
the starting byte, within the file, of the record of interest.

I grab 500 bytes starting at that location. The first 30 bytes are known
entities - fixed length fields. What comes after the first 30 bytes is from
50 to 350 bytes of ASCII data separated by tabs. Exactly 12 tabs. I start
UNSTRINGING DELIMITED by TAB until I've got the 12 fields discarding the
remainder of the 500 bytes from the initial read.

My thought was I could take the twelve fields and compress them on the
build, before the file is written. Then, when read, take the 470-byte hunk
and de-compress it. I would get the same twelve fields as before. The
un-compress routine would turn the fixed-length fields of the next record
into jibberish, but I don't care.

Here's the math. Right now I have 5 million records.

Each record has, on average, 250 bytes of ASCII data. These ASCII data total
1.25 Gb of disk space. If I could compress the ASCII data by 50%, I could
save over 600 Mb of disk space.

The problem scales up rapidly, as the medium-term plan calls for doubling
the file size.


>
> I lost track here... what is the goal again? Is is to save memory
> (disk or RAM) or encrypt the data?

To save disk space. I don't care about performance or encryption.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Routine Needed

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-09T22:53:23-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308092153.2e434374@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com> <zoCdnaElgviUa6-iU-KYuQ@comcast.com> <W7Gdnbc-NaxBka6iU-KYgw@giganews.com> <gmKYa.2593$Ih1.939549@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <u7Ocnf2CDuSDBq6iU-KYuQ@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote 

> Sure. The scheme you suggest (and the one I use) works swell (the
> cross-reference file point to the beginning byte of each record). For
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> read at diplacement 3456 for 500 bytes.

A 4 byte long int can only hold up to 2 billion, so any file that may
be larger than this (2 GByte) will need to be broken to sub-files and
the index will need to keep the file-number.
You probably can just use a bigger number because the underlying fseek
only takes a long for the offset.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Routine Needed

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-10T12:42:47-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308101142.6cb0a30a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com> <zoCdnaElgviUa6-iU-KYuQ@comcast.com> <W7Gdnbc-NaxBka6iU-KYgw@giganews.com> <gmKYa.2593$Ih1.939549@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <u7Ocnf2CDuSDBq6iU-KYuQ@giganews.com> <217e491a.0308092153.2e434374@posting.google.com> <XcqZa.92504$3o3.6436695@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote

> | A 4 byte long int can only hold up to 2 billion,
> 
> In COBOL you can use unsigned numbers, the max value (x'FFFFFFFF') is
> 4,294,967,295.

Have you tried this with Fujitsu ?  I have, it may not work as you
expect.

Of course a PIC 9(9) <something> is only required to hold 9,999,999. 
While NOTRUNC does have certain effects, it doesn't allow a VALUE or a
literal move greater than this.  I suspect that it also does not make
the underlying long int unsigned as this is not needed for maximum
values.

But the problem is not getting the bits set in the long int, but
having these actioned correctly by fseek.  As fseek only takes a
_signed_ long int as the offset then 2 Gig is the limiting size for
accessing a file using this mechanism, which is why, for example,
there is a 2Gbyte limit on ISAM files.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Routine Needed

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com...
> I need a routine for a Windows environment that can be tickled to life by a
> COBOL program (subroutine, plain code, Active-X, whatever) that will
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any suggestions?

The Flexus Thin Client includes:

1.  Data encryption routines which can be turned on or off.
2.  Data compression routines which can be turned on or off.
3.  File transfer routines to send and receive files across a TCP/IP
network.

You don't even have use sp2 or FormPrint to utilize these routines.
You can just use them to send and receive compressed and encrypted
files across the network.





Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

```
> application, there are up to 8 million records with an average of about
350
> bytes each (about 3 Gig).
>
> A reduction of 75% would be nice, but I'll settle for what I can get.
>
> Any suggestions?

One possibility, not mentioned, is to use shift encoding as was
used with Baudot. Three shift codes would allow for three
alphabets (upper-case, lower-case, and numbers and punctuation)
each of which may be encoded in five bits. This may approach
35% compaction. A similar method is shown in James Martin,
"Computer Data-Base Organization" in the chapter titled, "Data
Compaction".

To achieve a greater reduction Huffman encoding may be used.
This could reach the goal of 75%; but 50% may be more realistic.
The compaction ratio for Huffman encoding relies upon some
characters occurring far more frequently than others, such as may
be found with the leading zeros and trailing spaces that have
already be removed. This method has been mentioned, just not as
Huffman encoding.

Both shift encoding and Huffman encoding work well on character
data.

The Huffman encoding method is sufficiently generalized that
a program may be written that accepts, as samples, the data
to be compacted; then generates the program sources to
accomplish the actual encoding and decoding. The first phase,
collecting frequency counts for each character, may be used to
assess how much compaction may be achieved and thus
whether to proceed with writing the program generator.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Routine Needed

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-08T14:45:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308081345.25661071@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com> <vj7sgnq1f5j3a2@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote

> One possibility, not mentioned, is to use shift encoding as was
> used with Baudot. Three shift codes would allow for three
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Compaction".

Each alphabet could be 27 characters including space (allowing 16
punctuations) leaving 5 'special codes'.  3 of which are shifts.  Use
one as 'field terminator' (eg all zero bits).  Then there is 1 which
can be given a special task.  If the data was 'proper case names' this
could be a special shift which is 'next single character is upper'
which can save one shift per proper case word.
I recall having to read baudot paper tapes with square holes back in
the late 60s. A special adaptor had to be made for our ICL 1921(?)
Reader/Punch which was 8 hole plus sproket. The sproket was offset so
the normal tape was 5|3 allowing the baudot tape to be read on the 5
side.  They had to change the electronics somewhat as the sproket
holes were used to trigger the read.  The square holes gave a fast
rise time on the photdetectors and they used that on an or-gate, a
slight delay, then trigger.

Converting the data was just 3 look up tables so no problem - unless
the operator put the tape in upside down or backwards.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Routine Needed

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-08T18:49:52-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vj8ab1msv2c627@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com> <vj7sgnq1f5j3a2@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0308081345.25661071@posting.google.com>`

```

Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0308081345.25661071@posting.google.com...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> which can save one shift per proper case word.

If there are consecutive numeric fields, those may be decoded
without additional shifts. If the alpha fields are always proper
case, the upper-case shift could default to 'next is upper' allowing
for an additional character, such as '.' to occur in all three
alphabets. This may reduce the number of shifts for initials and
for abbreviations for both names and street addresses.

I can imagine this encoding scheme appoaching 5.2 bits per
character or 35% compaction.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Routine Needed

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>

- **References:** `<87mdndshFp-o_6-iU-KYgw@giganews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0308091504.77154c29@posting.google.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
>
> There are a bazzillion replies - but I happen to know you use fujitsu
> - so if you save them in an indexed file - you get data compression
> automatically.
> No work to do.

As it turns out, I've abandoned the project and taken up pig farming.

However, FJ ISAM doesn't compact random data - only strings of repeating
characters - of which I have none: never more than one consecutive space,
maybe as many as four zeros in a row, maybe a doubled letter every once in a
while ("little"). Not a good candidate for RLE.

While we're on the subject of FJ, how come the max size for an ISAM file is
2 Gig and for a sequential file only 1 Gig? How would you reorganize a 1.9
Gig ISAM file? (Yes, I know they upped it to 2 and 2 in Version 7, but the
reorg problem remains.)

FJ has a utility to reorganize, but it, too, builds an intermediate
sequential file.

Mind you, I haven't tried to reorg a large ISAM file yet, just asking.
```

```
Using netexpress 4.0 I get the following error

   665     ADD 1 TO WS-STAT-LAYT-COUNT
   666           (LAYT-PAPER,((WS-YMD-MM * 100) + WS-YMD-DD) ).
* 309-S*****************************************************           (
3)**
**    Malformed subscript

The field is declared like this.

   390 01  WS-STATISTICS.
   391     03  WS-STAT-PAPER            OCCURS 10.
   392         05  WS-STAT-LAYT-COUNT   PIC 9(4) COMP OCCURS 1231.
   393         05  WS-STAT-VERS-COUNT   PIC 9(4) COMP OCCURS 1231.


According to the documentation this should be allowed.

Any ideas.
```

```
In this thread
Concerning  Steve Rainbird's <steve.rainbird@nospam.mssint.com>
orginal 'malformed subscript'

"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote in message
news:nOwYa.91336$0v4.6208142@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...

snips
> ...
>
> Try this ?
> (LAYT-PAPER, ((WS-YMD-MM * 100) + WS-YMD-DD)).

Steve Rainbird steve.rainbird@nospam.mssint.com 
replied
Date: 8/8/03 3:56 AM EST
Message-id: <bgvoji$5ed$1@hercules.btinternet.com>

>Nope still errors

That is curious. Independent of the multidimmensional array subscripting snafu
so eloquently discoursed here I am very interested in knowing if the problem is
generalized even to single dimmensional arrays, in the compiler Steve is using.

Like this.

* terminate-previous-statement-clearly
* with a period.

ADD 1 TO
SINGLE-DIM-ARRAY, (simpe-subscript).

With a comma followed by a space in the array reference. If your compiler's
problem is generalized this ought to compile cleanly!

My point is really to disagree with a posting by Bill Klien to the effect that
the compiler ought to know that the item is or is not an array. Well, not
really. This kind of thing is actually type checking which is frequently
defered until _after_parse_ in a compiler. 

So the compiler authors are stuck with trying to sort things out lexically or
syntactically. And regrettably the COBOL comma is the worst thing in the
compiler writers life, a transparency.

In the discussions that have occured here it is definitely surprising that the
orginal poster states that a space after the comma did not solve the problem. 

How general is it? Does it happen in a MOVE as well as an ADD. Will it happen
in a COMPUTE, in the compiler under discussion?

A humorous variation of this inquiry would be what happens if you place the
space in front of the comma?  If we are dealing with internals that are
scanning for lookahead ciphers, then the internal trick needed to make the
comman transparent are good enough to swallow the comma but can't quite see
over the horizon to the following space according to the original posters
response.

Best Wishes,
Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: malformed subscript

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-23T03:27:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%aB1b.1912$O03.1529@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<bgvoji$5ed$1@hercules.btinternet.com> <20030822221626.22063.00001315@mb-m07.aol.com>`

```
Just so it is clear, the "comma" is NOT a separator in ANSI/ISO COBOL (any
version - '68, '74, '85 or 2002).  To be a separator, the comma MUST be
followed by a space (in Standard COBOL).  Furthermore, where it IS followed
by a space it has NO special meaning other than that which is valid for a
space by itself.  This has been true in the '85 Standard and the 2002
Standard (I am not positive about earlier ones).  This means that the
following is PERFECTLY valid (Standard-conforming) code (no matter how UGLY)
and will compile with any "'85 Standard compliant" compiler:

 01 B.
    05 D   Pic,  9.
    05 A   Pic X.
    05 C occurs,  5 times Pic X.
        ...
  Move, A, of, B to, C (D, +, 1, )

On the other hand, although some compiler MIGHT allow it, the following is
NOT ANSI/ISO conforming (any version of the Standard):

 01 B.
    05 D   Pic 9.
    05 E   Pic 9.
    05 A   Pic X.
    05 C occurs 5 times.
         10 F   occurs 5 times   Pic X.
        ...
* no space between the comma and E in the following line
  Move A  to  F (D,E)
```

```
> Standard (I am not positive about earlier ones).  This means that the

UGLY)

>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…

space,
trigger an error, as is done now.

    Or possibly a warning, and assume a space.


    Seems like life would be easier if they were allowed to make a comma a
separator.  Would that really hurt anything, other than the ability to take
your nonconforming code elsewhere?
```

```
I predict that the parenthesised expression, while valid, is being
treated as a subcript of LAYT-PAPER rather than the 2nd subscript of
WS-STAT-LAYT-COUNT because there is an invalid separator between them.

(ie comma+space is valid, space is valid, comma by itself is not).
```
