- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <jMXYa.93451$0v4.6346051@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bh8cog$utr2p$2@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <r0PZa.96449$0v4.6643248@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
>>> Harley<dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> 08/11/03 09:56AM >>>
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>You get a simple explicit structure that way.

I don't disagree.  I just wasn't sure if I'd hit a minimum number of lines
that would justify a new paragraph.

>| The GOTO just seems, well, cleaner and clearer.
>| I'm iffy on the splitting of the 0871 and 0872 logic into their own
>| paragraphs, especially since you didn't do that for the code that has
only
>| one or two lines.
>
>That's because the size of the in-line 871, and 872 were making it harder
to
>see the structure of the outer EVALUATE, and it's a programmer's call on
>what to do with the smaller procedures.

Yep.  I'm always torn as to how many lines of code make up 'too many lines'
and need to be restructured into their own paragraph.  Certainly if the code
is performed from multiple places it's justified based solely on that.  But
when it's only used once...  (Who wants to make decisions.  I want hard and
fast rules that are always right!  <g>)

>|But the more I look at it the more reasonable it looks.
>| I'll have to ponder it...
>
>You might want to code WHEN OTHER, CONTINUE to make your intentions more
>explicit.

Yeah, I thought about that.  Could be helpful.

>And you can remove the now obsolete paragraph 0870-FINISHUP.
>I left it in to make it easier to see what I did.

True enough.  The code was originally a bit worse than even what I had.  It
was something to the effect of...

IF CONDITION-1 AND CONDITION 2
    NEXT SENTENCE
ELSE
IF CONDITION-3 AND CONDITION 4
    NEXT SENTENCE
ELSE
IF CONDITION-5
    NEXT SENTENCE
ELSE
    MOVE LOW-VALUES TO MAP
    GO TO SEND-MAP.

IF CONDITION-1 AND CONDITION-2
    DO SOMETHING HERE
ELSE
IF CONDITION-3 AND CONDITION-4
    DO SOMETHING ELSE HERE
ELSE
IF CONDITION-5
    DO A THIRD OPTION HERE.

...SET HEADER BANK NUMBER HERE...
...UPDATE HEADER RECORD AND REWRITE HERE...
MOVE LOW-VALUES TO MAP.
GO TO SEND-MAP.

I was needing to change the GO TO SEND-MAP to something else and noticed
that it was pretty much in two places (two exits!!!) and I didn't care for
that.  (Actually it was even worse than that, but I'm afraid if I posted it
some people's heads would explode because of the sheer horror of it all. 
Mine almost did!)



--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications Development
FirstBank Data Corporation
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Restructure this!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-12T11:14:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YZ3_a.95613$3o3.6650512@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <jMXYa.93451$0v4.6346051@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bh8cog$utr2p$2@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <r0PZa.96449$0v4.6643248@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bh953q$vc1ba$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de>`

```

"Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote in message
news:bh953q$vc1ba$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de...
|
| Yep.  I'm always torn as to how many lines of code make up 'too many
lines'
| and need to be restructured into their own paragraph.  Certainly if the
code
| is performed from multiple places it's justified based solely on that.
But
| when it's only used once...  (Who wants to make decisions.  I want hard
and
| fast rules that are always right!  <g>)

Sorry Frank, there are no hard, and fast rules, only guidelines.

On the "only used once" thing, be careful, it could be used to justify the
800 line, nested 15 deep, paragraph.
Before we had the END-????? statements, you had to use the period, and that
caused people to break routines out of the main flow.

I have a variety of guidelines.

The one screen rule.
If it's over one screen, can I break the routine into components.

The outline rule.
Can I outline the process to make it easier to understand.
This also provides a check list when you're doing development.

The significant process rule.
Is this a process that should be named because it implements a business
rule.

The nested rule.
If it's nested over 2 levels, don't make things worse.
(See the one screen rule.)

In general, write for the maintenance programmer.
```

#### ↳ Re: Restructure this!

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-08T20:16:06-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c6dnf_NUZrK1qmiXTWJjw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> I almost mentioned the term GOTO in my subject but decided it might
> be too off-putting.  Let me give you a bit of some code.  It's bit
> verbose, but it was easier to use real code than to make something up!
>

[snip a lot of code]

All that mishmash can be replaced with:

Call 'Get'
Call 'Mull'
Call 'Put'
Stop Run.
```

<a id="t-a4026f4fd1"></a>
## Thread: presentation logic vs. business logic — 7 msgs

### presentation logic vs. business logic

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick<Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-08T16:12:30-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bh178h$snlsh$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de>`

```
OK boys and girls...  I am doing a bit(!) of a rewrite on an existing
'maintenance' program.  It's currently a single CICS program that can be
used to:

Add an incoming wire record.
Add an outgoing wire record.
Browse the wire record file (can browse all or can chose some selection
criteria).
View an incoming wire record and possibly change it.
View an outgoing wire record and possibly change it.
Print wire notices from the wire records (some or all).
View 'wire totals'.

I am wanting to break up this rather large and disorganized program into two
separate programs.  One would be the presentation logic (still CICS at this
point) and the other would be business logic.

My basic questions is what do I allow for with regards to errors in my
COMMAREA.  We don't currently have any programs broken up this way, so I
don't have any examples to look at.  (This one will hopefully be a good
example for further development!)

Currently when we determine that a user has entered invalid input into a
field we do the following:
- Generate an error message to be displayed on the screen.
- Set the cursor position to go to that field.
- Use SEND MAP to display the screen.

With the business logic separate from the presentation logic I will not, of
course, be able to say "MOVE -1 TO FIELD1L".  I will have to set some field
in the COMMAREA to something such that the presentation logic program will
be able to evaluate that field to know where to place the cursor.  Show I
just use a numeric field and set it pretty much at random as long as each
number corresponds to only one field?  IE

**business logic**
  PERFORM LOOKUP-REF-NBR
  IF REF-NBR-ALREADY-USED
      MOVE 'REF NBR ALREADY USED' TO CA-ERROR-MESSSAGE
      MOVE +1 TO CA-ERROR-FIELD
      GO TO EXIT-PARA.

  PERFORM VALIDATE-ABA
  IF ABA-NBR-IS-VALID
      CONTINUE
  ELSE
      MOVE 'INVALID ABA NUMBER' TO CA-ERROR-MESSSAGE
      MOVE +2 TO CA-ERROR-FIELD
      GO TO EXIT-PARA.

**presentation logic**
  IF CA-ERROR-FIELD > ZERO
      PERFORM ERROR-LOGIC
  ELSE
     PERFORM COMMIT-LOGIC
  END-IF

ERROR-LOGIC.
  MOVE CA-ERROR-MSG TO WIRB-ERROR-MSGO
  EVALUATE CA-ERROR-FIELD
    WHEN +1
        MOVE -1 TO WIRB-REF-NBRL
    WHEN +2
        MOVE -1 TO WIRB-ABA-NBRL
  END-EVALUATE
  EXEC CICS SEND MAP(WIRB) END-EXEC
  EXEC CICS RETURN END-EXEC

This seems decent enough I guess.  It just forces me to figure out which
error-field value correponds with which field, but that's no big deal.  I
guess I was just wondering if there's a better way.

Also brings up the issue of, do I validate *all* of the fields rather than
short-circuiting on the first error.  If so, do I have an error table with,
say, 10 occurances where each occurance has both an error message (text) and
an error field value.

Of course the whole point to this is moving the presentation logic off of
CICS.  After this current project is complete we'll be starting on allowing
business customers to create wires from an Internet site.  Breaking this up
would allow the Internet server to "link" to the mainframe business logic
fairly seemlessly.

Anyway, would love to hear things from someone who's been there and done
that!

Thanks,


--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications Development
FirstBank Data Corporation
```

#### ↳ Re: presentation logic vs. business logic

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>

- **References:** `<bh178h$snlsh$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de>`

```
allowing
| business customers to create wires from an Internet site.  Breaking this

| fairly seemlessly.

If it's through CICS, there's no need to break the program up.
You could just use a different Tran-ID, and bypass the screen formatting.
```

```
logic
>| fairly seemlessly.

>How will the Internet Server link to the mainframe business logic?

>You could just use a different Tran-ID, and bypass the screen formatting.

Yes, it is through CICS.  And you're right that the same program could be
used, but I'm not sure how much I'd gain by doing that.  I still would have
to make pretty much the same changes.  I still would not want to, in the
business logic, move the error message to a BMS map field, and I still would
have to have someway if indicating back to the 'remote' side exactly which
fields have errors.



--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications Development
```

```
Just don't send the map.

You MAY be able to use "middleware" to convert the 3270 display to a Web
Page.
I THINK that IBM has some products that may be of use.
```

```
But we still need something in the 'other output' to indicate what field to
cursor should be positioned on, so I still don't see that we're gaining much
there.

>You MAY be able to use "middleware" to convert the 3270 display to a Web
>Page.
>I THINK that IBM has some products that may be of use.

We do have a product to do this (actually uses COM objects or some such
thing I know nothing about), but we really want to try and see if
segregating the business logic out makes things cleaner.  Basically what
brought this up is we're adding some fields to the screen, only there's not
enough room so we have to make a 'second page'.  I thought that while I was
at it it might make sense to separate these things out, but for this
specific project and the one coming up (the Internet interface).

We'll see.  It may just blow up in my face!  :-)  The program as it is is
quite a horror.  I've already cut out over 1000 lines, most of it duplicate
code (not 100% duplicate, but enough that judicious use of a few IF
statements made it quite re-usable).



--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications Development
FirstBank Data Corporation
```

```
As you may recall, I reported here a couple of months ago about the
heck-of-a-deal from Micros~1 for being a "Microsoft Partner" regarding the
$25,000 worth of software you can get for a piddly $299.

It's not over.

The next quarterly, um, shipment just arrived. It contains fully functional:

Micros~1:
Small Business Server RC (5 CDs) (10 licenses)
Windows Server 2003 (10 licenses)
SQL Server SP 3a
Business Solutions Customer Relationship Management (5 CDs) (ten licenses)
[To run, this sucker requires SQL Server, Exchange Server, Small Business
Server, and other high-powered things, plus a dual processor CPU, more disk
space than NASA, and six fingers on your left hand]

Bunch of "Partner Guides" (how to explain/sell this stuff)

There are two more "quarterly" dumps to come. I think the next one will
contain a Jaguar. Maybe two.

With Red Hat, you get a mouse pad.
```

## Thread: SHARE (IBM User Group) "COBOL interested" sites — 2 msgs

### SHARE (IBM User Group) "COBOL interested" sites

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-09T02:10:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yKYYa.5437$BC2.5100@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`

```
In the very near future, I will be undertaking the position of "requirements
coordinator" for the COBOL (LE & PL/I) project at SHARE, the IBM user group.

There is now an "online" way to vote on SHARE requirements which can
"encourage" sites that ARE members of SHARE and are interested in COBOL
(even if they don't usually send a "COBOL or LE" representative to SHARE) to
participate in the prioritizing of future IBM enhancements.

I am interested in getting (starting) a *personal* (for me only) email list
of those of "you" who are employees of IBM sites that belong to SHARE *and*
are interested in COBOL, LE (or PL/I) issues - or know of the "responsible"
person in your shop who would be interested in such.

If you would LIKE to be on this list, please send me a PRIVATE email (don't
use reply) to the address below.
```

## Thread: RM/COBOL 6.61 on Unixware and RUN.MSG — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### RM/COBOL 6.61 on Unixware and RUN.MSG

- **From:** cbloy@hotmail.com (Chris)
- **Date:** 2003-08-08T19:26:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a2af27ee.0308081826.2f521f3d@posting.google.com>`

```
I'm having a problem when trying to access an existing system that
"works" for everyone else....  I am receiving the message "unable to
find system message file" when trying to access sub-programs from the
main program.  The main thing that is different is that I am accessing
the system remotely via modem vs everyone else who are connecting via
telnet.  I'm sure it is some kind of resource issue, just not sure
where to start.

Here is what I have done...I've verified that the RUN.MSG file is
located in the current working directory with the runtime and I've
made sure it has read-write-execute permissions.  The runtime can find
the RUN.MSG (at least the runcobol -v, returns version, serial #,
etc).  I've modified the startup script to make sure both the current
directory is in both the PATH and RUNPATH environment settings but not
having any luck.  I've even tried "su" as other users including root
and get the same results.

Anyone have some ideas they would like to share? 

Thanks

Chris
```

```
Thanks for your help.  I've checked everything that you listed above
and my menu program is a COBOL program that calls other sub-programs. 
I've narrowed the problem with having something to do with resources
associated with the dial-up connection.  I noticed that trying to run
the "man" command gave some errors about opening files as well.  My
solution:  once logged in, I just telnet to the local host and login
using same credentials I dialed in with and then execute the script to
start the application and like magic...it runs just fine! 
UnixWare....got to love it.

Anyhow, instead of trying to figure out why the dial-in connection
doesn't work correctly, I got the work around.

Thanks again for all your tips!

Chris
```

## Thread: Restructure this! — 30 this month / 47 total

### Re: Restructure this!

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-09T18:20:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oXaZa.5689$vo2.4807@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <%RWYa.5405$BC2.2463@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net> <t31ajvsg887opr4p2nlrb3asa75je0tepk@4ax.com>`

```
There are a couple of "advantages" to the EXIT PARAGRAPH statement - over GO
TO XXXX-exit.

1) It is an "unnamed" destination for the (admittedly implicit) GO TO.  This
means that ONLY an EXIT PARAGRAPH statement may be executed to go to this
"spot".  It canNOT lead to "eventual" spaghetti code with overlapping GO
TO's.

2) As it is unnamed and is ALWAYS the "nearest" end-of-paragraph, it is
impossible for maintenance to "accidentally" insert a new paragraph-name
between the statement's location and the actual paragraph exit (i.e. it is
impossible to introduce "fall-thru" logic with this statement and its
maintenance).

3) Some, but not all, definitions of "structured programming allow for such
a "branch to end of current structure" - as long as there is still only one
entrance and one exit of the structure.

  ***

IMHO, the first two reasons above explain WHY (again in my personal opinion)
this statement avoids all the maintenance and other "problems" that
structured coding were intended to "fix" - even in those environments where
the 3rd reason is not true.
```

#### ↳ Re: Restructure this!

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-10T01:20:08+00:00

- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <%RWYa.5405$BC2.2463@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net> <t31ajvsg887opr4p2nlrb3asa75je0tepk@4ax.com> <oXaZa.5689$vo2.4807@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`

```

| There are a couple of "advantages" to the EXIT PARAGRAPH statement - over
GO
| TO XXXX-exit.
|
| 1) It is an "unnamed" destination for the (admittedly implicit) GO TO.
This
| means that ONLY an EXIT PARAGRAPH statement may be executed to go to this
| "spot".  It canNOT lead to "eventual" spaghetti code with overlapping GO
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
| maintenance).

You can change the scope of the EXIT PARAGRAPH.
If you move a logic structure from a paragraph, and replace it with a
perform of the moved logic, you would introduce an error if the moved logic
contained an EXIT PARAGRAPH.
With the GO TO EXIT, the error may be obvious, but once the EXIT PARAGRAPH
is moved there is no obvious error.

THAT'S ALL

| 3) Some, but not all, definitions of "structured programming allow for
such
| a "branch to end of current structure" - as long as there is still only
one
| entrance and one exit of the structure.
|
|   ***
|
| IMHO, the first two reasons above explain WHY (again in my personal
opinion)
| this statement avoids all the maintenance and other "problems" that
| structured coding were intended to "fix" - even in those environments
where
| the 3rd reason is not true.
|
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
| > >"Ask your vendor of choice WHEN they will be providing the EXIT
PARAGRAPH
| > >syntax from the 2002 COBOL Standard"
| >
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
|
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Restructure this!

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-08-10T17:45:24+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bh4m8d$p8h$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <t31ajvsg887opr4p2nlrb3asa75je0tepk@4ax.com> <oXaZa.5689$vo2.4807@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net> <c5hZa.94533$0v4.6467706@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Harley wrote:

> You can change the scope of the EXIT PARAGRAPH.
> If you move a logic structure from a paragraph, and replace it with a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> is moved there is no obvious error.

If the same structure was moved and it contained a GO TO exit then it, too, 
would suffer the same problem if the GOTO was changed to the new paragraph 
exit.  The problem would be considerably worse if the goto was left as it 
was, the program would become spagetti.

The answer is: don't use  short cuts: 'goto exit' or 'next sentence' or 
'exit paragraph/section' are all equally vulnerable to misuse in the way 
you describe.

I am still wavering on exit perform, the rest I avoid entirely for exactly 
you reason you give.
```

```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:bh4m8d$p8h$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz...
| Harley wrote:
|
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
| > With the GO TO EXIT, the error may be obvious, but once the EXIT
PARAGRAPH
| > is moved there is no obvious error.
|
| If the same structure was moved and it contained a GO TO exit then it,
too,
| would suffer the same problem if the GOTO was changed to the new paragraph
| exit.

|The problem would be considerably worse if the goto was left as it
| was, the program would become spagetti.

I don't know, depending on the compiler, it may still work, even though it
is spaghetti.
To me, the GO TO errror would be more obvious than the EXIT PARAGRAPH.

|
| The answer is: don't use  short cuts: 'goto exit' or 'next sentence' or
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
| you reason you give.

The IBM compiler(s) I use don't support EXIT PERFORM.
I don't have any problems developing structured programs without it.
I don't know if I would use the EXIT PERFORM in new development if it were
available.

I'm wary of new features used as "fixes" to the language, because I've seen
some abuses of features in the past.
I'm not convinced that the EXIT PARAGRAPH is a necessity.
The alternative, GO TO EXIT, may be easier to deal with in a maintenance
environment.

What I can see happening with EXIT PARAGRAPH is people will write an
evaluate equivalent using it.
i.e.

If CONDITION-A
   do something
   EXIT PARAGRAPH
END-IF
.......
If CONDITION-Z
   do something
   EXIT PARAGRAPH
END-IF
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Restructure this!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-10T13:20:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308101220.12541339@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <t31ajvsg887opr4p2nlrb3asa75je0tepk@4ax.com> <oXaZa.5689$vo2.4807@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net> <c5hZa.94533$0v4.6467706@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bh4m8d$p8h$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <X7qZa.92501$3o3.6436908@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote 

> To me, the GO TO errror would be more obvious than the EXIT PARAGRAPH.

That is probably because GO TO is on your 'automatic check list' and
EXIT xxx is not (yet).  It is a matter or training oneself to do these
checks.

> I'm not convinced that the EXIT PARAGRAPH is a necessity.
> The alternative, GO TO EXIT, may be easier to deal with in a maintenance
> environment.

You make it sound as if 'EXIT PARAGRAPH' and 'GO TO xxx-EXIT' are the
alterrnates and there is no other way.

 
> What I can see happening with EXIT PARAGRAPH is people will write an
> evaluate equivalent using it.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-IF

Haven't they heard of ELSE even ?

I dislike EVALUATE true and so tend to use IF .. ELSE IF .. when there
is a variety of different tests.  I tend to only use EVALATE variable

  IF Condition-A
      do-a
  ELSE
  IF Condition-B
      do-b
  ELSE
  IF ...

It is necessary to count the IFs for the END-IFs but it works for me,
and doesn't require GOTO _or_ EXIT PARAGRAPH or any other exit
mechanism.  Some of these may be CONTINUE, but that has no problems
associated with it and cannot be abused.

My code also has the advantage that it cannot suffer from the problem
you started this with: taking a block of code out of line and
replacing it with a PERFORM - in fact I do that all the time to break
code down into simpler modules or to reuse.  eg in my above if
Condition-C also needs do-a then this will be an out-of-line perform -
I don't need to contrive a more complicated set of IFs to avoid
repeating in line code for do-a.

Actually the main beef that I have with EXIT PARAGRAPH/SECTION is that
it is NOT equivalent to 'EXIT PERFORM CYCLE' in an out of line PERFORM
as it may be used for.

For example if PERFORMed SECTIONs are being used the EXIT PARAGRAPH
only goes to the end of the current paragraph.  Some here have said
they use commentary paragraph names in PERFORMed SECTIONs.  This makes
the code liable to change depending on how 'comments' are added or
taken away.

Another used PERFORM section THRU paragraph where the exit paragraph
was the last paragraph in the section.  Put an EXIT SECTION in that
and the code fails.
```

```
| eg in my above if

…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
| taken away.

Luckily, I haven't seen many PERFORMed SECTIONS lately.
```

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0308101220.12541339@posting.google.com...
| "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote
|
<<snip>>
|
|
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
| is a variety of different tests.  I tend to only use EVALATE variable

As I said in my other post, I find EVALUATE TRUE handy.

Here is an example of EVALUATE TRUE.
How would you code it?

*    ==============================================
*    THIS ROUTINE MATCHES TRANSACTIONS TO A MASTER
*    USING SEQUENTIAL PROCESSING
*    BECAUSE RANDOM IS TOO SLOW.
*    ------------------------------------------------
*    THERE MAY BE MULTIPLE TRANSACTIONS FOR A MASTER
*    ------------------------------------------------
*    THE READ-MAST ROUTINE WILL REWRITE UPDATED MASTER
*    RECORDS BEFORE READING THE NEXT MASTER RECORD.
*    ----------------------------------------------
     PERFORM  READ-MAST
     PERFORM  READ-TRAN

     PERFORM  UNTIL  EOF-MAST
                AND  EOF-TRAN
         EVALUATE  TRUE
             WHEN  EOF-MAST
                   PERFORM  TRAN-MISSING-MAST
                   PERFORM  READ-TRAN
             WHEN  EOF-TRAN
                   PERFORM  READ-MAST
                   SET  EOF-MAST  TO  TRUE
             WHEN  MAST-KEY  =  TRAN-KEY
                   PERFORM  PROCESS-TRAN
                   PERFORM  READ-TRAN
             WHEN  MAST-KEY  <  TRAN-KEY
                   PERFORM  READ-MAST
             WHEN  OTHER
                   PERFORM  TRAN-MISSING-MAST
                   PERFORM  READ-TRAN
         END-EVALUATE
     END-PERFORM.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Restructure this!

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-11T13:20:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308111220.3987324f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <t31ajvsg887opr4p2nlrb3asa75je0tepk@4ax.com> <oXaZa.5689$vo2.4807@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net> <c5hZa.94533$0v4.6467706@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bh4m8d$p8h$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <X7qZa.92501$3o3.6436908@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0308101220.12541339@posting.google.com> <qKNZa.93846$3o3.6560831@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote 

> | I dislike EVALUATE true and so tend to use IF .. ELSE IF .. when there
> | is a variety of different tests.  I tend to only use EVALATE variable
 
> Here is an example of EVALUATE TRUE.
> How would you code it?

With IF ... ELSE IF ... END-IF END-IF
 
As a dirct mechanical change it would be:

       PERFORM Read-Mast
       PERFORM Read-Tran
 
       PERFORM  
           UNTIL EoF-Mast
             AND EoF-Tran

            IF ( EoF-Mast )
                PERFORM Tran-Missing-Mast
                PERFORM Read-Tran
            ELSE 
            IF ( EoF-Tran )
                PERFORM Read-Mast               
                SET EoF-Mast      TO TRUE
            ELSE
            IF ( Mast-Key = Tran-Key )
                PERFORM Process-Tran
                PERFORM Read-Tran
            ELSE      
            IF ( Mast-Key < Tran-Key )
               PERFORM Read-Mast
            ELSE
               PERFORM Tran-Missing-Mast
               PERFORM Read-Tran
            END-IF
            END-IF
            END-IF
            END-IF
       END-PERFORM
       .

Though I would not write it exactly this way, for example I tend to
use the record keys as 'EoF-Flags' by having the Read-xxx move
HIGH-VALUES at end.

I also wouldn't have the Read-Mast do the rewrite of the previous, but
would have a separate ReWrite-Mast.  It would be too easy for the next
programmer to delete the Read-Mast on EoF-Tran because he may not see
the need to Read before setting EoF.

In general I would either have the Read-Mast as a random read (there
are very few cases in my systems where the transaction hit-rate is
high enough to require sequential), or I would be processing _every_
master in some way, such as aging the debtors balances, and so would
not short cut with the SET.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Restructure this!

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-08-12T17:51:21+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bh9vcc$g1v$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <qKNZa.93846$3o3.6560831@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0308111220.3987324f@posting.google.com> <bh95fl$vc1ba$2@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:

> Not to cause offense, but... that's pretty horrifying!  At least do a
> step-ladder format.  

<shrug> I dislike indenting IFs when they are a case statement at the same 
level and are alternates as in an IF ... ELSE IF.

> Yikes.  The whole point of EVALUATE is to avoid this
> kind of thing!  :-)  

<shrug> A case statement is a case statement whatever the implementation. I 
can use EVALUATE if I want to or a series of IF ELSE if I want (which is 
the way that I have done it in '74 Cobol).  In some other languages I use 
switch {}, or if .. elif ...  The important thing about a _case_ statement 
is that it be recognisable by being a series of choices at the same indent 
level.

That is to say, for this to be a case statement there must be no statements 
between the ELSE and the next IF.

> Sorry to rag on you, but we have some code in some of
> our programs that look a lot like this.  Mostly they were written with the
> COBOL-74 standard 

How farsighted of them to implement the style of a case statement using '74.

> and then someone decided to add a lot of END-IFs.  I
> can't make heads or tails of it without a lot of effort.

Keep working at it.  We all like what we are used to seeing.  Newer styles 
take some time to get used to.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Restructure this!

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick<Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-12T09:37:58-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bhb1kq$vhkjn$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <qKNZa.93846$3o3.6560831@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0308111220.3987324f@posting.google.com> <bh95fl$vc1ba$2@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <bh9vcc$g1v$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> 08/11/03 11:51PM >>>
>Frank Swarbrick wrote:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
><shrug> I dislike indenting IFs when they are a case statement at the same

>level and are alternates as in an IF ... ELSE IF.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
><shrug> A case statement is a case statement whatever the implementation. I

>can use EVALUATE if I want to or a series of IF ELSE if I want (which is 
>the way that I have done it in '74 Cobol).  In some other languages I use 
>switch {}, or if .. elif ...  The important thing about a _case_ statement

>is that it be recognisable by being a series of choices at the same indent

>level.
>
>That is to say, for this to be a case statement there must be no statements

>between the ELSE and the next IF.
>
>> Sorry to rag on you, but we have some code in some of
>> our programs that look a lot like this.  Mostly they were written with
the
>> COBOL-74 standard 
>
>How farsighted of them to implement the style of a case statement using
'74.
>
>> and then someone decided to add a lot of END-IFs.  I
>> can't make heads or tails of it without a lot of effort.
>
>Keep working at it.  We all like what we are used to seeing.  Newer styles

>take some time to get used to.

I guess I'm a bit confused as to why you want to *simulate* as case
statement (using IF ELSE IF ELSE) rather than use the actual case statement
supported by the language (EVALUATE).

As for not understanding it, I have to admit I was exaggerating for effect. 
If done correctly they're not *that* hard to understand.  But I would say
they're probably more prone to error, especially if there are more than a
few conditions.  IBM mainframe compilers have a nice feature that lists each
statement's "level".  Take the following:

0 EVALUATE TRUE
0   WHEN CONDITION-1
1     PERFORM HANDLE-CONDITION-1
0   WHEN CONDITION-2
1     PERFORM HANDLE-CONDITION-2
0   WHEN CONDITION-3
1     PERFORM HANDLE-CONDITION-3
0   WHEN OTHER
1     PERFORM HANDLE-CONDITION-DEFAULT
0 END-EVALUATE

All of the performs are at the same statement level.  (Level 0 is actually
displayed as a blank.) But if I use IFs like you prefer I get:

0 IF CONDITION-1
1     PERFORM HANDLE-CONDITION-1
0 ELSE
1 IF CONDITION-2
2     PERFORM HANDLE-CONDITION-2
1 ELSE
2 IF CONDITION-3
3     PERFORM HANDLE-CONDITION-3
2 ELSE
3     PERFORM HANDLE-CONDITION-DEFAULT
2 END-IF
1 END-IF
0 END-IF

Logically, in terms of a case statement, each of the performs is at the same
level (level 1).  But in terms of COBOL, we have one at level 1, one at
level 2 and two at level 3.

With indentation that matches the statement levels we get:

0 IF CONDITION-1
1     PERFORM HANDLE-CONDITION-1
0 ELSE
1     IF CONDITION-2
2         PERFORM HANDLE-CONDITION-2
1     ELSE
2         IF CONDITION-3
3             PERFORM HANDLE-CONDITION-3
2         ELSE
3             PERFORM HANDLE-CONDITION-DEFAULT
2         END-IF
1     END-IF
0 END-IF

Anyway, I suppose it's just a matter of preference, but I just have a hard
time seeing why either of those IF/ELSE combos would be preferable to the
original EVALUATE...  Don't want to start a style war, though!  :-)



--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications Development
FirstBank Data Corporation
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Restructure this!

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-12T14:29:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308121329.7f286f28@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <qKNZa.93846$3o3.6560831@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0308111220.3987324f@posting.google.com> <bh95fl$vc1ba$2@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <bh9vcc$g1v$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <bhb1kq$vhkjn$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de>`

```
Frank Swarbrick<Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote 

> I guess I'm a bit confused as to why you want to *simulate* as case
> statement (using IF ELSE IF ELSE) rather than use the actual case statement
> supported by the language (EVALUATE).

Because I prefer to not use EVALUATE TRUE in which the WHENs are
actually IFs. I tend to only use EVALUATE variable where this is an
_actual_ case statement.

I didn't put the code up to convince anyone, I was specifically asked
as to "how I did it".
 
> As for not understanding it, I have to admit I was exaggerating for effect. 

Yes, I did think that was the case <grin>.

> If done correctly they're not *that* hard to understand.  

Given that I have been doing case statements this way since well
before EVALUATE, and it is also the way this coding is done in other
languages, then I find it particularly easy to understand.  It is only
a matter of what one is used to.

In C for example one can say:

             switch ( expression )
             {
             case 1:  statement; break;
             case 2:  ...
             }

This is equivalent of EVALUATE expression, but an EVALUATE TRUE can't
be done because the 'case labels' must be integers, except by:

              if ( condition 1)
                  {}
              else 
              if ( condition 2 )
                  ....

I just do it this way in Cobol, C, Python and whatever else I am using
at the time.

> But I would say they're probably more prone to error, 

In what way ?

All coding is 'prone to error'.  In fact there is one way that
EVALUATE can lead to a coding error which the compiler would pick up
when IF .. ELSE is used, that is when the actions of a condition are
commented out - perhaps because no action is actually required, or
hasn't yet been coded in development:  The compiler complains that an
IF has no statements, the EVALUATE silently executes the actions of
the next WHEN.

The actual example was within a PERFORM - the compiler should complain
if the wrong number of END-IF are used (see difference between
conditional and imperitive statements).  Without the PERFORM this is
one place where a full stop may be used to indicate to the next
programmer that this is and must return to level zero (see previous
'discussions').

> IBM mainframe compilers have a nice feature that lists each
> statement's "level".  Take the following:

Yes, the ICL compilers could do that too.  Handy if one has trouble
counting IFs and END-IFs.  Actually it is not such a problem when
END-IFs are used, it was useful when we had to use ELSE NEXT SENTENCE
to step out of a level.

> With indentation that matches the statement levels we get:

<shrug> it's a case statement so indentation need not apply.  As long
as there is nothing between the ELSE and the IF then it is the 'same
level' conceptually because it is an 'ELIF'.

> Anyway, I suppose it's just a matter of preference, but I just have a hard
> time seeing why either of those IF/ELSE combos would be preferable to the
> original EVALUATE...  Don't want to start a style war, though!  :-)

It was my ELSE IF combos that were the original and the EVALUATE
emulated this.
```

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0308111220.3987324f@posting.google.com...
| "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote
|
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
| HIGH-VALUES at end.

The 88 level could have been specified under the Key areas.
I usually save the Keys in working storage.

In the IBM mainframe environment a single byte flag generates more efficient
code.
I could be saving hundreths of a nano-second.
|
| I also wouldn't have the Read-Mast do the rewrite of the previous, but
| would have a separate ReWrite-Mast.

IMHO, it may simplify things to have the READ control the rewite.
i.e. Before I read, make sure the previous record was rewritten, if it was
changed.
IMHO, it's easier to implement a pre-tran, tran, and post-tran update
process.

| It would be too easy for the next
| programmer to delete the Read-Mast on EoF-Tran because he may not see
| the need to Read before setting EoF.

Most programmers who would screw it up, are also afraid to remove code.
You recognized why it was there.
A simple comment in the code could explain why it was there.

| In general I would either have the Read-Mast as a random read (there
| are very few cases in my systems where the transaction hit-rate is
| high enough to require sequential), or I would be processing _every_
| master in some way, such as aging the debtors balances, and so would
| not short cut with the SET.

If that were the case,
    WHEN  EOF-TRAN
                 PERFORM  READ-MAST
                       UNTIL   EOF-MAST
                 PERFORM  LAST-MAST

I saw Frank's response <G>.

The group we belong to sets the standards for the group, and so you find
different standards, some good, some bad, throughout the COBOL world.
There is no universal standard.

In the end, we are masters of our own destiny.
If our practices are acceptable within the organization, there is no need to
change them.
If our practices are not acceptable within the organization, it's wise to
consider changing either the practice, or the organization.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Restructure this!

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-11T16:42:43-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vjg01aepj0nkef@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <t31ajvsg887opr4p2nlrb3asa75je0tepk@4ax.com> <oXaZa.5689$vo2.4807@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net> <c5hZa.94533$0v4.6467706@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bh4m8d$p8h$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <X7qZa.92501$3o3.6436908@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0308101220.12541339@posting.google.com> <bh8ks8$e33$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:bh8ks8$e33$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 10-Aug-2003, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> PARAGRAPH is available, why do we need procedure division SECTIONs?

GO TO ... DEPENDING; just perform the section, no THRU.

Simulation of OO constructs. Use method names as section
names and class names for paragraphs. Evaluate a class ID
to determine which paragraph to execute within the method
(section). Make the 'object' accessable to the method then
perform method-name (that is, perform the section).

Simulation of modern modular programming. Use the module
name to identify the section and the entry name to identify
procedures within that module. Perform entry-name of
module-name.

Simulate the source structure of some other languages where
procedures are defined before use. Sections contain major
divisions of code. Each section is built of detailed procedures
(bottom-up). The driver for the section is last. Perform driver
of section-name. The driver performs its predefined
procedures. Such programs *may* be read, with
understanding, from the first source line to the last.

As to whether any of these is an improvement over common
practices is a matter of conjecture. I have experimented with
each and see more good than bad, for me; though I have not
yet undertaken a substantial project to exploit these ideas.

Your question was "[W]hy do we need procedure division
SECTIONs?". The answer is "We don't. I do."
```

```
> > I suppose they included EXIT SECTION for completions sake.  But once EXIT
> > PARAGRAPH is available, why do we need procedure division SECTIONs?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> PARAGRAPH/SECTION.

The argument that I have grudgingly given into, is that doing maintenance,
sometimes a GO TO EXIT is significantly less disruptive of the current flow, and
thus less likely to introduce unexpected results.   (and more likely to be
traceable by future maintenance programmers).

My code doesn't have them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Restructure this!

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-12T12:59:44-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308121159.1471a719@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <t31ajvsg887opr4p2nlrb3asa75je0tepk@4ax.com> <oXaZa.5689$vo2.4807@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net> <c5hZa.94533$0v4.6467706@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bh4m8d$p8h$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <X7qZa.92501$3o3.6436908@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0308101220.12541339@posting.google.com> <bh8ks8$e33$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0308111316.445dd44@posting.google.com> <bhaslg$j5e$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote

> The argument that I have grudgingly given into, is that doing maintenance,
> sometimes a GO TO EXIT is significantly less disruptive of the current flow, and
> thus less likely to introduce unexpected results.   (and more likely to be
> traceable by future maintenance programmers).

As I may have briefly mentioned once: At the time and place of adding
a GO TO there is absolutely nothing wrong with it, it is always
obvious at to its action.  At the label, however, the next programmer,
not having seen the GO TO or EXIT PARAGRAPH may make assumptions that
are defeated by the shortcut.

Usually there is an idiom of having a xxx-exit paragraph which forms a
warning that this may be expected.  The EXIT PARAGRAPH lacks this
warning mechanism and thus, in my view, ranks with NEXT SENTENCE +
END-IF as bug-breeders.
 
> My code doesn't have them.

No need to be so defensive   ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Restructure this!

```

On 10-Aug-2003, "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

> I'm wary of new features used as "fixes" to the language, because I've seen
> some abuses of features in the past.
> I'm not convinced that the EXIT PARAGRAPH is a necessity.
> The alternative, GO TO EXIT, may be easier to deal with in a maintenance
> environment.

Only if there IS an exit paragraph.    But with an EXIT PARAGRAPH available for
maintenance programmers, the last reasonable argument for PERFORM THRU is
destroyed.   And that is worth a lot.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Restructure this!

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-10T10:53:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u-ecnaVyHsQL96uiXTWJkg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <t31ajvsg887opr4p2nlrb3asa75je0tepk@4ax.com> <oXaZa.5689$vo2.4807@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net> <c5hZa.94533$0v4.6467706@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bh4m8d$p8h$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
>
> I am still wavering on exit perform, the rest I avoid entirely for
> exactly you reason you give.

There are a few cases where EXIT PERFORM comes in handy. The most obvious is
when you don't know the perform's range.

PERFORM WITH NO LIMIT
    (la-de-da...)
   IF (terminal condition)
      EXIT PERFORM
      END-IF
END-PERFORM.

or to bail early:

PERFORM UNTIL EOF = 'Y'
   (get a record)
   (check record against case 1)
   IF REALLY-BIG-BLUNDER EXIT PERFORM END-IF
   (check record against 100 other records)
   (look up everything in OED)
   (arrange Pokemon deck)
   (boil water)
   (consult queen)
   etc.
END-PERFORM.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Restructure this!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-10T13:31:28-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308101231.3f56831@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <t31ajvsg887opr4p2nlrb3asa75je0tepk@4ax.com> <oXaZa.5689$vo2.4807@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net> <c5hZa.94533$0v4.6467706@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bh4m8d$p8h$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <u-ecnaVyHsQL96uiXTWJkg@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote

> There are a few cases where EXIT PERFORM comes in handy. The most obvious is
> when you don't know the perform's range.

I know that it is 'handy', and I have used it for exactly what you
exemplify, but if the content of the PERFORM gets unwieldy I may take
it to another paragraph and make it an out-of-line perform.  With EXIT
PERFORM [CYCLE] this fails to work.  However a flag and 'UNTIL flag'
works either way.  While not 'modern' or 'super-efficient', it always
works and is not a trap for the next programmer (which may be me) to
fall into.

I tend to throw blocks of code around for reuse of to simply deep
nesting. Any style or idiom that gets in the way of doing this is
replaced by code that doesn't. No: GO TO, THRU, EXIT <anything>,
performs of SECTIONs, commentary labels, NEXT SENTENCE, or anything
else that changes meaning when moved.
```

```
> The answer is: don't use  short cuts: 'goto exit' or 'next sentence' or
> 'exit paragraph/section' are all equally vulnerable to misuse in the way
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> you reason you give.

Where this command would be used is in maintenance where you want to make a
minimal change.   New code can be written without needing it (I do it all the
time), but it can be costly to restructure paragraphs in maintenance when a
EXIT-PARAGRAPH can do the job.
```

#### ↳ Re: Restructure this!

- **From:** codecrafters@cix.compulink.co.uk
- **Date:** 2003-08-18T23:31:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bhrnk2$jaa$1@thorium.cix.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<oXaZa.5689$vo2.4807@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`

```
In article <oXaZa.5689$vo2.4807@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>, 
wmklein@nospam.netcom.com (William M. Klein) wrote:

Much stuff about exit this, that and the other. But one trick seems to 
have gotten lost. The stuff below is a slightly modified version of a 
post of mine back in 1994 (alt.cobol days!):

While I agree with the notion of "exit section/paragraph" clauses, the 
problem I tend to have is that often one needs to <do> something, usually 
a quite simple something, at exit time. While it's possible (of course) to 
arrange things around the new exit clauses, the old 
paragraph-qualification trick (although an undisguised GO TO) can actually 
be much more convenient, IMHO. It works by using the requirement that 
duplicately-defined paragraph names (at program level) are automatically 
qualified to the current section - allowing the more useful Go to 
Section-Exit construct. It's the only GO TO you'll ever need. Example:
 
1000-Office-Process    Section.
    If Office-Not-Required
        Go to Section-Exit.
 
    < Other code >
Section-Exit.
    Perform 9000-Get-next-office.  (or anything *simple*)
 
2000-Salesman-Process Section.
    If Whatever-True
        Go to Section-Exit.
 
     < Other Code >
 
Section-Exit.
     Whatever you like. But keep it simple.
 
And so on. Each get-out GO TO can't go anywhere except the right place, 
your end-of-section code doesn't have to be replicated or in the 
performing section, and it even reads well (I'm a fan of COBOL for the 
english-like syntax).

You can of course break this by putting complex code in the exit 
paragraph. I restrict myself to either "continue" or a perform of a READ 
NEXT section, and it works a treat. 
 
Shown to me by a bloke years ago, and I've never even been tempted to use
anything else - mostly because I can't think of an improvement!
 
Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Restructure this!

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-19T13:58:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bhtae3$dl6$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<oXaZa.5689$vo2.4807@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net> <bhrnk2$jaa$1@thorium.cix.co.uk>`

```
So what is the difference between this and the GO TO EXIT's that have plagued
code that I have had to maintain for decades?

(I replace EXIT with CONTINUE when maintaining such code, making it easier to
put in debugging commands - but I NEVER write code with multiple paragraphs
performed).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Restructure this!

- **From:** codecrafters@cix.compulink.co.uk
- **Date:** 2003-08-19T16:58:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bhtkvb$g9f$1@thorium.cix.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<bhtae3$dl6$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
In article <bhtae3$dl6$1@peabody.colorado.edu>, howard@brazee.net (Howard 
Brazee) wrote:

> So what is the difference between this and the GO TO EXIT's that have 
> plagued
> code that I have had to maintain for decades?

Well, with the scheme you discuss it's tragically easy to make a mistake 
and GO to the wrong exit point, at which time things go horribly 
pear-shaped (to say the least) - that first observation being doubly true 
if the section numbers had been changed. I ran into a number of these 
mistakes one after the other and for a time was an advocate of the no-go's 
style. But even with the 85 extensions, I still found I was either 
indenting code (or nesting paragraphs) more deeply than I felt was 
consistent with clarity of intent (I firmly believe that layout of code 
on screen/paper is a woefully neglected art).

Anyway, I was chatting about this problem with a colleague and he 
suggested the scheme I outlined. I still like it, although it does offend 
purists (maybe that's part of the attraction)  :-) 

If you'll forgive a general observation not specifically aimed at 
yourself, I'll point out the last of George Orwell's six elementary rules 
("Politics and the English Language", 1946): "Break any of these rules 
sooner than say anything outright barbarous". I think we need to keep that 
in mind when discussing these points of style - it's easy to construct 
examples (and counter-examples**n, n usually a painfully large number) 
that show the awfulness of almost any of these methods, my own included. 
But if we keep the goal of _clarity_of_intent_ foremost in mind, we can 
focus on that instead, which to my mind is a potentially *much* more 
interesting discussion.

It's clear that you don't like the way I would do this - well, so be it. 
I'm afraid that you must take my word for it that you would find the code 
very easy to comprehend - which is, to my mind, the point.         
 
> (I replace EXIT with CONTINUE when maintaining such code, making it 
> easier to
> put in debugging commands - but I NEVER write code with multiple 
> paragraphs
> performed).

Oh, go on, let yourself go. Live a little. Orwell would smile at you :-)

Pete.
```

<a id="t-4dfb73bba0"></a>
## Thread: Restructure this! — 1 msgs

### Re: Restructure this!

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-08-10T07:47:48+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bh3j7m$640$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <%RWYa.5405$BC2.2463@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net> <t31ajvsg887opr4p2nlrb3asa75je0tepk@4ax.com>`

```
Volker Bandke wrote:

> I must be overlooking the obvious.  Where is the difference between
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> better readability, but as far as avoiding GO TOs it is not a solution

The point about 'avoiding GOTO' is not the GO TO.  There is absolutely 
nothing wrong with GO TO, it a simple and obvoius statement that is very 
clear about what will happen.

The point about 'avoiding GOTO' is the label, the paragraph name that is 
the target.

When examining a program it is done small bits at a time.  When a part 
includes a label then it must be resolved as to the efeect of that label on 
the flow of logic.  It may be dropped thru from above, be the target of a 
goto, it may terminate the scope of a perform or be the target of a perform.

Other languages use different types of labels for different actions and so 
at any label (or function name etc) it is absolutely clear what will 
happen, and where that may be from, the language guarantees it.

In Cobol in the general case it is necessary to examine the whole program 
in order to be sure what happens at each label.  Some styles use idiomatic 
mechanisms to give confidence that only certian logic flow is used.  eg 
nnn-exit should only ever be used as a drop through or goto from within the 
nnn-name paragraph or section.

However, because this is not checked by the compiler there is actually no 
guarantee that 'perform 3120-name thru 3140-exit' has not been coded either 
deliberately or accidentally, nor that 3150-name does not contain a goto 
3140-exit.  Or, indeed, that there is not a perform 3140-name without the 
required thru.

This means that to check a program in Cobol that contains a PERFORM THRU, 
or a GO TO, or a PERFORMed SECTION, it is necessary to check every path 
through the code for correctness manually.  It is possible to mechanise the 
process and have the idiom so formalised that a program can check it.  
Judson did this with some success allegedly.  In the general case this is 
not viable.

Another way to guarantee (with simple checks) how a program works around 
each label is to eliminate performed sections and perform thru.  This can 
be checked for with grep.  A consequence of this is that goto cannot be 
used (but EXIT PARAGRAPH can).

The aim is _not_ to eliminate GOTO, the aim is to make a program 
understandable in small modules and have this understanding _guaranteed_.  
The mechanisms to do this happens to make goto unavailable.
```

---

## Thread: NetExpress + Listview — 1 msgs

- **From:** mfcobol2002 <marcos_as@terra.com.br>
- **Date:** 2003-08-10T10:13:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3222071.1060510402@dbforums.com>`

```

Respected Gentlemen,

Scenery:

I have been receiving some questions regarding listview on USER-EVENT.
I use Win98 IF without any fix type.
I also use Netexpress 3.1 without any fix.
My programs CBL are based on the same format type used by
Netexpress/Dialog System Wizard.
I never had problems with the controlling program of Listview of NetX.

Problem:
My friends tell me that has the same scenery type but the controlling
programs are with problem.

USER-EVENT
         IF = 34591 $EVENT-DATE LVIEW-ITEM-SELECTED
         IF = 34592 $EVENT-DATE LVIEW-ITEM-ACCEPTED
         IF = 34593 $EVENT-DATE LVIEW-ITEM-RIGHT-CLICK
The times it is only to re-begin Windows that works. The times we have
to re-create the whole process from yours I begin. The times it doesn't
work any in way and we changed for a listbox.

They are used Windows 98 and Windows 2000 with and without
correction fix.

Does anybody know because that happens?

Respectfully

Marcos
Brasil
```

#### ↳ Re: The most powerfull book of rm-cobol

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>

```
company that is making the painful transition from a Unisys-based COBOL
system to a new ERP (yet to be decided).  My part in this is taking apart

E Mc
```

<a id="t-256c724aa5"></a>
## Thread: COBOL variable length record — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### COBOL variable length record

- **From:** alan.turnbull@qbemm.com.au (alan)
- **Date:** 2003-08-10T17:06:51-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bea19d35.0308101606.31ab11b0@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi

In COBOL/MVS on OS390 V2.6 is it possible to have programs that create
and read a sequential file with variable length records that are
longer than 32760? I would like to have a file with variable length
records up to 100K long.

Does anyone know what the COBOL FD and the JCL DD statement need to be
to have a program that creates such records?

Thanks
Alan
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL variable length record

- **From:** David Speight <dsp8@comcast.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-10T20:50:32-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2uScnRL5x_ZkequiXTWJgA@comcast.com>`
- **References:** `<bea19d35.0308101606.31ab11b0@posting.google.com>`

```
alan wrote:

> Hi
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Alan

The variable length records still had the 32767 byte limit.
That count had to include the 4 byte record length at the begining of the
record making the maximum usable record length 32763 (32767 - 4).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL variable length record

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-11T01:39:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jtCZa.6925$BC2.5691@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<bea19d35.0308101606.31ab11b0@posting.google.com> <2uScnRL5x_ZkequiXTWJgA@comcast.com>`

```
I am NOT certain, but I think it may be possible to have a larger than 32K
record *if* you use "Spanned" records.

Also, if the file is ONLY stored on tape, then you might want to look at the
section on "LBI" - Large Block Interface at:

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/1.9.1.2.3
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL variable length record

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-10T21:13:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gMudnWUMy450ZquiU-KYgw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<bea19d35.0308101606.31ab11b0@posting.google.com>`

```
alan wrote:
> Hi
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> to have a program that creates such records?

We used to read tape records in excess of 250,000 bytes on a 128k RAM,
360-44 machine. It took channel programming.

As it turns out, it IS possible to accomplish the result on a PC using
direct block reads to the disk and assembling the record in memory (likewise
writing the record piecemeal).

In either event, I'm guessing it's not directly doable in a COBOL-only
environment.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL variable length record

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-11T12:36:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308111136.a608e00@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bea19d35.0308101606.31ab11b0@posting.google.com>`

```
alan.turnbull@qbemm.com.au (alan) wrote

> In COBOL/MVS on OS390 V2.6 is it possible to have programs that create
> and read a sequential file with variable length records that are
> longer than 32760? I would like to have a file with variable length
> records up to 100K long.

One would just have to ask why.  Are these BLOBs (Binary Large
OBjects) such as pictures or word processing documents ?

Typically these could be stored in separate files in a directory with
the name of the file stored in the data record.
If they are 'normal' variable length with an OCCURS DEPENDING ON for
repeated data then you should revisit the design and consider having
two files, one for the fixed information and the other with records
for each of the repeating group.
```

<a id="t-57493fa9fb"></a>
## Thread: restructuring — 1 msgs

### restructuring

- **From:** "Carole Devillers" <carole@raincode.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-11T13:12:26+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f377a7b$0$16287$6c56d894@feed0.news.be.easynet.net>`

```
I have seen on previous posts that quite a lot of people are interested in
code restructuring and in particular in GOTO removal.

If you want to see how an automated service can restructure your code it is
easy and free. All you have to do is to connect to RainCode Online
(http://www.raincode.com/online).
Once you've logged on, select "COBOL restructuring", then "My submissions",
then "New submission", and "Add file": you can then upload several of your
COBOL programs you want to restructure.



Once your programs are uploaded, click on "add job", and select the
transformations you'd like to see applied on your sources. You can also keep
the default settings.

Click on "commit" to start the renovation.



After a week or so, you will receive an email telling you your job is
finished and your results can be seen online without paying anything.



To give you an idea of what RainCode Online is able to accomplish, we
recorded the following statistics on the first ten applications we
restructured:

   - On average, 85% of the GOTOs were removed.
   - In each program, we managed to re-create between 30 and 85 loops from
GOTO-based equivalent constructs.
   - We typically reduced the size of a program to 60-90% of its original
size, in terms of number of statements.


http://www.raincode.com/online
```

<a id="t-57c3f3d929"></a>
## Thread: Restructure this! — 1 msgs

### Re: Restructure this!

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-11T17:39:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bh8kce$drv$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<bh1257$tb83u$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <%RWYa.5405$BC2.2463@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net> <t31ajvsg887opr4p2nlrb3asa75je0tepk@4ax.com>`

```

On  9-Aug-2003, Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com> wrote:

> I must be overlooking the obvious.  Where is the difference between
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> GO TO exit-of-paragraph?

There is no confusion as to what is being performed, no need for PERFORM THRU.

> It is just a GO TO "in disguise", just like EXEC CICS HANDLE CONDITION
> .  AFAIK, the purist of structured programming require that any
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> better readability, but as far as avoiding GO TOs it is not a solution

The goal is not to avoid GO TO.  The goal is to avoid having multiple ways to
"come from".   The goal is to avoid drop through code.  The goal is to avoid
errors.

If your code already has EXIT paragraphs, sure there is no difference.   But
with an EXIT-PARAGRAPH command, there will no longer be an excuse for PERFORM
THRU logic in new programs.
```

<a id="t-c267519a01"></a>
## Thread: Want to know length of the field passed to a program — 23 msgs

### Want to know length of the field passed to a program

- **From:** randy_clark@ahm.honda.com (randy)
- **Date:** 2003-08-11T17:49:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<acbbbc55.0308111649.24c2c90b@posting.google.com>`

```
The program calculates elapsed time.

I want the called program to handle being passed either a 6 or 8 byte
time field.   I would like the calling program to pass either 6 or 8
bytes times and not have to let the called program know what it was
passed.   I expect the the called program to determine what it has
been passed and act accordingly.

The subroutine accepts 2 arguements in and one output
arguement(elapsed time).

Again the called program needs to be able to determine if it has been
passed a 6 byte or 8 byte time field.

The linkage field would be set to the maxiumum lenght of 8 and the
after determining the lenght the linkage section field would be moved
to the appropriate length working storage field.

The "length of"  and or the len function does not seem to help as it
always returns the size of the field defined in the linkage section -
which I have defined as the max.

What I want to know is the length of the field that is at the address
that has been passed to me.  In other words the actual length of the
data item passed.

I am open to various suggestions - I do not want to put ideas
(pollute) in anyones head.  The program is batch.   IBM Enterprise
COBOL for z/OS and OS/390 3.1.0.

PS.  'Inspecting the contrents of the field would be the least
desireable solution in my opinion.
```

#### ↳ Re: Want to know length of the field passed to a program

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-11T20:20:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2OacnZyHQc5G3aWiU-KYvA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<acbbbc55.0308111649.24c2c90b@posting.google.com>`

```
randy wrote:
> The program calculates elapsed time.
>
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> desireable solution in my opinion.

Actually what is passed is the address of the first byte of each argument.
It is up to the called program to define the lengths and format of the
passed data. There is no easy way for a called program to know, for sure,
the characteristics of what's actually passed. The called program takes it
on faith that it's getting handed the correct data.

Even though the called program can't determine what's been passed; the
calling program can.

SOLUTION I.

Try this (called program):

LINKAGE SECTION.
01  8BYTE-PARAM        PIC X(8).
01  6BYTE-PARAM        PIC X(6).
(other params)

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
    ENTRY 'TIME-6' USING 6BYTE-PARAM (other params).
    MOVE 6BYTE-PARAM to WS-6
    Perform MESS-WITH-SIX-BYTES
    GOBACK.

    ENTRY 'TIME-8' USING 8BYTE-PARAM (other params).
    MOVE 8BYTE-PARAM to WS-8.
    Perform MESS-WITH-EIGHT-BYTES
    GOBACK.

Then the calling program does one of the following:

    CALL 'TIME-6' USING MY6BYTES

or

    CALL 'TIME-8' USING MY8BYTES

depending on what the calling program has at hand.

SOLUTION II.

Don't pass any times. Let the subprogram access the clock and figure out
everything.

For example:

CALL 'START-CLOCK'
(time passes)
CALL 'END-CLOCK' USING DURATION.

(Subroutine "MYCLOCK" has two entry points (as above): "START-CLOCK" and
"END-CLOCK")

In sum:

I don't see how you can inspect a field and determine whether it contains
six bytes of valid data or eight. That is, suppose the passed field contains
a six-byte time, but the next two bytes in memory, accidentally, contain
random data that SEEM (to the subprogram) to be part of an eight-byte value?
No joy in that.

Let us know what you decide.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Want to know length of the field passed to a program

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-12T01:31:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8sXZa.13910$vo2.10835@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<acbbbc55.0308111649.24c2c90b@posting.google.com> <2OacnZyHQc5G3aWiU-KYvA@giganews.com>`

```
Next solution:

In both the calling and the called field's Working-Storage Section define:

01  Either-Time External.
      05  6-byte-time   Pic  X(6)
      05  8-byte-time   Pic   X(8)

Then move the "desired" time to the correct field and spaces to the other
field in the calling program - and then check for spaces vs numeric in the
called program.  (Don't pass anything as a PARM or use Linkage Section)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Want to know length of the field passed to a program

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-12T02:40:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dsYZa.35380$K4.1686805@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<acbbbc55.0308111649.24c2c90b@posting.google.com> <2OacnZyHQc5G3aWiU-KYvA@giganews.com> <8sXZa.13910$vo2.10835@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`

```
You could add an 88 level and then you would do a straight if check (of
course you would have to do the set in the calling).

01  Either-Time External.
      05  Pic  X(1)
            88 USE-8-BYTE  VALUE '8'
            88 USE-6-BYTE  VALUE '6'
      05 time-8-BYTE   Pic   X(8).

I would do one of two things...

01 TIME-FIELD.
    05 TIME-LENGTH PIC 9(04) COMP.
    05 TIME-VALUE    PIC X(08).

or

I would write two programs.  Each one has a very clear and simple goal and
very specific input and output.
This is I guess what JerryMouse said in 1st response.  As he said - the
calling program knows, the called program just does its thing.

But BEFORE that I would look at CEEGMT LE Callable Service to see if that
does what I want.

JCE
```

- **From:** randy_clark@ahm.honda.com (randy)

```
First I want to thank everyone for their replies slash solutions.

First the reason I did not just create two programs is that the
program is widely used and I just wanted to change the called program.

I was hoping i could use a function like length to determine the
length of the passed arguement.  I am not big on inspecting it.  I
maybe able to use the parm-length solution.  I am still holding oout
hope that maybe using by content I cna use lenght function to
determine lenght of passed field.  That is really what I am after a
way to have COBOL tell me the length of the passed parms.

Having the program also access clock and determine time and calculate
the time is not going to work as the calling program is taking the
time from a an MQSeries puttime field and the time of the get and
passing these to determine the elapsed time between the message being
put on the queue and its being retrieved.


Thanks again for all the help.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Want to know length of the field passed to a program

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-12T14:22:31-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bhbba0$93s$1@news.btv.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<acbbbc55.0308111649.24c2c90b@posting.google.com> <2OacnZyHQc5G3aWiU-KYvA@giganews.com> <acbbbc55.0308120927.5d647ec1@posting.google.com>`

```
"randy" <randy_clark@ahm.honda.com> wrote in message
news:acbbbc55.0308120927.5d647ec1@posting.google.com...
> First I want to thank everyone for their replies slash solutions.
>
> First the reason I did not just create two programs is that the
> program is widely used and I just wanted to change the called program.
This is my understanding of the problem domain.....
If you had a widely used program that had a 6 or 8 byte time you would have
no original question.
If you had a widely used program that had a 6 byte time and you wanted to
send in 8 you could create a new program and we would have no original
question.
If you had a widely used program that had a 8 byte time and you wanted to
send in 6 you could create a new program and we would have no original
question.
Therefore - there is no widely used program.
If there is no widely used program then what's the problem again?

> I was hoping i could use a function like length to determine the
> length of the passed arguement.  I am not big on inspecting it.  I
> maybe able to use the parm-length solution.

INSPECT is a loaded word in cobol and implies overkill...checking one
position isn't going to kill performance.  If you know that you have to
check position 7 for a blank it's no big deal...

> hope that maybe using by content I cna use lenght function to
> determine lenght of passed field.  That is really what I am after a
> way to have COBOL tell me the length of the passed parms.
It ain't gonna do it for you...why would a length function be better than a
check for a position?

01 TIME-PARM PIC X(08).
01 TIME-R REDEFINES TIME-PARM.
       05 FILLER PIC X(07).
       05 EIGHTH-POSITION  PIC X(1)
             88 SIX-BYTE VALUE ' '.

Then in the code...
IF SIX-BYTE THEN
    DO SIX-BYTE
ELSE
    DO EIGHT-BYTE
END-IF

or if EIGHT-POSITION IS NUMERIC then.....
You should of course then always pass in the full 8 characters with spaces
which you don't seem to want to do.

> Having the program also access clock and determine time and calculate
> the time is not going to work as the calling program is taking the
> time from a an MQSeries puttime field and the time of the get and
> passing these to determine the elapsed time between the message being
> put on the queue and its being retrieved.

The PUTTIME is *always* a MQCHAR8.
This means that your get time must be variable - what's wrong with fixing
the inconsistency rather than worrying about handling it in the called
program ?

JCE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Want to know length of the field passed to a program

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-12T18:37:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Uta_a.6553$Ih1.1975098@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<acbbbc55.0308111649.24c2c90b@posting.google.com> <2OacnZyHQc5G3aWiU-KYvA@giganews.com> <acbbbc55.0308120927.5d647ec1@posting.google.com>`

```
"randy" <randy_clark@ahm.honda.com> wrote in message
news:acbbbc55.0308120927.5d647ec1@posting.google.com...
> I am still holding oout  hope that maybe using by content I cna use lenght
function to
> determine lenght of passed field.  That is really what I am after a
> way to have COBOL tell me the length of the passed parms.

By content (explict or implied based on value) is the ONLY way to do it.

Remember, this external program was "not there" when the calling program was
compiled (that's why it's external) ... so it has no clue what the
definition of the data was.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Want to know length of the field passed to a program

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-12T19:04:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fTa_a.14385$vo2.13463@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<acbbbc55.0308111649.24c2c90b@posting.google.com> <2OacnZyHQc5G3aWiU-KYvA@giganews.com> <acbbbc55.0308120927.5d647ec1@posting.google.com> <Uta_a.6553$Ih1.1975098@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Nope - (or at least not with all compilers) BY CONTENT *still* passes the
address of a TEMPORARY copy of the passed parameter and there is no way to
determine the length of what was passed (as it still is only an address).
BY VALUE (where available) *might* work for some compilers - but even that
isn't guaranteed to do what is desired.

The bottom-line is that "standard COBOL" ('85 Standard) simply has NO way to
determine the length (in a subprogram) of the parameter passed to it. The
2002 Standard introduces the "ANY LENGTH" feature for Linkage Section items,
but I don't think that will give you what you want either.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Want to know length of the field passed to a program

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2jgijv8n9t513505n3crbuvpq96cuqoqdg@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<acbbbc55.0308111649.24c2c90b@posting.google.com> <2OacnZyHQc5G3aWiU-KYvA@giganews.com> <acbbbc55.0308120927.5d647ec1@posting.google.com> <Uta_a.6553$Ih1.1975098@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <fTa_a.14385$vo2.13463@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`

```
On Tue, 12 Aug 2003 19:04:43 GMT "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

:>Nope - (or at least not with all compilers) BY CONTENT *still* passes the
:>address of a TEMPORARY copy of the passed parameter and there is no way to
:>determine the length of what was passed (as it still is only an address).
:>BY VALUE (where available) *might* work for some compilers - but even that
:>isn't guaranteed to do what is desired.

:>The bottom-line is that "standard COBOL" ('85 Standard) simply has NO way to
:>determine the length (in a subprogram) of the parameter passed to it. The
:>2002 Standard introduces the "ANY LENGTH" feature for Linkage Section items,
:>but I don't think that will give you what you want either.

I remember on COBOL on the CDC machines (back in 1977 or so) where addresses
were 18 bits but the word size was 60 bits, that COBOL would use the other 42
bits to put a description of the parameter.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Want to know length of the field passed to a program

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-12T15:29:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OqOdnVe6l4iC06SiXTWJkQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<acbbbc55.0308111649.24c2c90b@posting.google.com> <2OacnZyHQc5G3aWiU-KYvA@giganews.com> <acbbbc55.0308120927.5d647ec1@posting.google.com>`

```
randy wrote:
> First I want to thank everyone for their replies slash solutions.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> way to have COBOL tell me the length of the passed parms.

The called program, with no prior knowledge, cannot determine the length of
a passed field. All the called program gets is the starting address of the
passed field; not the format, not the length, not a handkerchief with a
little embroidered flower in the corner, nothing else.
```

#### ↳ Re: Want to know length of the field passed to a program

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-12T00:33:24-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bh9q9l$s5o$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<acbbbc55.0308111649.24c2c90b@posting.google.com>`

```
You'd probably want to treat the actual parameter as variable length and
(for example) define it something like this -

01  LS-PARM-REC.
      03  LS-PARM-LGTH PIC  9(04) BINARY.
      03  LS-PARM-DATE.
            05  LS-PARM-DATE-08 PIC  9(08).
            05  FILLER REDEFINES LS-PARM-DATE-08.
                  07  LS-PARM-DATE-06 PIC  9(06).
                  07  FILLER PIC  X(02).

Using the value in LS-PARM-LGTH (6 or 8), would then determine whether to
use LS-PARM-DATE-06 or LS-PARM-DATE-08.

This is common practice when dealing with variable length parameters.

Bill

"randy" <randy_clark@ahm.honda.com> wrote in message
news:acbbbc55.0308111649.24c2c90b@posting.google.com...
> The program calculates elapsed time.
>
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> desireable solution in my opinion.
```
