##### ↳ ↳ Re: Want to know length of the field passed to a program

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-12T16:18:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xr8_a.97999$0v4.6745015@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<acbbbc55.0308111649.24c2c90b@posting.google.com> <n74_a.95625$3o3.6650886@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

> "randy" <randy_clark@ahm.honda.com> wrote in message
> news:acbbbc55.0308111649.24c2c90b@posting.google.com...

> The program calculates elapsed time.

> I want the called program to handle being passed either a 6 or 8 byte
> time field.   I would like the calling program to pass either 6 or 8
> Bytes times and not have to let the called program know what it was
> passed.   I expect the the called program to determine what it has
> been passed and act accordingly.

01  XFER-TM-BEG.
      05  XFER-TM-BEG-X-8.
            10  XFER-TM-BEG-9-8                            PIC 9(08).
      05  XFER-TM-BEG-RED                                REDEFINES
            XFER-TM-BEG-X-8.
            10  XFER-TM-BEG-X-6.
                 15  XFER-TM-BEG-9-6                        PIC 9(06).
                 15  XFER-FL-BEG                                PIC X(02).
                       88  XFER-TM-BEG-IS-LTH-6       VALUE 'XX'.

01  XFER-TM-END.
      05  XFER-TM-END-X-8.
            10  XFER-TM-END-9-8                            PIC 9(08).
      05  XFER-TM-END-RED                                REDEFINES
            XFER-TM-END-X-8.
            10  XFER-TM-END-X-6.
                 15  XFER-TM-END-9-6                        PIC 9(06).
                 15  XFER-FL-END-X-2                        PIC X(02).
                       88  XFER-TM-END-IS-LTH-6       VALUE 'XX'.

In the CALLING program

IF (length of BEGin field to be passed is 8)
     Populate XFER-TM-BEG-9-8
ELSE
     Populate XFER-TM-BEG-9-6
     Initialize  XFER-FL-BEG  to  'XX'.

IF (length of END field to be passed is 8)
     Populate XFER-TM-END-9-8
ELSE
     Populate XFER-TM-END-9-6
     Initialize  XFER-FL-END  to  'XX'.

In the CALLED program
Determine what it has been passed
and act accordingly
by interrogating the LEVEL 88s.

IF  XFER-TM-BEG-IS-LTH-6
     MOVE  XFER-TM-BEG-9-6  TO  (your choice of work area)
ELSE
     MOVE  XFER-TM-BEG-9-8  TO  (your choice of work area).

IF  XFER-TM-END-IS-LTH-6
     MOVE  XFER-TM-END-9-6  TO  (your choice of work area)
ELSE
     MOVE  XFER-TM-END-9-8  TO  (your choice of work area).
```

#### ↳ Re: Want to know length of the field passed to a program

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-08-13T01:57:01+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f38f33c_5@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<acbbbc55.0308111649.24c2c90b@posting.google.com>`

```

"randy" <randy_clark@ahm.honda.com> wrote in message
news:acbbbc55.0308111649.24c2c90b@posting.google.com...
> The program calculates elapsed time.
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> data item passed.

I don't think so...<G>

The length is irrelevant; see below. (Besides, you know it is 8 bytes...<G>)

>
> I am open to various suggestions - I do not want to put ideas
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> desireable solution in my opinion.

I read the posts on this and was amazed...It isn't complex or difficult and
it doesn't require all kinds of different parameters

I believe your requirement is a simple and fair case of polymorphism by
overloading a function.

And I agree with you that the called function SHOULD be able to figure out
what it was passed, given the conditions stated above.

The following is a quick attempt that has not been compiled and is not
intended to be comprehensive. I believe it should compile OK in the
environment you stated (given that you provide the missing "infrastructure"
<G>) I have catered for theIBM environment only insofar as forcing Flag
checks to generate CLI, and using true binary for some calculation fields.
There are no platform dependent extensions in here as far as I know.

My comments are interspersed in the code inside square brackets [ ]...

CALLING PROGRAM:

01  Time-parameters.
      12 tp-result       pic x.
           88 tp-ok            value '0'.
           88 tp-bad-start  value '1'.
           88 tp-bad-end   value '2'.
      12 start-time     pic x(8).
      12 end-time      pic x(8).
      12 elapsed-time pic x(8).

[I don't personally like to have more parameters than I can help. And I
don't use RETURNING unless I have to... Instead, I pass a single block which
has all the necessary parameters and a status field to indicate what
happened. This block is returned intact to the calling program so there is
no need for agonizing over BY...REFERENCE, BY...CONTENT, etc...we can never
corrupt his data....]

CALLED PROGRAM
LINKAGE SECTION

01  Time-parameters.
      12 tp-result       pic x.
           88 tp-ok            value '0'.
           88 tp-bad-start  value '1'.
           88 tp-bad-end   value '2'.
      12 start-time     pic x(8).
      12 end-time      pic x(8).
      12 elapsed-time pic x(8).

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

01  ws-start-time value zero.
      12 ws-st-hh     pic 99.
      12 ws-st-mm   pic 99.
      12 ws-st-ss     pic 99.
      12 ws-st-hs     pic 99.

01  ws-end-time value zero.
      12 ws-et-hh     pic 99.
      12 ws-et-mm   pic 99.
      12 ws-et-ss     pic 99.
      12 ws-et-hs     pic 99.

01  ws-work-time pic x(8).

01  ws-work-time-flag   pic x value space.
      88 ws-work-time-valid     value '1'.
      88 ws-work-time-invalid   value '0'.

01  ws-time-format pic x value space.
       88 format-1     value '1'.
       88 format-2     value '2'.
       88 format-3     value '3'.

01  ws-ticks value low-values.
      12 ws-st-ticks             pic s9(9) comp.
      12 ws-et-ticks             pic s9(9) comp.
      12 ws-elapsed-ticks    pic s9(9) comp.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
Main Section.
on-entry.
       set tp-ok to TRUE
       perform validate-passed-data
       if  tp-ok
          perform calculate-ticks
          perform convert-elapsed-ticks
       end-if
       .
return-to-caller.
       exit program.
---------------------------------------------------------------------
validate-passed-data section.
[ The validation proceeds on the the following assumptions:

1. The passed times can be in any of the following formats:
     1. HHMMSSCC - (CC = 100ths of a second) an 8 byte unformatted time.
     2. HH:MM:SS    -  a formatted time
     3. HHMMSS     -   a 6 byte unformatted time

2. Hundredths of a second will not be included in the calculations.
Obviously, they could be, but it is more code and I'm too busy to do it. The
object of the exercise is to show that polymorphism can be obtained fairly
easily by overloading in COBOL.]

check-the-times.
       move start-time to ws-work-time
       perform check-time
       if  ws-work-time-valid
           move end-time to ws-work-time
           perform check-time
           if ws-work-time-invalid
              set tp-bad-end to TRUE
           end-if
       else
           set tp-bad-start to TRUE
       end-if
       .
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
check-time section.
check-format-and-content.

       if  ws-work-time (3:1) = ':'
           AND
           ws-work-time (6:1) = ':'
                 set format-2 to TRUE
       else
            if  ws-work-time (7:2) = spaces
                set format-3 to TRUE
            else
                set format-1 to TRUE
            end-if
        end-if

[The following validations are basic and could be "toughened" with extra
checks... I opted not to because the data is coming from a calling program
which should have already validated it, and the extra code would obfuscate
what I'm really trying to show here...What is here should realistically be
adequate for most real environments.]

        if format-1
           OR
           format-3
              move zero to ws-work-time (7:2)
              if ws-work-time is NOT NUMERIC
                  OR
                  ws-work-time (1:2) > 23
                  OR
                  ws-work-time (3:2) > 59
                  OR
                  ws-work-time (5:2) > 59
                        set ws-work-time-invalid to TRUE
               else
                   set ws-work-time-valid to TRUE
               end-if
        else
            if  (ws-work-time (1:2) > 23
                OR
                ws-work-time (3:2) > 59
                OR
                ws-work-time (5:2) > 59
                    set ws-work-time-invalid to TRUE
            else
                set ws-work-time-valid to TRUE
            end-if
            .
---------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
calculate-ticks section.
do-the-sums.
[ This section will convert the start time and the end time into "Ticks"
(seconds since midnight).

It would cater for midnight rollover by checking the relation of the start
time to the end time... there are many different possible requirements here
and I have coded a number of such routines at various times in my career.

Once we have the number of ticks for both the start and end time we can
calculate the elapsed ticks by (simple) subtraction...it may be complicated
by rollover...]

----------------------------------------------------------------------------
---------------
convert-elapsed-ticks section.
do-more-sums.
[Finally it is necessary to convert the calculated elapsed ticks into a
formatted time and return it to the user.

I would probably opt for format-2 (HH:MM:SS) as being tidiest...

So, there we have a subroutine that can accept different formats of
parameter and carry out the same operations on them (polymorphism), by
allowing the same code to accept and interpret different parameters
(overloading). In OO this is a very necessary part of re-use, but it isn't
totally confined to OO as the above procedural code shows.

The function could be extended to do many things; different actions
depending on rollover at midnight, the acceptance of dates as well as times,
the possibility to calculate days between dates, a general "Time
Machine"...<G>

However none of these facilities should impact the existing functionality.
In OO these new Methods would be achieved by extending the Class; in
Procedural code they could be achieved by putting a "wrapper" round the
existing function. The Wrapper would have a different parameter block with
days and dates in it as well as the times shown above, but internally, it
would call this function when it needed elapsed time...]

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Want to know length of the field passed to a program

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-12T14:58:08-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bhbdcp$hfa$1@news.btv.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<acbbbc55.0308111649.24c2c90b@posting.google.com> <3f38f33c_5@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3f38f33c_5@news.athenanews.com...
> I believe your requirement is a simple and fair case of polymorphism by
> overloading a function.
Polymorphism yes, but I don't see overloading.

> [I don't personally like to have more parameters than I can help. And I
> don't use RETURNING unless I have to... Instead, I pass a single block
which
> has all the necessary parameters and a status field to indicate what
> happened. This block is returned intact to the calling program so there is
> no need for agonizing over BY...REFERENCE, BY...CONTENT, etc...we can
never
> corrupt his data....]
I wholly agree with this "style"...hopefully you won't start a style war

> So, there we have a subroutine that can accept different formats of
> parameter and carry out the same operations on them (polymorphism), by
> allowing the same code to accept and interpret different parameters
> (overloading). In OO this is a very necessary part of re-use, but it isn't
> totally confined to OO as the above procedural code shows.
What you call overloading is just a parametric form of polymorphism.

Overloading is more often where the calls have different meaning based on
the context e.g.:

function calcTimeDiff(String, String);
function calcTimeDiff(int, int);

So the function can be called in many contexts.

calcTimeDiff("2003-05-01","2003-06-01")
calcTimeDiff(1000,1000)

> However none of these facilities should impact the existing functionality.
> In OO these new Methods would be achieved by extending the Class
or overloading :-)

> Procedural code they could be achieved by putting a "wrapper" round the
> existing function. The Wrapper would have a different parameter block with
> days and dates in it as well as the times shown above, but internally, it
> would call this function when it needed elapsed time...]
I wholly agree with this too.  This is something we've done with some LE
calls to check date formats and so forth...call a program that calls LE
service with a date format of "YYYY-MM-DD" to check a valid date format etc

> Pete.
Thanks for taking the time to post this....as well as the [comments].  A + B
is always the same...why someone chooses A + B is *always* more interesting.

JCE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Want to know length of the field passed to a program

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-12T20:20:44-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308121920.4bc120b7@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<acbbbc55.0308111649.24c2c90b@posting.google.com> <3f38f33c_5@news.athenanews.com> <bhbdcp$hfa$1@news.btv.ibm.com>`

```
"jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote 

> Overloading is more often where the calls have different meaning based on
> the context e.g.:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> calcTimeDiff(1000,1000)

Actually in most languages they are _different_ functions which happen
to appear to have the same name.  In C++, for example, name mangling
changes the function names so that they are (eg): calcTimeDiff_2zz()
and calcTimeDiff_2ii() based of the parameters in the definitions. 
When used the appropriate mangled name is created by the compiler so
that the linker will resolve the correct function.
 

Untyped languages have to contrive this somewhat differently and the
programmer can handle it with type checking code.
If you want to do it like C++ then just have different functions to
call depending on the parameters you want to supply.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Anyone ever heard of JSB?

- **From:** "Kjeld Hansen" <paabol@12mail.dk>
- **Date:** 2003-08-14T21:43:00+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f3be64c$0$32518$edfadb0f@dread16.news.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<3f390a90$0$310$ba620e4c@reader0.news.skynet.be> <3f3b302c$0$886$ba620e4c@reader1.news.skynet.be>`

```
When I started working in professional IT-development 16 years ago, the
installation had some 40 source code modules done in Jackson Structured
Programming on IBM mainframe.

I didn't get training in JSP, since the organisation already had decided not
to do further development in this tool, but I did some maintenance work on
existing modules in 3-4 years.

Among the features (as I recall by heart, I don't have any documents on JSP
left) were:

The structuring tool enforced you to code processing in both the true (if)
and the false (else) conditions. It also featured a case structure.
You could generate structure diagrams of your modules, but that were in the
days were all host print were character based line printing, so even a
simple module produced more than 10 pages of  boxes and lines that quickly
became more difficult to navigate than the source code.

One of the major drawbacks was that the tool as a precompiler produced Cobol
code with lots of go to's. Even worse, since JSP not at that time supported
test and debugging on the JSP source, you had to do all debugging on the
Cobol not-so-structured code with a lot of JSP-generated paragraph names in
it.

A good thing was that JSP let you declare cobol sections where you could
code native Cobol to your hearts content. ;-)

  Good luck,
Kjeld P. Hansen
Systems Designer,
Fredericia, Denamrk


"Georgie" <kriweed@yahoo.com> skrev i en meddelelse
news:3f3b302c$0$886$ba620e4c@reader1.news.skynet.be...
> Well it was JSP (Jackson structured programming, by mr. Michael Jackson
> ;-))and not JSB.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > knowledge of JSB was a plus. I don't have a clue of what JSB is. They
only
> > mentioned it as some kind of COBOL generator (but I'm not sure of it)
> > It's in a DLI, COBOL II shop.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Anyone ever heard of JSB?

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2003-08-15T16:31:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0308151531.173e8135@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3f390a90$0$310$ba620e4c@reader0.news.skynet.be> <3f3b302c$0$886$ba620e4c@reader1.news.skynet.be> <3f3be64c$0$32518$edfadb0f@dread16.news.tele.dk>`

```
Bottom posting this time - snipped a bit

"Kjeld Hansen" <paabol@12mail.dk> wrote in message news:<3f3be64c$0$32518$edfadb0f@dread16.news.tele.dk>...
> When I started working in professional IT-development 16 years ago, the
> installation had some 40 source code modules done in Jackson Structured
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > It's in a DLI, COBOL II shop.

I think you slate JSP too much.  Michael Jackson was one of the first
proponents of avoiding the use of GO TO and his JSP was based around
that idea.  I admit I don't like using the method myself as I think
that the use of structure charts is unwieldy in much the same way as
you describe and makes it harder to see the logic.  However, in the
context of the time, it was probably an improvement on the way many
organisations used flowcharts.

I was taught to use paragraphs (sections weren't even considered,
though the original COBOL standard included them with what I thought
was brilliant foresight), the paragraph names were taken from the grid
identifiers on the flowchart (supplied by IBM).  An additional letter
or two was used to uniquely identify each flowchart page.  Notes were
discouraged on the grounds that they often weren't accurate,
especially after several amendments.  However, datanames were supposed
to be meaningful.  Flowcharts were supposed to be kept up-to-date, but
rarely were.  In mitigation of this method, programs were to be
subdivided into modules of no more than 400 lines of code and were to
fully specified to the point that a programmer could write them
without further reference to the author (usually!!).  Practice at
writing module specification was a useful step towards writing program
and system specifications.  Despite the handicap of using flowcharts
in this way, the resultant programs were fast and accurate, and were
produced quite quickly.  Maintaining them could be a pain though.

I think his idea of corresponding data and procedure structures was
worth investigating as a theoretical exploration, though it only works
effectively for fairly simple logic and doesn't work with sorts, etc. 
I think that another of the disadvantages of JSP was that it
unnecessarily discouraged many people from using internal sorts,
sometimes/often external sorts are more appropriate, but certainly not
always.

Michael Jackson also proposed the use of pseudo-code though with some
rather idiosyncratic names, again he probably wasn't the first, but
was a strong and influential advocate.

I suspect that many organisations adopted aspects and parts of his
techniques in ways of which he wouldn't approve.

Edsger Djikstra was the first published proponent of the avoidance of
the use of GO TOs, though one doesn't know for sure how many other
people were beginning to have similar ideas around the same time. 
Apart from having the idea he also propounded it very lucidly, to the
point that when in 1977 I was considering whether to buy his book
(actually a monograph, published in the same volume as two other
authors) or Michael Jackson's, I chose his, despite it being written
for an Algol type language when I had previously learned mainly COBOL,
a little Assembler, very little Fortran and no ALGOL.  Michael
Jackson's book on structured programming was written using COBOL to
illustrate his method, if you are interested, then his book is the
most authoritative source on his method, most organisations that say
they use JSP, only use bits of it, usually the structure charts in my
experience.

Robert
```

## Thread: Sending e-mail to and receiving e-mail into Acucobol programs — 13 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Sending e-mail to and receiving e-mail into Acucobol programs

- **From:** selectsoftware@knology.net (Keith)
- **Date:** 2003-08-12T09:55:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<de923f33.0308120855.6d35318b@posting.google.com>`

```
I develop accounting software for timeshare resorts, membership
campgrounds and comdominiums using Acucobol. These properties would
like for their owners/members to be able to view their charge/payment
histories on-line and, if desired, make a payment. There will be
identical files on the Server and Client side and they will have to
stay in sync. The way I would like to do this is to send e-mails to
and from the server/client when a person's account is changed, either
by a charge or payment. I would welcome any ideas/help along these
lines. Thank you. Keith
```

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

You've worked out what happens when two (or more) entities change the same
record at the same time?

It is usually a rule of the universe that there can be only one (copy of the
master database).
```

#### ↳ Re: Sending e-mail to and receiving e-mail into Acucobol programs

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-12T14:47:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308121347.1a941d93@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<de923f33.0308120855.6d35318b@posting.google.com>`

```
selectsoftware@knology.net (Keith) wrote

> These properties would
> like for their owners/members to be able to view their charge/payment
> histories on-line and, if desired, make a payment. 

That would seem to me to be a good case for a Web based CGI system. 
It isn't particularly hard to do CGI in Cobol and process transactions
against one live set of data on the server.  You need to have a
mechanism for users to login and to restrict the information to only
that appropriate to each user.

> There will be
> identical files on the Server and Client side and they will have to
> stay in sync. 

I do have clients who maintaing branch offices with nominally
identical data.  Originally using dial in to collect updates and
upload orders etc, now using EMail.  Because of the volumes I mark
records with a block of flags when they are updated, one for each
active branch, and then extract daily where the flag is set for each
branch.  But then a product may be updated 20 or 100 times in one day,
but extracted once.

> The way I would like to do this is to send e-mails to
> and from the server/client when a person's account is changed, either
> by a charge or payment. I would welcome any ideas/help along these
> lines. Thank you. Keith

Is this a 3 tier system:

        Central server
        Client resort
        Owners/Members

Are you keeping all data on the central server and making payment
records there and EMailing from the server to the client resort, or
were you wanting to have the records maintained on the resort and the
EMail is to the Owner/Membrers machine to keep a set of records there
?  Or were you EMailing from the resort to keep the central server
updated ?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sending e-mail to and receiving e-mail into Acucobol programs

- **From:** selectsoftware@knology.net (Keith)
- **Date:** 2003-08-13T13:13:41-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<de923f33.0308131213.16d3c9f9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<de923f33.0308120855.6d35318b@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0308121347.1a941d93@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote in message news:<217e491a.0308121347.1a941d93@posting.google.com>...
> selectsoftware@knology.net (Keith) wrote
> 
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> updated ?
Hi Richard,
Thank you for your quick response. I'll try to explain my situation a little
more clearly. I was in a rush yesterday and. frankly, didn't know if I would
get any response at all.

Here's what I want to do. My clients, mainly timeshare resorts and
membership campgrounds, running my property management software, want to be
able to do 2 things over the internet. 1) Allow members to check
availability of rooms/sites and make reservations. 2) Allow members to view
their charge/payment histories and, if desired, make a payment (probably by
sending an e-mail to that effect with credit card info to the property.

We will have a web server, initially with an ISP and then there will be the
property computer (client). Both computers will have a member data file,
payment data file and reservations data file.

When a member logs into the server they will be able to check their payment
history and/or check the availability of units/sites for rent. If they
reserve a site/unit we want an e-mail sent to the client computer. When the
client computer receives the e-mail, a program will read it and update the
reservations file on the client. If the property (client) makes or changes a
reservation the client will create an e-mail to the server and when received
a program will read it and update the server reservations file.

The same thing will happen, mainly from the client, when new
charges/payments are added to a member's file. The client will create an
e-mail and send it to the server and the server will update the member data
file.

The e-mails would have to include the property number, member number, type
of transaction (reservation addition/change or payment history update), the
date, reservation dates, site number, etc.

If this makes sense and you have any programs that do something like this, I
sure would appreciate seeing them. Any help will be greatly appreciated.
Thanks again. Keith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sending e-mail to and receiving e-mail into Acucobol programs

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-13T18:45:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308131745.49f58f5b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<de923f33.0308120855.6d35318b@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0308121347.1a941d93@posting.google.com> <de923f33.0308131213.16d3c9f9@posting.google.com>`

```
selectsoftware@knology.net (Keith) wrote

> Here's what I want to do. My clients, mainly timeshare resorts and
> membership campgrounds, running my property management software, want to be
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> sending an e-mail to that effect with credit card info to the property.

Sounds reasonable.
 
> We will have a web server, initially with an ISP 

Ah, now there's the rub.  Many ISPs will only allow certain types of
CGI processing.  Typically they will allow .ASP, or Perl/Python/PHP,
perhaps SQL Server or MySQL.

The chances of you doing it in Cobol on their server may be quite
small.

Some ISPs seem to allow hosting of user servers.  ie you can put your
machine will your software in their room for a fee.  This allows you
to run anything you like but management becomes an issue.

> and then there will be the
> property computer (client). Both computers will have a member data file,
> payment data file and reservations data file.

This is where the _real_ problems arise.  Due to latency between ISP
based server and client machine there _will_ be cases where one
customer reserves via Web at the same time as another has phoned the
resort directly for the same reservation.
 
> When a member logs into the server they will be able to check their payment
> history and/or check the availability of units/sites for rent. If they
> reserve a site/unit we want an e-mail sent to the client computer. 

Easy to do (or should be).

> When the
> client computer receives the e-mail, a program will read it and update the
> reservations file on the client. 

Should be quite easy to do.  A program needs to run continuously in
background looking for incoming EMails and sleeping if none found.  I
do this with a batch file, but there needs to be an EMail server that
will put new EMails out as separate files and distributed by user.  I
have used Mercury for this (see www.PMail.com).  The batch file uses
MIME64.EXE (http://www.ibiblio.org/pub/micro/pc-stuff/freedos/files/net/
) to extract the attachment to a file - in this case a .ZIP which is
unzipped.  Then the processing Cobol program runs on the unzipped
text.  You won't need the zipping.

> If the property (client) makes or changes a
> reservation the client will create an e-mail to the server and when received
> a program will read it and update the server reservations file.

If you can get the ISP to do this it may be OK, but I would probably
do a html connect from the client system and send data in through the
CGI program.  ie send it a CGI Query with the data items attached as
if it was coming from an HTML <form>.  This way there is only one
program to worry about and and it can be 'instant'.  The CGI would
'know' that it was the resort client computer sending and could reply
with a much simplified page that is easy to parse.  Or even use SOAP
or similar.

Because it would quick this could be part of the confirmation process.
 basically the ISP's database becomes the reservation 'master' and the
resort's
machine must bounce reservations off that before confirming.

> The same thing will happen, mainly from the client, when new
> charges/payments are added to a member's file. The client will create an
> e-mail and send it to the server and the server will update the member data
> file.

Or send a CGI query to update.

> The e-mails would have to include the property number, member number, type
> of transaction (reservation addition/change or payment history update), the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks again. Keith

Once the EMail attachment has been received by Mercury (or some other)
and broken out by MIME64 (and PKUNZIP) then it is just a text that can
easily be read as a LINE SEQUENTIAL.

To send EMails from Windows I use BLAT (see
http://www.interlog.com/~tcharron/blat.html ) it will add a file as an
attachment and bang it out.

If you can do a CALL "system" or similar to execute a batch file you
can do this directly, otherwise just write out a file to a specific
place and have a batch file cycling look for files there to be BLATted
out. This too needs to have a real sleep for a minute or two betwen
activations, or could be another part of the incoming process.

Of course if the Web Servers were in-house at the resort the CGI
program could directly interract with the live data files.  For that I
use Xitami server (Xitami Pro does https secure serving) (see
http://www.imatix.com ) on Windows or Apache on Linux and just did my
own CGI code - it isn't particularly hard if you can get the program
to use stdin/stdout and read environment variables.  The code is 99%
the same for indows+Xitami /OS/2+IBMServer /Linux+Apache
/UnixWare+NetScape.
```

On Tue, 12 Aug 2003 09:55:36 +0000, Keith wrote:

> I develop accounting software for timeshare resorts, membership
> campgrounds and comdominiums using Acucobol. These properties would like
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

Keith;
   All of the proposed methods that you have recieved are way more work
than is necessary with AcuCobol.  Buy the AcuThin license, (it will cost a
hell of a lot less than the kludge you are proposing,)  and set up your
application to allow the customer only those screens you want him to see. 
He then posts to the standard data files (no syncronization problems) and
the remaing problem you have to solve is the charge card payments.  Those
are modules which you can also purchase off the shelf.  You can fire off
acurcl from the web page or have the customer install a small rdp program
from the web. (Try AcuCobol's demo to see how it works).  We started down
this path in the 1960's and CICS was the result.....you don't want to
invent that again.  If you want to try ours, email me and I will set you
up on our demo machine.   It is FAST.                 fdr
                                                      mytemyke@mytemyke.com
```

would this be a 'kludge'.

<a id="t-862badeec2"></a>
## Thread: Passing EXTERNAL Data to AMODE(24) Assembler Sub-Program — 6 msgs

### Passing EXTERNAL Data to AMODE(24) Assembler Sub-Program

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-13T10:15:02-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bhdgo2$dnv$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Greetings,

        I have a COBOL/Batch "Main" program, compiled with DATA(31) and
linked as AMODE(31) RMODE(ANY) and have defined a WS "01" area as EXTERNAL,
which from what I gather, is allocated BELOW THE LINE.

        With this in mind, I need to Dynamically CALL an ASSEMBLER
sub-program, which was linked as AMODE(24) and RMODE(24) and must remain as
such, passing the 24-bit EXTERNAL data as a parameter.

        However, when the PARMLIST is created by the compiler as an
"intermediate field" in the "Main" program prior to the actual CALL(BALR),
the address of the EXTERNAL data is stored into the PARMLIST field (which is
fine) and then R1 is loaded with the address of the PARMLIST, which is above
the line (and is the problem), causing the ASSEMBLER sub-program to choke
when it attempts to establish parameter addressability using R1.

        Does anyone have a resolution or any thought on this, other than
monkeying around with the addressing-mode of the sub-program (BASSM, BSM,
GETMAIN, etc) to attain R1 addressability?

Thanks,

Bill
```

#### ↳ Re: Passing EXTERNAL Data to AMODE(24) Assembler Sub-Program

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-08-13T15:14:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f3a5160.28205062@news.central.cox.net>`
- **References:** `<bhdgo2$dnv$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com> wrote:

>        I have a COBOL/Batch "Main" program, compiled with DATA(31) and
>linked as AMODE(31) RMODE(ANY) and have defined a WS "01" area as EXTERNAL,
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>GETMAIN, etc) to attain R1 addressability?

Put a 31-bit wrapper around it. Do a STORAGE OBTAIN,LENGTH=(?),LOC=BELOW and
copy the parmlist below the line.

Sample code:
http://www.urz.uni-heidelberg.de/ADSM/ibmdoc.tsm52/mvs/html/guide/anrmgd51198.htm
```

#### ↳ "Must remain as such" - why?

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attgloabl.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-13T11:10:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F3A7F30.336A52DE@attgloabl.net>`
- **References:** `<bhdgo2$dnv$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Why "must" your Assmebler routine remain 24-bit?  Your COBOL routine didn't
"have to" remain 24-bit.  Any program would be able to call a 31-bit Assembler
routine, whether the caller is 24-bit or 31-bit.  So, 31-bit code is the "lowest
common denominator".  It's time to upgrade the Assembler rather than trying to
work around it.

WOB wrote:

> Greetings,
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "Must remain as such" - why?

- **From:** wobconsult@sprynet.com (WOB Consulting)
- **Date:** 2003-08-13T18:58:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<173f80ae.0308131758.6025c60f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bhdgo2$dnv$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3F3A7F30.336A52DE@attgloabl.net>`

```
Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attgloabl.net> wrote in message news:<3F3A7F30.336A52DE@attgloabl.net>...
> Why "must" your Assmebler routine remain 24-bit?  Your COBOL routine didn't
> "have to" remain 24-bit.  Any program would be able to call a 31-bit Assembler
> routine, whether the caller is 24-bit or 31-bit.  So, 31-bit code is the "lowest
> common denominator".  It's time to upgrade the Assembler rather than trying to
> work around it.

Unfortunately, it's a proprietary sub-program that reads a sequential
file and subsequently, addresses a DCB. The vendor has been real slow
in making the appropriate changes for 31-bit. Hence, my hands are
tied.

<Rest Snipped>
```

#### ↳ Re: Passing EXTERNAL Data to AMODE(24) Assembler Sub-Program

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-13T19:30:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<plw_a.17581$vo2.9431@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<bhdgo2$dnv$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The detailed (and somewhat complex) rules on when EXTERNAL data is above the
line and when it is below is discussed at:


http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/1.2.6.4

There was BUG in some earlier release of COBOL or LE that always caused
EXTERNAL data to be below the line.  I can't remember when this was "fixed"
but it is implied in the information at:

    http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igymg202/1.2.4
```

#### ↳ Re: Passing EXTERNAL Data to AMODE(24) Assembler Sub-Program

- **From:** "Don Higgins" <don.higgins@microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-14T05:28:07-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bhfl0q$4v0$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<bhdgo2$dnv$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
You can add the Micro Focus COBOL directive:
DATA(24) to the calling COBOL program which will force the data division to
be placed below the 16 MB line so the data can be passed to other 24 bit
application programs.

Don Higgins
don.higgins@microfocus.com

"WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com> wrote in message
news:bhdgo2$dnv$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net...
> Greetings,
>

> linked as AMODE(31) RMODE(ANY) and have defined a WS "01" area as
EXTERNAL,
> which from what I gather, is allocated BELOW THE LINE.
>
>         With this in mind, I need to Dynamically CALL an ASSEMBLER
> sub-program, which was linked as AMODE(24) and RMODE(24) and must remain
as
> such, passing the 24-bit EXTERNAL data as a parameter.
>
>         However, when the PARMLIST is created by the compiler as an
> "intermediate field" in the "Main" program prior to the actual CALL(BALR),
> the address of the EXTERNAL data is stored into the PARMLIST field (which
is
> fine) and then R1 is loaded with the address of the PARMLIST, which is
above
> the line (and is the problem), causing the ASSEMBLER sub-program to choke

<a id="t-7323213095"></a>
## Thread: For US (ANSI) - 2002 COBOL Standard is now "official" — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### For US (ANSI) - 2002 COBOL Standard is now "official"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-13T20:12:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dZw_a.17596$vo2.10734@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`

```
For anyone interested in US-only (ANSI - "American National Standards
Institute") final/official (July 16, 2003) adoption of the 2002 ISO
Standard, see:

 http://public.ansi.org/ansionline/Documents/Standards%20Action/SAV3432.pdf

which says (in part),

"ITI (INCITS) (InterNational Committee for Information Technology Standards)

New National Adoptions

INCITS/ISO/IEC 1989-2002, Information Technology - Programming Languages -
COBOL (identical national adoption and revision of ANSI INCITS 23-1985
(R2001), ANSI INCITS 23a-1989 (R2001), ANSI INCITS 23b-1993 (R2001)):
7/16/2003"

Among other "implications" of this (AS I UNDERSTAND IT), this means that as
far as ANSI goes, the "'85 Standard" is no longer a *currently in effect*
ANSI COBOL Standard.

As stated in another thread (in comp.lang.cobol) and as announced on the J4
"public website" at:
  http://www.cobolstandards.com/

"The revised COBOL standard was approved by ISO in September 2002 and
published in December 2002. Copies can be obtained from ISO

http://www.iso.ch/iso/en/CatalogueDetailPage.CatalogueDetail?CSNUMBER=28805&ICS1=35&ICS2=60&ICS3
or your national member body of ISO
     http://www.iso.ch/addresse/address.html.
ANSI adopted the ISO standard as a U.S. standard in July 2003. Copies can be
obtained from ANSI
    http://webstore.ansi.org/ansidocstore/dept.asp?dept_id=10
for $18."
```

<a id="t-4eafaf1cfb"></a>
## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2003-08-14T09:05:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1060851833@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

<a id="t-a6b07f1e8a"></a>
## Thread: Inspect & differing lengths — 13 this month / 15 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Inspect & differing lengths

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-15T17:21:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vjqn6s2vjn7c8b@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Good afternoon.

I ran into a problem today, and I'm curious to hear from some of the J4 
folks around here if I'm doing something wrong, and if so, why it is wrong.

I have a fixed set of characters (41 of 'em) that I'm trying to 
eliminate in a string.  I want to replace them with a single space, 
thereby shrinking up the original string.  With the following...

77  My-Tag  Pic X(41) Value '[stuff]'.
...
Inspect My-String Replacing All My-Tag by " "

... I get a syntax error telling me that the targets have to be the same 
size.  Checking the 2002 standard, it said that if literal-1 and 
literal-3 were both specified, they must be of the same length. 
However, I'm mixing a literal and an identifier.

Could I do what I'm wanting by making a data item as "Pic X(01) Value 
Space", and use it on the right side of the replacing clause?

If not, why will Inspect not vary lengths like that, using rules similar 
to Move (truncation / space filling)?

Thanks....
```

#### ↳ Re: Inspect & differing lengths

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <hugh.candlin@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-15T23:20:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rVd%a.102052$0v4.7049047@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<vjqn6s2vjn7c8b@corp.supernews.com>`

```

LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message
news:vjqn6s2vjn7c8b@corp.supernews.com...
> Good afternoon.
>
> I ran into a problem today, and I'm curious to hear from some of the J4
> folks around here if I'm doing something wrong, and if so, why it is
wrong.
>
> I have a fixed set of characters (41 of 'em) that I'm trying to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Inspect My-String Replacing All My-Tag by " "

What you need to do is to successively compare
each 41-byte portion of My-String to My-Tag.

I.E. My-String (1 thru 41) My-String (2 thru 42)
My-String (3 thru 43) etc, until you reach the last
41-byte portion of My-String.

As you do so, if the comparison fails,
move the subscripted starting position character
from My-String to the subscripted output string field,
increment the output string subscript by 1,
increment the My-String start position subscript by 1,
and continue the search.

If you get a hit,
move a space to  the subscripted output string field,
increment the output string subscript by 1,
increment the My-String start position subscript by 41,
and continue the search.
```

#### ↳ Re: Inspect & differing lengths

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-08-16T11:57:20+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bhjs5c$1nn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<vjqn6s2vjn7c8b@corp.supernews.com>`

```
LX-i wrote:

> I have a fixed set of characters (41 of 'em) that I'm trying to
> eliminate in a string.  I want to replace them with a single space,
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> to Move (truncation / space filling)?

INSPECT will only replace character strings of the _same_ length because it 
does it in-situ and never moves stuff aroound.

You need:

      MOVE SPACES TO Work-1 Work-2
      UNSTRING My-String DELIMITED BY My-Tag
          INTO Work-1 Work-2
      MOVE SPACES TO My-String
      STRING Work-1 " " Work-2 DELIMITED BY SPACES
          INTO My-String

Actually you probably need to do more than this such as getting UNSTRING to 
supply the sizes so that you can put, say, a HIGH-VALUE into Work-1 and 
Work-2 to act as a delimiter for the STRING.  Also if you can have more 
than one My-Tag in My-String you need to cater for this.

Or Use INSPECT TALYING My-Count FOR CHARACTERS BEFORE My-Tag and use 
referenece notaion to extract the two parts for reassembly.
```

#### ↳ Re: Inspect & differing lengths

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-08-15T20:41:04-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<y5f%a.8777$kp4.1174494@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<vjqn6s2vjn7c8b@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"LX-i" <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message
news:vjqn6s2vjn7c8b@corp.supernews.com...
> Good afternoon.
>
> I ran into a problem today, and I'm curious to hear from some of the J4
> folks around here if I'm doing something wrong, and if so, why it is
wrong.
>
> I have a fixed set of characters (41 of 'em) that I'm trying to
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I think you want:  (actually, it is not what you want, but is the way
inspect works)

Inspect My-String Replacing All My-Tag by "a literal of 41 spaces here"

You have to have a literal in the second string the same size as the
original string because they correspond ... ie the first character in the
first literal is replaced by the first character in the second literal, the
second by the second, the third by the third, etc.  The example below, which
changes upper case to lower, illustrates what I mean.

Inspect my-string replacing all "ABCDEFGHJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ" by
"abcdfeghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz".

That gets rid of the bad characters, but does *not* compress the string.  To
do that, you need to unstring delimited by ALL " ", then string delimited by
a single " ".

The inspect cannot move data within the string ... it only replaces, counts,
etc.

Donald
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Inspect & differing lengths

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-16T11:31:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

| "abcdfeghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz".

CONVERTING will work.
The example is a string replacement, not a character replacement.

|

by
| a single " ".

#### ↳ Re: Inspect & differing lengths

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-08-16T09:10:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f3df463.17707852@news.central.cox.net>`
- **References:** `<vjqn6s2vjn7c8b@corp.supernews.com>`

```
LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote:


>I have a fixed set of characters (41 of 'em) that I'm trying to 
>eliminate in a string.  I want to replace them with a single space, 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>Space", and use it on the right side of the replacing clause?

The right side should be Pic x(41) value spaces or a literal with exactly 41

```

#### ↳ Re: Inspect & differing lengths

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2003-08-16T02:27:50-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0308160127.2fa975bc@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<vjqn6s2vjn7c8b@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Bottom posting below (where else would it be!)

LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message news:<vjqn6s2vjn7c8b@corp.supernews.com>...
> Good afternoon.
> 
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks....

Having read your question and the other responses, it seems to me that
your question is ambiguous.

Do you mean that you want to replace one or more strings containing
all the 41 characters in the same sequence as a group together?  If
so, then the other answers are appropriate.

Or do you mean that you have a set of 41 different characters each
individual one of which is to be replaced by a space and that you also
want to replace any multiple adjacent spaces by single spaces?

If the latter then your INSPECT statement should contain a space
literal that is 41 characters in length and you should follow up with
a PERFORM statement to removed the adjacent multiple spaces.  A small
twist to this, is whether you would also want to convert any
pre-existing multiple adjacent spaces to single spaces or leave those
as they were before.  My solution would also compress any pre-existing
multiple spaces, but if that isn't what you want, then a much more
complex solution would be required, which I don't want to solve unless
you say it is necessary.

Robert
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Inspect & differing lengths

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-16T07:55:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vjsadgh0l9rrd8@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<fcd09c56.0308160127.2fa975bc@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<vjqn6s2vjn7c8b@corp.supernews.com> <fcd09c56.0308160127.2fa975bc@posting.google.com>`

```
Thanks for all the replies - looks like I'll be writing some extra code. 
  (What I was really wanting Inspect Replacing to do was the same thing 
as the VB command Replace - oh well...)  I'm especially intrigued by 
RP's Unstring-then-String idea...   Anyway, more details about the 
problem are below.

Robert Jones wrote:
> Bottom posting below (where else would it be!)
> 
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> so, then the other answers are appropriate.

Yes, this is what I'm trying to do.  Our system runs on a Unisys 
ClearPath IX mainframe, and we have a standard ("green screen") 
interface and a GUI.  When we output message traffic to the green 
screen, we can put out start-of-entry (SOE) and tab characters, and 
users can tab to places in the output and transmit, which submits a new 
transaction with the parameters between the SOE and where the cursor was.

The GUI folks had a JavaScript function that would go through each line 
of output (which is written in HTML for them), and replace these 
SOE-to-Tab combinations with a link that the users could click to submit 
the same transaction they would on the green screen.  This function has 
to read every line of every output, and our performance guys said that 
it was really being a drain on systems, especially older ones.

The solution was to modify the mainframe process to insert these links 
as the traffic is being written.  This way, all the GUI has to do is 
display (or redisplay, which is an option) what's already there.  The 
anchor tag that creates the link is 41 characters, and the closing 
anchor tag is 4 (</a>).

Another option that the GUI folks have is "exporting" their HTML output 
to a file with a .txt or .doc extension.  This export process reverses 
any HTML encoding that was done when the traffic was generated.  Now 
that we're putting these tags in, we need to pull them out, and replace 
the starting tag by a single space - this will preserve the columnar 
formatting of the original output.

I think the unstring-then-string solution may be the most 
straightforward, although I had considered others (which went into a 
continuous loop!), and I may try two or three of them.

> A small
> twist to this, is whether you would also want to convert any
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> you say it is necessary.

No, we need all the embedded spaces.  :)  Thanks again to everyone for 
the great help...
```

#### ↳ Re: Inspect & differing lengths

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-16T11:29:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OAo%a.8382$Ih1.2770824@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<vjqn6s2vjn7c8b@corp.supernews.com>`

```
> I have a fixed set of characters (41 of 'em) that I'm trying to
> eliminate in a string.  I want to replace them with a single space,
> thereby shrinking up the original string.

'Shrinking'  an INSPECTed string is impossible. That's asking for a change
in the PICTURE clause at run time.. except the PICTURE clause only has
meaning at compile time.

If an INSPECTed string has a PICTURE clause of X(41) or X(123), it will
always be 41 or 123 characters in length regardless of its contents.

I think you are actually looking for the REARRANGE verb. (But don't look too
hard, because that doesn't exist).

MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Inspect & differing lengths

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-16T11:35:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2Go%a.100975$3o3.7028657@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<vjqn6s2vjn7c8b@corp.supernews.com> <OAo%a.8382$Ih1.2770824@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```

"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:OAo%a.8382$Ih1.2770824@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com...
| > I have a fixed set of characters (41 of 'em) that I'm trying to
| > eliminate in a string.  I want to replace them with a single space,
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
| I think you are actually looking for the REARRANGE verb. (But don't look
too
| hard, because that doesn't exist).

No he's looking for the INSPECT COMPRESSING.

INSPECT MY-STRING
 COMPRESSING ALL SPACES TO SPACE
END-INSPECT
```

#### ↳ Re: Inspect & differing lengths

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-16T11:31:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gCo%a.100969$3o3.7028917@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<vjqn6s2vjn7c8b@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"LX-i" <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message
news:vjqn6s2vjn7c8b@corp.supernews.com...
| Good afternoon.
|
| I ran into a problem today, and I'm curious to hear from some of the J4
| folks around here if I'm doing something wrong, and if so, why it is
wrong.
|
| I have a fixed set of characters (41 of 'em) that I'm trying to
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
| to Move (truncation / space filling)?

1. Inspect will not compress the string.
2. Are you removing characters, or a phrase?

What you may want to do is:
Convert the characters or string to spaces.
Have a general purpose string compression routine (re-use) to remove
multiple spaces.

Be careful:

01  MSG.
    05  PIC X(60)  VALUE    'VERB IS THE VERBIAGE'.

INSPECT MESSAGE-AREA  REPLACING ALL 'VERB'  BY 'MOVE'.
Produces:
"MOVE IS THE MOVEIAGE"
```

#### ↳ Re: Inspect & differing lengths

- **From:** "ShadowFox" <me@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-17T11:10:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zoJ%a.135327$cF.35247@rwcrnsc53>`
- **References:** `<vjqn6s2vjn7c8b@corp.supernews.com>`

```
You could have 41 statemants of
 Inspect My-String Replacing All "v" by " ".
 Inspect My-String Replacing All "l" by " ". ETC:
You could have one
  Inspect My-String converting
      'abcdefghijk' to 'ABCDEFGHIJK'.
This would be a lower to UPPER case conversion.
In your case the the first string would be your 41 characters
and the second string woud be all spaces 41 of them.
Enjoy Bob (kobolkid)

<a id="t-35a91871f7"></a>
## Thread: Compressing a string. — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Compressing a string.

- **From:** "Anon" <anon@anon.org>
- **Date:** 2003-08-17T12:28:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qsycnfLKL7_NJqKiXTWQkQ@giganews.com>`

```
Okay this thread was interesting so I wrote a little test program.
I tried to be a bit dynamic so it would replace multiple
occurrences (I chose 2 to test), and strings of varying lengths.
I tested this using MicroFocus Workbench 4.0.38 and the ANS85
compiler option. This worked pretty well except for one thing.
The reference modification (1:string-length) in the 'inspect
replacing' did not work as expected. Even though animator
showed that string-length resolved as 3 bytes it replaced starting
at the first 'abc' the following 20 bytes with high-values (ie the
picture length of 'inspect-string'). In other words, it appears to
have located (1:3) accurately but replaced 20. When I hard coded
'inspect-string (1:3)' the replacement occurred only on the
expected 3 bytes. I think this is a compiler bug but I'd like
anyone's opinion. Otherwise, it worked successfully.




       identification division.
       program-id. test1.
       environment division.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01  in-area  pic x(41) value 'This isabcmy testabcarea'.
       01  inspect-string pic x(20).
       01  string-length  pic 99.
       01  tc             pic 99.
       01  unstring-area.
           05 us-1 pic x(41).
           05 us-2 pic x(41).
           05 us-3 pic x(41).

       procedure division.
      *--> get inspect string from whatever source
      *--> using literal for test program.
           move low-values to inspect-string
           string 'abc' delimited by size into inspect-string
           move zero to string-length
           inspect inspect-string tallying string-length
             for characters before initial low-value
           inspect in-area
             replacing all inspect-string (1:string-length)
             by high-values
           move spaces to unstring-area.
           unstring in-area delimited by all high-values
             into us-1 us-2 us-3
           move zeros to tc
           inspect function reverse (us-1)
             tallying tc for leading spaces
           move low-values to us-1 (length of us-1 - tc + 1:)
           move zeros to tc
           inspect function reverse (us-2)
             tallying tc for leading spaces
           move low-values to us-2 (length of us-2 - tc + 1:)
           move zeros to tc
           inspect function reverse (us-3)
             tallying tc for leading spaces
           move low-values to us-3 (length of us-3 - tc + 1:)
           move spaces to in-area
           string us-1  delimited by low-values
                  space delimited by size
                  us-2  delimited by low-values
                  space delimited by size
                  us-3  delimited by low-values
             into in-area

           goback.
```

#### ↳ Re: Compressing a string.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-17T20:39:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xKR%a.27601$vo2.10763@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<qsycnfLKL7_NJqKiXTWQkQ@giganews.com>`

```
What do you think is happening when you code:

           move low-values to inspect-string
           string 'abc' delimited by size into inspect-string
           move zero to string-length
           inspect inspect-string tallying string-length
             for characters before initial low-value

Do you think that (after the STRING) inspect-string includes "abc" followed
by LOW-VALUES?  I don't think so (although I haven't tried it yet).  I think
that "abc" is followed by SPACES, i.e. the same thing as if you coded

    move 'abc' to inspect-string

I believe (but haven't verified this in the LRM yet) that the rules of
STRING are that the ENTIRE receiving item is used "just like" a receiving
item in a MOVE statement.  Therefore, your inspect tallying will come up
with a length of the ENTIRE inspect-string and the results you get are the
results you should expect.

If someone else "looks at" the rules for STRING and finds out that I am
wrong, please correct me here (and I may still look at the documentation
after posting this).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Compressing a string.

- **From:** "Anon" <anon@anon.org>
- **Date:** 2003-08-17T23:01:46-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sOKdneZRZ50x0t2iXTWQlg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<qsycnfLKL7_NJqKiXTWQkQ@giganews.com> <xKR%a.27601$vo2.10763@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`

```
> What do you think is happening when you code:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> move 'abc' to inspect-string.

I think you are wrong and yes that is what I think happens.
That is what happens with the MF compiler and what happens with mainframe
Enterprise COBOL as well. As the Enterprise COBOL manual clearly states:

Characters from the sending fields are transferred to the receiving fields in the following manner:

  a.. For national sending fields, data is transferred using the rules for elementary national-to-national moves, except
that no space filling takes place;

  b.. For DBCS sending fields, data is transferred using the rules for DBCS-to-DBCS elementary moves, except that no
space filling takes place;

  c.. Otherwise, data is transferred to the receiving fields using the rules for alphanumeric-to-alphanumeric elementary
moves, except that no space filling takes place.
>
> I believe (but haven't verified this in the LRM yet) that the rules of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> after posting this).

Not true. See documentation above. String and move are not equivalent.
You ALWAYS MUST initialize a string receiving field first because the entire
field may not be filled.

05  inspect-string pic x(20).
string 'abc' delimited by size into inspect-string.
Only 3 bytes are moved, the remaining 17 are whatever was there.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Compressing a string.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-18T04:13:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CnY%a.22033$BC2.8765@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<qsycnfLKL7_NJqKiXTWQkQ@giganews.com> <xKR%a.27601$vo2.10763@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net> <sOKdneZRZ50x0t2iXTWQlg@giganews.com>`

```
Yes, I "sit" corrected.  The more relevant part of:


http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGY3LR10/6.2.37.6

is the statement,

"After STRING statement execution is completed, only that part of the
receiving field into which data was transferred is changed. The rest of the
receiving field contains the data that was present before this execution of
the STRING statement.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Compressing a string.

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-18T06:25:49-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vk1du447dpqhb7@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<xKR%a.27601$vo2.10763@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<qsycnfLKL7_NJqKiXTWQkQ@giganews.com> <xKR%a.27601$vo2.10763@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

> What do you think is happening when you code:
> 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> results you should expect.

I don't know about the rules for String (though I'm going to go look), 
but this is certainly consistent with the behavior I've observed with 
our compiler using String.  I usually move spaces to the receiving item 
before stringing (unless I know that the item is spaces already).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Compressing a string.

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-18T06:33:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vk1ebiljnj7f7d@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<xKR%a.27601$vo2.10763@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<qsycnfLKL7_NJqKiXTWQkQ@giganews.com> <xKR%a.27601$vo2.10763@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

> I believe (but haven't verified this in the LRM yet) that the rules of
> STRING are that the ENTIRE receiving item is used "just like" a receiving
> item in a MOVE statement.  Therefore, your inspect tallying will come up
> with a length of the ENTIRE inspect-string and the results you get are the
> results you should expect.

The behavior (behaviour) he describes is intended.  An excerpt from the 
2002 standard, section 14.8.39.3, paragraph 3a, states...

"Characters from literal-1 or from the content of the data item 
referenced by identifier-1 are transferred to the data item referenced 
by identifier-3 in accordance with the MOVE statement rules for 
alphanumeric-to-alphanumeric moves or, when identifier-3 is of class 
national, national-to-national moves, except that no space filling is 
provided. If an identifier-1 references a zero-length item, that sending
operand is ignored."

Note the "no space filling is provided" phrase - that's where it 
differs.  :)
```

#### ↳ Re: Compressing a string.

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-17T15:44:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308171444.d042a53@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<qsycnfLKL7_NJqKiXTWQkQ@giganews.com>`

```
"Anon" <anon@anon.org> wrote 

>        procedure division.
>       *--> get inspect string from whatever source
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>            move low-values to us-1 (length of us-1 - tc + 1:)

You can do this automatically using the COUNT clause of UNSTRING to
give the length of the field that was output.

>            move zeros to tc
>            inspect function reverse (us-2)
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>            goback.

You could also do this in a loop using 'pointer's:

         MOVE SPACES TO Out-Area
         MOVE 1      TO In-Point
         MOVE 1      TO Out-Point
         PERFORM 
             UNTIL In-Point > <length of in-area>

             MOVE SPACES     TO US-1
             UNSTRING In-Area
                 DELIMITED BY <some terminator>
                 INTO US-1 COUNT IN TC
                 WITH POINTER In-Point
             MOVE LOW-VALUE  TO US-1(TC+1:1)
             
             STRING US-1  DELIMITED BY LOW-VALUE
                 INTO Out-Area
                 WITH POINTER Out-Point          END-PERFORM 
This would cater for any number of copies of <some terminator>.

However it seems that you want to remove blocks of the form
'<something>' in which case you may want to use the loop and have two
UNSTRINGs in it, one looking fof '<' and outputting to the out-string,
another delimited by '>' which discards the result but increments the
POINTER.  This way you won't care about what is between the '<' and
'>'.
```

#### ↳ Re: Compressing a string.

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-18T12:37:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QL30b.104550$0v4.7320148@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<qsycnfLKL7_NJqKiXTWQkQ@giganews.com>`

```
It seemed interesting to me also.
Here is another sample, different method.
I address removing multiple spaces, after the offending characters, or
strings are removed.


       01  STRC-COMPRESS-STRING-WORK.

           05  STRC-STRING-IN          PIC X(100).
           05  STRC-STRING-OUT         PIC X(100).

           05  STRC-IN-LEN             PIC 9(4)  BINARY  value 41.
           05  STRC-IN-POS             PIC 9(4)  BINARY.
           05  STRC-OUT-POS            PIC 9(4)  BINARY.

           MOVE  SPACES  TO  STRC-STRING-OUT (1:STRC-IN-LEN)

           MOVE  1  TO  STRC-IN-POS
                        STRC-OUT-POS
           PERFORM  UNTIL  STRC-IN-POS  >  STRC-IN-LEN
             PERFORM  VARYING STRC-IN-POS  FROM  STRC-IN-POS  BY  1
                        UNTIL STRC-IN-POS  >  STRC-IN-LEN
                           OR STRC-STRING-IN (STRC-IN-POS:1) = SPACE
                MOVE  STRC-STRING-IN  (STRC-IN-POS:1)
                  TO  STRC-STRING-OUT (STRC-OUT-POS:1)
                ADD  1  TO  STRC-OUT-POS
             END-PERFORM
             IF  STRC-IN-POS <  STRC-IN-LEN
                 MOVE  SPACE
                   TO  STRC-STRING-OUT (STRC-OUT-POS:1)
                  ADD  1  TO  STRC-OUT-POS
             END-IF
             PERFORM  VARYING STRC-IN-POS  FROM  STRC-IN-POS  BY  1
                        UNTIL STRC-IN-POS  >  STRC-IN-LEN
                           OR STRC-STRING-IN (STRC-IN-POS:1) NOT = SPACE
               CONTINUE
             END-PERFORM
           END-PERFORM
```

#### ↳ Re: Compressing a string.

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-18T12:42:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_Q30b.104555$0v4.7320532@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<qsycnfLKL7_NJqKiXTWQkQ@giganews.com>`

```
It seemed interesting to me also.
Here is another sample, different method.
I address removing multiple spaces, after the offending characters, or
strings are removed.


       01  STRC-COMPRESS-STRING-WORK.

           05  STRC-STRING-IN          PIC X(100).
           05  STRC-STRING-OUT         PIC X(100).

           05  STRC-IN-LEN             PIC 9(4)  BINARY  value 41.
           05  STRC-IN-POS             PIC 9(4)  BINARY.
           05  STRC-OUT-POS            PIC 9(4)  BINARY.

           MOVE  SPACES  TO  STRC-STRING-OUT (1:STRC-IN-LEN)

           MOVE  1  TO  STRC-IN-POS
                        STRC-OUT-POS
           PERFORM  UNTIL  STRC-IN-POS  >  STRC-IN-LEN
             PERFORM  VARYING STRC-IN-POS  FROM  STRC-IN-POS  BY  1
                        UNTIL STRC-IN-POS  >  STRC-IN-LEN
                           OR STRC-STRING-IN (STRC-IN-POS:1) = SPACE
                MOVE  STRC-STRING-IN  (STRC-IN-POS:1)
                  TO  STRC-STRING-OUT (STRC-OUT-POS:1)
                ADD  1  TO  STRC-OUT-POS
             END-PERFORM
             PERFORM  VARYING STRC-IN-POS  FROM  STRC-IN-POS  BY  1
                        UNTIL STRC-IN-POS  >  STRC-IN-LEN
                           OR STRC-STRING-IN (STRC-IN-POS:1) NOT = SPACE
               CONTINUE
             END-PERFORM
             ADD  1  TO  STRC-OUT-POS
           END-PERFORM
```

## Thread: Cobol Copybook mapper. — 8 msgs

### Cobol Copybook mapper.

- **From:** "Ron Davis" <rdavis@ozemail.com.au>
- **Date:** 2003-08-18T11:17:26+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TOV%a.25$Cn1.2292@nnrp1.ozemail.com.au>`

```


Hi

I'm searching for a facility which will take a COBOLcopybook and produce
storage map from it (similar to the Record Print Facility of FileAid, which
I'm ditching at my site) This will be used in conjunction for COBOL For
OS/390 at a mainframe site.

It need to handle arrays (tables) and redefines etc.  In the case of tables
it should print out a record for each table element, eg, 05 ELEMENT(3,3)
PIC XXX  and the offset from the start of the Level 1 for that structure.

Any help would be appreciated, especially by email to
ron.davis@dva.gov.au...

I'm trying to write one but it's proving a real pig!

Cheers

Ron Davis
```

I think it only maps the first occurrence item/group of an array,
but I'm pretty sure that it remaps the redefines for you.
I remember cutting and pasting some of them into my PDS
to help me with problem diagnosis, and to include in specs.

Ron,

I think Hugh's advice is good.

It's a long time ago, and I may be confusing compilers, but you could try
OPTION SYM, MAP.

(I think it provides the generated Assembler code and all the DSECTs. (these
are what you need ?)).

I have something that does a map of Record layouts (I wrote it myself) but
without knowing exactly what you are hoping to get and in what format, I
don't know how suitable it would be.

Try the compiler options; at least they won't cost you anything...<G>.

Pete

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Copybook mapper.

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

```
"Ron Davis" <rdavis@ozemail.com.au> wrote in message
news:TOV%a.25$Cn1.2292@nnrp1.ozemail.com.au...
> It need to handle arrays (tables) and redefines etc.  In the case of
tables
> it should print out a record for each table element, eg, 05 ELEMENT(3,3)
> PIC XXX  and the offset from the start of the Level 1 for that structure.

It's old, it's ugly, it runs on a PC under MS-DOS not OS/390, but....

The price is right (free), but it works and the offsets from the start of
each '01' are in decimal rather than hex. (Hmm, if you're familar with IBM
mainframe software, "ugly but it works" should make you feel right at home).

Get file COBFD.ZIP from the Flexus download page at
http://www.flexus.com/download.html  or just use the direct link:
ftp://ftp.flexus.com/cobfd.zip

(Yes, I have been threatening for several years to update this to a
Windows(r) version. Maybe 2003 will be the year).

And please speak kindly of me ...
```
