#### ↳ Re: Cobol Copybook mapper.

- **From:** Alain Feler <member15854@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-18T08:30:42-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3255531.1061209842@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<TOV%a.25$Cn1.2292@nnrp1.ozemail.com.au>`

```

I found this somewhere (on CobolReport.com probably).

It is free, and it works. Here is the doc, cut a bit to stay below the
10,000 bytes limit of dbForum:





                   Tal Systems' COBOL FD Analyzer - COBFD

                            Version 1.50 August 1999

                      by Michael Mattias dba Tal Systems



   COBFD is a tool for COBOL programmers. Very simply, what it
   does is parse

   a COBOL source file (either a copylib or complete source),
   identifies all

   group and  elementary data items, then breaks down all '01' and
   subordinate

   group items into their components and produces a report file
   showing the

   size in bytes and the offset within the '01' record of every
   subordinate

   data item. All group and elementary data items are included.
   The user has

   an option to include the input file in the report output (follows
   analysis).



   COBFD supports all levels of REDEFINES and OCCURS: REDEFINES
   which OCCUR,

   OCCURS which OCCUR while REDEFINE'ing an item which OCCURS; OCCUR'ing

   group items with mixed data types. These are typically the greatest

   challenges to counting bytes "by hand".



   COBFD runs on MS-DOS and compatible PC's and requires DOS 2.0
   or higher.

   It will run in a DOS session under Windows(r).It is a single user

   program; there is no provision for sharing files, but the program has

   been run on a network.



   The program creates and deletes an internal workfile, COBFD@@.WRK, in

   the  user's current directory.



   Both the input and output files are user-specified at run time.
   The output

   file is 80 bytes per record, formatted, with page headers (all text).

   The user must provide a file viewer/printer for screen or
   printed output.



   COBFD is [.....] intended as advertising for the author.



   To use the COBFD program interactively:



      COBFD <Enter>



   You will be given a screen with boxes for input file and output file.

   (The mouse is not enabled on this screen).



   To use the COBFD program from the command line or in a batch
   file, enter

   four parameters separated by semicolons, commas or spaces:



   [drive]:[path]COBFD
   SourceFile;OutputFile;IncludeSource;Append;CompStorage



   SourceFile     = Cobol input file, drive/path optional

   OutputFile     = Disk File for report, drive/path optional

   IncludeSource  = Y or N (any other character is an error)

   Append         = Y will append the report to the specified
   output file

                    if found, or create a new output file if not found.

                    N will always open the report file OUTPUT, erasing

                    any previous report file.

   CompStorage    = Used for COMP, COMP-4 and COMP-5 only. B is
   byte storage;

                    storage is based on the minimum bumber of whole
                    bytes

                    required. W is Word storage; data sizes are rounded

                    up to the next 2, 4 or 8 bytes.

                    For reference: IBM mainframe COBOL uses WORD
                    storage;

                    Microfoucus Personal Cobol for MS-DOS uses BYTE
                    storage.

                    Consult your COBOL system manual and/or compile
                    scripts

                    for the option used with your programs.



   COBFD /H   or COBFD /?

   Displays the syntax for the command-line options.



   Sample Batch files:



   COBFD  File1.cbl;Report1.txt;Y;N;W

   COBFD  File2.Cbl;Report1.txt;Y;Y:B



   Another batch file:



   IF exist LIBFILE.TXT del libfile.txt

   FOR %%F IN (*.cbl) DO COBFD %%F;LIBFILE.TXT;Y;Y;W



   Print one report file, LIBFILE.TXT, containing all *.cbl files in

   the current directory.(word storage)



   What Tal Systems' COBFD COBOL FD Analyzer assumes:



   A valid COBOL FD. If PICTURE clauses are missing or illegal,
   sentences are

   not terminated with a period, or levels are inconsistent, the program

   will return unreliable data, generally without a warning.



   If column seven (7) of the input file is non-blank, the line is
   a comment.



   All PICTURE, OCCURS, REDEFINES, USAGE DISPLAY|COMP|SIGN
   SEPARATE and other

   COBOL keywords appear in columns 13-72 . These clauses may,
   however, span

   multiple physical lines with comment lines interspersed. The
   order in which

   keywords appear is immaterial. A COBOL sentence is not
   terminated until

   a non-quoted period is found.



   The QUOTE character is the first double-quote (") or apostrophe (')
   found

   in a non-comment input record. The QUOTE character is redefined for
   each

   file processed during a program run; i.e., the user can process
   multiple

   files, some using quote and others using apostrophe as the literal
   delimiter.



   A REDEFINES clause below the '01' level assumes that the area being

   redefined is the same size as the redefined area. (Not an ANSI COBOL

   requirement). If this is not true, the program will appear to finish

   normally, but the data may be erroneous. REDEFINES is ignored at the

   '01' dataname level.



   When parsing the input file, if the first two words in a non-
   comment line

   are PROCEDURE and DIVISION, the program stops looking for data;
   however,

   if printing the input file, it prints the entire file.



   RENAMES ('66-level') clauses are not supported



   SYNC clauses are not supported.



   PICTURE clauses containing "P" (scaled numeric) are not supported.



   POINTER and PROCEDURE POINTER data items are not supported



   USAGE BINARY is considered USAGE COMPUTATIONAL.



   USAGE PACKED-DECIMAL is considered USAGE COMPUTATIONAL-3



   COMP, COMP-4 AND COMP-5 are used for Binary integers. COMP-3 is

   Binardy-coded-decimal, often called "packed."



   Only EJECT, SKIP1, SKIP2, COPY and ++INCLUDE are recognized as

   compiler directives (treated as comments). Other directives may

   cause the program to insert an extra SYSTEM-GENERATED-01-DATANAME

   in the output following the directive. The offset information should

   not be affected unless the unsupported directive is contained within

   the physical boundaries of an explicit or implicit '01' dataname.



   No form of COPY is supported; but copylibs themselves may be
   analyzed.



   If an '01' record is not found as the first COBOL data item,
   the system

   creates one with the name SYSTEM-GENERATED-01-DATANAME. This
   allows the

   program to be used with copylibs not containing an '01 -level'
   dataname.





   COBFD is a copyright product. It may be freely used and
   distributed without

   payment or registration. However, it may not be distributed for
   monetary

   consideration, either standalone or "bundled" with other utilities,

   applications or instructional material without the permission of the

   copyright owner.



   Distribution to individual programmers is encouraged.



   Copyright 1996, 1998 Michael C. Mattias dba Tal Systems, Racine WI
   USA.



   The author would appreciate any comments, remarks, bug reports, or

   desired enhancements be submitted to:



   Michael C. Mattias dba Tal Systems

   5329 Charles St.

   Racine, WI 53402-2119 USA

   414-681-3895

   EMail  michael.mattias@gte.net



** ENDS **
```

```
talking about an IBM mainframe compiler, you might want to look at the
information in the SYSADATA file (requested by the ADATA compiler option).
Check out the details at:


http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/APPENDIX1.7
```

<a id="t-239545a86a"></a>
## Thread: EOF Technique — 1 this month / 22 total

_Thread started [2003-07](2003-07.md#t-239545a86a)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** codecrafters@cix.compulink.co.uk
- **Date:** 2003-08-18T22:12:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bhrj19$hee$1@thorium.cix.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<bf10ug$oab$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
In article <bf10ug$oab$1@peabody.colorado.edu>, howard@brazee.net (Howard 
Brazee) wrote:

> [snip]
> > "88" levels.
> 
> Which I use.    But we don't have shop and certainly not industry 
> standard 88 levels the way we have IDMS return codes.

A pity really - as I recall the IDMS 88 levels were /very/ well put 
together.  

I can't help feeling that there should be some sort of "standard copy 
library" (think of stdio.h) at, say, the machine/compiler vendor level for 
stuff like this -- the new standards make it easy to use unique names for 
each file, something like:

01   WB-File-Control-Stuff.

Copy STDSEQFS with prefix "WB-IP-Master-". *> I know the syntax is off 
                                           *> but you get the idea.
Which would yield something like

     05  WB-IP-Master-Status   Pic X(002).
         88  WB-IP-Master-Available       Value '00'.
         88  WB-IP-Master-EOF             Value '10'.
     ... etc.     

Of course, the cogent argument against this (on the mainframe platform at 
least - and for sequential files only!) is that a sweeping majority of 
cases just don't need anything cleverer than the AT END clause - the IBM 
diagnostics are actually preferable for the error cases, because (a) we 
all know how careful people tend to be when coding error messages 
(i.e. not at all, and they don't test the damned things either) and (b) 
they often present more information than is readily available without 
trawling through control blocks.

Expanding on that thought, it's a pity we can't tell COBOL which cockups 
we want to deal with and which ones we're happy to leave to the runtime to 
handle - preferably in some _very_ informative and obvious way (like 
ABending after producing a helpful message). Let's face it, 99% of 
programs need only know about the cases which are admirably dealt with by 
AT END and INVALID KEY (and their mirror images, obviously) - anything 
else can (and to my mind _should_) usefully be left to the runtime. Much 
easier to deal with those 3am phone calls if the error messages are at 
least consistent...

Any thoughts?       

Pete.

[codecrafters@cix.compulink.co.uk]
```

---

<a id="t-fa4a9e7077"></a>
## Thread: The Solution to my Inspect question — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### The Solution to my Inspect question

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-18T17:51:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vk2m2ps2srdq7c@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Here's how I solved the problem...

       *>   Link start tag
        77  WS-Link-Start-Tag             Pic X(41) Value
            '<a href="#" onclick="sendGAGLink(this);">'.

       *>   Hold area for removing tags
        01  WS-Unstring-Hold-Area.
            12  WS-Unstring-Entry    Occurs 20 Times
                                       Indexed by WS-Unstring-Idx
                                          Pic X(250).

        01  WS-Entry-Length-Table.
            12  WS-Entry-Length      Occurs 20 Times
                                       Indexed by WS-Entry-Idx
                                          Pic 9(03).
...
        . 2320-Remove-Anchors.
       *>***************************************************************
       *>* Revmove the anchor tags from GAG links
       *>***************************************************************

            Move 0 to WS-Count

            Unstring WS-Hold-Out-Line
              Delimited by WS-Link-Start-Tag Or "</a>"
                into WS-Unstring-Entry  (1)  Count WS-Entry-Length  (1)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry  (2)  Count WS-Entry-Length  (2)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry  (3)  Count WS-Entry-Length  (3)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry  (4)  Count WS-Entry-Length  (4)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry  (5)  Count WS-Entry-Length  (5)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry  (6)  Count WS-Entry-Length  (6)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry  (7)  Count WS-Entry-Length  (7)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry  (8)  Count WS-Entry-Length  (8)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry  (9)  Count WS-Entry-Length  (9)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry (10)  Count WS-Entry-Length (10)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry (11)  Count WS-Entry-Length (11)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry (12)  Count WS-Entry-Length (12)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry (13)  Count WS-Entry-Length (13)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry (14)  Count WS-Entry-Length (14)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry (15)  Count WS-Entry-Length (15)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry (16)  Count WS-Entry-Length (16)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry (17)  Count WS-Entry-Length (17)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry (18)  Count WS-Entry-Length (18)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry (19)  Count WS-Entry-Length (19)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry (20)  Count WS-Entry-Length (20)
                Tallying WS-Count
            End-Unstring

            Move 1      to WS-Out-Idx
            Move Spaces to WS-Hold-Out-Line

            Perform Varying WS-Out-Nbr from 1 by 1
              Until WS-Out-Nbr > WS-Count

                String WS-Unstring-Entry (WS-Out-Nbr)
                         (1:WS-Entry-Length (WS-Out-Nbr))
                       " "
                   Delimited by Size into WS-Hold-Out-Line
                     Pointer WS-Out-Idx
                End-String

            End-Perform
...

Yes, I know I never used the table indexes...  Oh well.  :)

(p.s. - I sure am glad a certain fowl is not a regular denizen of 
c.l.cobol..)
```

#### ↳ Re: The Solution to my Inspect question

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <hugh.candlin@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-18T23:21:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ncd0b.105141$0v4.7366761@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<vk2m2ps2srdq7c@corp.supernews.com>`

```

LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message
news:vk2m2ps2srdq7c@corp.supernews.com...
> Here's how I solved the problem...
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>             Unstring WS-Hold-Out-Line

The example is hard to follow, with this WS entry definition omitted.

>               Delimited by WS-Link-Start-Tag Or "</a>"
>                 into WS-Unstring-Entry  (1)  Count WS-Entry-Length  (1)
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>             End-Unstring

The 41-byte string can not appear more than once
in each 250 bytes?  I thought you said it could.
>
>             Move 1      to WS-Out-Idx

Shouldn't this be a SET statement?

>             Move Spaces to WS-Hold-Out-Line
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yes, I know I never used the table indexes...  Oh well.  :)

Now, how do you plan to expand it again?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: The Solution to my Inspect question

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-18T20:31:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vk2vfdfm2ess6d@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<ncd0b.105141$0v4.7366761@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<vk2m2ps2srdq7c@corp.supernews.com> <ncd0b.105141$0v4.7366761@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Hugh Candlin wrote:

> LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message
> news:vk2m2ps2srdq7c@corp.supernews.com...
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> The example is hard to follow, with this WS entry definition omitted.

Sorry - Pic X(500).

>>              Delimited by WS-Link-Start-Tag Or "</a>"
>>                into WS-Unstring-Entry  (1)  Count WS-Entry-Length  (1)
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> in each 250 bytes?  I thought you said it could.

It can.  I split the difference in half, figuring that if there was a 
link, it would be in the first 250 characters.  :)  That will handle up 
to 10 <a blah..></a> occurrences (which is a total of 450 of the 500 
characters - I don't think we'll ever have 10, as most of these output 
are still only originally 80 columns).

>>            Move 1      to WS-Out-Idx
> 
> 
> Shouldn't this be a SET statement?

Omitted that one too - it's Pic 9(10) Binary.

>>            Move Spaces to WS-Hold-Out-Line
>>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Now, how do you plan to expand it again?

I don't.  It's stored with the links when it's created, and this is only 
run if people want to convert it to "plain text".  We leave the original 
version in the database (unless they explicitly release it), so if they 
want a linked version, we just display what's stored.
```

#### ↳ Re: The Solution to my Inspect question

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-18T19:53:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<y8mcnWQTypFv6dyiXTWJhA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<vk2m2ps2srdq7c@corp.supernews.com>`

```
LX-i wrote:
> Here's how I solved the problem...
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> *>***************************************************************
Move 0 to WS-Count

            Unstring WS-Hold-Out-Line
              Delimited by WS-Link-Start-Tag Or "</a>"
                into WS-Unstring-Entry  (1)  Count WS-Entry-Length  (1)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry  (2)  Count WS-Entry-Length  (2)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry  (3)  Count WS-Entry-Length  (3)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry  (4)  Count WS-Entry-Length  (4)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry  (5)  Count WS-Entry-Length  (5)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry  (6)  Count WS-Entry-Length  (6)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry  (7)  Count WS-Entry-Length  (7)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry  (8)  Count WS-Entry-Length  (8)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry  (9)  Count WS-Entry-Length  (9)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry (10)  Count WS-Entry-Length (10)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry (11)  Count WS-Entry-Length (11)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry (12)  Count WS-Entry-Length (12)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry (13)  Count WS-Entry-Length (13)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry (14)  Count WS-Entry-Length (14)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry (15)  Count WS-Entry-Length (15)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry (16)  Count WS-Entry-Length (16)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry (17)  Count WS-Entry-Length (17)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry (18)  Count WS-Entry-Length (18)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry (19)  Count WS-Entry-Length (19)
                     WS-Unstring-Entry (20)  Count WS-Entry-Length (20)
                Tallying WS-Count
            End-Unstring

Better:
MOVE 1 TO UNSTRING-PTR
PERFORM VARYING WS-INDX FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL WS-INDX > 20
   UNSTRING WS-Hold-Out-Line
      Delimited by WS-Link-Start-Tag OR "<a>"
         INTO WS-Unstring-Entry(WS-INDX) COUNT WS-Entry-Length(WS-INDX)
         WITH POINTER UNSTRING-PTR
         Tallying WS-Count
   END-PERFORM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: The Solution to my Inspect question

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-18T22:36:54-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308182136.5ec147f3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<vk2m2ps2srdq7c@corp.supernews.com> <y8mcnWQTypFv6dyiXTWJhA@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote 

> Better:
> MOVE 1 TO UNSTRING-PTR
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>    END-PERFORM

Even better (IMHO):
    MOVE ALL SPACES    TO WS-Unstring-Hold-Area
    MOVE ALL ZEROS     TO WS-Entry-Length-Table
    MOVE 1             TO UNSTRING-PTR
    PERFORM VARYING WS-INDX FROM 1 BY 1 
        UNTIL WS-INDX > 20 
           OR UNSTRING-PTR > <lengthofws-hold-out-line>

        UNSTRING WS-Hold-Out-Line
            Delimited by "<"
            INTO WS-Unstring-Entry(WS-INDX) 
            COUNT WS-Entry-Length(WS-INDX)
            WITH POINTER UNSTRING-PTR
            Tallying WS-Count
    
        UNSTRING WS-Hold-Out-Line
            Delimited by ">"
            INTO WS-Junk 
            WITH POINTER UNSTRING-PTR

    END-PERFORM
```

#### ↳ Re: The Solution to my Inspect question

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-19T12:11:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308191111.37fb867c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<vk2m2ps2srdq7c@corp.supernews.com>`

```
LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote 

You may also want to check whether any character conversions occured
when they added the HTML to the text.  Characters such as '<', '>',
'&', are special signals in HTML and if they are required (or exist)
in text they should be represented as '&lt;' '&gt;' '&amp;'.

Typically these (and several others) would be replaced before the tags
were added.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: The Solution to my Inspect question

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-19T19:59:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vk5i093msbjo69@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<217e491a.0308191111.37fb867c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<vk2m2ps2srdq7c@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0308191111.37fb867c@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard wrote:

> LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote 
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> were added.

Actually, that does happen later in the code.  Remember that XML/HTML 
encoder/decoder I posted a few weeks back?  It's being used on the line 
(after the unstring and re-string) to replace them with their actual 
characters.  (That's why I used the &#nn; format - I can just strip the 
&, #, and ;, add 1, and use Function Char to put it back.  :>  Plus, the 
fact that it works for both HTML and XML is a plus...)

Thanks for the heads-up, though - it would truly suck if I were to leave 
those entities in the output...  :)
```

```
to fax reports out, AND be able to comfirm that they got sent.  PC platform,
MS compiler.

Donald
```

#### ↳ Re: Faxing from Cobol

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-08-21T13:00:52+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f441a9d_7@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<8iu0b.4$c_.298@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

"Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:8iu0b.4$c_.298@news20.bellglobal.com...
> Has anybody an recomendations for fax server software? What we want to do
is
> to fax reports out, AND be able to comfirm that they got sent.  PC
platform,
> MS compiler.
>
> Donald
>

As you would expect, I use an ActiveX for this. (Simply invoke methods to
send, receive, and check status.)

You can get excellent componets from $99.00 upwards, that do everything you
want and a lot more besides.

Check out this link:
http://www.componentsource.com/Search.asp?fl=&bc=&sc=CS&gf=+AUTHORA1COMP&gd=A1+Computer+Software&mc=&sr=FAX&ec=Y&go=Search&pp=10&pc=Y&ap=Y&ss=Y&sd=&ab=500

If you really don't want the component approach, have you considered simply
treating FAX as another printer and just selecting the fax software as the
printer, from COBOL, then doing a normal print? (Obviously, I am assuming
you are interested in sending only...?)

I'm not sure how you would check the status as it is unlikely to return the
usual print status codes... might be worth trying.

The standard FAX software that comes with XP is very good in this regard and
I have dropped several of the FAX packages I used to use.

Are you serious about "MS Compiler"?  I thought you were on MicroFocus...

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Faxing from Cobol

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-21T12:13:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308211113.5de4b7cc@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8iu0b.4$c_.298@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
"Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca> wrote 

> Has anybody an recomendations for fax server software? What we want to do
> is to fax reports out, AND be able to comfirm that they got sent.  

Faxing reports out is relatively easy. I still have a couple of smart
fax modems that are accessed as a serial text printer from DOS or,
well anything actully, and if the first thing is 'FAX 1234567' then it
is faxed to that number, if not it is sent through to the printer that
is connected to it.

There have been FAX 'print drivers' for Windows since 3.0. Some of
these, however, tend to need manual intervention of some sort, such as
selecting where to go.

The really hard part is 'confirm they got sent' if you want the
_program_ to know.  Obviously the user can check the print queue for
what is still there.  'Confirming' that it actually resulted in black
marks on paper at the destination is impossible.  There is no way to
determine if the fax machine ran out of ink (where used) or paper or
the whole thing was thrown in the bit bucket.

> PC platform, MS compiler.

The last _MS_ Compiler released was 5.0, which was roughly equivalent
to MF 3.0 - and could do MS-DOS and Windows 3.x 16 bit programs (MS
had ripped out the OS/2 support which was in MF and MS 4.5).

The 'PC platform' may be some form of MS-DOS or compatible (I still
have some use of Multiuser-DOS/Real32) or Windows 3.0 or later.

You may need to be more specific to get a _useful_ answer.  Is it
MS-DOS programs or Windows 3.x ?
```

```
Donald
```

#### ↳ Re: Faxing from Cobol

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-08-22T00:57:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f4561bd.183134090@news.central.cox.net>`
- **References:** `<8iu0b.4$c_.298@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
"Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

>Has anybody an recomendations for fax server software? What we want to do is
>to fax reports out, AND be able to comfirm that they got sent.  PC platform,
>MS compiler.

A possible solution is to use efax.com. You can format the report in any of
15-20 formats including .txt and .doc, email it to efax. They will send it to
the user's fax machine or email it in their highly compressed format, and send
you a confirmation report.

Printing on a fax machine is expensive. The user might prefer to receive it as
email so (s)he can print it on regular printer. Having a file also simplifies
archiving. Fax output tends to get lost/trashed. I've had to send important
documents two and three times.Your request for confirmations indicates you want
to eliminate lost reports, yet you've chosen the least reliable delivery
mechanism. Email will eliminate lost reports much more effectively than
confirmations. 

The user can Annotate an emailed image and send it back to you, just like a fax.
I email my time sheet to my manager in efax format. (S)he signs it
electronically with a signature image and emails it back to me. Then I send the
signed timesheet to my contracting company. The process is completely paperless
until it reaches the contracting company and timesheets never get lost. It takes
less than an hour from beginning to end. When I was faxing timesheets, they had
three opportunities to get lost .. and were lost more than half the time.
```

<a id="t-4530779882"></a>
## Thread: WinAPI Sleep function and a ".DLL Browser". — 20 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### WinAPI Sleep function and a ".DLL Browser".

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-08-20T23:53:28+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f436210_3@news.athenanews.com>`

```
I have a need to use the Windows API "Sleep" function.

Having coded many API Calls for a number of different functions, I was
pretty confident about this and wasn't expecting what happened  <G>...

It is part of the Kernel32 Lib, so, as such, it doesn't need linking with
any special libraries, or exports.

I coded the folllowing:

   call "Sleep" with STDCALL
                     using by value 2000
   end-call

The 2000 is a value in milliseconds of how long you want the thread
suspended for (in my case, 2 seconds, while some error recovery is carried
out and OO instances are garbage collected in an adjacent thread) and it is
then "safe" to retry the activity that had a problem.

The function returns no value. It is really as simple as it could be.

But I can't get it to link...<G>

I get an unresolved external "SLEEP@4" every time...

I tried using the Fujitsu ALPHAL(WORD) option which I seem to remember was
required but then EVERYTHING fell apart and COPY libraries could not be
found, with all the knock on errors this generates... As this option simply
translates stuff to upper case I tried coding the call in Upper case...still
unresolved.

I am starting to wonder whether the Fujitsu supplied Kernel32.lib actually
contains this entry point....

And this brings me to my second point...

Does anybody have, or use, or is able to recommend a tool for looking at
.DLLs to check the SYMBOL Table and the entry points or whatever is in
there? I've been doing this with a hex editor but it is pretty tedious and
I'd rather have something like an Object Browser, but for .DLLs. (The
Fujitsu Object Browser needs to have a database of Repository information as
far as I can tell... as I tend to use late binding for most things this
isn't much use to me and it wouldn't work with a non-OO .DLL anyway)

This is a Fujitsu NETCobol V6 program (which has to run in CGI on a Web
Server), running under Win XP Pro for development (so it is the XP Pro
Kernel32 that I have available. (I believe this should be the same as Win 2K
anyway...))

If anyone has any ideas or thoughts or experience on this please share it,
even if you think it might be silly...

I need to get this solved and I don't want to have to write a component that
incorporates an ActiveX interrupt timer as this would really be an overkill.
(Last resort only...)

Thanks in advance,

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: WinAPI Sleep function and a ".DLL Browser".

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-20T12:24:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nMJ0b.9988$Ih1.3693303@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3f436210_3@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3f436210_3@news.athenanews.com...
> I have a need to use the Windows API "Sleep" function.
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> out and OO instances are garbage collected in an adjacent thread) and it
is
> then "safe" to retry the activity that had a problem.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> I get an unresolved external "SLEEP@4" every time...

Sounds like the library definition of the alias is wrong. When the program
is linked, I'll bet the compiler looks in its own definition file for the
alias; that, or it automatically is doing "C" style "alias decoration."  (I
checked my Kernel32.dll and the fi\untion is defnitely exported as "Sleep"
(case sensitive).

If you can't modify whatever file specifies the import alias to statically
link, you can call the function dynamically by..

GetModuleHandle to KERNEL32.DLL   (loaded by all Windows processes)
GetProcAddress of "Sleep"
Call-by-address   the address you get from getprocaddress

If you really get stuck on this, let me know and I'll write you a DLL you
can call from your program which won't have the problem.  (I assume you can
create the necessary import specifications for the compiler if you have a
DLL name and the exported procedure name and parameter count/type).

Free. (Take me 15 mins tops).

Alternately, try changing your CALL to make the delay time a dataname with
correct USAGE instead of a numeric literal.
(CALL "Sleep" WITH STDCALL  USING BY VALUE ws-sleep-milliseconds). Maybe the
compiler really really wants a dataname since you did not specify a SIZE in
your BY VALUE clause.

MCM
```

```
call, but 'copy books falling apart' seems to be a different issue.

Could it be that by inserting the ALPHAL directive you knocked out the "Copy
Folders" location?

Nah, probably not.

Or maybe the ALPHAL directive makes the COPY command case sensitive? COPY
commands in the procedure division don't seem to be affected by upper/lower
casing. Of course we NEVER use copy commands in the data division (using
#INCLUDE instead), so who knows?

I hope you get this solved and report back. This is weirding me out, man.
```

```
Pete,
  Have you tried both ALPHAL(ALL) and ALPHAL(WORD)?

Also *IF* (and I don' t know if you are) you are using the Fujitsu IDE,
there is also the following in the V7 manual,

"Lowercase letters in a library name in the source program are treated as
uppercase letters when compiling the program by specifying compiler option
ALPHAL (ALL) or ALPHAL (WORD). If the library name is specified in lowercase
letters in this dialog box, the designated library file is not read. When
compiling the program by specifying compiler option ALPHAL (ALL) or ALPHAL
(WORD), be sure to use uppercase letters in this dialog box. For details,
see the compiler option "ALPHAL" in Appendix A, "Compiler Options"."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: WinAPI Sleep function and a ".DLL Browser".

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-21T11:05:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BH11b.10508$Ih1.3924735@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3f436210_3@news.athenanews.com> <B4O0b.2361$c_.94178@news20.bellglobal.com> <3f4412f3_3@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3f4412f3_3@news.athenanews.com...
> "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
> news:B4O0b.2361$c_.94178@news20.bellglobal.com...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> changed to Fujitsu.)

The actual name of a procedure "as it is to be called" is found in the
export table of KERNEL32.DLL (or any executable, EXE or DLL).  The name
which the calling program is going to use is in that calling program's
import table.  I'm not really too confident about how the FJ linker works,
but my best guess is that the "definition"  files used by the compiler
convert a "user-friendly API function name" into a DLL name and Procedure
name which are then stored in the compiled module's import table.

Even if the program links, if the calling program cannot find the library
DLLS (e.g., KERNEL32) and the referenced procedure names AS THEY ARE SPELLED
IN THE EXPORT TABLE OF THE LIBRARY DLL, Windows will give the now-famous
"missing a component" message and not even complete loading a program.

 When you see an underscore prepended ("_myfunction") or the stack usage
appended ("sleep4") or the function name uppercased or other decoration done
(_SLEEP@4) it is because the programmer who created the DLL told his/her
compiler to create it that way. Prepending underscores, uppercasing, adding
an "@",suffixing the stack usage and other ''name decoration" is common with
'C' compilers and programmers (who often take the 'default' options).

For the Sleep, and just about all WinAPI calls, the function names have no
underscore, no"@" sign and no stack usage integer. (Although many have
either an "A" or "W" suffixed).

Pete had asked about the tool I use to read the import table. It is a
product called "PEBrowse" which I got off a shareware BBS somewhere. When I
tried to send in my $25.00 to register it, the envelope came back (twice!)
with "party not found" and the phone number is 'not in service.'  (I think
this is an indication of less-than-ideal support availability).

If you can find it, it also can show you all the resources in an executable
(EXE or DLL), the size of the PE sections, etc and a whole bunch of other
geeky stuff. Me, I just use it to get the 'actual' names of exports AND
IMPORTS for various modules.

I'm sure there are some supported things available to do this. PEBrowse has
a bad bug: when there are too many imports, exports or resources in an
executable it GPFs; looks like a table bound bound violation of some kind.

If a BBS search for "+import +export +view +PE" does not turn up anything
which looks right for you, drop me a note and I'll send you
PEBrowse-in-a-zip.  That or I'll clean up this program I have here on which
I stopped work when I found PEBrowse about two years ago. It works for
imports and exports but it's ugly as sin and *only* does imports and exports
whereas PEBrowse does the other stuff mentioned above.

Related but not quite the same: I recently produced a freeware piece of
software which launches a GUI program which will show you all loaded modules
and the path from which they are loaded. You run the program by doing a
createprocess for this EXE program, passing the process ID as a parameter.

This is really handy for  A) creating an 'install' package and making sure
you have all the files you need and B) when testing and your program seems
to be loading a DLL form the wrong place. (You'd be amazed how often
developers and/or Windows do strange and wondrous things with "path,"
resulting in DLLs loading from an unexpected place).

You can get a full package WRITTEN FOR USE WITH THE BASIC LANGUAGE at:
http://www.powerbasic.com/files/pub/pbwin/tools/ModList.zip   (Executable
and BASIC source).

While you could use this with COBOL-based programs, the porting might be
beyond the reach of those not terribly familiar with WinAPI calls - i.e.,
the very audience for whom this might be most useful. (You need to do a
CreateProcess either explictily or implicitly, converting the calling
process ID to a USAGE IS DISPLAY variable, but you'll see that in the source
code).

If there is an interest, well, I haven't contributed a whole lot to the
COBOL community in a while; I could take out some of the BASIC-language
interface and make this a bit easier to use from a COBOL program. (Drop me a
note. You can also volunteer to test/tune the interface from YOUR particular
brand compiler at that time).
```

```
SPELLED
> IN THE EXPORT TABLE OF THE LIBRARY DLL, Windows will give the now-famous
> "missing a component" message and not even complete loading a program.
>
>  When you see an underscore prepended ("_myfunction") or the stack usage
> appended ("sleep4") or the function name uppercased or other decoration
done
> (_SLEEP@4) it is because the programmer who created the DLL told his/her
> compiler to create it that way. Prepending underscores, uppercasing,
adding
> an "@",suffixing the stack usage and other ''name decoration" is common
with
> 'C' compilers and programmers (who often take the 'default' options).
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

executable

> IMPORTS for various modules.

Well, I did some looking and found out that PE stands for "Portable
Executable" and is the format that MS use for their code.

I haven't located PEBrowse yet but that is certainly a tool I could use. My
Hex editor (I use Axe; superb product) is excellent but it is VERY tiresome
browsing symbol tables in large DLLs with it.

>
> I'm sure there are some supported things available to do this. PEBrowse
has
> a bad bug: when there are too many imports, exports or resources in an
> executable it GPFs; looks like a table bound bound violation of some kind.
```

<a id="t-9f7651f5d6"></a>
## Thread: No of records in file — 8 msgs

### No of records in file

- **From:** "Steve Rainbird" <steve.rainbird@nospam.mssint.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-20T17:02:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bi09jo$gjr$1@hercules.btinternet.com>`

```
I suspect the answer to this is no but...

Is there a way in MF Cobol to get the number of records in a line sequential
file (without counting them myself!).  I would like a generic solution so
that the programs will run on both Windows and Unix.


TIA

Regards
```

#### ↳ Re: No of records in file

- **From:** "N. Shamsundar" <shamsundar@uh.edu>
- **Date:** 2003-08-20T13:44:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bi0fmb$54c3$1@masala.cc.uh.edu>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<bi09jo$gjr$1@hercules.btinternet.com>`
- **References:** `<bi09jo$gjr$1@hercules.btinternet.com>`

```
With a filesystem where all files are regarded as unstructured byte 
streams, the answer would be 'no', regardless of what programming 
language is used.

The Unix utility 'wc' will, however, give you the answer to the question 
outside of Cobol, and you can possibly call the utility from inside 
Cobol. Versions of wc for Windows are freely available. The example 
below shows 63 lines, 317 `words' and 3185 characters in the file tested.

N. Shamsundar
University of Houston

----------------------------
S:\ddc>wc csort.pli
      63     317    3185 csort.pli
----------------------------
Steve Rainbird wrote:
> I suspect the answer to this is no but...
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: No of records in file

- **From:** "Steve Rainbird" <steve.rainbird@nospam.mssint.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-20T20:14:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bi0kqb$crp$1@hercules.btinternet.com>`
- **References:** `<bi09jo$gjr$1@hercules.btinternet.com> <bi0fmb$54c3$1@masala.cc.uh.edu>`

```
Good idea thanks.

Steve

so

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: No of records in file

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-20T17:16:39-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hT2dnY_Q_4_abt6iXTWJjg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<bi09jo$gjr$1@hercules.btinternet.com>`

```
Steve Rainbird wrote:
> I suspect the answer to this is no but...
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Unix.

*IF* the records are the same size, it is relatively easy in Windows to get
the size of the file. Simple division gets the answer you want. We use this
technique to modulate a progress bar.

Just thinking out loud, if the record sizes vary (that is, you have trailing
blank truncation), you could read the first 100 or so records and get the
average length of a representative number of records, then divide using that
length - plus the file size - to get an approximate number.
```

```
not an approximate one.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: No of records in file

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-21T06:28:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZtKcnbAeYKhFMdmiU-KYvw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<bi09jo$gjr$1@hercules.btinternet.com> <hT2dnY_Q_4_abt6iXTWJjg@giganews.com> <bi1rid$cbs$1@titan.btinternet.com>`

```
Steve Rainbird wrote:
>>
>> *IF* the records are the same size, it is relatively easy in Windows
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> figure not an approximate one.

We have a similar condition and we deal with it by counting the records as
they're added by various processes then storing the results in the first
record of the file.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: No of records in file

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <hugh.candlin@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-21T15:43:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qM51b.108304$0v4.7650497@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<bi09jo$gjr$1@hercules.btinternet.com> <hT2dnY_Q_4_abt6iXTWJjg@giganews.com> <bi1rid$cbs$1@titan.btinternet.com>`

```

Steve Rainbird <steve.rainbird@nospam.mssint.com> wrote in message
news:bi1rid$cbs$1@titan.btinternet.com...
>
> Unfortunately the records will be variable length and I need an exact
figure
> not an approximate one.

Create a one-record, one-field (record-count) control file.
Initialize the record-count field to zeroes.

Whenever the data file is created or maintained,
update the control file's record-count field.

To determine how many records the data file has,
open the control file and read the record.

You can add support fields to the control file
if you like, such as last date/time updated,
last update module, last update user,
last record count delta, etc.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: No of records in file

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-21T12:22:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308211122.39133101@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bi09jo$gjr$1@hercules.btinternet.com> <hT2dnY_Q_4_abt6iXTWJjg@giganews.com> <bi1rid$cbs$1@titan.btinternet.com>`

```
"Steve Rainbird" <steve.rainbird@nospam.mssint.com> wrote 

> Unfortunately the records will be variable length and I need an exact
> figure not an approximate one.

Then either:

a) Don't use LINE SEQUENTIAL, use RECORD SEQUENTIAL and write CR+LF
terminators as the last two bytes of a space padded fixed length
record so that you can calculate. (as long as your system will write
this as fixed size blocks of course).

b) Read the file and count.

With a line sequential file the number of 'records' is the number of
record terminators in the file, usually CR+LF.  Think of the problem
of knowing how many 'A's there are in the file.
```

### Liant's RM/COBOL and ORACLE (OCI and PRO*COBOL) availability

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-20T21:38:53+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<t1n7kv0sgqo2kpe2pb54la7fci4oiom4dd@4ax.com>`

```
Hi all,

Following the advent of the availability of COMP-5 with RM/COBOL we
have now made available the following products for use with RM/COBOL.


Oracle OCI (Oracle Call Interface) Version 7.
Available for Windows and Linux (intel). 
   Evaluation download available as of 20 August at
www.syssoft-int.com.

Oracle PRO*COBOL
Available for Windows and Linux (intel). 
   Download will be available at any time soon. Please check our
webpage for updates.


Oracle OCI V8 will hopefully be available by the end of the year.




Best regards




Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

<a id="t-50195fc65a"></a>
## Thread: Calling a Mainframe COBOL module from a C or C++ — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Calling a Mainframe COBOL module from a C or C++

- **From:** pgindia <member36452@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-21T02:51:08-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3268244.1061448668@dbforums.com>`

```

Hi,



Can any body tell me how I can call a COBOL module on mainframe from a C
or C++ program. As I am quite new to to mainframe and COBOL but I know
bit of COBOL.



Specify major steps to link and work them together.



Thanks in advance.

PG
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling a Mainframe COBOL module from a C or C++

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-21T06:07:10-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vk99v0c9jfkkd5@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3268244.1061448668@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3268244.1061448668@dbforums.com>`

```
pgindia wrote:
> Can any body tell me how I can call a COBOL module on mainframe from a C
> or C++ program. As I am quite new to to mainframe and COBOL but I know
> bit of COBOL.
> 
> Specify major steps to link and work them together.

You need to be more specific about environments - what sort of 
mainframe, and on that mainframe, what versions of C/C++ and COBOL?  In 
general, the concept is similar as it is in PC development (i.e., 
compile each separately, and let the linker resolve the references - he 
doesn't care what language the object files are in), but if you provide 
more specifics, we can give you a more specific answer.  :)
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling a Mainframe COBOL module from a C or C++

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-21T14:30:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UH41b.5004$sV.4785@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3268244.1061448668@dbforums.com>`

```
If you are running on IBM's z/OS operating system and have the latest LE,
COBOL, and C products, you should read the ILC manual.  See, for example:

    "4.0 Chapter 4. Communicating between C and COBOL"

at:

    http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/CEEA4110/4.0
```

## Thread: Microfocus COBOL — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Microfocus COBOL

- **From:** pillasso <member36490@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-21T07:34:27-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3268995.1061465667@dbforums.com>`

```

I have a requirement where in I have to create an output file of fixed
length ie. 300 characters. Each record should also be delimited by the
newline character.



But my output file layout has the last field of length 8 characters
which could have variable length values ranging from spaces to 8
characters.



In my understanding, when we define the file as a line sequential file
then we would have the newline character after the last non-space
character.So defining the file this way would amount the file length to
vary from 292 to 300.



I tried defining the file as record sequential file...and I am using the
"write after advancing 1 line" option while writing to the output
file.But I see a ^M character after the 300th character which is not
desirable.



Is there any way we could keep the file length fixed always to 300 and
also have a newline character after each record so that I am able to
view the file using the "vi" editor in unix and also keep up the
vendor's requreiment of fixed length?



Thanks

Pillasso
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-21T12:11:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<3268995.1061465667@dbforums.com>`

```
pillasso wrote:
> I have a requirement where in I have to create an output file of fixed
> length ie. 300 characters. Each record should also be delimited by the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In LINE SEQUENTIAL two things happen:
1. The record has trailing blanks truncated, and
2. The system adds CR/LF.

First, ditch the WRITE BEFORE or WRITE AFTER. Just "WRITE"

Second, go with OUTPUT IS SEQUENTIAL

Third:

If the record is to be 300 bytes PLUS the CR/LF, define the record as 302
bytes and manually insert a CR/LF as the last two bytes. Here's how:

01  CRLF PIC X(2) VALUE X'0D0A'.

MOVE CRLF TO OUTPUT-REC(301:2).
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-08-21T18:29:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F45101D.C625460D@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3268995.1061465667@dbforums.com>`

```
Pillasso,

It might help if you posted the code for defining the file and record format
if the following doesn't help. This is Net Express, but from a recent query
from a student using  M/F Object COBOL Compiler Version (?) on Unix, the
same should apply :-

*>-------------------------------------------------
FILE-CONTROL.

     Select               Data-File
     assign               Data-filename
     organization      SEQUENTIAL
     (organization      LINE SEQUENTIAL)
     access              sequential
     file status          ws-FileStatus.

*>-------------------------------------------------
FILE SECTION.

*> FIXED

FD  Data-File
 01  Data-record           pic x(1500).

*> VARIABLE

FD  Data-File
    record varying from 1 to 1500 characters
       depending on ws-RecordSize.
01  Data-record           pic x(1500).

*>-------------------------------------------------

Taking either the FIXED or VARIABLE record format there is no difference
between SEQUENTIAL  and LINE SEQUENTIAL - the record size you specify is
exactly the same for each. However, in the case of the LINE SEQUENTIAL  the
file routines AUTOMATICALLY tack on CR/LF (x"0d0a') after your specified
record length (and the CR/LF is NOT included in your record size).

The difference between the two - viewing them on a screen, (using  a DOS
text viewer), SEQUENTIAL wraps around when it hits the screen width, whereas
the LINE SEQUENTIAL displays each succeeding record on a NewLine when it
hits x"0d0a'. Hence the latter can be viewed easily from Notepad or
whatever, or just use the O/S to print the file.

Check the on-line help - the following from WRITE for FORMAT 1 - Record and
Line Sequential files, (which also covers Frederico Fonseca's suggestion of
WRITE BEFORE......)..

"The WRITE statement releases a logical record for an output or input-output
file. For sequential files it can also be used for vertical positioning of
lines within a logical page."

The student I referred to had used M/F "shorthand" to define files :-

INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
         SELECT INPUT-RECORD ASSIGN TO 'proj4.oms'.
         SELECT ERROR-RECORD ASSIGN TO 'proj4.err'.
         SELECT ERROR-REPORT ASSIGN TO 'proj4.rpt'.

The fixed length input file 'proj4.oms' was DEFINITELY a SEQUENTIAL but I
could read it as either SEQUENTIAL or LINE SEQUENTIAL - and the following
clarification from Chris Glazier on M/F Answer Exchange :-

"You can read this, (.... a SEQUENTIAL FILE....), as a LINE SEQUENTIAL file
because the READ statement will fill the contents of the data record until
either the data record is full or a CRLF pair is found to terminate the
record.

The next record read will do the same thing and again will fill the
contents of the data record. "

I *think* you are after fixed length records, but if you were using Variable
- as you go to write you must stipulate your current record size using
ws-RecordSize. When reading records the current ws-RecordSize is
automatically returned to you. If you truly have a requirement to have the
record varible from 292 to 300 then change the record format to :-

FD  Data-File
    record varying from 292 to 300 characters
       depending on ws-RecordSize.
01  Data-record           pic x(300).

But, do you really want to "economize" on 8 characters ? <G>

Hope above helps - if not post the file definitions  you are having a
problem with.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

----------------
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-21T11:52:54-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308211052.10bb4cbd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3268995.1061465667@dbforums.com>`

```
pillasso <member36490@dbforums.com> wrote 
> I have a requirement where in I have to create an output file of fixed
> length ie. 300 characters. Each record should also be delimited by the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> vary from 292 to 300.

In most cases, yes.  Some versions of Fujitsu didn't/don't truncate
spaces and a fixed record is written.  There is a run-time variable
that can set this for versions that recognise it.
 
> I tried defining the file as record sequential file...and I am using the
> "write after advancing 1 line" option while writing to the output
> file.But I see a ^M character after the 300th character which is not
> desirable.

The ^M is Carriage-Return.  You don't say what your OS or compiler is
so it is hard to give definite advice.  The standard terminator for
DOS/Windows is CR+LF.  For Mac it is CR, for Unix/Linux it is LF.  You
are getting CR+LF. Note that you may be getting a CR+LF at the _start_
of the file because you are using AFTER advancing.  It must output an
'advance' and then the record data, surely.  It should have been
BEFORE ADVANCING.

> Is there any way we could keep the file length fixed always to 300 and
> also have a newline character after each record so that I am able to
> view the file using the "vi" editor in unix and also keep up the
> vendor's requreiment of fixed length?

Yes, just make the record sequential file's record length as 301 by
adding a field called, say, Record-Terminator and moving x'0D' to it.
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-08-21T23:52:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F455B9A.F5E962B@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3268995.1061465667@dbforums.com>`

```


pillasso wrote:

> I have a requirement where in I have to create an output file of fixed
> length ie. 300 characters. Each record should also be delimited by the
> newline character. <snip>

Pillasso,

Having read Richard's message that your 'M" is the NewLine character - I
guess Unix must do it slightly differently from PCs for Windows. I'd be
willing to bet that the Newline is automatically added as is the CR/LF.

If your reference books are on-line check the Micro Focus site, or
alternatively check your  hard copy. You wont find it in the LRMs (Language
Reference Manuals). Look at the book on File Handling----> File Types.

You learn something new every day ! I was aware of two types of Sequentials,
but on the PC there are three :-.

1 - SEQUENTIAL - records follow contiguously (technically 'Record
Sequential')

2 - LINE SEQUENTIAL - denoted by the CR/LF (x"0d0a") , AND - surprise -

3. - PRINTER SEQUENTIAL FILES -  If you have this third one, your options
    (i.e. the hex characters), may differ from the Net Express quote below
:-
----------------------------------------------------------
2.1.3 Printer Sequential Files

Printer sequential files are files which are destined for a printer, either
directly or by spooling to a disk file. They consist of a sequence of print
records with zero or more vertical positioning characters (such as
line-feed) between records. A print record consists of zero or more
printable characters and is terminated by a carriage return (x"0D").

With a printer sequential file, the OPEN statement causes a x"0D" to be
written to the file to ensure that the printer is located at the first
character
position before printing the first print record. The WRITE statement
causes trailing spaces to be removed from the print record before it is
written to the printer with a terminating carriage return (x"0D"). The
BEFORE or AFTER clause can be specified in the WRITE statement to
cause one or more line-feed characters (x"0A"), a form-feed character
(x"0C") or a vertical tab character (x"0B") to be sent to the printer before

or after writing the print record.

Printer sequential files should not be opened for INPUT or I/O.

You can define a file as printer sequential by specifying ASSIGN TO
LINE ADVANCING FILE or ASSIGN TO PRINTER in the SELECT
statement, for example:

 select printseq
     assign to line advancing file "printseq.dat"..
---------------------------------------------------

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus COBOL

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-22T15:33:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308221433.1b3fdb4b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3268995.1061465667@dbforums.com> <3F455B9A.F5E962B@shaw.ca> <ubKcnUW3aJUrBtiiU-KYvA@comcast.com>`

```
"Russell Styles" <RWS0202@comcast.net> wrote 

> access sequential.  It will give you the desired fixed record size
> and CR/LF at end.

Yes, but it is the CR that he doesn't want - that is showing as the ^M
in the text editor.

The '^x' symbolism is from the early days.  A serial terminal keyboard
generates control codes using the 'Ctrl' (Control) key + a letter. So
Ctrl+A generates ASCII control code x'01'.  This is shown as ^A. 
ASCII x'0D' is the Carriage-Return (CR) control code and is generated
by Ctrl+M and shown as ^M in vi (and many others).

Unix linefeeds are LF, ASCII x"0A", ^J only.
```

```
As I recall, you simply make static calls to the functions you're interested
in using standard call linkage.  It's equivalent to calling C functions from
your COBOL program.  Link with the import library.  You don't explicitly
load the DLL.  You should be able to find the details in the programming
guide for calling functions written in C.  See:

   http://www-3.ibm.com/software/awdtools/cobol/va/library/

Al...
```

```
clvrmnky wrote:

> I'm having a hella time trying to CALL functions in external DLLs from 
> IBM's Visual Age for COBOL on Win32.  I've read a variety of sometimes 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
To _partially_ answer my own question, the idea is to defer linking with 
the DYNAM compiler option (-qdynam for cob2) and CALL the DLL as a 
literal or identifier.  The runtime stuff will look for the DLL in the 
standard search path, and will append ".dll" to the call identifier.

So far, so good.  I still have to find a way, if there is one, of 
calling a particular entry point within the DLL.  Passing and return 
values or references are contingent on this, as well.  I've heard rumors 
that VA COBOL allows only one entry point per DLL; the entry point is 
the name of the DLL.

Does this jibe with the VA COBOL experience out there?

-- cm
```

<a id="t-1d9fbba81d"></a>
## Thread: WinAPI Sleep function solved - Thanks to all. — 1 msgs

### WinAPI Sleep function solved - Thanks to all.

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-08-22T12:27:14+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f456442_4@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Due to the fantastic help from you guys I was able to solve this and it
didn't hurt as much as I thought it would <G>.

Here is the comedy of errors and the solutions:

1. PROBLEM: Trying to compile and link a Fujitsu NETCobol V6.1 CGI program
that is a main program with 5 nested subprograms, I received a message from
the Linker regarding an unresolved external reference on the WinAPI function
"Sleep", which was used in the main program.

ATTEMPTED SOLUTION:  Dimly recall from previous experience with the WinAPI
that the Fujitsu ALPHAL(WORD) option needs to be set. Set it (after
grappling with the Fujitsu IDE that has help in pidgin English and is not
always clear or intuitive when you want to do things that deviate from the
defaults) for the Main program and recompiled. Disaster! Hundreds of knock
on errors from COPY books not found.

Under pressure to get this working, post to CLC and comment out the Sleep
function.

Responses are all valuable. JerryMouse confirms it works on his system with
ALPHAL(WORD), Bill gives the References that explain what ALPHAL does,
Michael offers to write a .DLL! Other posts correctly identify problem with
Entry point and exports. (I am blown away by the response...sincere thanks
to everybody.)

2. Armed with confidence that it works if ALPHAL(WORD) is used, go back and
address this problem. Used IDE and manually set the ALPHAL(WORD) option AND
the LIB entry to point to my Libraries for the main program. Re-built the
project. Unresolved reference is gone, COPY books are all included, but now
we have a sad message from the Linker saying "Entry point must be
specified..."   No clues... there are 6 programs in this compile and no
indication of WHICH one doesn't contain the required Entry point, or WHICH
required Entry point is not there. (At least the previous message quoted the
SLEEP entry as being problematic.)

Gave up on the IDE as counter intuitive and checked the .CBI file (this
contains the compiler options) directly. Sure enough, the ALPHAL entry was
set to NOALPHAL. Fixed it manually, checked that the LIB entry was intact,
rebuilt the lot, and everything worked fine.

I can only assume that my clumsy attempts with the IDE, setting options for
the main program only, must have blown away the LIB entry  that would point
to the COPY libraries.  (It should be noted that JerryMouse suggested
exactly this as a possible cause of the problem.)  Then, having got that bit
right, I was apparently in conflict with the main COBOL options...

BOTTOM LINE:

Sometimes the development environment is more problematic than the
application.

I have been using the Fujitsu IDE for some years now and, in general, find
it useful. However, when it comes to more esoteric things like changing
options for nested programs, it is not immediately obvious what is required
and the help is irritating and NOT helpful. Who has time to read pages of
documentation (the Fujitsu docs are generally "good"...now) when under
pressure to have something working as quickly as possible?

On-line help needs to be helpful and simple; diagnostic messages need to be
to the point and specific.

And I need to do more homework and take a break occasionally <G>.

Thanks again to all who contributed.

Pete.
```

---

<a id="t-49ce5a21ef"></a>
## Thread: Looking for address parsing code — 8 this month / 9 total

### Looking for address parsing code

- **From:** "Simeon Nevel" <snevel@sonic.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-22T18:49:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RAt1b.14270$dk4.497898@typhoon.sonic.net>`

```
Hi All..

I've a new project at work where I need to parse a free-format address
line into the pieces necessary to do a Zip+4 lookup.

Does anyone know if there is a product (commercial or otherwise) that
will do the parsing and/or the Zip+4 assignment.

I feel I can make a good case w/ my management to buy a solution rather
than spend months interpreting US Postal regulations and re-inventing the
wheel.

For those who know the postal biz, the eventual goal is CASS (Coding
Accuracy Support System) certification.

Clues gratefully accepted.

adTHANKSvance!

Simeon
```

#### ↳ Re: Looking for address parsing code

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-22T14:54:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B1qdnedyg9eS6NuiXTWJkA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<RAt1b.14270$dk4.497898@typhoon.sonic.net>`

```
Simeon Nevel wrote:
> Hi All..
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Clues gratefully accepted.

If it were easy, everyone would be doing it and there would be no need for
the three or four companies in the field.

The pitfalls are too numerous to count (is "St." an abbreviation for "Saint"
as in St. Lewis, or for "Street"? How many abbreviations can you think of
for "Boulevard"?)

Also, you can't just DO it; you have to keep DOING it. That is, your
software must undergo periodic re-certification by the USPS. Using a
commercial product, the certified updates come to you as part of your annual
maintenance.

But, look at your end goal. If the end result is save money on mailings, you
might be WAY ahead to farm the whole thing out to a mailing service. The
mailing service can give you back your addresses already parsed into the
fields you want.

This won't do you any good for future mailings, though, because your mailing
software won't be certified by USPS. But you will have a cleaned list.
```

#### ↳ Re: Looking for address parsing code

- **From:** "Anon" <anon@anon.org>
- **Date:** 2003-08-22T16:29:31-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
On my last job we used a product called Code-1 Plus from
"Group 1 Software". Type in "Code-1" on Google. It
worked pretty well on some pretty screwed up input
addresses returning USPS valid addresses. It ain't
cheap however. Good Luck.
```

```
Simeon,

melissadata.com has something called "Address Object".  It's
competively priced and is an OLE server.  I use it with Sp2 and any of

can see it.  Email me at thane at softwaresimple.com if you want some
consulting assistance on this.
```

<a id="t-a5ca71ccd9"></a>
## Thread: *OBSCURE* issue in using Procedure-Pointers instead of Dynamic CALLs for IBM "performance" reasons — 1 msgs

### *OBSCURE* issue in using Procedure-Pointers instead of Dynamic CALLs for IBM "performance" reasons

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-23T04:01:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QGB1b.1940$O03.582@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`

```
There was an OLD thread about using procedure-pointers to improve
performance (over dynamic CALLs) in an IBM mainframe environment.  I found
the comment in GOOGLE at (long URL follows):


http://www.google.com/groups?q=pointer+dynamic+ibm+performance+group:comp.lang.cobol&hl=en&lr=&ie=UTF-8&oe=UTF-8&selm=3D5F709F.89D88CFC%40worldnet.att.net&rnum=2

While looking for something totally unrelated, I found this VERY interesting
(dox) APAR (another long URL),

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/zidocmst/666.1?ACTION=MATCHES&REQUEST=cobol&TYPE=FUZZY&SHELF=&DT=20030816095151&searchTopic=TOPIC&searchText=TEXT&searchIndex=INDEX&rank=RANK&ScrollTOP=FIRSTHIT#FIRSTHIT

If your site has started using PROCEDURE-POINTERs instead of dynamic CALL
statement, I suggest you look at this (pending) change to the Programming
Guide.  This may (or may not) turn out to be a problem for YOUR site, but it
certainly represents (IMHO) a potentially HARD TO DIAGNOSE problem.
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2003-08-23T10:35:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1061634868@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

### OT: humour

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SvuVgWB5v6R$EwNr@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
I was browsing a web site that many of you may be familiar with and 
encountered the following examples of humour:


A rap homage to computers:

If a packet hits a pocket on a socket on a port,
And the bus is interrupted as a very last resort,
And the address of the mem’ry makes your floppy disk abort,
Then the socket packet pocket has an error to report!

If your cursor finds a menu item followed by a dash,
And the double-clicking icon puts your window in the trash.
And your data is corrupted ’cause the index doesn’t hash,
Then your situation’s hopeless, and your system’s gonna crash!

If the label on the cable on the table at your house
Says the network is connected to the button on your mouse,
But your packets want to tunnel on another protocol,
That’s repeatedly rejected by the printer down the hall,

And your screen is all distorted by the side effects of gauss,
So your icons in the window are as wavy as a souse,
Then you may as well just reboot and go out with one big bang,
’Cause as sure as I’m a poet (ha!), the sucker’s gonna hang!

When the copy of your floppy’s getting sloppy on the disk,
And the microcode instructions cause unnecessary RISC,
Then you have to flash your mem’ry and you’ll want to RAM your ROM.
Quickly turn off your computer and be sure to tell your mom!


Undocumented Windows error codes:

000 - Unknown Error Occurred But Was Lost. Windows Will Try To Remember
001 - Intimidation Failed; Attempting to Crash Repeatedly
002 - Erroneous Error; No Error Occurred (Yet)
003 - RAM Depleted; Annex Japan (Y/N)?
004 - Deluxe Error. Please Send $75 to Upgrade Your Error
005 - Long File Name Error; Tape Erased to Make Room for Filename
006 - Insufficient RAM to Crash Properly; Attempting Fake Crash
007 - Alphanumeric Sequence “OS2” Prohibited
008 - This License Has Expired; Please Purchase Another Copy
009 - Error Buffer Overflow; Too Many Errors
00A - Non-Microsoft Application Encountered
00B - Push Error; Removing Files to Make Room for Advertisement
00C - Windows Loaded Correctly This Time
00D - User Error; Lemming Not Found
00E - Open Standard Encountered; Attempting to Redmondize
00F - Reserved for Future Coding Errors
010 - Virus Error — Other Applications Will Be Closed Instead
011 - Orwell Not Found; You Must Use MSN
012 - Cash Underflow — Credit Card Number Will Be Assimilated
013 - Keyboard Error; User Must Learn to Slow Down
014 - User Error; Reading License Agreement Mandatory to Continue
015 - Error Message Deleted
016 - Expected Error Did Not Occur; Attempting to Restart Error Sequence
017 - Multitasking Attempted; System Confused
018 - Network Error — Your Crash Will Be Replicated to All Stations
019 - Freedom-of-Choice Error; Select a Microsoft Browser To Continue
01A - Insult Detected — Your Bill Gates Joke Will Be Deleted
01B - Error Removing Temp File; a Permanent File Will Be Substituted
01C - Wrong Disk Formatted. Sorry About That.
01D - Mandatory Error Inserted to Meet Error Quota
01E - Please Insert Your Favorite Error Here
01F - Error In Progress; Please Wait...
020 - Unexpected Intelligent User Detected; Please Reload Everything
021 - Error Parsing Error List; Please Wait For Next Error
022 - Upgrade Error; Please Format Your Drive And Reload Everything


Finally, in a similar vein to the WMD not found page go and look at:
http://www.mamselle.ca/error.html
```
