<a id="t-60454e5545"></a>
## Thread: Designing CoBOL from scratch — 17 this month / 49 total

### Designing CoBOL from scratch

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-26T14:10:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bifpoa$3h6$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
If I were to design CoBOL from scratch (no legacy), I think there would be a
different syntax for OO, there would be a different design for on-line, and a
different type of copy using databases.


But as I was thinking of this, I wonder - while we have many languages trying to
be a better C or C++, being invented all the time - we don't have languages
trying to be the next CoBOL.

Why is this?
```

#### ↳ Re: Designing CoBOL from scratch

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-26T15:27:43+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bifrb0$k0j$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<bifpoa$3h6$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard,

We have had languages that have tried to be the next COBOL, particularly in
the 1980's but they fell by the wayside. Who remembers all those 4GL's that
are no longer with us.

PL/1 is an exception to this and is still used. People who use it swear by
it.

COBOL remains the best language for doing what it was designed to do -
processing business rules.

Paul

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:bifpoa$3h6$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
> If I were to design CoBOL from scratch (no legacy), I think there would be
a
> different syntax for OO, there would be a different design for on-line,
and a
> different type of copy using databases.
>
>
> But as I was thinking of this, I wonder - while we have many languages
trying to
> be a better C or C++, being invented all the time - we don't have
languages
> trying to be the next CoBOL.
>
> Why is this?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Designing CoBOL from scratch

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-27T05:10:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<X2X2b.5928$O36.348139@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<bifpoa$3h6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <bifrb0$k0j$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`

```
"Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com> wrote in message
news:bifrb0$k0j$1@hyperion.microfocus.com...
> Howard,
>
> PL/1 is an exception to this and is still used. People who use it swear by
> it.
I swear and curse by it all the time.

> COBOL remains the best language for doing what it was designed to do -
> processing business rules.
This is often said.  I don't even think that this holds true as a statement
anymore.  More and more "business rules" are becoming decision and rules
engines with complex data dynamic data structures and management. The recent
thread on UNSTRING for example highlights the difficulties with even simple
expression parsing.  COBOL does not lend itself to this very well at all.
Simple linear processing I would agree. As systems have matured they have
left behind COBOL.  As business rules matured they have left behind COBOL.

A lot of the IBM Enterprise compiler was build around extending COBOL to
complement other architectures which I think is a noble but ultimately
failing cause for the simple reason that you're dealing less with different
languages but more with different environments.  In the long run it will be
more beneficial to upgrade the environments.  (This is not the same argument
as COBOL is dead - but more is becoming less useful).

JCE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Designing CoBOL from scratch

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-27T09:20:34-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-16DCCE.09203427082003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<bifpoa$3h6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <bifrb0$k0j$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <X2X2b.5928$O36.348139@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
In article <X2X2b.5928$O36.348139@twister.tampabay.rr.com>,
 "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote:

> "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com> wrote in message
> news:bifrb0$k0j$1@hyperion.microfocus.com...
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> JCE

If you are trying to parse expressions with Unstring then you will have 
problems.  That is like trying to build a brick house out of a single, 
properly shaped brick.

Fall back to parsing a single byte at a time and expression parsing in 
Cobol is actually easy.  Being able to recursively execute statements  
is sometimes missed, but can be worked around.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Designing CoBOL from scratch

- **From:** gary drummond <gdrumm@sbcglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-28T17:12:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F4E24B4.2127CAB1@sbcglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<bifpoa$3h6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <bifrb0$k0j$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <X2X2b.5928$O36.348139@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```


jce wrote:
> 
> "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com> wrote in message
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> JCE

Well, the environment which is supported is determined by the vendor of
the software chosen. Most have extensions to "talk" to the other libs,
or callable routines. 

Take my challenge, and try to duplicate the results in ANY other
language, portable to ANY other OS,  keeping track of the total time
invested, and publishing the cost of the tools used. Other than the
specific hard-coded implementor file names, my COBOL program is ANSII. I
used personal COBOL from MF ($50-student price), and spent about 2 hours
developing it to get the results. It was a quick-and-dirty program, but
it did do what I needed-labels and line numbers, plus some crap which
was ignored...

The original source and resulting cross-reference file are available on
my web site.
http://pages.sbcglobal.net/gdrumm/ASMXREF.CBL  the source of my "hacked"
program.
http://pages.sbcglobal.net/gdrumm/ROMJXREF.TXT the (~flawed, but
workable XREF).

Nobody has ever responded to my original posting about doing one of my
"off-the-wall" projects, which in this case was to use COBOL/something
to produce a cross-reference for an ASM program. The "Super C"
programmers I worked with wasted a week or two... 

I would expect that this would fill the requirements for a
"non-business-rules" or "left-behind" type of program using the
"out-dated" COBOL environment. COBOL can and will provide the right
results, you just have to understand it, and be able to use it
effectively for the desired results. I have updated my XREF program
several times over the years, using several compilerss and OSs, and I
would like to hear a little input from others, using another language,
about how long it took to "remember" how they wrote their program, and
then modified it, and tested it.

I have developed other programs, including one that read an Inforex Data
Entry System (3100?) source, and produced a Unisys Checkbox/COBOL source
program source. It did take about a week to develop, but it ran on any
1100/2200, converted an Inforex source tape to an ANSII source tape
(1100/2200/CADE), and cut the time from 1-2 weeks conversion to 1-2 days
(worst case) per program. I produced a memory map of the labels and
scratch area used, a cross reference to the variables, and converted the
checkbox code to checkbox/COBOL at about about a 97%-99% rate. Neither
of the two mentioned programs is a "standard" type of COBOL application,
but they worked, saved a few years of "people" time, and was done MUCH
faster than any other language, conversion tool, or cross compiler has
achieved (for the cost of the development) than I know of-just standard
ANSII 1974 COBOL.

Several freeware C compilers are available, as well as other tools and
utilities. Pick one, use it, but add the time you try to use them to
develop the program to the total time. Perhaps it's time to 'fess up and
match your (or my) views to a real world environment, or be quiet...

If you need to research the format of the ASM source, search for 8051 or
drop a line to my address below.

Gary

My reply address is forwarded to UCE@FTC.GOV use my userid <at> ont
<dot> com,
or post to the group to give everyone a chance to respond/comment.
```

#### ↳ Re: Designing CoBOL from scratch

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-08-26T22:24:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f4bcfcd.240863244@news.central.cox.net>`
- **References:** `<bifpoa$3h6$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>If I were to design CoBOL from scratch (no legacy), I think there would be a
>different syntax for OO, there would be a different design for on-line, and a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>But as I was thinking of this, I wonder - while we have many languages trying
to
>be a better C or C++, being invented all the time - we don't have languages
>trying to be the next CoBOL.
>
>Why is this?

It is unfashionable to design a programming language for ease of reading, and
assessment of readability is subjective. 

Programming language designers are motivated by:

.. Beauty/purity of a model.
.. Ease of writing or generating a compiler, evident in context freedom and
simple formal definition (e.g. BNF).
.. Writing speed.

--------------------------------------------------------
BOS and APS were failed attempts to produce a better Cobol. AccuCobol is still
trying.

Some would put Ezytrieve and DYL/Quikjob/Vision in that category. Further
afield: COOL:Gen and Powerbuilder.
```

#### ↳ Re: Designing CoBOL from scratch

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-08-27T13:13:35+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f4c06a4_8@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<bifpoa$3h6$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:bifpoa$3h6$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
> If I were to design CoBOL from scratch (no legacy), I think there would be
a
> different syntax for OO, there would be a different design for on-line,
and a
> different type of copy using databases.
>
>
> But as I was thinking of this, I wonder - while we have many languages
trying to
> be a better C or C++, being invented all the time - we don't have
languages
> trying to be the next CoBOL.
>
> Why is this?

1. No-one (except a few dedicated vendors and a tiny percentage of the
programming user base) cares, and the language is perceived to be in
decline.

2. The existing investment in COBOL Legacy code would make it non-viable to
implement sweeping changes to the Language. It has to be possible to
maintain existing code with it and the Language is therefore stuck in
backward compatibility. A victim of its own success...

3. As it stands, the Language is very well suited to what it was originally
intended for (machine independence  and quicker development/easier
maintenance for batch programming of commercial applications.) The  systems
environment has moved on way past this and better languages, more suited to
the Network environment are cheaply and easily obtainable. This is not a
problem with COBOL. It is simply a reality.

4. There is no need for another Procedural "One Language Wonder", so there
is no need to re-invent COBOL.

(OO COBOL should be excepted from points 2 through 4 above. The failure of
OO COBOL is not down to the implementation, it is down to the User base.)

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Designing CoBOL from scratch

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-28T18:57:22+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<biljai$4r1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<bifpoa$3h6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3f4c06a4_8@news.athenanews.com>`

```

On 26-Aug-2003, "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> 4. There is no need for another Procedural "One Language Wonder", so there
> is no need to re-invent COBOL.
>
> (OO COBOL should be excepted from points 2 through 4 above. The failure of
> OO COBOL is not down to the implementation, it is down to the User base.)

Well, I don't use CoBOL because it's a Procedural "One Language Wonder".   I use
it because it handles business rules well.    In the future, business rules will
still be important, but I haven't seen an emphasis on this in "modern"
languages.

Maybe they figure it will all be done with spreadsheet programs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Designing CoBOL from scratch

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-08-29T12:46:35+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f4ea354_1@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<bifpoa$3h6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3f4c06a4_8@news.athenanews.com> <biljai$4r1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:biljai$4r1$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 26-Aug-2003, "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> > 4. There is no need for another Procedural "One Language Wonder", so
there
> > is no need to re-invent COBOL.
> >
> > (OO COBOL should be excepted from points 2 through 4 above. The failure
of
> > OO COBOL is not down to the implementation, it is down to the User
base.)
>
> Well, I don't use CoBOL because it's a Procedural "One Language Wonder".
I use
> it because it handles business rules well.    In the future, business
rules will
> still be important, but I haven't seen an emphasis on this in "modern"
> languages.
>
> Maybe they figure it will all be done with spreadsheet programs.
(tut tut, Howard...spreadsheets are an important part of the IT scene and
will continue to be so for the foreseeable future...)

Howard,

someone else (sorry, I can't remember who and I don't have time to look it
up at the moment) commented recently that Business Rules can be implemented
in other languages, just as well as in COBOL.

I believe this is true.

It isn't the languages that are lacking, it is OUR perception, because we
have used COBOL for decades.

Just because we can't read a "Business Rule" in "English-like" syntax in
say, Java, for example, doesn't mean that Business rules cannot be
implemented in Java. (Or even are not handled "well"...)

Most of us who have used COBOL all our lives have become "conditioned" to
the need for maintenance and ease of  amendment.  COBOL, because it is
English-like, shines in this department. It is natural to assume that any
language where this is NOT emphasised cannot be as good.

There are several fallacies in this assumption:

1.It isn't HOW a language is implemented that is important, it is WHAT it
can do that matters. COBOL is a brilliant response to the problem of
implementing Batch Programming in a commercial environment. It was extended
into On-line processing by the addition of teleprocessing monitors like CICS
and IMS/DC and that is all fine. But today, we have objects running on a
Network with the need for distribution of the objects and remote invocation
of Methods. While OO COBOL is up to doing this, standard COBOL isn't.

2. As Richard has pointed out, what is familiar is considered "good". A "new
breed" of programmers who have never been exposed to "English-like" syntax
in programming, simply find it verbose and cumbersome when they encounter
it. These people will implement Business Rules according to an algorithm,
and the shorter the code the better. They will argue that they can maintain
their code just as easily as we can maintain COBOL. (The fact that we might
consider this code obscure and obtuse, does not make it so...)

3. The need for ease of maintenance is no longer as critical as it was.
Whether you agree with my postulate that non-maintainable components
containing encapsulated functionality will gradually replace standard
programming or not (and I agree it is arguable to what extent this will
happen), the fact is that modern programmers write small encapsulated
functions as a matter of course. There is no need to maintain 10,000 lines
of source code so it doesn't matter whether it is English-like or not.

As I mentioned in my original response to you:

"3. As it stands, the Language is very well suited to what it was originally
intended for (machine independence  and quicker development/easier
maintenance for batch programming of commercial applications.) The  systems
environment has moved on way past this and better languages, more suited to
the Network environment are cheaply and easily obtainable. This is not a
problem with COBOL. It is simply a reality."

As for COBOL being a Procedural "One Language Wonder", it isn't COBOL that
makes it that, it is COBOL programmers who are not prepared to expand their
skill set. "Everything I want to do I can do in COBOL". Fine. This says more
about limited aspirations than it does about a programming language.

I love COBOL (although it is stupid to be emotional about a programming
language). Always have, and will continue writing it as long as I have eyes
and fingers. But I will use other languages and tools as well. I don't
consider that a "betrayal" of COBOL.

Some scholars speak Latin and Greek. They may even love the cultures and
literature embodied in these Languages. But they still speak their local
"langue du jour" when they go to buy groceries at the corner store or
converse with friends...

COBOL doesn't need re-designing any more than Latin does.

If we did, it wouldn't be COBOL. The extensions to enable it to take a
meaningful place in the modern world have been dutifully implemented by the
vendors (and even the ANSI Standards people) and rejected by the User Base.

I reckon that has sealed its fate.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Designing CoBOL from scratch

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-29T07:49:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<binp61$2g0e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<bifpoa$3h6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3f4c06a4_8@news.athenanews.com> <biljai$4r1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3f4ea354_1@news.athenanews.com>`

```

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3f4ea354_1@news.athenanews.com...
> But today, we have objects running on a
> Network with the need for distribution of the objects and remote
invocation
> of Methods. While OO COBOL is up to doing this, standard COBOL isn't.

A terminological (and perhaps a perspective) quibble:  with the introduction
of ISO/IEC 1989:2002, *standard* COBOL *is* OO COBOL.  The trick is getting
vendors to make *standard* COBOL available to their users.

> The extensions to enable it to take a
> meaningful place in the modern world have been dutifully implemented by
the
> vendors (and even the ANSI Standards people) and rejected by the User
Base.

I don't think I agree that that's always the sequence of events;
specifically, I don't believe that *all* new features are *first* designed
and implemented as extensions by one or more vendors and *then* incorporated
into the standard as your description implies (although that does *often*
occur).   When developing large extensions, I think it's more common that a
vendor will develop such extensions to prior standards on the basis of
preliminary standards draft (I believe a number of "OO COBOL"
implementations are based on Committee Draft 1.4 of the 2002 standard, for
example).  Sometimes the standards folks will come up with a feature on the
basis of user requests only to have the vendors discover that the feature
does not have broad enough support in their market space to warrant the
expense of implementing it.   And I know of at least one case in which a
vendor implemented a feature from the draft standard on the basis of user
*demand* only to find once the feature was available that no users were
actually interested enough in using the feature to experiment with it.
This massive effort, as far as actual customer usage is concerned, has
functionally ended up as dead code.  I don't blame the vendors or the
standards committee for that one!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Designing CoBOL from scratch

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
news:binp61$2g0e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
>
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> A terminological (and perhaps a perspective) quibble:  with the
introduction
> of ISO/IEC 1989:2002, *standard* COBOL *is* OO COBOL.  The trick is
getting
> vendors to make *standard* COBOL available to their users.
>

OK, Chuck, so how would YOU differentiate in describing sites using COBOL
with NO OO, and sites implementing Component Based Design, OOD/P, OO
Modelling, using COBOL? (Well, I guess you could argue that as there ARE no
sites in the latter category <G>, it isn't necessary to differentiate...)
but, just in case there is a site somewhere that is actually using 21st
Century COBOL, how would YOU refer to it? (In a way that differentiates it
from sites NOT using OO COBOL facilities...)


> > The extensions to enable it to take a
> > meaningful place in the modern world have been dutifully implemented by
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> I don't think I agree that that's always the sequence of events;

I never said it was!

I am saying it WAS the sequence of events in the case of OO COBOL. It was
available from Fujitsu, and some of us were using it, while the Standards
committee were still deciding the venue for the next boondoggle...
"Florida or Las Vegas...? Oh, by the way, has anybody looked at OO COBOL?
No? Oh, some "preliminary investigation"...never mind... We'll put it on the
agenda for the next session. Just like we did for the last session...."

> specifically, I don't believe that *all* new features are *first* designed
> and implemented as extensions by one or more vendors and *then*
incorporated
> into the standard

neither do I, so we can agree on that...

> as your description implies (although that does *often*
> occur).

The problem is not with my non-existent implication, it is with your
erroneous inference...


>  When developing large extensions, I think it's more common that a
> vendor will develop such extensions to prior standards on the basis of
> preliminary standards draft (I believe a number of "OO COBOL"
> implementations are based on Committee Draft 1.4 of the 2002 standard, for
> example).  Sometimes the standards folks will come up with a feature on
the
> basis of user requests only to have the vendors discover that the feature
> does not have broad enough support in their market space to warrant the
> expense of implementing it.

That WOULD be true, IF we had a responsive Standards committe that was in
touch with the shop-floor...

Now please stop waving red flags at me... I gave credit to the committee in
my original post and that's the best I can do. I am STILL very pissed off
about the whole fiasco.

If you want to extol the virtues of a committee that took 17 years to do a 2
year job, go and start your own thread. (I promise I won't post to it...)
Don't try and slip it in to a response to one of MY posts...you know what
will happen.

> And I know of at least one case in which a
> vendor implemented a feature from the draft standard on the basis of user
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Neither do I. The failure of OO COBOL is not down to the vendors or the
Committee as you rightly observe.

I guess we all get what we deserve in the end.

The irony is that a lot of the kids who are learning programming at the
moment would be quite interested in using OO COBOL as another possible tool
(I get mail from students who express interest in OO COBOL and using it for
Network objects). But it is unlikely the existing vendors will continue to
support it without a strong user base, and, by the time the emerging crop
get to a point where they have the power to use it, it will have withered on
the vine.

In the meantime, the Colleges and Universities are teaching Fortress COBOL
and students come away believing THAT is the extent of COBOL.

It makes me heartsick when I see such a brilliant product rejected because
of resistance to change, prejudice, and short-sightedness.

Oh, well, back to that Applet I was writing...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Designing CoBOL from scratch

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-08-30T14:16:51+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f5009fd_1@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<bifpoa$3h6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3f4c06a4_8@news.athenanews.com> <biljai$4r1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3f4ea354_1@news.athenanews.com> <binq98$8vc$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:binq98$8vc$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 28-Aug-2003, "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Most of us who have used COBOL all our lives have become "conditioned"
to
> > the need for maintenance and ease of  amendment.  COBOL, because it is
> > English-like, shines in this department. It is natural to assume that
any
> > language where this is NOT emphasised cannot be as good.
>
> I am used to writing some type of pseudo-code that can be verified by my
users
> is correct.   This pseudo-code is quite a bit CoBOL like.
>
> How does this change with these more cryptic (to non-programmers)
languages?

It doesn't.

But pseudo-code is just that.

You can present a problem solution to your users in a number of ways
(pseudo-code is just one of many).

There is no relationship between this and the actual solution you code (in
whatever language you code it).
(Other than the fact that the coded solution will implement what's in the
pseudocode <G>).

If this wasn't true, you would simply skip the pseudocode and get the users
to review the actual code.

In COBOL, with the right users, this might well be possible; in more cryptic
languages it probably wouldn't be, but as long as you maintain a layer
between the end user and the technical solution (Programmer), it doesn't
matter whether the code used to implement the solution is "cryptic" or not.

Pete.
```

```
> But as I was thinking of this, I wonder - while we have many languages
trying to
> be a better C or C++, being invented all the time - we don't have
languages
> trying to be the next CoBOL.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Designing CoBOL from scratch

- **From:** gnuoytr@rcn.com (robert)
- **Date:** 2003-08-29T09:23:46-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<da3c2186.0308290823.7464ca52@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bifpoa$3h6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <JWW2b.5926$O36.347692@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
"jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message news:<JWW2b.5926$O36.347692@twister.tampabay.rr.com>...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
> news:bifpoa$3h6$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> What's trying to be a better C++ ?

first java, now c#

there's been a better COBOL for rules processing since 1970:  it's called
Prolog.  it is not, however, much use as a general purpose programming
language.  this i know, since i am up to my eyeballs in a Mutant Bastard
Spawn of Prolog; a closed proprietary language built on Prolog with a
bit of RDBMS tossed in.  not a good idea since Prolog is both declarative
and typeless.  such languages (not even sure there is another) make a 
bad foundation for what needs to be an imperative syntax and semantics.

BUT, what makes some folks (generally those who live Across the Pond),
take the bait on Prolog is that it is designed around the concept of 
self-modifying code; thus business rule management can more easily be
moved to the user domain.  BRs can be "stored" in a "database", and
used to change (and i mean literally) executed code.  

an additional level of abstraction to a regular (SQL) database talking to
a regular language (e.g. COBOL) will do the same thing.  lots of BR
management suites are showing up in java, but that's only because
java is the language de jour.

but if you want to allow the user to execute arbitrary code (which may
not yet exist) based on arbitrary input (the apotheosis of BRs), then
COBOL probably won't cut it.

robert
```

```
What do you actually WANT to do (what function)?  There may be a way to do
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-26T19:59:22-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-BE06AD.19592226082003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<big0jm$mhr$1@atlantis.news.tpi.pl>`

```
In article <big0jm$mhr$1@atlantis.news.tpi.pl>,
 "Janek" <neuros@polbox.com> wrote:

> Hi.
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


You can not call a JCL procedure from CICS.  Though you can write a job 
stream using the SPOOL OPEN/WRITE/CLOSE, it is one way.  There is no way 
to get any results or return status about the stream.

You can use Rexx if your sysprogs have installed the necessaries.  You 
can also use Java under the same conditions.

You can use the various Unix API calls, but only if the region you are 
running in has certain things configured.  For example, the CICS region 
will at the very least need a USS security segment or every call is 
going to cause the region to die with an S913.  There are other possible 
requirements depending on what you want to do, again, check with your 
sysprogs.
```

```
On Wed, 27 Aug 2003 10:10:36 +0200 "Janek" <neuros@polbox.com> wrote:

:>I want to use commands compress/uncompress from omvs.
:>I have file that was compressed by compress command. I want to uncompress
:>this file in Cobol program under CICS. Next i want to read this file.

Well, I really don't think there is a way to do this without having your CICS
systems programmer breaking your fingers.

But, if I had to do it, I would create an MVS subtask under CICS, have your
CICS transaction post a request to the subtask and then do a CICS wait for the
subtask to complete the compression / decompression.

This is not stuff for those weak at heart.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CICS

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-27T09:15:04-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-68D575.09150327082003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<big0jm$mhr$1@atlantis.news.tpi.pl> <joe_zitzelberger-BE06AD.19592226082003@corp.supernews.com> <bihp41$l8p$1@nemesis.news.tpi.pl>`

```
In article <bihp41$l8p$1@nemesis.news.tpi.pl>,
 "Janek" <neuros@polbox.com> wrote:

> I want to use commands compress/uncompress from omvs.
> I have file that was compressed by compress command. I want to uncompress
> this file in Cobol program under CICS. Next i want to read this file.

It sounds like this is not something you really want to get done under 
CICS.  If the file is compressed, can I assume it is quite large?  It 
would be quite detrimental to the region to uncompress and then read an 
entire file.

If you really need to process the file online, do the uncompress in a 
batch job (perhaps triggered by a SPOOLed job).  And have your 
transaction wait for the file to be opened by your batch job.

That is a big if though.  Could you not do the processing in a batch job?


> Where can i find documentation for Unix API?

There are many books.  I happen to like Curry's Unix System Programming 
for SvR4 and Chan's Unix System Programming Using C++.  But your 
compiler reference will have all the various system calls, including the 
ones to start a new shell and pass it the uncompress command.  Also 
check out the z/OS C/C++ Programmers Guide.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CICS

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2003-08-27T21:40:32+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<270820032140327927%josephk@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<big0jm$mhr$1@atlantis.news.tpi.pl> <joe_zitzelberger-BE06AD.19592226082003@corp.supernews.com> <bihp41$l8p$1@nemesis.news.tpi.pl>`

```
Look into using the EXCI interface of CICS.

This allows you to create a batch job which will uncompress the file,
extract the information and then call invoke a program in CICS passing
the information you just extracted. This program can then do any CICS
things it might need to do.

EXCI is an RPC interface from any MVS address space to CICS.

In article <bihp41$l8p$1@nemesis.news.tpi.pl>, Janek
<neuros@polbox.com> wrote:

> I want to use commands compress/uncompress from omvs.
> I have file that was compressed by compress command. I want to uncompress
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-08-27T17:09:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030827130950.17961.00000023@mb-m01.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<big0jm$mhr$1@atlantis.news.tpi.pl>`

```
Janek, neuros@polbox.com 
On: 8/26/03 11:06 AM EST

posted<<

Can I call JCL, REX or unix functions(OMVS) from Cobol program under CICS?
I'm looking for documentation.
>>

It is possible for CICS programs to write to what is called the 'internal
reader.' (also note that REXX can write to it also). This can be code as normal
CICS. I'm thinking it is actually a queue with a name like 'intrdr' or some
such. The CICS documentation should give you cross references to this.

The successful approaches I have seen submit jobs that invoke PROCs (an
instream PROC can be included any time in this sort of thing, if that matters
to you.)  But the only status information you are going to get is the status of
the CICS command sequence that writes the lines of the JCL. So it is not clear
that that will help you.

For my two cents worth, I would agree with the responses you are getting that
say if you are
considering decompressing some received file in CICS, then don't.

You may want to be a little more detailed in your
statement that, among other functions, you want to 'receive' a compressed file.
 Is it permissible for you to post what technology is sending it?

If some technology is going to send it and receive it into your OS environ, and
if, this is somehow sensible to an executable CICS transaction, you could use
the internal reader to schedule a job to decompress it and do any number of
things. 

It is possible to schedule routine jobs that might, on a time basis, look for
decompressed results and get yet another transaction for a CICS program that
would then read your file as you indicate you want to do, etc.  But if you want
this all as one transaction function it sounds too big, from your initial post.

However, trust that it is true that there are some really big CICS programs
that work. You might, if you actually need realtime processing for all of this,
and if you can get to the compression functionality as a callable capability
just write the file yourself instead of using JCL or REXX; those after all are
just scripting languages.

CICS has writes.  And CICS can definitely call programs with its CALL command
(and in some shops is allowed to do native language calls).

What do you plan to use to do the decompress?

After you do this, you can actually just read and process in the original
transaction process or CALL some more good code to read and process.

Is there anything about the decompression logic that makes it unavailable to
you in the initial 
real-time transaction?  If so that problem is leading you to JCL and REXX, and
the core issue is how to sense the end (and sense the success for that matter)
of the task that executes as a batch job or Time Share program. That is less
straight forward. 

There are probably good folks here who can tell you how to get batch MVS to
trigger a CICS transaction, that could complete your loop back.
(my experience is complicated by having seen IMS BMPs get into all this which
is not relevant).

So getting to JCL is probably not hard, getting back the return code (besides
the file) is hard.
So, if you can tell us about your decompressor,
as potentially a CICS transaction subfunction
plug-in.  And if you can tell us what 'receive' means. What technology
telecommunicates it into CICS in you plan?

Best Wishes,
Robert K. Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

```
> DaveM

Sympathize about going nuts when you can't see the obvious. Not a solution
but following might help your grey cells kick-in to correct solution.

1. I note you have :-

Copy \\proghq\unit32\PROG\CPY\NUMSECHK.WS .

Never done it that way - is the space acceptable in - CHK.WS^. ???

2. Have you done a complete 'Update ALL Dependencies" ? Have you got any
"Buttons", (i.e., the official name for the [+] or [-]  ), hanging 'loose'
in the left pane
Treeview - particularly if you expand on the specific programs ?

3. I created project A which had the mfres.h and mfres.cpy. I decided to
re-vamp and create Project B as a new project. Copied the dialogs.rc file
across from project A to B. Project B compiled OK. Sent test data for
Project B, (a zipfile to include *.* from Project B), to two people, but
the mfres.h and mfres.cpy were missing ! They didn't get them - because
they were sat in my directory for Project A.

>>>>>>
>There should be quotation marks around it ... don't know if that is the
>problem or not.  I'd also try shortening it to a standard 8 characters,
as I
>have a few problems here and there with long filenames.

> Donald

Donald,

Wont swear to it but I think the quotes around the copyfile name are
optional, just like quotes around method names. I agree stick to 8
characters lowercase for class names - but would have thought he should be
OK for copyfiles.

Filenames - problems possibly if you have space in the directory or
filename, (I use hyphens). This will have been resolved in the very latest
Fixpacks to N/E V 3.1 and now N/E V 4.0.


Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Net Express compiler keeps adding ".CPY" suffix

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** renfrewthejust@yahoo.com (Renfrew)
- **Date:** 2003-08-29T05:12:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d6e1fab7.0308290412.748fa577@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<d6e1fab7.0308261200.1e90887a@posting.google.com> <3F4C04E2.DFD559A0@shaw.ca> <d6e1fab7.0308280506.2739e081@posting.google.com> <9Tt3b.12924$Cg2.1077396@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
[[[stuff snipped]]]


> > 1. The space should be acceptable.  We started preceding the period
> > following each copy statement with a space ever since a long time ago
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

No, the actual file name does not have a space.  The space in question
here is a delimiter that exists between the end of the filename and
the period that terminates the sentence.

    Copy \\proghq\unit32\PROG\CPY\NUMSECHK.WS .
     ***** (filename is "\\proghq\unit32\PROG\CPY\NUMSECHK.WS")

This COPY statement always worked fine in our old 16-bit system, which
would not have supported spaces within filenames anyway (well, i THINK
it wouldn't).  Good flail though <gr>.

I must say though that I do see some definite readability advantages
to using quotation marks even when no spaces are involved -- these
will leave absolutely no doubt as to where a filename begins and ends.
 Much obliged for the advice!

DaveM
```

#### ↳ Re: Net Express compiler keeps adding ".CPY" suffix

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-26T20:18:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308261918.193ec27d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<d6e1fab7.0308261200.1e90887a@posting.google.com>`

```
renfrewthejust@yahoo.com (Renfrew) wrote 

> Trying to do a REBUILD ALL against three programs within a project,
> but keep getting the message "Dependent file not found
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>      Copy \\proghq\unit32\PROG\CPY\NUMSECHK.WS .

I would put the "\\proghq\..\CPY" in a COBCPY environment variable
along with all the other places there may be copy files.  Then I would
just have:

        COPY "NUMSECHK.WS".

in the program. Note that I usually put quotes around the copyname.

I suspect that MF is looking for the file as specified and then trying
it with .cpy added and failing to find either.  It then just uses the
last name it tried to search for.

Have you any others COPY statements that serach unisng full server
specifier and do actually work ?
```

#### ↳ Re: Net Express compiler keeps adding ".CPY" suffix

- **From:** "Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-27T09:06:57+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bihpgc$qlm$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<d6e1fab7.0308261200.1e90887a@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Dave.

By default, the Net Express compiler will search for copy files using either
'.cbl' or '.cpy' extensions. This is determined by the COPYEXT compiler
directive. Search for COPYEXT in the Net Express Help for more information
on this.

I believe that if you were to specify COPYEXT "cbl,cpy,ws" within either the
Project Properties or Build Settings, it *should* resolve your problem.

SimonT.
```

<a id="t-97776ab226"></a>
## Thread: Complexity = documentation quantity? — 5 msgs

### Complexity = documentation quantity?

- **From:** gary drummond <gdrumm@sbcglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-27T02:01:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F4BFDBF.C7DE2CC8@sbcglobal.net>`

```
I was talking to a recruiter (headhunter) in Austin about a COBOL
position, and we both started talking about the old days of comparing
mainframes and OSs.

The Sperry (Unisys/Univac/...) 1100/2200 had 1 Exec PRM, 1 COBOL,
1 ASM (and MASM), 1 TIP, etc. We had lots of fun knocking IBM sites
for having more JCL programmers than our site had total programmers,
and 40-50 times as much required documentation. IBM is still selling 
lots of hardware, Unisys???

I had just received an email about Addison/W... professional books, and 
we made a quick search of their books.

Java              574 titles
Object-orented... 240 titles
C++               213 titles
C                 96 titles
COBOL             9 titles (Well 1, is for Java, and 2 for .NET)


Does this mean JAVA will still be around in 30 years, with OO and C++ 
a close second, or does this mean it takes a LOT of docs to understand
how they work VS COBOL?

Gary

PS- Every project I worked on as a vendor is still running COBOL, but 
has been trying to convert to C/C++ for 4-8+ years-go figure...
```

#### ↳ Re: Complexity = documentation quantity?

- **From:** "funguy" <johnjoseph0101@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-27T03:05:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XdV2b.11732$jY2.6752@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3F4BFDBF.C7DE2CC8@sbcglobal.net>`

```
Now you know, that's a most interesting observation.  As a longtime COBOL
programmer, recently transfered to a Web/PC team with C++ and Java, that
dovetails nicely with one of my own observations.

After many years of coding COBOL, I had reached the point where I could
pretty much "code through my fingers".   The general technique, verbs,
procedures, etc. would just flow from my brain through my fingers into the
computer.  And the code was in general very good, easy to work with, and
easy for others to understand.

When I began Java and C++ programming one of the first things that struck me
was the dichotomy.  The languages themselves are simple, very simple.  The
primary keywords and the language syntax are simple to master.  But the
extensions!  Ah, now those are where things get wild, particularly in Java.
Going to Sun's web site and perusing a Java help file with all the possible
CLASSes can be an exercise in humility.  There is so much to learn, so much
that can be done, that "coding through my fingers" seems to be a forlorn
hope at present.  Add the new paradigm of object orientation and the keen
reliance of Java on the OO paradigm and the task grows even more difficult
(at least for us old time COBOLers).  Another programmer on the team, one
who has been doing this far longer, tells me that it can't be done, that
there is simply to much to know.  And from what I've seen so far, she may be
right.

To answer your question, I think both.  Java/OO will be around in 30 years.
And more importantly, yes, it takes a lot of doc to understand all the
aspects of Java.  Something that may end up happening is that
sub-specialists of Java will come about.  Some specializing in database
applications, others in socket code, others in GUI applications, etc.  It's
hard to tell.  As rich and widespread as the language is, possibly the
biggest problem will simply be getting a controlling handle on the thing.

Paul

"gary drummond" <gdrumm@sbcglobal.net> wrote in message
news:3F4BFDBF.C7DE2CC8@sbcglobal.net...
> I was talking to a recruiter (headhunter) in Austin about a COBOL
> position, and we both started talking about the old days of comparing
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> has been trying to convert to C/C++ for 4-8+ years-go figure...
```

<a id="t-910fc9fc6b"></a>
## Thread: File Handlers with MicroFocus Server Express — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### File Handlers with MicroFocus Server Express

- **From:** Helmut Leininger <h.leininger@bull.at>
- **Date:** 2003-08-27T10:25:33+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bihptu$9h7ih$1@ID-54033.news.uni-berlin.de>`

```
Hi,

I want to use my own file handlers (dependent on file type) with 
ServerExpress programs. Basically, that is no big deal.

The challenge is:
The decision if my own file handler or the MF standard file handler 
should be used for a specific file must be made at run time depending on 
an environment variable.
This could be done in the Object Cobol Developer Suite with the help of 
ufhtab.o which is not existing any more. Now, if I try to call the 
standard file handler (e.g. sqfile) from my switching handler (-msqfile 
mysqfile), I enter a recursive loop.

Are there any ideas how to do?

Best regards
```

#### ↳ Re: File Handlers with MicroFocus Server Express

- **From:** "Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-27T13:31:30+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bii90i$s69$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<bihptu$9h7ih$1@ID-54033.news.uni-berlin.de>`

```
Hi Helmut.

I've just replied to your posting on the Micro Focus forum, but I'll include
my response here also. With Server Express, the command-line required for
linking in your own file handler has changed. The text below is taken from
the Server Express Migration Guide :

If you call your own file handler, you should relink your application. For
example, you would previously have used a command similar to:
cob -xvo rts32 $COBDIR/ufhtab.o
    user-module -muixfile=user-file-handler
    -m urlfile=rlfile -m usqfile=sqfile -m uixfilev=ixfilev
    -m urlfilev=rlfilev -m usqfilev=sqfilev -e ''whereas you would now use:

cob -mixfile=user-file-handler -x callmyprog.cbl myprog1.cbl
user-module.oSimonT.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: File Handlers with MicroFocus Server Express

- **From:** "Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-27T13:33:13+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bii93o$s6i$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<bihptu$9h7ih$1@ID-54033.news.uni-berlin.de> <bii90i$s69$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`

```
Apologies for the formatting problem. The command line should read :


cob -mixfile=user-file-handler -x callmyprog.cbl myprog1.cbl user-module.o


SimonT.
```

```
On Wed, 27 Aug 2003 11:57:58 GMT Jack Straw <jackstraw@localhost.localdomain>
wrote:

:>I'd like to have my Cobol/MVS program read multiple templates
:>from in-line DD statements [1], and I don't know how to code the
:>FD for those -- or, for that matter what the DCB of that data
:>would be.  For the time being, I've put these templates in a PDS,
:>where that's all known, but I would like to be able to quickly
:>adjust them in-stream just before I submit the JCL to run.

:>Would someone shove me in the right direction please?

:>Thank you.

:>[1] Something like:

:>//TMPLT1  DD *
:>  THIS IS MY TEMPLATE.
:>  IT CONTROLS MY PROGRAM.
:>/*

Default instream would be 80 byte records.

I would assume your PDS is the same.
```

#### ↳ Re: DCB for reading from inline DD statements

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2003-08-27T11:07:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0308271007.8fd5bd8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<es6pkvot4vqm94ftq8unadcr5s1sle7kg2@4ax.com> <9l9pkv4jsdgn4bd185i73rebj18soe39r9@4ax.com>`

```
Hi Jack,

If your template contains JCL, you might want to use: 

//MYTMPLT DD DATA

If this occurs at the end of the stream a /* is not required.
If the data in MYTMPLT contains a /* stmt, code:

//MYTMPLT DD DATA,DLM='cc'

where, cc can be any chars that don't appear in the DD DATA stream.
The aposts are only required if cc contains special chars.

Regards, Jack.



Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message news:<9l9pkv4jsdgn4bd185i73rebj18soe39r9@4ax.com>...
> On Wed, 27 Aug 2003 11:57:58 GMT Jack Straw <jackstraw@localhost.localdomain>
> wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> I would assume your PDS is the same.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Fsd3b.6730$O36.471199@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<es6pkvot4vqm94ftq8unadcr5s1sle7kg2@4ax.com> <9l9pkv4jsdgn4bd185i73rebj18soe39r9@4ax.com>`

```
"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
news:9l9pkv4jsdgn4bd185i73rebj18soe39r9@4ax.com...

<jackstraw@localhost.localdomain>
> wrote:
> Default instream would be 80 byte records.

But make sure you (a) Use only the bytes you know will be there, or (b) Make
sure NUM is off when editing.
Nothing worse than trying to figure out what's broke only to realize that
you have 0000102 on the end of your statement.

JCE
```

## Thread: Using GDG's in an CICS environment. — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Using GDG's in an CICS environment.

- **From:** Ace11 <member25371@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-27T11:25:37-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3291348.1061997937@dbforums.com>`

```

Respected gentlemen,





Can any one of you please tell me whether is it possible to use GDG's
(Generation Data Groups) in CICS ? If yes how ?





Thanx in advance,

Ace.
```

#### ↳ Re: Using GDG's in an CICS environment.

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-27T19:19:44+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hempkvkbrnnr73l83pl6cgb6ghn1r9fmrr@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3291348.1061997937@dbforums.com>`

```
On Wed, 27 Aug 2003 11:25:37 -0400 Ace11 <member25371@dbforums.com> wrote:

:>Can any one of you please tell me whether is it possible to use GDG's
:>(Generation Data Groups) in CICS ? If yes how ?

Yes.

What do you want to do with them? CICS system programmers do not take kindly
to CICS programmers who use QSAM to access files.
```

#### ↳ Re: Using GDG's in an CICS environment.

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-08-27T17:28:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030827132851.17961.00000024@mb-m01.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3291348.1061997937@dbforums.com>`

```
The opening and closing of a GDG in a realtime context, as a user file, is
almost a non-sequitur. But CICS can get to almost anykind of a file if you can
find a DBA or systems programmer willing to support your grand plan. 

I have seen CICS write SYSOUTs! So QSAM should not be impossible. (although my
knowledge could be outdated in some newer CICS releases). 

Getting to the generation number is highly unlikely. So if you need to know it,
or want to 
specify it way over in your application, that is 
unlikely, IMHO.

A GDG dataset could be anything, and can land on nearly anykind of device, but
from the get-go this kind of thing is usually huge, and oriented to some very
long process that is the antithesis of an online transaction.  It is not rare
that a GDG is on tape, again not exactly speedy.  And a tape mount is much more
of an issue than the access method.

Frankly, it would be better to think of some alternatives, like just a regular
old file name. Whatever is producing a GDG file name could just as easily
generate a regular file name for you or you can copy the thing.

But if you want dynamic selection of data based upon the generation number, I
would say you need a different design.  The real issue here 
might be the possible management of 
dataset names inside you online app, ... generally CICS is more of a planned
environment, where the DBA control dataset name and dataset selection.

Separately, to offer as much positive response as possible, please note that a
GDG ultimately just creates a unique DSN that happens to have a goovoo node. It
is not rocket science. But if you want to be trafficking in goovoos inside a
CICS transaction in some literal form or as generation numbers then rethink
this. But for special cases the CICS JCL can specify the full DSN.

Best Wishes,
Robert K. Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Using GDG's in an CICS environment.

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-28T12:15:54-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fhaskvc3udalegutgu4f3umtf31s37un8f@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3291348.1061997937@dbforums.com>`

```
On Wed, 27 Aug 2003 11:25:37 -0400, Ace11 <member25371@dbforums.com>
enlightened us:

>
>Respected gentlemen,
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Ace.

Why use a GDG?  Use a fully supported ESDS file then back it up at the
end of the day to a GDG if you must.

You can read and write a sequential GDG file through the use of the
Destination Control Table and possibly the transient data table.
Other means would require things your systems' programmer would not
want to do.

Regards,


          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Public Restroom Graffiti:


IF BUSH WERE CAPTAIN OF THE TITANIC, 
HE'D SAY WE WERE STOPPING FOR ICE. 
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Using GDG's in an CICS environment.

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-28T23:59:02-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-BD08FF.23590228082003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3291348.1061997937@dbforums.com> <fhaskvc3udalegutgu4f3umtf31s37un8f@4ax.com>`

```
In article <fhaskvc3udalegutgu4f3umtf31s37un8f@4ax.com>,
 SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com> wrote:

> Why use a GDG?  Use a fully supported ESDS file then back it up at the
> end of the day to a GDG if you must.
>snip...

There is a way to make the ESDS part of the GDG themselves...as I 
understand it it is completely unsupported/undocumented by IBM, but I 
have heard tale of using VSAM GDG's...
```

<a id="t-8b7d7b8b70"></a>
## Thread: Redefines in COBOL — 11 this month / 13 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Redefines in COBOL

- **From:** "Bill J." <bjacaruso<NO~~~SPAM>@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-27T16:03:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lD43b.346$CH4.166@newssvr22.news.prodigy.com>`

```
In C there is the ability to "redfine" a function.

In other words I can override a function name, so instead of calling the
orginal function, it call my function instead.

In particular I can create some IO routines that will use a different file
system if I so desire, without changing my original source code except by
including "include" files, (copy files in COBOL).

My COBOL knowledge is limited to maintence work on existing code, but we
would like to use some different IO routines instead of the native stuff
that happens when we call OPEN, READ, WRITE etc.

Is there a way to "redefine" these routines so the OPEN call and its
parameters are directed to our custom routines without having to change the
source where the OPEN call is made?

We realize we can use the CALL verb and redirect the calls to our C
routines, but that would requires use to change our source.

Any suggestions are very welcomed....

Bill J
Round Rock, TX
```

#### ↳ Re: Redefines in COBOL

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-27T16:11:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sK43b.15952$Ih1.5488673@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<lD43b.346$CH4.166@newssvr22.news.prodigy.com>`

```
> In particular I can create some IO routines that will use a different file
> system if I so desire, without changing my original source code except by
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> parameters are directed to our custom routines without having to change
the
> source where the OPEN call is made?

No, because OPEN, READ, WRITE et al are not 'routines' or 'procedures'  at
all... they are compiler directives. You are telling the compiler to include
ITS routines for file access.

Depneding on the compiler and environment, you may or may not be able to
hook onto those procedures. (The procedures are not part of any 'standard'
for COBOL. Only OPEN, CLOSE, READ and WRITE are COBOL-standard)

You want to use alternate I-O mechanisms, you have to CALL those routines
yourself.

MCM
```

```
> make it available (Tom ?).

Well, Bill is on AcuCOBOL, and doesn't supply too many other details, so my
response is really for the historical record.

We make the interface available on a contractual basis, since it is beyond
the scope of our normal product support.  We also *use* the interface for
our Btrieve adaptor.  Another company in France uses the interface to
substitute relational database products.  (I don't recommend the latter
without very careful considerations of the performance obtained.)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Redefines in COBOL

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-08-27T17:58:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030827135814.17961.00000031@mb-m01.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<_U43b.353$NR4.88@newssvr22.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Bill J posted,
<<
I am wondering if we could write a pre-compiler to take care of this...
>>

You can write a precompiler to do anything.  Some folks say parsing COBOL is
involved.

But if you just want to take over OPENs ..... WRITES. You could just preprocess
for thatl. That is lexical scanner and parser question. And really we would ask
you. Are you able to write a pre-compiler? 

Not a bad plan really, .... as long as the set of I/O behaviors you have in
mind in those existing programs is in fact simple! The issue is not the
difficulty of scooping up the lexical particles. The issue is the semantics of
those COBOL verbs. 

If you really think you have a simple set of requirements in your program
collection then go for it, write a scanner.  If you have a small set of
programs it may be quicker to just change the source code to CALLS manually. 
If you have a large set of programs, that implies a complex system (not an
assumption, just a suggestion, even big systems can be stupid and simple, ...
but not ususually).  

If you have a complex system, and the notion is to try to save you time later,
somewhere in there you will discover that the COBOL verbs are non trivial. From
afar, one can only guess. But if you have a limitted set of modules, then
accept it, maybe you ought to just get to the source code.

Do you have any relative files and dynamic files, or is it all sequential? Is
everything fixed length? Do you really have only reads and writes? Or do you
have any rewrites or deletes? 

You see out here from afar one wonders why you are reluctant to get into the
source code.
If this system is big, I bet strapping your routines on is not going to be
simple, because in the learning curve for COBOL is some detail about those
verbs.

If there is any chance that you can isolate the complicated programs then
consider divide and counquer. If it warrants your time do a scanner to master
the bulk, then do so; and then just go ahead and get your hands dirty on the
subset that is intricate and code the CALLS if you think you can map the
functionality.

Do have a look at the code to make certain that you are seeing what the COBOL
does. This is not meant as opposition, just as support. You said you are new to
COBOL. How many of the source modules have you looked at? Do you have a sense
from what you are seeing that you can achieve a level of confidence as to what
those I/O statements expect? If so, and you eventually do convince yourself
that the full set of
requirements are simple, then do a scanner.

You will need to learn where EOF and file status is stored to fully interface
to that other stuff in COBOL that is not always in the statements headed OPEN
.... READ ... 

If you have modern COBOL on your hands you may wish to state here if in
addition to variable length files, do you have any occurs depending on clauses?
There are FROM and INTO phrases in I/O verbs that will get you off into this 
regrettably esoteric realm. So if you do have ODOs and FROM/INTO, you might
continue to seek COBOL expertise as you port 
this valuable application to your own I/O routines.

Best Wishes,
Stay ambitious, its fun!
Robert K. Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Redefines in COBOL

- **From:** "JJ" <jj@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-27T18:30:05-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Kpadne4PhvVZrdCiXTWJig@comcast.com>`
- **References:** `<lD43b.346$CH4.166@newssvr22.news.prodigy.com> <_U43b.353$NR4.88@newssvr22.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Bill,

Have you talked to Acucobol support?

I am fairly certain that they provide the ability to hook into all I/O calls
to allow alternate indexed file handlers.  For example, they can use C-ISAM
or RMS (on VMS) in place of their indexed file system.

If you look in the filetbl.c file in the Lib directory where your runtime is
installed, that has a C struct where you install dispatch functions for
other file handlers.  It doesn't give any information on what functions you
must provide in your dispatch handler, but you can probably get that from
support.  I remember reading a post on their user forum a few months ago
where someone wrote a handler to use PostgreSQL as the file system, for
example.

file

by

> >
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: Redefines in COBOL

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-08-27T16:42:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030827124229.17961.00000022@mb-m01.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<lD43b.346$CH4.166@newssvr22.news.prodigy.com>`

```
The original post is quoted verbatim below.

Rather than thinking REDEFINES, which as COBOL parlance does not really mean
what you are thinking, you might instead begin to think relink. Capturing the
interface
at the point in actual executable code where the 'native' I/O routines are
engaged.

You then would have to understand all of the behavior of standard COBOL I/O
verbs. 

Observers here would be all for your ambitions. But if you are new to COBOL it
is perhaps fair to say that COBOL I/O verbs are rather detailed and have
behavior evolved by committee processes that have occurred over decades of
political process reflecting competitive corporate interests.. 

C has much more basic I/O that has behavior conceived by technical process
reflecting cooperating human invention (originally in a rather short period of
time).

One wants to be positive, but you may find 'basic' I/O in COBOL to be a much
larger time sink than anticipated if you are new to this. But we are all for
you. If you have a set of COBOL programs that actually do pretty simple stuff
you can just interface into them the small subset of I/O functionality they
need. But look before you leap.

As important as SQL is in the market place, COBOL product venfors must escape
it into COBOL in non COBOL syntax. As  important as CICS and EXEC DLI are to
the economic success of IBM they crowbar these vast I/O facilities in with
comparable escapes from the normal COBOL syntax. Perhaps take that as a guide
(why wouldn't they just 'REDEFINE' or relink, taking command of I/O interface).

Instead, consider doing what you say you don't want to. Change those OPEN/CLOSE
and READ/WRITE statement in the source to CALLS to your interface, ... but have
a good look at those programs and how they must actually behave before you let
yourself believe that ordinary simple I/O is going to be enough. 

I am really just amplifying feedback on the one aspect of your excellent and
frank post. If you are new to COBOL, lets talk about those I/O verbs a bit
before you conclude your analysis.

Most vendors will provide some information about naming conventions on I/O
subroutines. And most compilers will give you some kind of assembler map and
you can get a basic list of things just from the linkage process.

Please post further detailed questions with code snippets if you feel the
interaction can help you get up to speed. You can get excellent detailed
responses here, and many generous folks here will go off-line with you also in
detailed exchanges.

Very Best Wishes, and encouragement
to post further details !

Robert K. Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com 


Original post verbatim follows:

@yahoo.com>

orginal function, it call my function instead.

In particular I can create some IO routines that will use a different file
system if I so desire, without changing my original source code except by
including "include" files, (copy files in COBOL).

My COBOL knowledge is limited to maintence work on existing code, but we

that happens when we call OPEN, READ, WRITE etc.

Is there a way to "redefine" these routines so the OPEN call and its
parameters are directed to our custom routines without having to change the
source where the OPEN call is made?

We realize we can use the CALL verb and redirect the calls to our C

Any suggestions are very welcomed....

Bill J
Round Rock, TX
```

#### ↳ Re: Redefines in COBOL

```
It is not that we find anything lacking, the main reason is we use a
differnet file system...I would prefer not to talk about that.

But, the bottom line is we want to basically redirect the data file requests
to our "alternative" file system.  On the way we would be able to translate
some data types (IE: COMP 6).

Our thought is we could use a precommpiler to change the "directives" like
OPEN, READ, etc to somehting that was calling local routines which already
call into our DLLs.  Anywork to move the data in and out of the COBOL
structures would be handled in the local routines we have in COPY books, any
of the data minipulation at the file system level is handled in our DLLs.

Maybe we are way off base about the appraoch, thats why I am asking for any
alternatives someone may have....

Bill J.

Round Rock
```

```
Michael,
   SourceRecovery creates new COBOL (semi-readable) from IBM mainframe
OBJECT code, not "pseudo-code" (or interpreted code or anything other than
an "executable").

I (personally) don't know of any similar functionality for any OTHER type of
COBOL compiler created machine code (eg AcuCOBOL - where this original
question was aimed).
```

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>I (personally) don't know of any similar functionality for any OTHER type of
>COBOL compiler created machine code (eg AcuCOBOL - where this original
>question was aimed).

There is one for AS/400:

http://www.sstglobal.com/cobol.htm

Sperry Univac developed one for their machines in 1972-76:

http://homepages.cwi.nl/~arie/rewiki/FatherOfDecompilation.html

------------------------------------------
Thinking outside the box, COBOL-generated code might more easily be decompiled
to C, for which there are many decompilers .. but not for AcuCOBOL code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Decompiler for Acucobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-08-29T11:55:27+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<761041e6.0308271408.1d867334@posting.google.com> <x3a3b.16056$Ih1.5550705@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <40b3b.117694$3o3.8233463@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Q5l3b.16219$Ih1.5677623@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Michael,

I agree with you and I thought your "cow/hamburger" analogy was a
masterpiece <G>.

When I saw the responses saying it COULD be done, I thought some more about
it.

I guess if you write a compiler (I worked on a Burroughs one many years ago)
you DO know what constructs of Source code will generate which constructs of
object code. (Code generation is really a "fill in the blanks" operation).

But, despite the marketing hype and the fact that pseudocode may contain
more information than pure Assembler, I believe your observation remains
true.

Without a debug file to obtain the actual Source names used, any
"reconstruction" will be a long way from the original.

I reckon the reconstructed "cow" will actually be a camel...<G>

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Decompiler for Acucobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-29T03:39:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F4ECAFB.569CFDDB@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<761041e6.0308271408.1d867334@posting.google.com> <x3a3b.16056$Ih1.5550705@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <40b3b.117694$3o3.8233463@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <Q5l3b.16219$Ih1.5677623@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <3f4e9757_3@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" wrote:
> 
> Michael,
> 
> I agree with you and I thought your "cow/hamburger" analogy was a
> masterpiece <G>.

I second that.  I once had to disassemble a small IBM assembler
subprogram with only a 370 reference card and ABMLIST.  It took me a
whole day to recover a program of about 100 lines.  Even with the
HLASM disassembler it's quite a chore. 

> 
> When I saw the responses saying it COULD be done, I thought some more about
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> object code. (Code generation is really a "fill in the blanks" operation).

I visited the source-recovery site when this discussion began.  The
most recent "news" item was from 1998!  Part of the difficulty is that
they have to figure out *which* IBM mainframe COBOL compiler was
used.  The recovered code has gibberish for names (at least in the
example available on their website).  A good programmer could clean it
up, but it's tedious work.

Every programmer could learn from being forced to disassemble a
program or recreate it.  It will teach you to back up the source code.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Decompiler for Acucobol

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-08-28T18:38:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f4e4819.402754800@news.central.cox.net>`
- **References:** `<761041e6.0308271408.1d867334@posting.google.com> <x3a3b.16056$Ih1.5550705@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:

>You cannot get COBOL source code from executables. That would be like
>getting 'cow' from hamburger.

If it was compiled with DEBUG information in the executable, you can recover
data and paragraph names. 

If file records are described in copybooks or other programs, you can use those
data names in the reconstructed source. 

If neither of the above, reasonable names can be assigned manually by someone
familiar with the data and application. This is a tedious reiterative process --
after some names are resolved others fall into place. You need an editor that
can display instant cross-references. That's how reverse-engineering is done. 

Pseudo-code, as produced by Acucobol, is no more nor less difficult than machine
language assuming you have a definition of the pseudo language.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Decompiler for Acucobol

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-28T20:27:15+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<57lskvkfi438ahijppqa3emucrj5e0j5kp@4ax.com>`

```

wrote:

>"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>language assuming you have a definition of the pseudo language. 
Robert,

As we are dealing with Acucobol IF the programs were compiled with
"source debbuging" on then the FULL source will be available.

If this is the case, and it is easy to verify by trying to run the
program in debug mode, then it is just a question of paging down the
source and copy_and_paste into another file.



Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Decompiler for Acucobol

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-28T15:34:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308281434.2dbec20b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<761041e6.0308271408.1d867334@posting.google.com> <x3a3b.16056$Ih1.5550705@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <3f4e4819.402754800@news.central.cox.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> >You cannot get COBOL source code from executables. That would be like
> >getting 'cow' from hamburger.
> 
> If it was compiled with DEBUG information in the executable, you can recover
> data and paragraph names. 

That is not necessarily true.  MF, for example, puts the debug data
into a _Separate_ file name.IDY and uses the actual source files for
the animation of source.

While _some_ compilers, notably C ones, put debug information into the
executable, do you have any _factual_ information on AccuCobol on this
point ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Decompiler for Acucobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-08-29T01:29:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f4ea7c1.427247001@news.central.cox.net>`
- **References:** `<761041e6.0308271408.1d867334@posting.google.com> <x3a3b.16056$Ih1.5550705@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <3f4e4819.402754800@news.central.cox.net> <217e491a.0308281434.2dbec20b@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>the animation of source.

In that case, debug information is not in the executable.

>While _some_ compilers, notably C ones, put debug information into the
>executable, do you have any _factual_ information on AccuCobol on this
>point ?

Frederico Fonseca provided it. Many Cobol compilers, notably IBM, do link some
debugging information into the executable. If not data names, at least a
cross-reference between line numbers and addresses.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Decompiler for Acucobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "JJ" <jj@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-29T22:13:35-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2g2dnZWLO7e7lc2iXTWJjQ@comcast.com>`

```
I use Acucobol and it does put the debugging information in the p-code
object file (i.e. there is only one file).

To me, the original post sounded like he wanted to decompile someone else's
Acucobol program and make changes (perhaps from a commercial package).  If
that is the case, most likely the software is not being shipped with
programs compiled with debug information.

recover
```

#### ↳ Re: Decompiler for Acucobol

- **From:** rrb_041303 <rrb_041303@cox.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-27T20:55:21-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<y2f3b.9069$QT5.7939@fed1read02>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<761041e6.0308271408.1d867334@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<761041e6.0308271408.1d867334@posting.google.com>`

```
Anand Ramamurthy wrote:
> I am looking for any available decompiler for Acucobol.
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any help appreciated.

The only way to retrieve the source code is if the program is compiled 
into debug mode. What you do is call Acucorp Support. They will give you 
an e-mail address to e-mail the debug mode compiled object to. After 
they have retrieved the source code they will e-mail it back to you. You 
will have to provide your Acucorp Account number.
```

<a id="t-4027e41b86"></a>
## Thread: Indent Blues - the woe of auto. conversions to COBOL from 4th gen. lang. — 9 this month / 24 total

### Indent Blues - the woe of auto. conversions to COBOL from 4th gen. lang.

- **From:** noname21@iname.com (J.B. Martin)
- **Date:** 2003-08-29T02:48:51-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fed16812.0308290148.50d4a43a@posting.google.com>`

```
The company I'm working for has decided to stop using it's fourth
generation language (which will not be supported after 2004), and go
back to COBOL II.

Of course this means that all existing code has to be converted to
COBOL, and to do this they have bought a program from a company in
Norway.  With homemade pre- and post converters the program more or
less works.

We've run out of time to perfect the process, and have to show some
results, which means that a number of us are correcting the last 20
types of errors by hand.

Here's the rub - one of the things that the source code conversion
program didn't do well, is code indentation.  In the complex
if/evaluate/perform structures created by the conversion program, a
lot of the code has unnecessarily been pushed far to the right.

 The bosses don't care about the way the code looks, but reading this
junk is a nightmare. I've been unsuccessfully looking for a program
that could redo the indentation.

Anybody know of a (preferably free) program that could do the job??

- J.B.
```
