#### ↳ Re: Indent Blues - the woe of auto. conversions to COBOL from 4th gen. lang.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-29T17:00:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uEL3b.275$EW1.232@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<fed16812.0308290148.50d4a43a@posting.google.com>`

```
"J.B. Martin" <noname21@iname.com> wrote in message
news:fed16812.0308290148.50d4a43a@posting.google.com...
> The company I'm working for has decided to stop using it's fourth
> generation language (which will not be supported after 2004), and go
> back to COBOL II.
>
Just a point of clairification:

I hope you aren't doing a conversion *to* IBM's VS COBOL II compiler.  That
compiler is not currently supported by IBM and hasn't been for a couple of
years.  Although the OBJECT code created by it is - IF AND ONLY IF you are
using LE (Language3 Environment) for the link-edit and run-time library.

The currently supported (by IBM) compiler is "Enterprise COBOL for z/OS and
OS/390".

This won't help your "indentation" issue, but if you are doing a conversion
TO "VS COBOL II" because of a future end-of-support issue, this seems ODD
(at best) to me.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Indent Blues - the woe of auto. conversions to COBOL from 4th gen. lang.

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2003-08-29T19:51:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030829155131.20965.00000062@mb-m21.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<uEL3b.275$EW1.232@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net>`

```
A couple of simple ideas to add to the collection of excellent responses you
already have. 

1) if any of the tools you are using was intended for multiple platforms or
infact you used it on a PC, there is atleast some chance that intermediate
files holding your 'converted/generated' code have tabs rather than spaces.
Tabs can interact with editors and filters in a way that makes some lines look
way over to the right (or in filtration actually instantiate big groups of
spaces to accomplish the 'tabbing'). 

Do you know if the indentation is actually spaces in all intermediate files, or
is there any chance that a tabs are  in there? If this is a tab problem you may
have easy solutions.

2) The other idea is to just write a simple fixer rather than a perfect fixer.
assuming no posted suggestion gets you to a pretty printer that can create the
proper output. 
Instead of writing something that understands all of COBOL or all of the
procedure division just think in terms of finding large sets of lines that are
way over to the right and just shift them enmass.  It may not be pretty, but
there is a chance that it would partially solve the problem of making the code
_more_ easily viewed. This way of looking at the problem gives you the chance
of getting something quick an economical.  If you parameterize it you could let
it be more aggressive on some programs selectively.

If the indentation you are getting offends you only because some things are
just way too far
right, you may have a really easy solution available to you with custom code to
fix the problem. If the intentation is only quatitatively problematic, but the
overall scheme is reasonable; you could just take all x space groups and reduce
them to y, recursively for
each line. Bothering to respect only the B margin. If the relative indentation
in the original is acceptable, the relative indentation in the result should be
okay.

If you cannot find ready tools also orient yourself in a divide and conquer
looking at the Data Division as a different problem than the Procedure
Division.

If you are in an IBM ISPF environment there is 
some chance that EDIT Change All commands can partially repair sever
indentation (but not
COBOL intelligently). We're talking manual, and not automatic as you requested.
But ISPF EDIT is truely powerful.  If all else fails ask for help with ISPF if
you need that (you may be quite knowledgeable in that area already, I don't
mean to imply otherwise).  The edit sessions could be a semi-automatic
approach. You can limit them in such a way that they effect only the columns to
the right of the B margin (You can use profile attributes to keep the integrity
of the various kinds of line numbers).

Likewise, if any of this material is in PC readable form and you can get to
Unix you can consider scripting with sed/awk to do some mass change of
excessive indentation. (This too would lake COBOL wisdom, but may bring a large
portion of the code into more acceptable condition economically).

Look hard at the tab issue if it has not occured to you yet.

Best Wishes,
Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Indent Blues - the woe of auto. conversions to COBOL from 4th gen. lang.

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2003-08-31T19:44:40-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0308311844.e195df6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<uEL3b.275$EW1.232@newsread1.news.atl.earthlink.net> <20030829155131.20965.00000062@mb-m21.aol.com>`

```
Hi Rob,

Just to clarify your comments re. ISPF. ISPF macros make it possible
to process every member (or selected members) in a PDS. They also
provide logical capabilites, while allowing use of the full range
(almost) of ISPF commands using variables as cmd params.

Regards, Jack.  


rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk) wrote in message news:<20030829155131.20965.00000062@mb-m21.aol.com>...

> already have.

> RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

<a id="t-ab3f0136c0"></a>
## Thread: Reg: Copy records from one file to another based on contents of a third file. — 5 this month / 8 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Reg: Copy records from one file to another based on contents of a third file.

- **From:** mail_ssb@yahoo.com (ssb)
- **Date:** 2003-08-29T06:30:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<de8e098d.0308290530.3c4665a0@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

Suppose I have a simple flat-file, say File-C which contains just one
field: Name of a person.

File-A contains many records one of which is Name. Now I wish to copy
all records from File-A to a new File-B only if the Name present in
the record of File-A is availble in the list of Names in File-C.

Can we use File Aid JCL (or Insync) to do this or any form of JCL to
achieve this..??

I wish to avoid writing a cobol program which can of course achieve
this.

Thanks...
```

#### ↳ Re: Reg: Copy records from one file to another based on contents of a third file.

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-08-29T22:55:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f4fd917.505424931@news.central.cox.net>`
- **References:** `<de8e098d.0308290530.3c4665a0@posting.google.com>`

```
mail_ssb@yahoo.com (ssb) wrote:


>Suppose I have a simple flat-file, say File-C which contains just one
>field: Name of a person.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>achieve this..??

You can do it easily with SQL. Load A and C into DB2 temp tables, extract the
matches (B) with a SELECT * FROM A WHERE A.Name IN (SELECT Name FROM C);
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Reg: Copy records from one file to another based on contents of a third file.

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>

```

That would be my solution too <G>.

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Copy records from one file to another based on contents of a third file.

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-30T12:11:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Av04b.17143$Ih1.6167182@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<de8e098d.0308290530.3c4665a0@posting.google.com>`

```
"ssb" <mail_ssb@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:de8e098d.0308290530.3c4665a0@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> achieve this..??

Yes.

FD Work-File
   RecordType     X(01)
   Name           X(30)
  Original Record-A contents

Step one:
 Using file A as input, create work file-A using the OUTREC/OUTFIL option of
SyncSort/DFSORT
 Fill record type at position 1 with "Z".
Step two
 Using file B AS input, create workfile-B. Fill record type at position one
with "A" Or anything which will sort prior to "Z"). Fill original record
type with spaces, nulls or anything (you won't be using it)

Step three
Concatenate workfile-A and workfile-B as input. Sort on name+ RecordType
(2,30,A,1,1,A). This will get you a file like:
Aname   <filler>
Zname   Original Record
Aname   <filler>
Zname   Original Record
Aname   <filler>
Zname   Original Record

Step four
Sort output of step three by name only; specify preservation of original
order and direct duplicates to a file.  The duplicates will be all the type
"Z" records where a type "A" record exists. The duplicates file will look
like:
Zname   Original Record
Zname   Original Record
Zname   Original Record

Step five.
Using the duplicates file created in step 4 input, strip the sort key from
the record. Output will be original File-A records where the name existed in
file-c.

Detailed syntax left to OP as exercise.

If your original file-A contains duplicate names this method fails. Of
course, you did not specify this possibility in your summary of your
'challenge.'   But there are variations of this you could manufacture to
cover this case.

MCM
```

---

<a id="t-7c28a6801f"></a>
## Thread: Sorting a whole lotta records — 37 this month / 123 total

### Sorting a whole lotta records

- **From:** "Gonzo" <ckhamel1961@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-29T18:10:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com>`

```
I am assisting a colleague of mine in trying to design a way of sorting a
whole bunch of records. We are talking over 400 billion... yes, you read
that correctly.... billion. This is part of a conversion effort from IMS to
DB2, so this only has to run one time within a three-hour window. A caveat
here is that most the records are different lengths, but are not variable as
COBOL defines variable (in other words, without a length descriptor at the
front of the record).

This is running on a MVS mainframe system, so we also have the benefit of
external sort power like DFSORT. Our initial thoughts here is to take the
sort completely out of COBOL and using DFSORT with the exits to handle the
different sizes and imbedding a standard sort key.

Sorry, I don't have a lot more details at the moment. We're designing this
right now over the next week, so really I'm just trying to get some ideas
here right now. Just what is the best way to sort a large number of records
of different sizes in a short amount of time?

Thanks in advance for your thoughts and ideas.
```

- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com>`

```

Please let us know how successful you are at doing that!
```

```

Uh huh.

> DB2, so this only has to run one time within a three-hour window.

300 hours is more like it.

> A caveat
> here is that most the records are different lengths, but are not variable
as
> COBOL defines variable (in other words, without a length descriptor at
the
> front of the record).
>
> This is running on a MVS mainframe system, so we also have the benefit of
> external sort power like DFSORT. Our initial thoughts here is to take the
> sort completely out of COBOL and using DFSORT with the exits to handle
the
> different sizes and imbedding a standard sort key.

Which will take no time at all?

> Sorry, I don't have a lot more details at the moment.

That is quite obvious.

> We're designing this
> right now over the next week, so really I'm just trying to get some ideas
> here right now. Just what is the best way to sort a large number of
records
> of different sizes in a short amount of time?

Partition the files based on the high order sort position
or positions in your case.
>
> Thanks in advance for your thoughts and ideas.

Given a choice, I'd vote for mendacity over veracity here.
I've been involved in major corporate acquisitions
and their related conversions, so scale doesn't faze me a bit.
However, 400 billion records is more than I care to accept
at face value.  A 3 hour sort is categorically impossible.

I also find it incomprehensible that a project of this magnitude
would be entrusted to someone who's first thought is to ask
for assistance in a Newsgroup.

Unless of course, this is all genuine and you, a rookie,
are simply fishing for a blinder of an idea to dazzle
everyone with at the project kickoff meeting?
```

#### ↳ Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-08-29T19:32:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bio9oj$flv$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com>`

```

On 29-Aug-2003, "Gonzo" <ckhamel1961@yahoo.com> wrote:

> I am assisting a colleague of mine in trying to design a way of sorting a
> whole bunch of records. We are talking over 400 billion... yes, you read
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> front of the record).

In the olden dayz, we handled too-large sorts by breaking the task up.    We
would sort a bunch of records onto one tape, a bunch onto another tape, etc.  
Then we would merge the tapes.   If there were more data than tape drives, this
process would be repeated.

Now space is cheap.  But that process can still be used - and you can make the
different record types match by adding sort headers.   How, exactly does your
variable record format work?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

- **From:** "Gonzo" <ckhamel1961@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-29T23:03:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eZQ3b.223$Ol6.178@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com> <bio9oj$flv$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Thanks, Howard, for the professional response, although you're probably
still scratching your head wondering if I'm serious or not. With a couple of
responses I just saw to my post, I would have thought I was Andrew Dice Clay
or George Carlin spouting off another inflammatory racial remark or
something. Of course, I have to put their responses into perspective....
I've been Assembler and COBOL programming before they were in diapers. I
have nothing to prove, and my paycheck is large enough to ignore them. I
didn't get laid off, either.

> In the olden dayz, we handled too-large sorts by breaking the task up.
We
> would sort a bunch of records onto one tape, a bunch onto another tape,
etc.
> Then we would merge the tapes.   If there were more data than tape drives,
this
> process would be repeated.

Yes, I would agree with this response, but probably using disk in this case.
Breaking up the task into smaller pieces is a conceivable option that we're
looking at. Over the last couple of hours, however, I've learned that this
job is a MERGE, not a sort, as we originally thought. (Sorry, folks, for the
misinformation.) That makes the job a bit easier, but we still have the
mechanics of running the job within the window. I believe we'll have the
option of splitting the task over several LPARs.

> Now space is cheap.  But that process can still be used - and you can make
the
> different record types match by adding sort headers.   How, exactly does
your
> variable record format work?

What we think we're going to do for variable handling is to use one of the
exits in DFSORT to compute the length of the logical record then append a
length attribute to the front so that SORT will handle this as just a
regular variable-length record. That exit right now will be doing a bunch
more than that (i.e. header/trailer summaries, defining a static key, etc.).
At the end of the merge processing we'll have another exit to trim off the
added excess and go from there.

You're probably wondering why 400 billion records? That is actually eight
months of historical data that this company generates. They generate two
billion transactions per day. It wouldn't be hard to figure out what kind of
client we're working with here with that kind of transaction volume. It's an
everyday company that you see everywhere.

Yes, IBM could do this job, and in fact we do a whole lot of work for them.
Like this one.

Gonzo
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-30T07:08:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<y3Y3b.2252$zK3.75754@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com> <bio9oj$flv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <eZQ3b.223$Ol6.178@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com>`

```
"Gonzo" <ckhamel1961@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:eZQ3b.223$Ol6.178@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com...
> You're probably wondering why 400 billion records? That is actually eight
> months of historical data that this company generates. They generate two
> billion transactions per day.

Initially I thought this was 400,000,000,000,000 but I forget this isn't the
UK only forum.
I feel like this is much more accomplishable now that I've found out it's
the "other" billion.

But I wondered what this company was:

I thought maybe it was Cyber promotions.
But my sister in law can still access AOL which would have failed by now
under that spam stress load.

Then I thought that it might be Credit Card.
But mastercard does 15 million transactions a day (and they are accepted at
more places than American Express)

Then I thought maybe it was the a phone company
But that would mean that they were responsibe for an average output of 6.9
phone per person in the United States (290,342,554).  Given that this
population includes children unable to talk, Vonage customers, bickering
families, and of course the elderly (who have bought their own pay phones)
this seems unlikely.  But, the anti-phone-spam law isn't in effect yet so it
could be.

Then I thought maybe it was the Chinese phone companies.
But then, even though they have a population of 1 300 000 000 ...no one
mentioned a DBCS problem....

Then it dawned on me.  It's either number of total connection to
alt.binaries.multimedia.erotica, or they're tallying the money that is going
out of the US.

JCE
```

```

"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

> Then why are you asking this question?

In my young buck days, I reinvented a lot of wheels because I thought most
of the time I could do a better job coding than someone else. I suppose now
that I'm an old fart (a relative term in the last decade) that I believe
more in colaboration more than making more wheels. More than likely someone
has done what I've done before, and if not, someone's bound to do so soon.


> | I
> | have nothing to prove, and my paycheck is large enough to ignore them. I
> | didn't get laid off, either.
>
> But, you need help with this?

Not just "no" but "heck, no." We've already panned out a tentitive solution.
Again, I believe in that colaboration factor. Back in the days when the PC
was just a relative toy I was versed in all the latest cutting-edge
mainframe statistics, space and CPU-saving techniques and just about
anything to save me processing time. Alas, now that my job has me trying to
stay up with mainframe AND PC technology, sometimes some things slip by and
I miss them now and then.

Perhaps nowadays the best love/hate relationship I have is the Internet and
all this interconnectivity we have today. While I my seemingly silly
question to the Community, I risk having my reality being laughed at. That
okay, though.... I don't mind. But it got you thinking, didn't it? I at
minimum got you laughing, didn't I?

> | Yes, IBM could do this job, and in fact we do a whole lot of work for
> them.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> You are going to charge IBM for your effort, aren't you?

Yes, IBM will get our bill. And I do appreciate your offer to do the work
for a fee. The bonus to you here is that when we get done with this one, I
feel obligated now to post the results when we get done. And you'll get it
for FREE. Stay tuned.

> BTW: You didn't describe VARIABLE length records.
> You described records of varying length.

 Yes, you are correct. There is a difference between variable-length records
and records of varying length.You'd be surprised (and then again, maybe not)
how many "professionals" I've run into that didn't know the difference.

Gonzo
```

#### ↳ Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

- **From:** Bat Guano <bat.guano@talk21dotcom>
- **Date:** 2003-08-29T22:35:16+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vkvhol24qvdq44@corp.supernews.com>`

- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Gonzo wrote:
> I am assisting a colleague of mine in trying to design a way of sorting a
> whole bunch of records. We are talking over 400 billion... yes, you read
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Yeah right - 400,000,000,000. For a minute there, I thought you meant 
400 billion.

Anyway, all you need to do is extract the sort key and a pointer to the 
record and then sort that, which should save some time - not to mention 
sortwork areas.

It hardly matters whether you do it in or out of COBOL seeing as how 
COBOL invokes DFSORT anyway. Wouldn't SYNCSORT be quicker?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-29T21:41:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rMP3b.911$Om1.16@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com> <vkvhol24qvdq44@corp.supernews.com>`

```
What makes you think that SyncSort would be faster than DFSort (in a COBOL
internal SORT)?

If the FASTSRT compiler option is specified *and* if no Input or Output
Procedure is used and (this is likely to be relevant to this thread) the
SORTWK files must NOT be on tape, then either DFSort or SyncSort will do ALL
I/O.

Personally, I don't think that this sounds like a GOOD candidate (to me) for
an internal SORT - but  without knowing more, I won't swear to that.
```

- **Date:** 2003-08-29T22:55:04+01:00

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> What makes you think that SyncSort would be faster than DFSort (in a COBOL
> internal SORT)?
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Faster because SYNCSORT is far more efficient than DFSORT.

I'm sorry I can't remember the implications of FASTSRT. Are you sure its 
still relevant?

I guess you're saying that the SORTWK files will have to be on tape? 
Doesn't this beg the question of how big the sort key is?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ SyncSort vs DFSort (was: Sorting a whole lotta records

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-29T22:17:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GhQ3b.944$Om1.295@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com> <vkvhol24qvdq44@corp.supernews.com> <rMP3b.911$Om1.16@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net> <vkvitof2qi9617@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Bat Guano" <bat.guano@talk21dotcom> wrote in message
news:vkvitof2qi9617@corp.supernews.com...
> William M. Klein wrote:
<snip>
> Faster because SYNCSORT is far more efficient than DFSORT.
>
> I'm sorry I can't remember the implications of FASTSRT. Are you sure its
> still relevant?
>

and your evidence for this is ????

As far as I know, there are some (specific) cases (with current releases of
each) where one is faster than the other, but there is certainly no evidence
(that I am aware of) that either of these products is "far more efficient"
than the other.  I believe that several "independent" benchmarks have been
done each time one or the other comes out with a new release and is almost
ALWAYS the "most recent" release (not one product over the other) that
"wins" over-all tests.

P.S. Yes, FASTSRT is VERY relevant for all current IBM compilers.  If it is
SPECIFIED (and the required situation applies) that the sort product  (ANY
sort product) does all I/O.  If NOFASTSRT is specified (or in effect) then
COBOL does the I/O and the sort product does the "sorting".  (This is a
"generalization" but describes the "normal" situation medium-well.)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

- **From:** "Gonzo" <ckhamel1961@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-29T23:28:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BkR3b.227$fG6.123@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com> <vkvhol24qvdq44@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Hi, Bat:

> Yeah right - 400,000,000,000. For a minute there, I thought you meant
> 400 billion.
>

Pretty funny, huh? I thought so initially when my colleague told me that.
Check my post back to Howard Brazee... that might help explain that a bit.
I've approached a billion in some of my other conversion efforts, but this
one certainly takes the cake.

> Anyway, all you need to do is extract the sort key and a pointer to the
> record and then sort that, which should save some time - not to mention
> sortwork areas.

I wish it would be that simple. Part of the process, however, is building a
temporary key that we can sort uniformly to all the records. If you read my
re-post, this turns out to be a merge rather than a sort. That's helpful in
processing, but 1) all our sources have to be in order, and 2) we still have
to have a uniform key applying to all the records.

>
> It hardly matters whether you do it in or out of COBOL seeing as how
> COBOL invokes DFSORT anyway. Wouldn't SYNCSORT be quicker?
>

When I'm referring to SORT in the context here, I maybe should have referred
to SORT/DFSORT/SYNCSORT as a whole. Since we work on contract on our
client's own computers, we're at the mercy of what utilities they have
available to us. Syncsort *seems* to work faster, has more bells/whistles
for usage, but we still have to work with what we're given.

Gonzo
```

#### ↳ Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-08-29T15:03:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0308291403.530033c4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com>`

```
"Gonzo" <ckhamel1961@yahoo.com> wrote

> I am assisting a colleague of mine in trying to design a way of sorting a
> whole bunch of records. We are talking over 400 billion... yes, you read
> that correctly.... billion. 

If there was one byte per record and no overhead at all then that is
400 Gigabytes of disk space.

Given, say, an average of 100 bytes per record plus 20 bytes overhead
for slack bytes, sizes, index then you are talking about 50 Terabytes.

Given that some filesystems have a 1 or 2 terabyte limit and that IMS
probably only has 31 bit addressing at most then I don't believe that
these exist.

Maybe there are roomfulls of tapes of transactions that over a few
decades have accumulated that many records.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-30T19:15:01-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-E026FD.19150030082003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com> <217e491a.0308291403.530033c4@posting.google.com> <1fh1lvs4r45ch5g25ifj2tktplpjieu76h@4ax.com>`

```
In article <1fh1lvs4r45ch5g25ifj2tktplpjieu76h@4ax.com>,
 SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com> wrote:

> On 29 Aug 2003 15:03:39 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
> enlightened us:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> in a day.  Someone is pulling someone's leg here.

Your stat might be a bit out of date -- like three decades.  We have 
measured transaction rates well over 300 credit card transactions per 
second - sustained throughout a day.

It ins't 2 billion a day, but it is pretty huge.  And we do other 
things, ATM, VRU, EFT...it adds up.  We aren't even the largest 
processor around...

BTW, the technology that both DB2 and IMS are based on, VSAM, has 
allowed 64-bit datasets for almost a decade.  Even if the DB2/IMS tasks 
didn't take advantage of 64-bit memory the underlying datasets can and 
do allow for really huge databases.

But there is no way they are getting it done in three hours...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

- **From:** "Gonzo" <ckhamel1961@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-31T05:13:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Utf4b.451$rf4.185@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com> <217e491a.0308291403.530033c4@posting.google.com> <1fh1lvs4r45ch5g25ifj2tktplpjieu76h@4ax.com>`

```

"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com> wrote:

> I find the 400 billion number too incredible to believe as is the 2
> billion a day transaction figure the original poster stated.  All EFT
> processing in the United States approaches 2 billion...in a year, not
> a day.  Worldwide credit card transactions would not reach 2 billion
> in a day.  Someone is pulling someone's leg here.

It's been interesting reading all the remarks from folks scoffing at the
numbers I tossed around here. I suppose that if one works enough years in
I/T that one feels that he's seen just about everything. Then something
comes around that makes you look twice and really wonder if its real. People
used to think the earth was flat... people used to say we'd never get to the
moon. Heck, if someone told me in the '70s that one day a computer called a
laptop would have more processing and memory power than the IBM 360 I was
working on at the time, I would have laughed them out of the building!

Back in my former job life in California, I worked for a bank that saw the
birth of ATMs, EFT, Fedwire, and switches. (I shudder to think that some of
my programming back then might still be in use today, but that's another
story!) As a customer we tend to think that a single swipe of a card
initiates a single transaction, since we see just one charge on our
statement. In the back office processing, however, there's a lot more going
on. A single swipe could generate a dozen or more related transactions in
the entire audit trail.... not just the original request, but a check to see
if it's a stolen or frozen card, what was the location of the transaction,
if its from a foreign destination, then a currency conversion takes place.
Does it require a manual authorization? Was a PIN used and was it valid? If
it's a debit card, are funds needing to be transferred? I could go on and
on, but I think you might get the picture. These are the types of
information that banks and card companies require to backup the single
swipe, especially if you happen to challenge that charge and say you didn't
make it in the first place.

I'm sorry if I told you stuff you already knew, and as I've said previously
I've got nothing to prove. Mine was a simple post, and if you (and everybody
else) got a laugh out of my reality, that's okay. I'll laugh, too.... I look
back and see how silly it seems on the surface. But, then, as a contractor,
doing the stupid and silly stuff for my clients helps keeps bread on the
table and videogames for my PS2. We all have to be a little crazy to be in
this business.

I hope that helps my sincerity level. I'll even buy you a cup of coffee if
that'll help next time you're in Northwest Indiana.


Gonzo
```

#### ↳ Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSpam@Anon.ORG>
- **Date:** 2003-08-29T17:21:56-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AGudnYCTA4ibT9KiXTWQkQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com>`

```
> Thanks in advance for your thoughts and ideas.

I doubt you're going to do this in three hours but you have a heck of
lot better chance of getting it done at all on the mainframe than you
ever would on even the largest server.

I would think the biggest problem would be SORTWK areas. I'm not that
familiar with DFSORT but SYNCSORT has an option they call MAXSORT which
is designed for sorting large datasets. DFSORT may have something similar.
It breaks the sort up into segments creating smaller size datasets of
sorted data in work datasets usually on tape. Then does a humongous
merge at the end. It takes checkpoints during the process so you can
restart without having to go back to the beginning if you get a tape
error or something. I'll tell you that with all that tape I-O it isn't
all that fast but with that kind of volume it may be your only chance.
Research your SORT manual thoroughly because this is a bit complicated
to set up.

Also if you need this fast you should get your systems folks to give this
sort job the absolute highest system priority. Running as regular batch
isn't going to do it.

I wish you luck.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-08-30T13:26:36+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f4ffe37_4@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Gonzo,

It is pretty obvious that you won't accomplish this in a 3 hour window, on a
Client's machine, UNLESS you break it into chunks that can be
multiprocessed. Howard was right in what he said. I would only add that the
merge phases could be accomplished in parallel if you arrange access to one
or more other CPUs.

Another point to consider...As it is all sequential processing, go for the
largest possible block size you can manage <G>.

I would be (agreeably) surprised if you manage to do it on one CPU (even if
that CPU is multithreading.)

Good Luck!

Pete.

"Gonzo" <ckhamel1961@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com...

to

> here is that most the records are different lengths, but are not variable
as
> COBOL defines variable (in other words, without a length descriptor at the

> here right now. Just what is the best way to sort a large number of
records
> of different sizes in a short amount of time?
```

#### ↳ Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-30T07:35:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qtY3b.2280$zK3.78358@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com>`

```

"Gonzo" <ckhamel1961@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com...
> whole bunch of records. We are talking over 400 billion... yes, you read
> that correctly.... billion. This is part of a conversion effort from IMS
to
> DB2, so this only has to run one time within a three-hour window. A caveat
> here is that most the records are different lengths, but are not variable
as
> COBOL defines variable (in other words, without a length descriptor at the

What on earth is the value of storing this much data in DB2 unless this is a
transaction level warehouse (which usually defeats the purpose of a
warehouse).
An 8 month data mart for this level of detail seems nutz - though apparently
this is the future...never enough detail....
There is a raw storage to actual storage ratio of  1.5 - 3 (this if for
indexing etc etc) so storage will be far more than just the data.

I would have thought that the DB2 setup and configuration would be FAR more
important.

A LOAD of DB2 will take *WAY* more than 3 hours.  I know you didn't ask
this, but we routinely load 1-2 million rows and it takes minutes and not
seconds.  To get 400,000 million would therefore take you a lot longer than
a few hours (granted we don't use any high performance load routines).

The sorting (well you said merging) is just the first step of you ETL.
I don't know why you need to merge the data...as you will surely have to
distribute the data again anyway.

Here is a link that was the performance benchmark for a 10 TB decision
warehouse....you can look here they have 5  P690 32 processor machines with
512 36 Gig 10000 rpm raid *each*.
The load of data took 10hrs + and the total system price is $15,000,000 over
3 years.

The load took 10hrs + with considerably less rows than what you have ~80
billion in total.
You must be working on the mac daddy of setups.

http://www.tpc.org/results/FDR/tpch/p690_10000GB_160proc_FDR.pdf


JCE
```

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)

#### ↳ Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-30T22:35:18+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<juu1lvcmj5e2p3eue0eghvlfts9lumq7ke@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com>`

```
On Fri, 29 Aug 2003 18:10:29 GMT "Gonzo" <ckhamel1961@yahoo.com> wrote:

:>I am assisting a colleague of mine in trying to design a way of sorting a
:>whole bunch of records. We are talking over 400 billion... yes, you read
:>that correctly.... billion. This is part of a conversion effort from IMS to
:>DB2, so this only has to run one time within a three-hour window. A caveat
:>here is that most the records are different lengths, but are not variable as
:>COBOL defines variable (in other words, without a length descriptor at the
:>front of the record).

:>This is running on a MVS mainframe system, so we also have the benefit of
:>external sort power like DFSORT. Our initial thoughts here is to take the
:>sort completely out of COBOL and using DFSORT with the exits to handle the
:>different sizes and imbedding a standard sort key.

:>Sorry, I don't have a lot more details at the moment. We're designing this
:>right now over the next week, so really I'm just trying to get some ideas
:>here right now. Just what is the best way to sort a large number of records
:>of different sizes in a short amount of time?

Many have addressed other issues, such as the remarkable hardware that you
have as well as your ability to increase the speed of light .......

As you are downloading from a hierarchical data system to a relative data
system, it would seem reasonable that your data is currently sorted.

If it isn't currently sorted, why do you have the need for it to be sorted
before the DB2 load?
```

```
On 30 Aug 2003 21:25:33 -0400 docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

:>In article <juu1lvcmj5e2p3eue0eghvlfts9lumq7ke@4ax.com>,
:>Binyamin Dissen  <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote:

:>>As you are downloading from a hierarchical data system to a relative data
:>>system, it would seem reasonable that your data is currently sorted.

:>>If it isn't currently sorted, why do you have the need for it to be sorted
:>>before the DB2 load?

:>It just might possibly be, Mr Dissen, that there are differences between 
:>the IMS and DB2 orders.

Unless you assert that the processing will be quite different, no.

RDMS's have less of a concept of sequence than HDB's.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

- **From:** "Gonzo" <ckhamel1961@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-31T03:59:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%oe4b.444$Pd3.44@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com> <juu1lvcmj5e2p3eue0eghvlfts9lumq7ke@4ax.com>`

```

"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote:

> As you are downloading from a hierarchical data system to a relative data
> system, it would seem reasonable that your data is currently sorted.

You are correct, Binyamin.  If you read my repost to Mr. Brazee, the process
is actually a merge, rather than a sort, as I originally stated... my bad.
The merge incorporates about two dozen related system files, with a combined
total mass of my figure of 400 billion records. The files are already in
order within their respective realms.


> If it isn't currently sorted, why do you have the need for it to be sorted
> before the DB2 load?

The merge is just an initial step for the conversion process. Since my
colleague only asked for my assistance for feedback, I don't have all the
particular details he's trying to nail down now. Likely after the merge and
key standardization, we would typically go through a normalization process,
the actual conversion run, then load to the target system.

Gonzo
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-31T09:07:40+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6j33lv49rqncbdnga2pk9k6ms47i9ane0d@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com> <juu1lvcmj5e2p3eue0eghvlfts9lumq7ke@4ax.com> <%oe4b.444$Pd3.44@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com>`

```
On Sun, 31 Aug 2003 03:59:55 GMT "Gonzo" <ckhamel1961@yahoo.com> wrote:

:>"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote:

:>> As you are downloading from a hierarchical data system to a relative data
:>> system, it would seem reasonable that your data is currently sorted.

:>You are correct, Binyamin.  If you read my repost to Mr. Brazee, the process
:>is actually a merge, rather than a sort, as I originally stated... my bad.
:>The merge incorporates about two dozen related system files, with a combined
:>total mass of my figure of 400 billion records. The files are already in
:>order within their respective realms.

In that case you can probably run the merge in parallel to the load, using an
E35 exit - in other words you do not have to materialize the combined file.

:>> If it isn't currently sorted, why do you have the need for it to be sorted
:>> before the DB2 load?

:>The merge is just an initial step for the conversion process. Since my
:>colleague only asked for my assistance for feedback, I don't have all the
:>particular details he's trying to nail down now. Likely after the merge and
:>key standardization, we would typically go through a normalization process,
:>the actual conversion run, then load to the target system.

And I suggest test runs with subsets of the data.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sorting a whole lotta records

- **From:** Steve Thompson <steve_nospam_t@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-08-30T23:35:48-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.19bb37239cdb80b5989694@News.CIS.DFN.DE>`
- **References:** `<pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com>`

```
In article <pGM3b.169$XL1.106@newssvr23.news.prodigy.com>, 
ckhamel1961@yahoo.com says...
> I am assisting a colleague of mine in trying to design a way of sorting a
> whole bunch of records. We are talking over 400 billion... yes, you read
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> front of the record).
<snip>

First, I'd write a COBOL program to CALL the SORT(MERGE). Then I 
think I'd start looking real hard at SYNCSORT.

Next, I'd make sure that the LPAR(s) being used are blessed with 
z/OS 1.2 or later, >3GB of C-STORE, FICON channels and 3390-9 
(or larger DASD) backed by RAID-5 (e.g., SHARK, EMC, etc.).

[I've noticed that this is a MERGE not a SORT.]

If you can break this up into several JOBs that you can then 
MERGE all the output into, you just might make your 3 hour 
window. However, even if you could do this with ALC, the I/O 
delays are probably what will kill you.
```
